The Request Object (`req`) represents the HTTP request in Express.js. It provides properties and methods for accessing request headers, URL components, client information, query parameters, and body data. The request object is an enhanced version of Node.js's `http.IncomingMessage` with additional Express-specific functionality. For information about the Response Object and its methods, see [Response Object](#2.3). For details on the complete request-response lifecycle, see [Request-Response Lifecycle](#2.4). --- ## Request Prototype Architecture The Express request object is created by extending Node.js's `http.IncomingMessage.prototype`. During application initialization, Express creates a prototype chain that allows all request objects to inherit Express-specific properties and methods. **Diagram: Request Object Creation and Prototype Chain** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Initialization" createApp["createApplication()
(lib/express.js:36)"] appReq["app.request
Object.create(req)"] reqProto["req prototype
(lib/request.js:30)"] httpIncoming["http.IncomingMessage.prototype"] end subgraph "Request Handling" appHandle["app.handle(req, res)
(lib/application.js:152)"] setProto["Object.setPrototypeOf(req, app.request)
(lib/application.js:169)"] incomingReq["Incoming HTTP Request"] end subgraph "Request Object Properties" reqApp["req.app"] reqRes["req.res"] reqNext["req.next"] end createApp --> appReq reqProto --> httpIncoming appReq --> reqProto incomingReq --> appHandle appHandle --> setProto setProto --> appReq setProto --> reqApp setProto --> reqRes setProto --> reqNext reqApp -.references.-> createApp reqRes -.circular reference.-> Response Response["Response Object"] style reqProto fill:#f9f9f9 style appReq fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/application.js:152-178]() The request prototype is defined in `lib/request.js` as `Object.create(http.IncomingMessage.prototype)`. Each Express application creates its own request prototype instance that inherits from this base. When handling requests, `app.handle()` sets the prototype of the incoming request object to the application's request prototype using `Object.setPrototypeOf()`. --- ## Property Categories Overview The request object properties can be organized into several functional categories: **Diagram: Request Object Property Categories** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "req Object" req["Request Object Instance"] end subgraph "Header Access" reqGet["req.get(name)
req.header(name)
(lib/request.js:63-83)"] end subgraph "Content Negotiation" reqAccepts["req.accepts(types)
(lib/request.js:127-130)"] reqAcceptsEnc["req.acceptsEncodings()
(lib/request.js:140-143)"] reqAcceptsChar["req.acceptsCharsets()
(lib/request.js:171-174)"] reqAcceptsLang["req.acceptsLanguages()
(lib/request.js:185-187)"] end subgraph "Client Information" reqProtocol["req.protocol (getter)
(lib/request.js:297-315)"] reqSecure["req.secure (getter)
(lib/request.js:326-328)"] reqIp["req.ip (getter)
(lib/request.js:340-343)"] reqIps["req.ips (getter)
(lib/request.js:357-366)"] reqHost["req.host (getter)
(lib/request.js:418-431)"] reqHostname["req.hostname (getter)
(lib/request.js:444-458)"] end subgraph "URL Components" reqPath["req.path (getter)
(lib/request.js:403-405)"] reqQuery["req.query (getter)
(lib/request.js:230-241)"] reqSubdomains["req.subdomains (getter)
(lib/request.js:383-394)"] end subgraph "Request State" reqFresh["req.fresh (getter)
(lib/request.js:469-486)"] reqStale["req.stale (getter)
(lib/request.js:497-499)"] reqXhr["req.xhr (getter)
(lib/request.js:508-511)"] end subgraph "Other Methods" reqIs["req.is(types)
(lib/request.js:269-281)"] reqRange["req.range(size, options)
(lib/request.js:214-218)"] end req --> reqGet req --> reqAccepts req --> reqAcceptsEnc req --> reqAcceptsChar req --> reqAcceptsLang req --> reqProtocol req --> reqSecure req --> reqIp req --> reqIps req --> reqHost req --> reqHostname req --> reqPath req --> reqQuery req --> reqSubdomains req --> reqFresh req --> reqStale req --> reqXhr req --> reqIs req --> reqRange style req fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:1-528]() --- ## Header Access The request object provides methods to retrieve HTTP request headers. ### `req.get(name)` and `req.header(name)` These methods retrieve the value of a request header by name (case-insensitive). They are aliases of each other. **Special case:** The `Referrer` and `Referer` headers are treated interchangeably, allowing either spelling. **Implementation:** [lib/request.js:63-83]() | Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | `name` | String | Header name (case-insensitive) | | Returns | String | Header value or `undefined` | **Example Usage:** ```javascript req.get('Content-Type'); // => "text/plain" req.get('content-type'); // => "text/plain" req.get('Referrer'); // Returns either referrer or referer header req.get('Something'); // => undefined ``` **Validation:** The method throws `TypeError` if `name` is not provided or is not a string. **Sources:** [lib/request.js:63-83](), [test/req.host.js]() --- ## Content Negotiation Express provides methods for HTTP content negotiation based on the `Accept*` headers sent by the client. ### `req.accepts(types)` Determines if the request accepts the given content type(s). Uses the `accepts` module to parse the `Accept` header and return the best match. [lib/request.js:127-130]() **Returns:** The best matching type, an array of acceptable types, or `false` if none match. ### `req.acceptsEncodings(...encodings)` Checks if the given encodings are acceptable based on the `Accept-Encoding` header. [lib/request.js:140-143]() ### `req.acceptsCharsets(...charsets)` Checks if the specified charsets are acceptable based on the `Accept-Charset` header. [lib/request.js:171-174]() ### `req.acceptsLanguages(...languages)` Checks if the given languages are acceptable based on the `Accept-Language` header. [lib/request.js:185-187]() **Sources:** [lib/request.js:127-187]() --- ## Client Information and Trust Proxy Several request properties provide information about the client making the request. Many of these properties are affected by the `trust proxy` application setting. **Diagram: Trust Proxy Data Flow** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Settings" trustProxy["app.set('trust proxy', value)
(lib/application.js:370-380)"] compileTrust["compileTrust(value)
(lib/utils.js:194-214)"] trustProxyFn["app.get('trust proxy fn')"] end subgraph "Request Properties Affected" reqProtocol["req.protocol"] reqIp["req.ip"] reqIps["req.ips"] reqHost["req.host"] reqHostname["req.hostname"] reqSecure["req.secure"] end subgraph "HTTP Headers Checked" xForwardedProto["X-Forwarded-Proto"] xForwardedFor["X-Forwarded-For"] xForwardedHost["X-Forwarded-Host"] end subgraph "Trust Proxy Values" trustTrue["true: trust all proxies"] trustFalse["false: trust no proxies"] trustNumber["number: trust N hops"] trustString["string: IP/CIDR list"] trustArray["array: IP/CIDR list"] trustFn["function: custom logic"] end trustProxy --> compileTrust compileTrust --> trustProxyFn trustTrue -.option.-> compileTrust trustFalse -.option.-> compileTrust trustNumber -.option.-> compileTrust trustString -.option.-> compileTrust trustArray -.option.-> compileTrust trustFn -.option.-> compileTrust trustProxyFn --> reqProtocol trustProxyFn --> reqIp trustProxyFn --> reqIps trustProxyFn --> reqHost trustProxyFn --> reqHostname reqProtocol --> xForwardedProto reqIp --> xForwardedFor reqIps --> xForwardedFor reqHost --> xForwardedHost reqHostname --> xForwardedHost reqProtocol --> reqSecure style trustProxyFn fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:370-380](), [lib/utils.js:194-214](), [lib/request.js:297-458]() ### `req.protocol` Returns the protocol string `'http'` or `'https'`. When trust proxy is enabled, the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header is consulted. [lib/request.js:297-315]() **Behavior:** - Default: Uses `req.socket.encrypted` to determine protocol - With trust proxy: Checks `X-Forwarded-Proto` header first - Handles comma-separated values by taking the first entry ### `req.secure` Boolean shorthand for `req.protocol === 'https'`. [lib/request.js:326-328]() ### `req.ip` Returns the remote IP address of the client. Uses the `proxy-addr` module for trust proxy logic. [lib/request.js:340-343]() **Behavior:** - Default: Returns `req.socket.remoteAddress` - With trust proxy: Parses `X-Forwarded-For` header to extract client IP ### `req.ips` Returns an array of IP addresses from `X-Forwarded-For`, ordered from farthest to closest, excluding the socket address. [lib/request.js:357-366]() ### `req.host` Parses the `Host` header to a host string (including port if present). When trust proxy is enabled, checks `X-Forwarded-Host` first. [lib/request.js:418-431]() **Special handling:** - IPv6 literals are supported (e.g., `[::1]` or `[::1]:3000`) - Handles comma-separated values in `X-Forwarded-Host` ### `req.hostname` Returns the hostname without the port number. Extracts the hostname portion from `req.host`. [lib/request.js:444-458]() **Sources:** [lib/request.js:297-458](), [test/req.protocol.js](), [test/req.ip.js](), [test/req.ips.js](), [test/req.host.js](), [test/req.secure.js]() --- ## URL Components ### `req.path` Returns the pathname portion of the request URL. [lib/request.js:403-405]() Uses the `parseurl` module to parse `req.url`. ### `req.query` Returns the parsed query string as an object. This is a getter property that lazily parses the query string using the configured query parser. [lib/request.js:230-241]() **Diagram: Query String Parsing Flow** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Configuration" queryParser["app.set('query parser', value)
(lib/application.js:367-369)"] compileQP["compileQueryParser(value)
(lib/utils.js:162-184)"] queryParserFn["app.get('query parser fn')"] end subgraph "Parser Options" simple["'simple': querystring.parse"] extended["'extended': qs.parse with allowPrototypes"] disabled["false: returns empty object"] customFn["function: custom parser"] end subgraph "Request Query Access" reqQuery["req.query getter"] parseurl["parseurl(req).query"] queryString["Raw query string"] parsedObject["Parsed object"] end queryParser --> compileQP compileQP --> queryParserFn simple -.option.-> compileQP extended -.option.-> compileQP disabled -.option.-> compileQP customFn -.option.-> compileQP reqQuery --> queryParserFn reqQuery --> parseurl parseurl --> queryString queryParserFn --> parsedObject style queryParserFn fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:230-241](), [lib/utils.js:162-184](), [lib/application.js:367-369](), [test/req.query.js]() **Query Parser Settings:** | Setting | Description | Implementation | |---------|-------------|----------------| | `'simple'` (default) | Uses Node.js `querystring.parse()` | [lib/utils.js:172]() | | `'extended'` | Uses `qs.parse()` with complex object support | [lib/utils.js:177]() | | `false` | Disables query parsing, returns empty object | [lib/utils.js:174]() | | Custom function | User-provided parser function | [lib/utils.js:165-167]() | ### `req.subdomains` Returns an array of subdomains in the domain name. The `subdomain offset` setting determines how many parts to remove from the end. [lib/request.js:383-394]() **Example:** For domain `tobi.ferrets.example.com`: - With offset 2 (default): `["ferrets", "tobi"]` - With offset 3: `["tobi"]` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:383-394](), [lib/request.js:403-405](), [lib/request.js:230-241]() --- ## Content Type Checking ### `req.is(types)` Checks if the incoming request contains the `Content-Type` header field and matches the given MIME type(s). Uses the `type-is` module. [lib/request.js:269-281]() **Returns:** The matching type as a string, or `false` if no match, or `null` if no `Content-Type` header exists. **Example:** ```javascript // With Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8 req.is('html'); // => 'html' req.is('text/html'); // => 'text/html' req.is('text/*'); // => 'text/*' // With Content-Type: application/json req.is('json'); // => 'json' req.is('application/*'); // => 'application/*' req.is('html'); // => false ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:269-281]() --- ## Range Header Parsing ### `req.range(size, options)` Parses the `Range` header field using the `range-parser` module. [lib/request.js:214-218]() | Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | `size` | Number | Resource size in bytes | | `options` | Object | Options (e.g., `{ combine: true }`) | | Returns | Number/Array | Parsed ranges or error code | **Return values:** - `undefined`: No `Range` header present - `-1`: Unsatisfiable ranges - `-2`: Syntactically invalid header - Array: Valid range objects with `start` and `end` properties **Sources:** [lib/request.js:214-218]() --- ## Request State Properties ### `req.fresh` Boolean indicating whether the request is "fresh" based on `Last-Modified` and/or `ETag` headers. Uses the `fresh` module. [lib/request.js:469-486]() **Conditions for freshness:** - Request method must be `GET` or `HEAD` - Response status must be 2xx or 304 - `Last-Modified` and `ETag` headers match conditional headers in request **Used for conditional GET optimization:** If `req.fresh` is true, the response should return 304 Not Modified. ### `req.stale` Boolean opposite of `req.fresh`. [lib/request.js:497-499]() ### `req.xhr` Boolean indicating if the request was issued by an `XMLHttpRequest` (AJAX request). Checks if the `X-Requested-With` header equals `'xmlhttprequest'` (case-insensitive). [lib/request.js:508-511]() **Sources:** [lib/request.js:469-511](), [test/req.xhr.js]() --- ## Implementation: Lazy Property Evaluation Many request properties are implemented as getters using the `defineGetter()` helper function. This enables lazy evaluation—properties are only computed when accessed. **Diagram: Getter Property Implementation** ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "defineGetter Helper" helper["defineGetter(obj, name, getter)
(lib/request.js:521-527)"] defineProperty["Object.defineProperty()"] end subgraph "Property Descriptors" configurable["configurable: true"] enumerable["enumerable: true"] getFunc["get: getter function"] end subgraph "Getter Properties" query["req.query"] protocol["req.protocol"] secure["req.secure"] ip["req.ip"] ips["req.ips"] subdomains["req.subdomains"] path["req.path"] host["req.host"] hostname["req.hostname"] fresh["req.fresh"] stale["req.stale"] xhr["req.xhr"] end helper --> defineProperty defineProperty --> configurable defineProperty --> enumerable defineProperty --> getFunc query -.uses.-> helper protocol -.uses.-> helper secure -.uses.-> helper ip -.uses.-> helper ips -.uses.-> helper subdomains -.uses.-> helper path -.uses.-> helper host -.uses.-> helper hostname -.uses.-> helper fresh -.uses.-> helper stale -.uses.-> helper xhr -.uses.-> helper style helper fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:521-527]() The `defineGetter()` function wraps `Object.defineProperty()` to create configurable, enumerable getter properties. This pattern provides several benefits: 1. **Lazy evaluation:** Expensive computations only occur when the property is accessed 2. **Always current:** Properties that depend on mutable state (like headers) always reflect the current state 3. **Clean API:** Properties appear as simple attributes rather than method calls **Example property definition:** ```javascript defineGetter(req, 'protocol', function protocol(){ var proto = this.socket.encrypted ? 'https' : 'http'; var trust = this.app.get('trust proxy fn'); if (!trust(this.socket.remoteAddress, 0)) { return proto; } var header = this.get('X-Forwarded-Proto') || proto; var index = header.indexOf(','); return index !== -1 ? header.substring(0, index).trim() : header.trim(); }); ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:297-315](), [lib/request.js:521-527]() --- ## Summary Table: Request Object Properties and Methods | Category | Property/Method | Type | Affected by Trust Proxy | Sources | |----------|-----------------|------|-------------------------|---------| | **Headers** | `req.get(name)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:63-83]() | | **Content Negotiation** | `req.accepts(types)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:127-130]() | | | `req.acceptsEncodings()` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:140-143]() | | | `req.acceptsCharsets()` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:171-174]() | | | `req.acceptsLanguages()` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:185-187]() | | **Client Info** | `req.protocol` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:297-315]() | | | `req.secure` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:326-328]() | | | `req.ip` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:340-343]() | | | `req.ips` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:357-366]() | | | `req.host` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:418-431]() | | | `req.hostname` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:444-458]() | | **URL Components** | `req.path` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:403-405]() | | | `req.query` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:230-241]() | | | `req.subdomains` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:383-394]() | | **Request State** | `req.fresh` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:469-486]() | | | `req.stale` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:497-499]() | | | `req.xhr` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:508-511]() | | **Other** | `req.is(types)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:269-281]() | | | `req.range(size, options)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:214-218]() | **Sources:** [lib/request.js:1-528]() --- # Page: Response Object # Response Object
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for generating this wiki page: - [History.md](History.md) - [lib/application.js](lib/application.js) - [lib/express.js](lib/express.js) - [lib/request.js](lib/request.js) - [lib/response.js](lib/response.js) - [lib/utils.js](lib/utils.js) - [package.json](package.json) - [test/fixtures/% of dogs.txt](test/fixtures/% of dogs.txt) - [test/fixtures/.name](test/fixtures/.name) - [test/fixtures/blog/index.html](test/fixtures/blog/index.html) - [test/fixtures/name.txt](test/fixtures/name.txt) - [test/res.attachment.js](test/res.attachment.js) - [test/res.format.js](test/res.format.js) - [test/res.json.js](test/res.json.js) - [test/res.jsonp.js](test/res.jsonp.js) - [test/res.send.js](test/res.send.js) - [test/utils.js](test/utils.js)
The Response Object is one of the three core prototypes in Express (alongside Application and Request). It extends Node.js's `http.ServerResponse` with additional methods and properties for sending various types of HTTP responses, managing headers, handling cookies, rendering views, and performing redirects. This page documents the Response prototype, its methods, and how it integrates with the Application instance. For information about the Request Object, see [2.2](#2.2). For details on how the response flows through the middleware stack, see [2.4](#2.4). For specific response method implementations like `res.send()` and `res.json()`, see [5.1](#5.1). ## Response Prototype Structure The Response Object is defined as a prototype in [lib/response.js:42]() that extends Node.js's native `http.ServerResponse.prototype`. This prototype is then instantiated per application in [lib/express.js:50-52](), where each Express application creates its own response prototype with a reference back to the application instance. **Diagram: Response Object Prototype Chain and Initialization** ```mermaid graph TB ServerResponse["http.ServerResponse.prototype
(Node.js built-in)"] BaseRes["res prototype
(lib/response.js:42)
Object.create(http.ServerResponse.prototype)"] AppResponse["app.response
(lib/express.js:50-52)
Object.create(res, {app})"] ActualRes["Actual response object
(Node.js http.ServerResponse instance)"] ServerResponse -->|"inherits from"| BaseRes BaseRes -->|"Object.create"| AppResponse AppResponse -->|"Object.setPrototypeOf
(lib/application.js:170)"| ActualRes AppResponse -.->|"app property"| AppInstance["Application instance"] ActualRes -.->|"req property
(lib/application.js:166)"| Request["Request object"] BaseRes -->|"defines methods"| Methods["res.send()
res.json()
res.redirect()
res.render()
etc."] ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/express.js:50-52](), [lib/application.js:164-177]() When an HTTP request arrives, the Application's `handle()` method sets up the prototype chain by calling `Object.setPrototypeOf(res, this.response)` at [lib/application.js:170](). This allows the actual Node.js response object to access all Express response methods while maintaining access to the Application instance through `res.app`. ## Core Response Methods The Response Object provides several methods for sending different types of response bodies. These methods form a hierarchy where higher-level methods delegate to lower-level ones. **Diagram: Response Method Hierarchy** ```mermaid graph TB send["res.send(body)
(lib/response.js:125)"] json["res.json(obj)
(lib/response.js:232)"] jsonp["res.jsonp(obj)
(lib/response.js:260)"] sendStatus["res.sendStatus(code)
(lib/response.js:321)"] sendFile["res.sendFile(path)
(lib/response.js:371)"] download["res.download(path)
(lib/response.js:433)"] render["res.render(view)
(lib/response.js:894)"] redirect["res.redirect(url)
(lib/response.js:812)"] format["res.format(obj)
(lib/response.js:569)"] json --> send jsonp --> json sendStatus --> send download --> sendFile render --> appRender["app.render()
(lib/application.js:522)"] appRender --> send redirect --> format format --> send send --> stringify["stringify()
(lib/response.js:1023)"] send --> etagFn["app.get('etag fn')
(lib/response.js:161)"] send --> fresh["req.fresh
(lib/request.js:469)"] send --> end["res.end()
(Node.js http.ServerResponse)"] json --> stringify jsonp --> stringify ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218](), [lib/response.js:232-304](), [lib/response.js:321-328]() ### res.send() The `res.send()` method at [lib/response.js:125-218]() is the foundation for sending responses. It performs the following operations: 1. **Type Detection** ([lib/response.js:133-158]()): Determines content type based on the body type: - `string`: Sets `Content-Type` to `text/html` with charset - `number`, `boolean`, or plain `object`: Delegates to `res.json()` - `Buffer` or `ArrayBuffer.isView()`: Sets `Content-Type` to `application/octet-stream` - `null`: Converts to empty string 2. **Content-Length Calculation** ([lib/response.js:164-181]()): Calculates and sets the `Content-Length` header 3. **ETag Generation** ([lib/response.js:184-189]()): If the `etag fn` setting exists and no ETag header is already set, generates an ETag using the configured function 4. **Freshness Check** ([lib/response.js:192]()): If `req.fresh` is true (request has matching ETag or Last-Modified), sets status to 304 5. **Status Code Handling** ([lib/response.js:195-207]()): - **204/304**: Strips `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, and `Transfer-Encoding` headers, sends empty body - **205**: Sets `Content-Length` to `'0'`, removes `Transfer-Encoding`, sends empty body 6. **HEAD Method** ([lib/response.js:209-211]()): Skips body transmission for HEAD requests Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218]() ### res.json() and res.jsonp() The `res.json()` method at [lib/response.js:232-246]() stringifies the provided object using application settings: - `json replacer`: Custom replacer function for `JSON.stringify()` - `json spaces`: Indentation for pretty-printing - `json escape`: HTML-escaping for security (using `stringify()` helper at [lib/response.js:1023-1047]()) The `res.jsonp()` method at [lib/response.js:260-304]() extends `res.json()` with JSONP support: - Reads callback name from `req.query[app.get('jsonp callback name')]` (default: `'callback'`) - Sanitizes callback name by removing non-safe characters at [lib/response.js:286]() - Wraps JSON in callback function with security prologue `/**/ typeof callback === 'function' && callback(...);` - Sets `Content-Type` to `text/javascript` and adds `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` header Sources: [lib/response.js:232-304](), [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() ## Header Management The Response Object provides several methods for managing HTTP headers. | Method | Location | Purpose | |--------|----------|---------| | `res.set(field, val)` | [lib/response.js:664-686]() | Set header field(s), auto-adds charset to Content-Type | | `res.header(field, val)` | [lib/response.js:664]() | Alias for `res.set()` | | `res.get(field)` | [lib/response.js:696-698]() | Get header field value | | `res.append(field, val)` | [lib/response.js:629-641]() | Append value to header field | | `res.vary(field)` | [lib/response.js:875-879]() | Add field to Vary header | Sources: [lib/response.js:629-698](), [lib/response.js:875-879]() ### res.set() and Content-Type Handling The `res.set()` method at [lib/response.js:664-686]() has special handling for the `Content-Type` header. When setting `Content-Type`, it calls `mime.contentType(value)` at [lib/response.js:676]() to automatically add the appropriate charset if not already present. ```mermaid graph LR resSet["res.set('Content-Type', value)"] check{"Is field
'content-type'?"} mimeContentType["mime.contentType(value)
(mime-types module)"] setHeader["res.setHeader(field, value)"] resSet --> check check -->|"Yes"| mimeContentType check -->|"No"| setHeader mimeContentType --> setHeader ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:664-686]() ## Status Code Management The `res.status()` method at [lib/response.js:64-76]() sets the HTTP status code with strict validation: - **Type Check** ([lib/response.js:66-68]()): Throws `TypeError` if not an integer - **Range Check** ([lib/response.js:70-72]()): Throws `RangeError` if not between 100 and 999 - **Chainable**: Returns `this` for method chaining The `res.sendStatus()` method at [lib/response.js:321-328]() is a convenience method that sets the status, sets `Content-Type` to `text/plain`, and sends the status message as the body. Sources: [lib/response.js:64-76](), [lib/response.js:321-328]() ## Content Negotiation with res.format() The `res.format()` method at [lib/response.js:569-594]() performs content negotiation based on the `Accept` header: 1. Extracts keys from the provided object (excluding `'default'`) 2. Calls `req.accepts(keys)` to find the best match 3. Sets `Vary: Accept` header 4. If a match is found, sets `Content-Type` and invokes the corresponding handler 5. If no match and `default` handler exists, invokes default handler 6. Otherwise, calls `next(createError(406))` for Not Acceptable **Diagram: res.format() Flow** ```mermaid graph TB format["res.format({
'text/html': handlerA,
'application/json': handlerB,
'default': handlerC
})"] keys["Extract keys
(exclude 'default')
(lib/response.js:573-574)"] accepts["req.accepts(keys)
(lib/request.js:127-130)"] vary["res.vary('Accept')
(lib/response.js:580)"] match{"Match found?"} setType["Set Content-Type
(lib/response.js:583)"] callHandler["Call handler
(lib/response.js:584)"] hasDefault{"Has 'default'
handler?"} callDefault["Call default handler
(lib/response.js:586)"] error406["next(createError(406))
(lib/response.js:588-590)"] format --> keys keys --> accepts accepts --> vary vary --> match match -->|"Yes"| setType setType --> callHandler match -->|"No"| hasDefault hasDefault -->|"Yes"| callDefault hasDefault -->|"No"| error406 ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:569-594]() ## File Operations ### res.sendFile() The `res.sendFile()` method at [lib/response.js:371-413]() transfers a file using the `send` module: 1. **Path Validation** ([lib/response.js:378-394]()): - Requires `path` argument - `path` must be a string - Path must be absolute or `opts.root` must be specified 2. **ETag Configuration** ([lib/response.js:400]()): Wires `app.enabled('etag')` to send options 3. **File Streaming** ([lib/response.js:401-412]()): Uses `send` module and internal `sendfile()` helper at [lib/response.js:921-1009]() to pipe the file to the response Sources: [lib/response.js:371-413](), [lib/response.js:921-1009]() ### res.download() The `res.download()` method at [lib/response.js:433-482]() is a convenience wrapper around `res.sendFile()` that: 1. Sets `Content-Disposition` header to `attachment` with optional filename 2. Resolves the full path if no `root` option is provided 3. Delegates to `res.sendFile()` with merged options Sources: [lib/response.js:433-482]() ## Redirects and Location ### res.location() The `res.location()` method at [lib/response.js:794-796]() sets the `Location` header. It encodes the URL using `encodeUrl()` from the `encodeurl` module. ### res.redirect() The `res.redirect()` method at [lib/response.js:812-864]() performs HTTP redirects: 1. **Status Code** ([lib/response.js:815-821]()): Defaults to 302, accepts optional status as first argument 2. **Location Header** ([lib/response.js:836]()): Sets using `res.location()` 3. **Content Negotiation** ([lib/response.js:839-853]()): Uses `res.format()` to send appropriate response body: - `text`: Status message + redirect text - `html`: Full HTML document with DOCTYPE, title, and body (as of Express 5.x per [History.md:5]()) - `default`: Empty body 4. **Method Handling** ([lib/response.js:859-863]()): Skips body for HEAD requests Sources: [lib/response.js:794-796](), [lib/response.js:812-864]() ## Cookie Management ### res.cookie() The `res.cookie()` method at [lib/response.js:742-775]() sets cookies: 1. **Value Encoding** ([lib/response.js:751-757]()): - Objects are prefixed with `'j:'` and JSON-stringified - Other values are converted to strings - Signed cookies are prefixed with `'s:'` and signed using `cookie-signature` module 2. **MaxAge Conversion** ([lib/response.js:759-766]()): Converts `maxAge` option (milliseconds) to `expires` date and `maxAge` (seconds) for the cookie module 3. **Default Path** ([lib/response.js:768-770]()): Sets `path` to `'/'` if not specified 4. **Serialization** ([lib/response.js:772]()): Uses `cookie.serialize()` to create Set-Cookie header value and appends it Sources: [lib/response.js:742-775]() ### res.clearCookie() The `res.clearCookie()` method at [lib/response.js:709-716]() clears a cookie by: - Setting `expires` to `new Date(1)` (January 1, 1970) - Deleting any user-provided `maxAge` (to prevent conflicts) - Delegating to `res.cookie()` with empty value Sources: [lib/response.js:709-716]() ## View Rendering The `res.render()` method at [lib/response.js:894-918]() renders views using the template engine: 1. **Options Merging** ([lib/response.js:908]()): Merges `res.locals` into options as `opts._locals` 2. **Default Callback** ([lib/response.js:911-914]()): If no callback provided, creates one that calls `res.send()` or `req.next(err)` on error 3. **Delegation** ([lib/response.js:917]()): Delegates to `app.render()` which handles view lookup and template engine invocation Sources: [lib/response.js:894-918](), [lib/application.js:522-575]() ## Special Headers ### res.links() The `res.links()` method at [lib/response.js:97-110]() sets the `Link` header from an object. As of Express 5.1.0, it supports arrays for multiple links with the same `rel` value: ```javascript res.links({ next: 'http://api.example.com/users?page=2', pages: ['http://api.example.com/users?page=1', 'http://api.example.com/users?page=2'] }); ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:97-110](), [History.md:29]() ### res.attachment() The `res.attachment()` method at [lib/response.js:604-612]() sets the `Content-Disposition` header to `attachment`: - If filename is provided, also sets `Content-Type` based on file extension - Uses `content-disposition` module for proper encoding Sources: [lib/response.js:604-612]() ### res.type() / res.contentType() The `res.type()` method at [lib/response.js:503-510]() (aliased as `res.contentType()`) sets the `Content-Type` header: - If type contains `/`, uses it directly - Otherwise, looks up MIME type using `mime.contentType()` - Defaults to `application/octet-stream` if lookup fails Sources: [lib/response.js:503-510]() ## Integration with Application Settings The Response Object integrates closely with Application settings to control behavior: **Diagram: Response Object and Application Settings** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Settings" etagSetting["app.set('etag', val)
(lib/application.js:364-366)"] jsonEscape["app.set('json escape', bool)"] jsonReplacer["app.set('json replacer', fn)"] jsonSpaces["app.set('json spaces', n)"] jsonpCallback["app.set('jsonp callback name', str)"] end subgraph "Compiled Settings" etagFn["app.get('etag fn')
(compileETag)"] end subgraph "Response Methods" resSend["res.send()
(lib/response.js:161-162)"] resJson["res.json()
(lib/response.js:235-237)"] resJsonp["res.jsonp()
(lib/response.js:263-267)"] end etagSetting --> etagFn etagFn --> resSend jsonEscape --> resJson jsonReplacer --> resJson jsonSpaces --> resJson jsonEscape --> resJsonp jsonReplacer --> resJsonp jsonSpaces --> resJsonp jsonpCallback --> resJsonp ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:161-162](), [lib/response.js:234-238](), [lib/response.js:262-267](), [lib/application.js:363-380]() ### ETag Generation The `etag` setting controls automatic ETag generation in `res.send()`: | Setting Value | Compiled Function | Behavior | |---------------|-------------------|----------| | `false` | `undefined` | No ETag generation | | `true` or `'weak'` | `utils.wetag` | Generates weak ETags (prefix `W/"`) | | `'strong'` | `utils.etag` | Generates strong ETags (no prefix) | | Custom function | The function itself | Custom ETag generation | The compiled function is stored as `app.get('etag fn')` and used at [lib/response.js:161-162](). Sources: [lib/utils.js:130-152](), [lib/response.js:161-189]() ### JSON Settings Three settings control JSON serialization: 1. **json replacer** ([lib/response.js:236]()): Passed as second argument to `JSON.stringify()` 2. **json spaces** ([lib/response.js:237]()): Passed as third argument to `JSON.stringify()` for indentation 3. **json escape** ([lib/response.js:235]()): When enabled, escapes `<`, `>`, and `&` characters to prevent HTML injection attacks The internal `stringify()` helper at [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() handles the HTML escaping. Sources: [lib/response.js:232-246](), [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() ## Response Method Chaining Most Response methods return `this` to enable method chaining: ```javascript res.status(201) .set('Location', '/users/123') .json({ id: 123, name: 'John' }); ``` Terminal methods that send the response (`res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.redirect()`, etc.) also return `this` for consistency, though chaining after them has no practical effect since the response is finalized. Sources: [lib/response.js:75](), [lib/response.js:217](), [lib/response.js:245](), [lib/response.js:685]() ## Summary The Response Object extends Node.js's `http.ServerResponse` with Express-specific functionality organized into several categories: - **Core sending methods**: `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()`, `res.sendStatus()` - **File operations**: `res.sendFile()`, `res.download()` - **Header management**: `res.set()`, `res.get()`, `res.append()`, `res.vary()` - **Content type**: `res.type()`, `res.format()` - **Redirects**: `res.redirect()`, `res.location()` - **Cookies**: `res.cookie()`, `res.clearCookie()` - **View rendering**: `res.render()` - **Special headers**: `res.links()`, `res.attachment()` The Response prototype is created per application with a reference to the app instance, enabling integration with application settings like `etag`, `json replacer`, and `json spaces`. During request handling, the actual Node.js response object's prototype is set to the application's response prototype, giving it access to all Express response methods. Sources: [lib/response.js:1-1048](), [lib/express.js:50-52](), [lib/application.js:152-178]() --- # Page: Request-Response Lifecycle # Request-Response Lifecycle

## Purpose and Scope This document explains the complete journey of an HTTP request through Express.js, from the moment it enters the application until a response is sent back to the client. It covers the initialization sequence in `app.handle()`, prototype extension, circular reference setup, and the handoff to the routing system. For details on the routing and middleware execution that occurs after initialization, see [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1). For information about specific response methods used to send data back to clients, see [Content Delivery Methods](#5.1). --- ## Overview of the Request-Response Flow When an HTTP request arrives at an Express application, it passes through several initialization steps before reaching application middleware and route handlers. The entry point is the Express application function itself, which delegates to `app.handle()` for request processing. **Request-Response Flow Overview** ```mermaid flowchart TB HTTP["HTTP Server
(Node.js http.Server)"] APP["Express Application
app(req, res, next)"] HANDLE["app.handle()
[lib/application.js:152]"] FINAL["Setup finalhandler
Error handling callback"] HEADER["Set X-Powered-By
(if enabled)"] CIRCULAR["Circular References
req.res = res
res.req = req"] PROTO["Prototype Extension
Object.setPrototypeOf()"] LOCALS["Initialize res.locals
Object.create(null)"] ROUTER["Router Delegation
this.router.handle()"] MIDDLEWARE["Middleware Stack
Execution"] ROUTE["Route Handlers
Execution"] RESPONSE["Response Sent
res.send/json/etc"] HTTP --> APP APP --> HANDLE HANDLE --> FINAL FINAL --> HEADER HEADER --> CIRCULAR CIRCULAR --> PROTO PROTO --> LOCALS LOCALS --> ROUTER ROUTER --> MIDDLEWARE MIDDLEWARE --> ROUTE ROUTE --> RESPONSE ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/express.js:36-56]() --- ## Entry Point: The Application Function The Express application created by `createApplication()` is itself a function that Node.js's HTTP server calls with incoming requests. This function immediately delegates to `app.handle()`. **Application Creation and Invocation** ```mermaid graph TB CREATE["createApplication()
[lib/express.js:36]"] APP_FN["app function
function(req, res, next)"] MIXIN1["mixin EventEmitter.prototype"] MIXIN2["mixin application prototype"] REQ_PROTO["app.request = Object.create(req)"] RES_PROTO["app.response = Object.create(res)"] INIT["app.init()"] HTTP_CALL["HTTP Server Invocation
server.on('request', app)"] HANDLE_CALL["app.handle(req, res, next)"] CREATE --> APP_FN APP_FN --> MIXIN1 APP_FN --> MIXIN2 APP_FN --> REQ_PROTO APP_FN --> RES_PROTO APP_FN --> INIT APP_FN --> HTTP_CALL HTTP_CALL --> HANDLE_CALL ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/application.js:152]() The application function is a simple wrapper: | Component | Location | Purpose | |-----------|----------|---------| | `app` function | [lib/express.js:37-39]() | Receives HTTP requests and delegates to `app.handle()` | | EventEmitter mixin | [lib/express.js:41]() | Enables event emission (e.g., 'mount' event) | | Application prototype | [lib/express.js:42]() | Provides methods like `app.get()`, `app.use()`, `app.set()` | | `app.request` | [lib/express.js:45-47]() | Template prototype for all request objects | | `app.response` | [lib/express.js:50-52]() | Template prototype for all response objects | --- ## Request Initialization in app.handle() The `app.handle()` method performs critical initialization before dispatching the request to the router. This method is defined at [lib/application.js:152-178](). **Initialization Sequence in app.handle()** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant HTTP as HTTP Server participant App as app.handle() participant Final as finalhandler participant Req as req object participant Res as res object participant Router as router.handle() HTTP->>App: app.handle(req, res, callback) App->>Final: Create finalhandler(req, res, options) Note over Final: Handles errors and
unhandled requests App->>Res: Set X-Powered-By header
(if enabled) App->>Req: req.res = res App->>Res: res.req = req Note over Req,Res: Circular references
for bidirectional access App->>Req: Object.setPrototypeOf(req, app.request) Note over Req: Gains Express req methods:
req.get(), req.accepts(), etc. App->>Res: Object.setPrototypeOf(res, app.response) Note over Res: Gains Express res methods:
res.send(), res.json(), etc. App->>Res: res.locals = Object.create(null) Note over Res: Initialize locals object
for template variables App->>Router: router.handle(req, res, done) Note over Router: Router takes over
request processing ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178]() ### Step-by-Step Breakdown **1. Final Handler Setup** [lib/application.js:154-157]() ``` var done = callback || finalhandler(req, res, { env: this.get('env'), onerror: logerror.bind(this) }); ``` The final handler is invoked if no route handles the request or if an error occurs. It sends appropriate error responses or 404 pages. **2. X-Powered-By Header** [lib/application.js:160-162]() ``` if (this.enabled('x-powered-by')) { res.setHeader('X-Powered-By', 'Express'); } ``` This optional header advertises that the server is running Express. It's enabled by default but can be disabled with `app.disable('x-powered-by')`. **3. Circular References** [lib/application.js:165-166]() ``` req.res = res; res.req = req; ``` These circular references allow middleware and handlers to access the response from the request object and vice versa. This is useful when passing only `req` or `res` to helper functions. **4. Prototype Extension** [lib/application.js:169-170]() ``` Object.setPrototypeOf(req, this.request) Object.setPrototypeOf(res, this.response) ``` This is the mechanism by which the raw Node.js `http.IncomingMessage` and `http.ServerResponse` objects gain all Express-specific methods and properties. **5. Response Locals Initialization** [lib/application.js:173-175]() ``` if (!res.locals) { res.locals = Object.create(null); } ``` The `res.locals` object stores variables that are passed to template engines during rendering. It's initialized as an empty object with null prototype to avoid prototype pollution. **6. Router Delegation** [lib/application.js:177]() ``` this.router.handle(req, res, done); ``` Control is passed to the router, which processes middleware and route handlers. See [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1) for details. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178]() --- ## Prototype Extension Mechanism Express extends the native Node.js HTTP objects to add framework-specific functionality. This is done through prototype chaining rather than property copying, which allows for efficient memory usage and inheritance. **Prototype Chain Structure** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Request Prototype Chain" REQ_INST["Incoming Request
instance"] APP_REQ["app.request
[lib/express.js:45]"] LIB_REQ["lib/request.js
Express req prototype"] HTTP_REQ["http.IncomingMessage
Node.js prototype"] REQ_INST -->|"__proto__"| APP_REQ APP_REQ -->|"Object.create()"| LIB_REQ LIB_REQ -->|"Object.create()"| HTTP_REQ end subgraph "Response Prototype Chain" RES_INST["Outgoing Response
instance"] APP_RES["app.response
[lib/express.js:50]"] LIB_RES["lib/response.js
Express res prototype"] HTTP_RES["http.ServerResponse
Node.js prototype"] RES_INST -->|"__proto__"| APP_RES APP_RES -->|"Object.create()"| LIB_RES LIB_RES -->|"Object.create()"| HTTP_RES end ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:45-52](), [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/application.js:169-170]() ### Prototype Layers | Layer | Created At | Purpose | Example Methods/Properties | |-------|-----------|---------|----------------------------| | `http.IncomingMessage` | Node.js | Base request functionality | `req.url`, `req.headers`, `req.method` | | `lib/request.js` | [lib/request.js:30]() | Express request methods | `req.get()`, `req.accepts()`, `req.query`, `req.ip` | | `app.request` | [lib/express.js:45-47]() | App-specific request extensions | User can extend per-app with custom properties | | Request instance | Runtime | Actual incoming request | Specific request data | | Layer | Created At | Purpose | Example Methods/Properties | |-------|-----------|---------|----------------------------| | `http.ServerResponse` | Node.js | Base response functionality | `res.writeHead()`, `res.end()`, `res.statusCode` | | `lib/response.js` | [lib/response.js:42]() | Express response methods | `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.render()` | | `app.response` | [lib/express.js:50-52]() | App-specific response extensions | User can extend per-app with custom properties | | Response instance | Runtime | Actual outgoing response | Specific response data | **Sources:** [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/express.js:45-52]() ### Extension Points Users can extend Express functionality globally or per-application: ```javascript // Global extension (affects all Express apps) express.request.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; express.response.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; // Per-app extension (affects only this app and its sub-apps) app.request.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; app.response.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:62-64]() --- ## Circular References: req.res and res.req The circular references established at [lib/application.js:165-166]() serve important practical purposes in Express middleware and route handlers. **Circular Reference Pattern** ```mermaid graph LR REQ["req object
(IncomingMessage)"] RES["res object
(ServerResponse)"] REQ -->|"req.res"| RES RES -->|"res.req"| REQ style REQ fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333 style RES fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333 ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:165-166]() ### Use Cases | Scenario | Pattern | Example | |----------|---------|---------| | Helper functions receive only `req` | Access `res` via `req.res` | `function helper(req) { req.res.send('OK'); }` | | Helper functions receive only `res` | Access `req` via `res.req` | `function helper(res) { res.send(res.req.query); }` | | Template rendering | Access request from response context | Inside `res.render()` accessing `this.req.app` | | Error handlers | Access both from either object | Error handler receives only `res`, needs to check `res.req.method` | ### Circular References in Response Methods Several response methods use the circular reference to access request properties: - **`res.send()`** [lib/response.js:128](): Accesses `req.fresh` via `this.req` to check if response is fresh for 304 status - **`res.format()`** [lib/response.js:570](): Accesses `req.accepts()` via `this.req` for content negotiation - **`res.redirect()`** [lib/response.js:859](): Checks `req.method` via `this.req` to determine if body should be sent - **`res.render()`** [lib/response.js:895](): Accesses `req.app` via `this.req.app` to call `app.render()` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:128](), [lib/response.js:570](), [lib/response.js:859](), [lib/response.js:895]() --- ## Router Delegation and Middleware Execution After initialization, `app.handle()` delegates to `this.router.handle()` at [lib/application.js:177](). The router is lazily initialized and manages the middleware stack and route handlers. **Router Lazy Initialization** ```mermaid graph TB HANDLE["app.handle() invoked"] CHECK["Access this.router"] GETTER["router getter
[lib/application.js:72]"] NULL_CHECK{"router === null?"} CREATE["new Router()
with settings"] RETURN["Return router"] ROUTER_HANDLE["router.handle(req, res, done)"] HANDLE --> CHECK CHECK --> GETTER GETTER --> NULL_CHECK NULL_CHECK -->|Yes| CREATE NULL_CHECK -->|No| RETURN CREATE --> RETURN RETURN --> ROUTER_HANDLE ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:69-82](), [lib/application.js:177]() The router is created on first access with configuration from app settings: ```javascript router = new Router({ caseSensitive: this.enabled('case sensitive routing'), strict: this.enabled('strict routing') }); ``` See [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1) for details on how the router processes the request through middleware and routes. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:74-77]() --- ## Response Sending Once a route handler or middleware decides to send a response, it uses one of the response methods. These methods prepare headers, handle content negotiation, generate ETags, and finally call `res.end()` to send the response. **Common Response Methods and Their Flow** ```mermaid graph TB HANDLER["Route Handler
or Middleware"] subgraph "Response Methods" SEND["res.send()
[lib/response.js:125]"] JSON["res.json()
[lib/response.js:232]"] RENDER["res.render()
[lib/response.js:894]"] SENDFILE["res.sendFile()
[lib/response.js:371]"] REDIRECT["res.redirect()
[lib/response.js:812]"] end subgraph "Common Processing" HEADERS["Set Headers
(Content-Type, etc.)"] ETAG["Generate ETag
(if enabled)"] FRESH["Check Freshness
(304 Not Modified)"] BODY["Prepare Body
(Buffer/String)"] end END["res.end()
(Node.js method)"] HTTP["HTTP Response
to Client"] HANDLER --> SEND HANDLER --> JSON HANDLER --> RENDER HANDLER --> SENDFILE HANDLER --> REDIRECT SEND --> HEADERS JSON --> HEADERS RENDER --> HEADERS SENDFILE --> HEADERS REDIRECT --> HEADERS HEADERS --> ETAG ETAG --> FRESH FRESH --> BODY BODY --> END END --> HTTP ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125](), [lib/response.js:232](), [lib/response.js:894](), [lib/response.js:371](), [lib/response.js:812]() ### res.send() Processing Flow The `res.send()` method at [lib/response.js:125-218]() is the core response method that handles various body types: | Body Type | Processing | Content-Type | |-----------|------------|--------------| | `string` | Set charset to UTF-8 | `text/html; charset=utf-8` (default) | | `Buffer` or `Uint8Array` | Use as-is | `application/octet-stream` (if not set) | | `object`, `number`, `boolean` | Convert to JSON via `res.json()` | `application/json; charset=utf-8` | | `null` | Convert to empty string | (existing or none) | | `undefined` | Convert to empty string | (existing or none) | **Special Status Code Handling** [lib/response.js:195-207](): - **204 No Content**: Strips `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Transfer-Encoding`, and body - **205 Reset Content**: Sets `Content-Length: 0`, strips `Transfer-Encoding`, removes body - **304 Not Modified**: Strips `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Transfer-Encoding`, and body **HEAD Request Handling** [lib/response.js:209-214](): For HEAD requests, `res.send()` calls `res.end()` without a body, sending only headers. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125-218]() ### ETag Generation and Freshness If the `etag` setting is enabled (default: `'weak'`), Express generates an ETag header and checks request freshness: **ETag and Freshness Check Flow** [lib/response.js:161-192]() ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Send as res.send() participant ETag as ETag Generator participant Fresh as Freshness Check participant Req as req.fresh participant Response as res.end() Send->>ETag: Check etag setting alt etag enabled && no existing ETag ETag->>ETag: Generate ETag from body ETag->>Send: Set ETag header end Send->>Fresh: Check req.fresh alt req.fresh === true Fresh->>Send: Set status 304 Note over Send: Client has fresh cache end alt statusCode === 304 Send->>Send: Strip content headers end Send->>Response: res.end(chunk, encoding) ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:161-192](), [lib/request.js:469-486]() The `req.fresh` getter at [lib/request.js:469-486]() compares the request's `If-None-Match` (ETag) and `If-Modified-Since` headers against the response's `ETag` and `Last-Modified` headers using the `fresh` module. --- ## Complete Request-Response Lifecycle The following diagram shows the complete journey of a request through Express: **End-to-End Request-Response Lifecycle** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as HTTP Client participant Server as Node.js http.Server participant App as Express Application participant Handle as app.handle() participant Router as Router participant MW as Middleware Stack participant Param as app.param() callbacks participant Route as Route Handler participant Res as Response Method participant End as res.end() Client->>Server: HTTP Request Note over Server: TCP connection
parsed headers Server->>App: app(req, res) Note over App: Application is a function
[lib/express.js:37] App->>Handle: app.handle(req, res, next) Note over Handle: [lib/application.js:152] Handle->>Handle: Setup finalhandler Handle->>Handle: Set X-Powered-By (if enabled) Handle->>Handle: req.res = res
res.req = req Handle->>Handle: Object.setPrototypeOf(req)
Object.setPrototypeOf(res) Handle->>Handle: res.locals = {} Handle->>Router: router.handle(req, res, done) Note over Router: [lib/application.js:177] Router->>MW: Execute app.use() middleware MW->>MW: next() between middleware Router->>Param: Execute app.param() callbacks Note over Param: Process route parameters Router->>Route: route.dispatch(req, res, next) Note over Route: Match HTTP method Route->>Route: Execute route handler(s) Route->>Res: res.send() / res.json() / etc. Note over Res: [lib/response.js:125] Res->>Res: Set Content-Type header Res->>Res: Generate ETag (if enabled) Res->>Res: Check req.fresh (304) Res->>Res: Prepare body (Buffer/String) alt HEAD request Res->>End: res.end() (no body) else Res->>End: res.end(body, encoding) end End->>Server: Response complete Server->>Client: HTTP Response ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:37-39](), [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/response.js:125-218]() --- ## Key Lifecycle Components Summary | Component | Location | Responsibility | |-----------|----------|----------------| | **Application Function** | [lib/express.js:37-39]() | Receives requests from HTTP server, delegates to `app.handle()` | | **app.handle()** | [lib/application.js:152-178]() | Initializes request/response, sets up prototypes and circular refs, delegates to router | | **finalhandler** | [lib/application.js:154-157]() | Handles errors and unhandled requests, sends 404/500 responses | | **Prototype Extension** | [lib/application.js:169-170]() | Adds Express methods to req/res via `Object.setPrototypeOf()` | | **Circular References** | [lib/application.js:165-166]() | Creates `req.res` and `res.req` for bidirectional access | | **res.locals** | [lib/application.js:173-175]() | Initializes empty object for template variables | | **Router Delegation** | [lib/application.js:177]() | Hands off to router for middleware and route processing | | **Response Methods** | [lib/response.js]() | Methods like `send()`, `json()`, `render()` for sending responses | | **ETag Generation** | [lib/response.js:161-189]() | Generates ETag headers and checks freshness for 304 responses | | **res.end()** | Native Node.js | Finalizes and sends HTTP response to client | **Sources:** [lib/express.js:37-39](), [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/response.js:125-218]() --- # Page: Settings and Utilities # Settings and Utilities
