## Purpose and Scope This document describes Express's error handling mechanisms within the routing system. It covers how errors propagate through middleware stacks using `next(err)`, the special behavior of 4-arity error-handling middleware, and control flow variants (`next('route')` and `next('router')`). For general middleware execution flow, see [Middleware Execution Flow](#4.1). For route matching and parameter handling, see [Path Matching and Parameters](#3.2). --- ## Error Propagation Mechanism Express provides three primary ways for errors to enter the error-handling pipeline: explicit propagation via `next(err)`, thrown exceptions, and rejected promises. ### Normal vs Error Flow The `next()` function behavior changes based on its argument: | Call Pattern | Behavior | |-------------|----------| | `next()` | Continue to next middleware in stack | | `next(err)` | Skip all non-error middleware, jump to next error handler | | `next('route')` | Skip remaining handlers in current route, try next route | | `next('router')` | Exit current router entirely, continue at parent level | **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:848-900](), [test/app.router.js:903-936]() ### Explicit Error Propagation with next(err) When `next()` is called with any truthy first argument (typically an Error object), Express skips all normal middleware and routes until it finds the next error-handling middleware. ```mermaid flowchart TD MW1["Middleware 1
next()"] MW2["Middleware 2
next(err)"] MW3["Middleware 3
3-arity"] ERR1["Error Handler 1
4-arity"] ERR2["Error Handler 2
4-arity"] DONE["Done"] MW1 --> MW2 MW2 -->|"next(new Error('boom'))"| MW3 MW3 -.skipped.-> ERR1 ERR1 --> ERR2 ERR2 --> DONE ``` **Diagram: Error propagation skips normal middleware** **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:903-936](), [test/Router.js:208-230]() ### Thrown Exceptions Express automatically catches synchronous exceptions thrown within middleware and route handlers. The thrown error is converted to `next(err)` internally. ```javascript // These are equivalent: app.get('/route1', function(req, res, next) { throw new Error('something failed'); }); app.get('/route2', function(req, res, next) { next(new Error('something failed')); }); ``` This applies to exceptions in: - Route handlers: [test/Route.js:197-220]() - Middleware functions: [test/Router.js:273-290]() - Parameter callbacks: [test/Router.js:251-271]() - Error handlers themselves: [test/Route.js:223-245]() **Sources:** [test/Route.js:197-220](), [test/Router.js:232-249](), [test/Router.js:273-290]() ### Promise Rejections Express automatically handles rejected promises returned from middleware and route handlers. Rejected promises are converted to `next(err)` with the rejection value (or a default Error if rejected without a value). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "Promise Support" MW["Middleware
returns Promise"] RESOLVE["Promise.resolve()"] REJECT["Promise.reject(err)"] IGNORED["Ignored
response already sent"] ERR_HANDLER["Error Handler
receives err"] end MW --> RESOLVE MW --> REJECT RESOLVE --> IGNORED REJECT --> ERR_HANDLER ``` **Diagram: Promise handling in Express routing** When a promise is rejected without a value, Express creates a default error: **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:965-1002](), [test/app.router.js:1024-1093]() --- ## Error Handler Middleware Error-handling middleware is distinguished from normal middleware by its function signature: it must accept exactly four arguments `(err, req, res, next)`. ### 4-Arity Function Recognition Express inspects middleware function length to determine if it's an error handler: ```javascript // Normal middleware (3 parameters) app.use(function(req, res, next) { // Skipped when error occurs }); // Error handler (4 parameters) app.use(function(err, req, res, next) { // Called when error occurs }); ``` The 4-arity requirement is strict - error handlers will not be invoked during normal flow, and normal middleware will not be invoked during error flow. **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:903-936](), [test/Router.js:208-230](), [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() ### Error Handler Stacking Multiple error handlers can be registered and will execute in sequence. Each must call `next(err)` to pass the error to the next handler, or send a response to terminate the chain. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Route as "Route Handler" participant MW as "Normal Middleware" participant EH1 as "Error Handler 1" participant EH2 as "Error Handler 2" participant Client Route->>Route: next(new Error('boom')) Route--xMW: skipped Route->>EH1: err object EH1->>EH1: Log error EH1->>EH2: next(err) EH2->>EH2: Format error response EH2->>Client: res.status(500).json(...) ``` **Diagram: Error handler execution sequence** **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:926-936](), [test/Route.js:170-195]() ### Error Handlers Within Routes Error handlers can be defined within a specific route's handler stack. They will only catch errors from handlers earlier in that same route: ```javascript app.get('/foo', function(req, res, next) { next(new Error('boom!')); }, function(req, res, next) { // Skipped (3-arity) }, function(err, req, res, next) { // Catches the error (4-arity) res.send('route go ' + err.message); } ); ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:938-962](), [lib/router/route.js]() --- ## Control Flow Variants Express provides special string values for `next()` that alter the control flow without triggering error handling. ### next('route') Calling `next('route')` skips all remaining handlers in the current route and attempts to match the next route for the same path. ```mermaid flowchart TD R1H1["Route 1, Handler 1
next('route')"] R1H2["Route 1, Handler 2"] R2H1["Route 2, Handler 1
matches same path"] RES["Response sent"] R1H1 -->|"next('route')"| R1H2 R1H2 -.skipped.-> R2H1 R2H1 --> RES ``` **Diagram: next('route') flow control** Example usage: ```javascript app.get('/foo', function(req, res, next) { res.set('X-Hit', '1'); next('route'); // Skip to next route }, function(req, res) { res.end('failure'); // Never reached } ); app.get('/foo', function(req, res) { res.end('success'); // This executes }); ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:848-869]() ### next('router') Calling `next('router')` exits the current router entirely and continues processing in the parent application or router. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Router["express.Router()"] RH1["Router Handler 1
next('router')"] RH2["Router Handler 2"] end subgraph App["app"] APP_H["App Handler"] end RH1 -->|"next('router')"| RH2 RH2 -.skipped.-> APP_H ``` **Diagram: next('router') exits current router** Example usage: ```javascript var router = express.Router(); router.get('/foo', function(req, res, next) { res.set('X-Hit', '1'); next('router'); // Exit this router }, function(req, res) { res.end('failure'); // Never reached } ); router.get('/foo', function(req, res) { res.end('also skipped'); // Also skipped }); app.use(router); app.get('/foo', function(req, res) { res.end('success'); // This executes }); ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:872-900]() ### Comparison Table | Method | Scope | Continues At | |--------|-------|--------------| | `next()` | Current handler | Next handler in current stack | | `next(err)` | All non-error middleware | Next error handler (4-arity) | | `next('route')` | Current route | Next matching route | | `next('router')` | Current router | Parent router/app | **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:818-900]() --- ## Error Handling Patterns ### Application-Level Error Handler Error handlers are typically registered after all routes as a catch-all: ```javascript // Routes app.get('/api/users', handler); app.post('/api/users', handler); // 404 handler (no error, but no route matched) app.use(function(req, res, next) { res.status(404); res.json({ error: 'Not found' }); }); // Error handler app.use(function(err, req, res, next) { res.status(err.status || 500); res.json({ error: err.message, stack: app.get('env') === 'development' ? err.stack : undefined }); }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:63-97]() ### Custom Error Status Codes Error objects can carry status codes via an `err.status` property: ```javascript app.get('/forbidden', function(req, res, next) { var err = new Error('not allowed!'); err.status = 403; next(err); }); app.use(function(err, req, res, next) { res.status(err.status || 500); res.render('error', { error: err }); }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:41-46](), [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() ### Content Negotiation for Errors Error handlers can use `res.format()` to send different error representations based on the `Accept` header: ```javascript app.use(function(req, res, next) { res.status(404); res.format({ html: function() { res.render('404', { url: req.url }); }, json: function() { res.json({ error: 'Not found' }); }, default: function() { res.type('txt').send('Not found'); } }); }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:63-77]() ### Error Recovery Error handlers can recover from errors by calling `next()` without arguments to continue normal processing: ```javascript app.use(function(err, req, res, next) { if (err.type === 'database') { // Log and continue console.error(err); next(); // Continue to next middleware } else { next(err); // Pass to next error handler } }); ``` Calling `next(err)` from an error handler passes the error to the next error handler. **Sources:** [test/Router.js:224-227](), [test/Route.js:184-187]() --- ## Complete Error Flow The following diagram shows the complete error handling flow through Express's routing layers: ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Request Arrives"] APP_MW["app.use middleware"] ROUTE_MATCH["Router matches route"] PARAM["app.param callbacks"] ROUTE_HANDLER["Route handler"] THROW{"Throws or
next(err)?"} SEND{"Response
sent?"} ROUTE_ERR["Route-level
error handler"] APP_ERR["App-level
error handler"] DEFAULT_ERR["Default error
handler"] CLIENT["Response to client"] NEXT_MW["Next middleware"] START --> APP_MW APP_MW --> ROUTE_MATCH ROUTE_MATCH --> PARAM PARAM --> ROUTE_HANDLER ROUTE_HANDLER --> THROW THROW -->|"next()"| SEND THROW -->|"next(err)"| ROUTE_ERR SEND -->|"no"| NEXT_MW SEND -->|"yes"| CLIENT NEXT_MW --> ROUTE_HANDLER ROUTE_ERR -->|"not found or
next(err)"| APP_ERR ROUTE_ERR -->|"handled"| CLIENT APP_ERR -->|"not found or
next(err)"| DEFAULT_ERR APP_ERR -->|"handled"| CLIENT DEFAULT_ERR --> CLIENT style THROW fill:#f9f9f9 style SEND fill:#f9f9f9 style ROUTE_ERR fill:#ffe6e6 style APP_ERR fill:#ffe6e6 style DEFAULT_ERR fill:#ffe6e6 ``` **Diagram: Complete error handling flow in Express** **Sources:** [lib/router/index.js](), [lib/router/route.js](), [test/app.router.js:903-936]() --- ## Implementation Details ### Router Error Processing The Router's `handle()` method processes layers sequentially. When an error is present (passed via `next(err)`), it only invokes 4-arity middleware: - File: [lib/router/index.js]() - Error detection checks `layer.handle.length === 4` - Non-error middleware is skipped when `err` is truthy - Special strings `'route'` and `'router'` are detected for control flow **Sources:** [lib/router/index.js](), [test/Router.js:208-291]() ### Route Error Dispatch The Route's `dispatch()` method handles errors within a specific route: - File: [lib/router/route.js]() - Maintains a stack of handlers with mixed 3-arity and 4-arity functions - Uses `try-catch` to capture thrown exceptions - Promise rejection handlers are automatically attached **Sources:** [lib/router/route.js](), [test/Route.js:170-273]() ### Promise Handling Express wraps async middleware/handlers to catch promise rejections: - Rejected promises are converted to `next(err)` - Rejection without value creates: `new Error('Rejected promise')` - Resolved promises are ignored (response already sent) **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:965-1094]() --- ## Summary Express error handling is based on: 1. **4-arity signature** - Error handlers must have exactly 4 parameters 2. **Error propagation** - `next(err)` skips normal middleware, invoking only error handlers 3. **Automatic catching** - Thrown exceptions and rejected promises are caught 4. **Control flow** - `next('route')` and `next('router')` provide non-error flow control 5. **Layered handling** - Errors can be handled at route, router, or application level The system provides flexibility in where and how errors are handled while maintaining a clear propagation path from error source to error handler. **Sources:** [test/app.router.js](), [test/Router.js](), [test/Route.js](), [examples/error-pages/index.js]() --- # Page: Middleware System # Middleware System

## Purpose and Scope This page explains the middleware system in Express.js, including the middleware concept, execution model, and the built-in middleware provided by the framework. Middleware functions are the core building blocks of Express applications, providing a modular way to process HTTP requests. For detailed information on specific topics: - Middleware execution flow and the `next()` function: see [Middleware Execution Flow](#4.1) - Body parsing middleware: see [Body Parsing Middleware](#4.2) - Static file serving: see [Static File Middleware](#4.3) - Error handling patterns: see [Error Handling in Routes](#3.3) ## Middleware Concept Middleware functions are functions that have access to the request object (`req`), the response object (`res`), and the next middleware function in the application's request-response cycle, conventionally denoted by `next`. Middleware can: - Execute any code - Modify the request and response objects - End the request-response cycle - Call the next middleware in the stack **Middleware Function Signature** ```mermaid graph LR Request["HTTP Request"] --> MW1["middleware(req, res, next)"] MW1 --> Decision{"End Response?"} Decision -->|"Yes"| Response["HTTP Response"] Decision -->|"No"| Next["next()"] Next --> MW2["Next Middleware"] MW2 --> MoreMW["..."] ``` **Standard middleware signature:** - `function(req, res, next)` - Regular middleware (arity 3) **Error-handling middleware signature:** - `function(err, req, res, next)` - Error handler (arity 4) Sources: [lib/application.js:190-244](), [test/app.use.js:125-256]() ## Middleware Types and Hierarchy Express supports multiple levels of middleware organization, each serving different scoping purposes: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Level" App["express() application"] AppUse["app.use(middleware)"] AppRoute["app.get/post/etc(path, middleware)"] end subgraph "Router Level" Router["express.Router()"] RouterUse["router.use(middleware)"] RouterRoute["router.get/post/etc(path, middleware)"] end subgraph "Route Level" Route["Route instance"] RouteHandlers["Multiple handlers per HTTP method"] end subgraph "Special Types" ErrorMW["Error Handling Middleware
(err, req, res, next)"] ParamMW["Parameter Middleware
app.param(name, callback)"] BuiltIn["Built-in Middleware
express.json, express.static, etc."] end App --> AppUse App --> AppRoute App --> Router AppUse --> BuiltIn AppUse --> ErrorMW App --> ParamMW Router --> RouterUse Router --> RouterRoute RouterUse --> ErrorMW AppRoute --> Route RouterRoute --> Route Route --> RouteHandlers ``` **Middleware Type Definitions:** | Type | Mounted With | Scope | Example | |------|--------------|-------|---------| | Application-level | `app.use()` or `app.METHOD()` | All routes in app | Logger, body parser | | Router-level | `router.use()` or `router.METHOD()` | All routes in router | Auth for API routes | | Route-level | Multiple handlers in route definition | Single route | Validation, handler | | Error-handling | `app.use()` with 4-argument function | Error propagation path | Error formatter | | Built-in | `app.use(express.json())` | Where mounted | Body parsing, static files | Sources: [lib/application.js:190-244](), [lib/express.js:77-81](), [test/app.use.js:1-150](), [test/app.router.js:144-167]() ## Middleware Stack and Execution Order Middleware functions are executed sequentially in the order they are defined. Each middleware must either end the request-response cycle or call `next()` to pass control to the next middleware. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant App as Application participant MW1 as Middleware 1 participant MW2 as Middleware 2 participant MW3 as Middleware 3 participant Handler as Route Handler participant Res as Response Client->>App: HTTP Request App->>MW1: Execute MW1->>MW1: Process request MW1->>MW2: next() MW2->>MW2: Process request MW2->>MW3: next() MW3->>MW3: Process request MW3->>Handler: next() Handler->>Res: res.send() Res->>Client: HTTP Response Note over MW1,Handler: Each middleware calls next()
to continue the chain ``` **Execution Order Example:** ```javascript // Middleware registered first executes first app.use(middleware1); // Executes 1st app.use('/api', middleware2); // Executes 2nd (if path matches) app.get('/api/users', handler); // Executes 3rd (if path and method match) ``` **Short-circuiting the stack:** Any middleware can end the request-response cycle by calling a response method (`res.send()`, `res.json()`, etc.) without calling `next()`. This prevents subsequent middleware from executing. Sources: [lib/application.js:152-178](), [test/app.use.js:152-171]() ## The `next()` Function The `next()` function is passed to each middleware and controls the flow through the middleware stack. It has several invocation patterns: | Invocation | Behavior | Use Case | |------------|----------|----------| | `next()` | Pass control to next matching middleware | Normal flow continuation | | `next(err)` | Skip to error-handling middleware | Error occurred | | `next('route')` | Skip remaining middleware in current route | Conditional routing | | `next('router')` | Exit current router entirely | Escape nested routers | ```mermaid graph TB MW["Middleware Executing"] NextCall{"next() variant"} MW --> NextCall NextCall -->|"next()"| NextMW["Next Middleware
in Stack"] NextCall -->|"next(err)"| ErrorMW["Skip to Error
Handler (arity 4)"] NextCall -->|"next('route')"| NextRoute["Skip to Next
Route"] NextCall -->|"next('router')"| ExitRouter["Exit Current
Router"] NextCall -->|"res.send()
(no next() call)"| EndCycle["End Request-Response
Cycle"] ``` **Important:** If `next()` is not called and no response is sent, the request will hang indefinitely. Sources: [test/app.router.js:169-241](), [test/Router.js:208-291]() ## Mounting Middleware with `app.use()` The `app.use()` method is the primary way to register middleware at the application level. It supports various signatures for flexibility. **Code Location:** [lib/application.js:190-244]() **Supported Signatures:** ```javascript // Mount at all paths app.use(middleware); // Mount at specific path app.use('/api', middleware); // Mount multiple middleware app.use(middleware1, middleware2, middleware3); // Mount arrays of middleware app.use([middleware1, middleware2]); app.use('/api', [authMiddleware, validationMiddleware]); // Mount nested arrays app.use([[middleware1], middleware2]); // Mount Express applications (sub-apps) app.use('/admin', adminApp); ``` **Path Matching Behavior:** ```mermaid graph LR Request["Request: /api/users"] --> UseCheck{{"app.use() path"}} UseCheck -->|"app.use(mw)"| Match1["✓ Matches
(all paths)"] UseCheck -->|"app.use('/api', mw)"| Match2["✓ Matches
(/api prefix)"] UseCheck -->|"app.use('/admin', mw)"| NoMatch["✗ No Match
(different prefix)"] Match1 --> Execute1["req.url = '/api/users'"] Match2 --> Execute2["req.url = '/users'
(path stripped)"] ``` When middleware is mounted at a path, that path prefix is stripped from `req.url` before passing the request to the middleware. Sources: [lib/application.js:190-244](), [test/app.use.js:258-362]() ## Built-in Middleware Express provides several built-in middleware functions exported from the main `express` module. These middleware are implemented using external packages but are re-exported for convenience. **Built-in Middleware Exports:** [lib/express.js:77-81]() ```javascript exports.json = bodyParser.json exports.raw = bodyParser.raw exports.static = require('serve-static'); exports.text = bodyParser.text exports.urlencoded = bodyParser.urlencoded ``` **Available Built-in Middleware:** | Middleware | Purpose | Package | Detailed Docs | |------------|---------|---------|---------------| | `express.json()` | Parse JSON request bodies | body-parser | [Body Parsing Middleware](#4.2) | | `express.urlencoded()` | Parse URL-encoded request bodies | body-parser | [Body Parsing Middleware](#4.2) | | `express.text()` | Parse plain text request bodies | body-parser | [Body Parsing Middleware](#4.2) | | `express.raw()` | Parse raw buffer request bodies | body-parser | [Body Parsing Middleware](#4.2) | | `express.static()` | Serve static files | serve-static | [Static File Middleware](#4.3) | **Usage Example:** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); // Parse JSON bodies app.use(express.json()); // Parse URL-encoded bodies app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // Serve static files from 'public' directory app.use(express.static('public')); ``` Sources: [lib/express.js:15-81](), [package.json:36-59]() ## Error-Handling Middleware Error-handling middleware functions are distinguished by having four arguments instead of three: `(err, req, res, next)`. When an error is passed to `next(err)` or thrown in middleware, Express skips all non-error-handling middleware and invokes the first error handler. **Error Handler Function Signature:** ```javascript function errorHandler(err, req, res, next) { // Must have 4 parameters // err: The error object // req: Request object // res: Response object // next: Next error handler (if any) } ``` **Error Propagation Flow:** ```mermaid graph TB MW1["Regular Middleware 1"] MW2["Regular Middleware 2"] Error["Error Occurs:
next(err) or throw"] MW3["Regular Middleware 3
(SKIPPED)"] ErrMW1["Error Handler 1
(err, req, res, next)"] ErrMW2["Error Handler 2
(err, req, res, next)"] FinalHandler["finalhandler
(default error handler)"] MW1 --> MW2 MW2 --> Error Error -.-> MW3 Error --> ErrMW1 ErrMW1 -->|"next(err)"| ErrMW2 ErrMW2 -->|"next(err)"| FinalHandler style MW3 fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#999,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` **Error Handling Registration:** ```javascript // Regular middleware app.use(function(req, res, next) { // If error occurs next(new Error('Something went wrong')); }); // This middleware is skipped when error occurs app.use(function(req, res, next) { console.log('This will not execute'); next(); }); // Error handler catches the error app.use(function(err, req, res, next) { console.error(err.stack); res.status(500).send('Internal Server Error'); }); ``` **Important Notes:** - Error handlers must be defined **after** all other `app.use()` and route calls - Error handlers must have **exactly 4 parameters** (even if you don't use them all) - If no error handler is defined, Express uses a default error handler (from `finalhandler` package) Sources: [lib/application.js:152-178](), [test/Router.js:208-291](), [test/Route.js:170-273]() ## Application vs Router Middleware Both the application object (`app`) and router instances (`express.Router()`) support middleware mounting, but they have different scopes and capabilities. **Comparison:** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Level - app.use()" AppObj["express() Application"] AppSettings["Has Settings
(etag, query parser, etc.)"] AppHandle["app.handle(req, res)
Entry point for all requests"] AppRouter["Internal Router
(lazy-loaded)"] end subgraph "Router Level - router.use()" RouterObj["express.Router()"] RouterOptions["Options:
caseSensitive, strict,
mergeParams"] RouterHandle["router.handle(req, res)
Processes matched requests"] RouterStack["Middleware Stack"] end AppObj --> AppRouter AppRouter --> RouterObj AppObj --> AppSettings AppObj --> AppHandle RouterObj --> RouterOptions RouterObj --> RouterHandle RouterObj --> RouterStack ``` | Feature | Application (`app`) | Router (`express.Router()`) | |---------|---------------------|------------------------------| | Created by | `express()` | `express.Router()` | | Mounted to | HTTP server | Application or other router | | Settings | Has application settings | Inherits parent settings | | `.use()` method | ✓ | ✓ | | Route methods (`.get()`, etc.) | ✓ | ✓ | | `.param()` method | ✓ | ✓ | | Middleware scope | Entire application | Routes within router | | Can be mounted | As sub-app | As modular router | **Router Creation and Mounting:** [lib/application.js:59-83]() - Application router initialization [lib/express.js:70-71]() - Router export ```javascript // Create modular router const apiRouter = express.Router(); apiRouter.use(authMiddleware); apiRouter.get('/users', getUsers); apiRouter.post('/users', createUser); // Mount router at path app.use('/api', apiRouter); ``` **Mounting Multiple Routers:** ```javascript const adminRouter = express.Router(); const apiRouter = express.Router(); const publicRouter = express.Router(); // Each router has its own middleware and routes app.use('/admin', adminRouter); app.use('/api', apiRouter); app.use('/', publicRouter); ``` Sources: [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/express.js:70-71](), [test/app.use.js:21-123](), [test/Router.js:19-42]() ## Middleware Registration Internals Understanding how middleware is registered internally helps debug issues and optimize application structure. **Middleware Registration Flow:** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Code as "app.use(middleware)" participant AppUse as "app.use()" participant Router as "app.router" participant RouterUse as "router.use()" participant Stack as "Middleware Stack" Code->>AppUse: app.use('/path', fn) AppUse->>AppUse: Parse arguments
(path, functions) AppUse->>AppUse: Flatten arrays
of middleware AppUse->>Router: Get/create router AppUse->>RouterUse: router.use(path, fn) RouterUse->>Stack: Add Layer to stack Stack-->>Code: Return app (chainable) Note over AppUse,Router: Router is lazy-loaded
on first use ``` **Key Code Locations:** - Argument parsing: [lib/application.js:190-214]() - Flattening arrays: [lib/application.js:210]() - Router delegation: [lib/application.js:217-242]() - Sub-app mounting: [lib/application.js:220-241]() **Sub-Application Mounting:** When an Express application is mounted as a sub-app, special handling occurs: 1. The sub-app's `parent` property is set to the mounting app 2. The sub-app's prototypes inherit from the parent app 3. Settings are inherited unless explicitly overridden 4. A special wrapper middleware restores the original app context after processing ```javascript // Sub-app mounting wrapper router.use(path, function mounted_app(req, res, next) { var orig = req.app; fn.handle(req, res, function (err) { // Restore original prototypes Object.setPrototypeOf(req, orig.request) Object.setPrototypeOf(res, orig.response) next(err); }); }); ``` Sources: [lib/application.js:219-241]() ## Summary The middleware system is the foundation of Express applications, providing: 1. **Modular Request Processing**: Break complex request handling into discrete, reusable functions 2. **Flexible Mounting**: Mount middleware at application, router, or route level with path-based filtering 3. **Sequential Execution**: Predictable execution order with explicit control flow via `next()` 4. **Error Handling**: Dedicated error-handling middleware with automatic error propagation 5. **Built-in Utilities**: Common middleware for body parsing and static file serving 6. **Composition**: Nest routers and sub-applications for large-scale application organization The middleware pattern enables the creation of composable, maintainable web applications by separating concerns into distinct processing steps. Sources: [lib/application.js:152-244](), [lib/express.js:15-82](), [test/app.use.js](), [test/app.router.js]() --- # Page: Middleware Execution Flow # Middleware Execution Flow

## Purpose and Scope This page documents how Express executes middleware functions, including the mechanics of the `next()` callback, execution order, error handling, and flow control mechanisms. It covers middleware registered via `app.use()`, route handlers registered via `app.get()`, `router.use()`, and similar methods. For information about specific built-in middleware (body parsers, static files), see [Body Parsing Middleware](#4.2) and [Static File Middleware](#4.3). For routing and path matching mechanics, see [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1). --- ## Middleware Stack Architecture Express maintains middleware and route handlers in a **stack** structure within each Router instance. The Application object (`app`) contains a lazily-initialized Router (accessed via `app.router` getter defined at [lib/application.js:69-82]()) that holds the application-level middleware stack. ### Stack Organization **Diagram: Router and Layer Stack Structure** ```mermaid graph TB App["app
(Application instance)"] RouterGetter["app.router
(getter property)"] RouterInstance["Router instance
(from 'router' package)"] RouterStack["router.stack
(Array of Layer objects)"] Layer0["stack[0]
Layer instance"] Layer1["stack[1]
Layer instance"] Layer2["stack[2]
Layer instance"] L0Props["layer.path = '/'
layer.method = undefined
layer.handle = function"] L1Props["layer.path = '/user'
layer.method = undefined
layer.handle = function"] L2Props["layer.path = '/user/:id'
layer.method = undefined
layer.route = Route"] RouteObj["Route instance"] RouteStack["route.stack
(Array of Layer objects)"] RouteLayer["Layer instance"] RouteLayerProps["layer.method = 'get'
layer.handle = handler fn"] App --> RouterGetter RouterGetter --> RouterInstance RouterInstance --> RouterStack RouterStack --> Layer0 RouterStack --> Layer1 RouterStack --> Layer2 Layer0 --> L0Props Layer1 --> L1Props Layer2 --> L2Props L2Props --> RouteObj RouteObj --> RouteStack RouteStack --> RouteLayer RouteLayer --> RouteLayerProps ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:69-82](), [lib/router/index.js](), [lib/router/layer.js](), [lib/router/route.js](), [test/app.router.js:144-167]() ### Layer Object Structure Each `Layer` object (from the `router` package) wraps either: - **Middleware function:** For `app.use()` or `router.use()` calls - stored in `layer.handle` property - **Route object:** For `app.get()`, `router.post()`, etc. - stored in `layer.route` property The Route object contains its own `route.stack` array of Layer objects, each wrapping a handler function for that specific route and HTTP method combination. **Key Properties:** - `layer.path` - Path pattern to match (string or RegExp) - `layer.method` - HTTP method filter (undefined for middleware) - `layer.handle` - Middleware function reference - `layer.route` - Route object reference (for route handlers) - `layer.match()` - Method to test if layer matches request path **Sources:** [lib/router/layer.js](), [lib/router/route.js]() --- ## The next() Function The `next` callback is the core flow control mechanism in Express middleware. It is provided as the third parameter to every middleware function: `function(req, res, next)`. The `next` function is an internal closure created by the Router during request processing. ### next() Variants | Call | Behavior | Implementation Location | Use Case | |------|----------|------------------------|----------| | `next()` | Continue to next middleware in stack | Router/Route internal `next()` closure | Normal flow | | `next('route')` | Skip remaining handlers in current route, jump to next route | Handled by Router's `next()` | Route-level early exit | | `next('router')` | Exit current router entirely | Handled by Router's `next()` | Router-level early exit | | `next(err)` | Skip to error handling middleware (arity 4) | Handled by Router's `next()` | Error propagation | **Sources:** [lib/router/index.js](), [lib/router/route.js]() ### Basic next() - Continue Execution Calling `next()` with no arguments proceeds to the next middleware or handler in the stack. The Router increments its internal `idx` counter and continues processing the `stack` array. **Diagram: next() Call Flow Through Stack** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as "HTTP Client" participant MW1 as "stack[0].handle()
(middleware fn)" participant Next1 as "next() closure
(idx=0)" participant MW2 as "stack[1].handle()
(middleware fn)" participant Next2 as "next() closure
(idx=1)" participant RouteLayer as "stack[2].route
(Route object)" participant Handler as "route.stack[0].handle()
(handler fn)" participant ResSend as "res.send()
[response.js:125]" Client->>MW1: "Request arrives" MW1->>MW1: "Execute logic" MW1->>Next1: "next()" Next1->>Next1: "idx = 1" Next1->>MW2: "stack[1].handle_request()" MW2->>MW2: "Execute logic" MW2->>Next2: "next()" Next2->>Next2: "idx = 2" Next2->>RouteLayer: "stack[2].route.dispatch()" RouteLayer->>Handler: "route.stack[0].handle_request()" Handler->>Handler: "Execute logic" Handler->>ResSend: "res.send(data)" ResSend->>Client: "Response sent" ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:144-167](), [test/app.use.js:126-150](), [lib/response.js:125-218]() ### next('route') - Skip to Next Route When called from a route handler, `next('route')` bypasses remaining handlers in the current route and attempts to match the next route: ```mermaid graph LR Start["Request: GET /foo"] Route1["Route 1: GET /foo
Handler 1"] Handler1b["Route 1: Handler 2"] Route2["Route 2: GET /foo"] End["Response"] Start --> Route1 Route1 -->|"next('route')"| Route2 Handler1b -.->|"skipped"| Route2 Route2 --> End ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:848-870]() Example from test suite: ```javascript app.get('/foo', fn, function (req, res) { res.end('failure') // Skipped }); app.get('/foo', function (req, res) { res.end('success') // Executed }) function fn(req, res, next) { res.set('X-Hit', '1') next('route') // Jumps to second route } ``` **Important:** `next('route')` only works in route handlers (created with `app.METHOD()` or `router.METHOD()`), not in middleware registered with `app.use()`. ### next('router') - Exit Router `next('router')` exits the current Router entirely and returns control to the parent router or application: ```mermaid graph TB App["Application"] Router["Sub-router"] MW1["Router middleware"] MW2["Router handler"] AppHandler["App handler"] App -->|"app.use(router)"| Router Router --> MW1 MW1 -->|"next('router')"| AppHandler MW2 -.->|"skipped"| AppHandler Router -.-> MW2 ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:872-901]() This is useful when mounting routers and needing to exit back to the parent: ```javascript var router = express.Router() router.get('/foo', function (req, res, next) { res.set('X-Hit', '1') next('router') // Exit this router }) router.get('/foo', function (req, res) { res.end('failure') // Skipped }) app.use(router) app.get('/foo', function (req, res) { res.end('success') // Executed }) ``` ### next(err) - Error Handling Passing any value to `next()` (except the special strings `'route'` and `'router'`) triggers error handling mode. Express skips all normal middleware and only invokes error handling middleware: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MW1 as "Middleware 1" participant MW2 as "Middleware 2" participant Handler as "Route Handler" participant ErrMW as "Error Middleware
(err, req, res, next)" participant Res as "Response" MW1->>MW1: Execute MW1->>MW2: next() MW2->>MW2: Detects error MW2->>ErrMW: next(err) Note over Handler: Skipped ErrMW->>ErrMW: Handle error ErrMW->>Res: res.status(500).send() ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:903-936](), [test/Route.js:170-195]() --- ## Error Handling Middleware Error handling middleware functions have a signature with **four parameters**: `function(err, req, res, next)`. Express identifies error handlers by their arity (parameter count). ### Error Handler Characteristics ```javascript // Normal middleware - 3 parameters app.use(function (req, res, next) { // Will NOT be called for errors next() }) // Error handler - 4 parameters app.use(function (err, req, res, next) { // ONLY called when next(err) is invoked res.status(500).send(err.message) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:926-931](), [test/Router.js:208-230]() ### Error Propagation Flow When `next(err)` is called with an error object, the Router skips all normal middleware (arity 3) and only invokes error handling middleware (arity 4). This is determined by checking `layer.handle.length === 4`. **Diagram: Error Propagation Through Stack** ```mermaid graph TB Start["Middleware calls next(err)"] NextErr["next() closure receives err"] Loop["Loop through stack[idx++]"] Check1{"layer.handle.length
=== 4?"} Check2{"Is Route?
(layer.route exists)"} RouteDispatch["route.dispatch(req, res, next)"] RouteCheck{"Route has
error handler?"} ErrorHandler["Execute error handler:
fn(err, req, res, next)"] Skip["idx++, continue"] FinalHandler["finalhandler(req, res)
[finalhandler package]"] Start --> NextErr NextErr --> Loop Loop --> Check1 Check1 -->|"Yes"| ErrorHandler Check1 -->|"No"| Check2 Check2 -->|"Yes"| RouteDispatch Check2 -->|"No"| Skip RouteDispatch --> RouteCheck RouteCheck -->|"Yes"| ErrorHandler RouteCheck -->|"No"| Skip ErrorHandler -->|"next() or next(err)"| Loop Skip --> Loop Loop -->|"stack exhausted"| FinalHandler ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:903-963](), [test/Route.js:170-273](), [lib/application.js:152-178]() ### Error Handler in Same Route Error handlers can exist within the same route as the handler that triggered the error: ```javascript app.get('/foo', function fn1(req, res, next) { next(new Error('boom!')) // Trigger error }, function fn2(req, res, next) { res.send('foo here') // Skipped (normal middleware) }, function fn3(err, req, res, next) { res.send('route go ' + err.message) // Executed (error handler) } ) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:938-962]() ### Throwing Exceptions Express catches synchronous exceptions thrown in middleware and route handlers using try-catch blocks in `layer.handle_request()` and `layer.handle_error()`, treating them as `next(err)`: ```javascript route.all(function(req, res, next){ throw new Error('boom!') // Caught by try-catch in layer.handle_request() }); route.all(function(err, req, res, next){ req.order += 'a' // Error handler receives the error next(err) }); ``` **Implementation Detail:** The Router package wraps each middleware invocation in a try-catch block: ```javascript // Conceptual representation from router package try { layer.handle_request(req, res, next) } catch (err) { next(err) } ``` **Sources:** [test/Route.js:197-221](), [test/Router.js:232-249](), [test/Router.js:273-290]() **Important:** Exceptions in error handlers are also caught and propagated to the next error handler. This applies recursively through the entire error handling chain. --- ## Promise Support Express automatically handles rejected promises returned from middleware and route handlers, converting them to `next(err)` calls. ### Rejected Promises ```javascript router.use(function createError(req, res, next) { return Promise.reject(new Error('boom!')) // Equivalent to: next(new Error('boom!')) }) router.use(function sawError(err, req, res, next) { res.send('saw ' + err.name + ': ' + err.message) // Receives: "saw Error: boom!" }) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:965-1002]() ### Rejected Promise Without Value If a promise is rejected without a value, Express creates a default error: ```javascript router.use(function createError(req, res, next) { return Promise.reject() // No error value }) router.use(function sawError(err, req, res, next) { res.send('saw ' + err.message) // Receives: "Rejected promise" }) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:985-1002]() ### Resolved Promises Resolved promises are **ignored**. Express does not wait for or interact with resolved promises: ```javascript router.use(function createError(req, res, next) { res.send('saw GET /foo') return Promise.resolve('foo') // Ignored by Express }) router.use(function () { // This middleware is NOT invoked }) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:1004-1022]() **Note:** If you want asynchronous execution flow, you must explicitly call `next()` after your async operations complete. Returning a promise does not automatically advance the middleware chain. --- ## Execution Order Middleware executes in the order it is registered, forming a linear chain through the application. ### Linear Execution Through Stack ```mermaid graph TB Request["Incoming Request"] AppMW1["app.use(middleware1)"] AppMW2["app.use(middleware2)"] Route1["app.get('/user/:id', ...)"] Match{"Path & Method
match?"} RouteHandler["Route handler"] AppMW3["app.use(middleware3)"] Response["Response"] Request --> AppMW1 AppMW1 -->|"next()"| AppMW2 AppMW2 -->|"next()"| Route1 Route1 --> Match Match -->|"No"| AppMW3 Match -->|"Yes"| RouteHandler RouteHandler -->|"res.send()"| Response AppMW3 -->|"next()"| Response ``` Example demonstrating execution order: ```javascript app.get('/*path', function (req, res, next) { path.push(0); // Executed 1st next(); }); app.get('/user/:id', function (req, res, next) { path.push(1); // Executed 2nd next(); }); app.use(function (req, res, next) { path.push(2); // Executed 3rd next(); }); app.all('/user/:id', function (req, res, next) { path.push(3); // Executed 4th next(); }); app.get('/*splat', function (req, res, next) { path.push(4); // Executed 5th next(); }); app.use(function (req, res, next) { path.push(5); // Executed 6th res.end(path.join(',')) }); // GET /user/1 produces: "0,1,2,3,4,5" ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:1115-1152]() ### Multiple Handlers in Same Route Route handlers registered together execute sequentially: ```javascript app.get('/foo', function (req, res, next) { calls.push('/foo/:bar?') // 1st next() }, function (req, res, next) { calls.push('/foo') // 2nd next() }, function (req, res) { calls.push('/foo 2') // 3rd res.json(calls) } ) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:818-845]() ### Middleware Arrays Middleware can be registered as arrays, which are flattened and executed in order: ```javascript function fn1(req, res, next) { assert.equal(++count, 1); next(); } function fn2(req, res, next) { assert.equal(++count, 2); next(); } router.use([fn1, fn2], function (req, res) { assert.equal(++count, 3); done(); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/Router.js:477-497](), [test/app.use.js:173-199]() Nested arrays are also supported and fully flattened: ```javascript app.use([[fn1], fn2], [fn3]); // Executes: fn1, fn2, fn3 ``` **Sources:** [test/app.use.js:229-255]() --- ## Router and Route Execution ### Router.handle() Dispatch The `Router.prototype.handle()` method (from the `router` package) is the entry point for processing a request through a router's middleware stack. This method is invoked by `app.handle()` at [lib/application.js:177](). **Diagram: Router.handle() Execution Flow with Code Symbols** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant AppHandle as "app.handle()
[application.js:152]" participant RouterHandle as "router.handle()
(router package)" participant NextFn as "next() function
(internal closure)" participant Layer as "Layer instance" participant LayerMatch as "layer.match(path)" participant HandleReq as "layer.handle_request()
or layer.handle_error()" participant Middleware as "middleware(req,res,next)" AppHandle->>RouterHandle: "router.handle(req, res, done)" RouterHandle->>RouterHandle: "idx = 0" loop "while idx < stack.length" RouterHandle->>Layer: "layer = stack[idx++]" RouterHandle->>LayerMatch: "layer.match(path)" alt "match === true" alt "no error (err === undefined)" LayerMatch->>HandleReq: "layer.handle_request(req, res, next)" HandleReq->>Middleware: "middleware(req, res, next)" Middleware->>Middleware: "execute logic" alt "next() called" Middleware->>NextFn: "next()" NextFn->>RouterHandle: "continue loop" else "next('route')" Middleware->>NextFn: "next('route')" NextFn->>RouterHandle: "skip to next route" else "next('router')" Middleware->>NextFn: "next('router')" NextFn->>AppHandle: "exit router" else "next(err)" Middleware->>NextFn: "next(err)" NextFn->>RouterHandle: "skip to error handler" end else "error exists (err !== undefined)" LayerMatch->>HandleReq: "layer.handle_error(err, req, res, next)" Note over HandleReq: "Only if layer.handle.length === 4" end else "match === false" LayerMatch->>RouterHandle: "idx++, continue" end end ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178](), [test/Router.js:162-178](), [test/app.router.js:144-167]() ### Route.dispatch() Execution Once a Route is matched by the Router, `Route.prototype.dispatch()` executes the route's handler stack. This method is similar to `Router.handle()` but operates on the route-level Layer stack. **Method Signature:** ```javascript route.dispatch(req, res, done) ``` **Execution Steps:** 1. Initialize `idx = 0` for route's internal stack 2. Iterate through `route.stack` array of Layer objects 3. For each layer, check if `layer.method` matches `req.method` (or is `_all`) 4. Execute `layer.handle_request(req, res, next)` or `layer.handle_error(err, req, res, next)` 5. Handle `next()`, `next('route')`, `next('router')`, and `next(err)` calls 6. Invoke `done(err)` callback when stack is exhausted or error occurs **Key Difference from Router.handle():** - `Route.dispatch()` operates on a single route's handlers - No path matching - all layers in route.stack are executed based on HTTP method only - `next('route')` exits the route immediately and returns to Router **Sources:** [lib/router/route.js](), [test/Route.js:10-14](), [test/Route.js:47-104]() --- ## Stack Overflow Prevention The Router (from the `router` package) implements trampolining to prevent stack overflow when executing large numbers of synchronous middleware functions. This protection is built into both `Router.handle()` and `Route.dispatch()`. ### The Problem Without protection, deeply nested synchronous `next()` calls can exhaust the JavaScript call stack: ```javascript // 6000 synchronous middleware functions for (var i = 0; i < 6000; i++) { router.use(function (req, res, next) { req.counter++ next() // Each call adds a frame to the call stack }) } // Without trampolining: RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded ``` ### The Solution The Router uses `setImmediate()` or `process.nextTick()` to periodically break the call chain, allowing the stack to unwind: ```javascript // Conceptual implementation in Router package's next() closure function next(err) { // Track synchronous calls if (++sync > 100) { // Trampoline: defer to next tick return setImmediate(next, err) } var layer = stack[idx++] // ... match and execute layer ... try { layer.handle_request(req, res, next) } catch (err) { next(err) } } ``` **Test Case Validation:** The Router can handle 6000+ synchronous middleware functions without stack overflow: ```javascript // From test/Router.js:108-133 var router = new Router() for (var i = 0; i < 6000; i++) { router.use(function (req, res, next) { req.counter++ next() }) } router.use(function (req, res) { assert.strictEqual(req.counter, 6000) // All 6000 executed successfully res.end() }) ``` **Sources:** [test/Router.js:91-106](), [test/Router.js:108-133](), [test/Router.js:135-160](), [test/Route.js:16-45]() --- ## Request Context Preservation ### req.params Restoration When entering and exiting routers, Express preserves the parent's `req.params` by storing and restoring the original value. This happens in the `mounted_app` wrapper function created by `app.use()` at [lib/application.js:230-237](). **Restoration Process:** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant AppRouter as "app.router" participant MountedApp as "mounted_app wrapper
[application.js:230]" participant SubRouter as "sub-router.handle()" participant Handler as "route handler" AppRouter->>AppRouter: "req.params = {user: '1'}" AppRouter->>MountedApp: "mounted_app(req, res, next)" MountedApp->>MountedApp: "Store: orig = req.app" MountedApp->>SubRouter: "sub-router.handle(req, res, cb)" SubRouter->>SubRouter: "req.params = {action: 'get'}" SubRouter->>Handler: "Execute handler" Handler->>Handler: "Use req.params.action" Handler->>MountedApp: "callback()" MountedApp->>MountedApp: "Restore prototypes" MountedApp->>AppRouter: "req.params = {user: '1'}
(restored)" ``` **Code Example:** ```javascript router.get('/:action', function (req, res) { res.send(req.params) // { action: 'get' } }) app.use('/user/:user', router) // GET /user/1/get // Inside router handler: req.params = { action: 'get' } // After router exits: req.params = { user: '1' } (restored by mounted_app) ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:230-237](), [test/app.router.js:12-37](), [test/app.router.js:402-420]() ### mergeParams Option The `mergeParams` option preserves parent parameters when entering a router: ```javascript var router = new express.Router({ mergeParams: true }) router.get('/:action', function (req, res) { res.send(req.params) // { user: 'tj', action: 'get' } }) app.use('/user/:user', router) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:303-317]() Without `mergeParams: true`, parent params are overwritten: ```javascript var router = new express.Router() // mergeParams: false (default) router.get('/:action', function (req, res) { res.send(req.params) // { action: 'get' } - parent param lost }) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:288-301]() --- ## Summary The Express middleware execution model follows these principles: 1. **Linear Stack Execution:** Middleware executes in registration order 2. **Explicit Flow Control:** The `next()` function controls execution flow 3. **Error Isolation:** Error handling middleware (4 parameters) only executes for errors 4. **Route Scoping:** `next('route')` and `next('router')` provide route-level exits 5. **Promise Integration:** Rejected promises are converted to errors automatically 6. **Stack Safety:** Trampolining prevents stack overflow in synchronous chains 7. **Context Preservation:** `req.params` and router state are properly managed Understanding this execution model is essential for building Express applications, as it governs how requests flow through your application and how errors are handled. **Sources:** [lib/router/index.js](), [lib/router/route.js](), [lib/router/layer.js](), [test/app.router.js](), [test/Router.js](), [test/Route.js]() --- # Page: Body Parsing Middleware # Body Parsing Middleware

## Purpose and Scope This document covers Express's built-in body parsing middleware functions that parse incoming request bodies and populate `req.body`. These middleware are re-exported from the `body-parser` package and handle JSON, URL-encoded forms, plain text, and raw binary data. For information about static file serving, see [Static File Middleware](#4.3). For request object properties, see [Request Object](#2.2). --- ## Overview Express provides four body parsing middleware functions that are re-exported from the `body-parser` dependency: | Middleware | Content-Type | Output Format | Primary Use Case | |------------|--------------|---------------|------------------| | `express.json()` | `application/json` | JavaScript Object | REST APIs, JSON payloads | | `express.urlencoded()` | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` | JavaScript Object | HTML forms | | `express.text()` | `text/plain` | String | Plain text data | | `express.raw()` | `application/octet-stream` | Buffer | Binary data, file uploads | All body parsers populate `req.body` with the parsed result and skip requests without matching `Content-Type` headers. **Sources:** [lib/express.js:77-81](), [package.json:36]() --- ## Module Exports and Dependencies ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Express Module" EXPRESS["lib/express.js
createApplication"] EXPORTS["Module Exports"] end subgraph "body-parser Dependency" BP_PKG["body-parser@^2.2.1"] BP_JSON["bodyParser.json"] BP_URL["bodyParser.urlencoded"] BP_TEXT["bodyParser.text"] BP_RAW["bodyParser.raw"] end subgraph "Exported Middleware" JSON["express.json"] URL["express.urlencoded"] TEXT["express.text"] RAW["express.raw"] end subgraph "Application Usage" APP["Express App"] MIDDLEWARE["app.use()"] REQ_BODY["req.body"] end BP_PKG --> BP_JSON BP_PKG --> BP_URL BP_PKG --> BP_TEXT BP_PKG --> BP_RAW EXPRESS --> EXPORTS BP_JSON --> JSON BP_URL --> URL BP_TEXT --> TEXT BP_RAW --> RAW EXPORTS --> JSON EXPORTS --> URL EXPORTS --> TEXT EXPORTS --> RAW JSON --> MIDDLEWARE URL --> MIDDLEWARE TEXT --> MIDDLEWARE RAW --> MIDDLEWARE MIDDLEWARE --> APP APP --> REQ_BODY style EXPRESS fill:#e1f5ff style BP_PKG fill:#fff4e1 style REQ_BODY fill:#ffe1e1 ``` **Middleware Re-export Architecture** The body parsing middleware are simple re-exports from the `body-parser` module. Express does not implement body parsing logic itself; it delegates to the external `body-parser` package. **Sources:** [lib/express.js:15](), [lib/express.js:77-81](), [package.json:36]() --- ## express.json() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: application/json` and populates `req.body` with a JavaScript object. Returns an empty object `{}` for empty bodies. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse JSON bodies app.use(express.json()); app.post('/api/users', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body.name); // Access parsed JSON res.json({ received: req.body }); }); ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `reviver` | Function | `null` | JSON.parse reviver function | | `strict` | Boolean | `true` | Only accept arrays and objects | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'application/json'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Behavior - Returns `400` when JSON is malformed - Returns `413` when body exceeds `limit` - Returns `415` when `Content-Encoding` is unsupported (if `inflate: false`) - Sets `req.body = {}` for empty request bodies - Skips requests without matching `Content-Type` ### Error Scenarios ```javascript // Malformed JSON POST /api/users Content-Type: application/json {"name": // Missing closing brace // Response: 400 [entity.parse.failed] Unexpected end of JSON input // Body too large POST /api/users Content-Type: application/json Content-Length: 1034 {"str": "...1028 bytes..."} // Response: 413 [entity.too.large] request entity too large ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:1-569](), [lib/express.js:77]() --- ## express.urlencoded() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and populates `req.body` with a JavaScript object. This is the format used by HTML form submissions. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse URL-encoded bodies app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: false })); app.post('/login', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body.username); // Access form field res.send('Logged in'); }); ``` ### Extended Option The `extended` option determines which parsing library is used: | Mode | Parser | Supports | Example | |------|--------|----------|---------| | `extended: false` | `querystring` | Simple key-value pairs | `user=tobi&age=2` | | `extended: true` | `qs` | Nested objects, arrays | `user[name]=tobi&user[age]=2` | When `extended: false` (default since Express 5.x): ```javascript // Input: user=Tobi&user=Loki // Output: { user: ["Tobi", "Loki"] } ``` When `extended: true`: ```javascript // Input: user[name][first]=Tobi // Output: { user: { name: { first: "Tobi" } } } // Input: foo[0]=bar&foo[1]=baz // Output: { foo: ["bar", "baz"] } ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `extended` | Boolean | `false` | Use `qs` (true) or `querystring` (false) | | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `parameterLimit` | Number | `1000` | Maximum number of parameters | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Depth Limiting When using `extended: true`, the `qs` parser limits nesting depth to prevent deep object attacks: ```javascript // Default depth limit (32 levels) app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // Custom depth app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true, depth: 10 })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.urlencoded.js:1-497](), [lib/express.js:81]() --- ## express.text() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: text/plain` and populates `req.body` with a string. Returns an empty string `""` for empty bodies. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse plain text bodies app.use(express.text()); app.post('/notes', (req, res) => { console.log(typeof req.body); // 'string' res.send(`Received: ${req.body}`); }); ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `defaultCharset` | String | `'utf-8'` | Default character encoding | | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'text/plain'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Character Encoding The parser respects the `charset` parameter in the `Content-Type` header: ```javascript // Request with explicit charset POST /notes Content-Type: text/plain; charset=koi8-r // Body bytes: 6e616d6520697320cec5d4 // Parsed as KOI8-R: "name is нет" // Default charset configuration app.use(express.text({ defaultCharset: 'iso-8859-1' })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.text.js:1-410](), [lib/express.js:80]() --- ## express.raw() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: application/octet-stream` and populates `req.body` with a `Buffer`. Returns an empty buffer for empty bodies. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse raw binary bodies app.use(express.raw()); app.post('/upload', (req, res) => { console.log(Buffer.isBuffer(req.body)); // true console.log(req.body.length); // byte count res.send('Uploaded'); }); ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'application/octet-stream'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Binary Data Handling ```javascript // Receiving binary data POST /upload Content-Type: application/octet-stream // Handler app.post('/upload', (req, res) => { const hex = req.body.toString('hex'); const base64 = req.body.toString('base64'); res.json({ bytes: req.body.length, hex: hex }); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.raw.js:1-350](), [lib/express.js:78]() --- ## Common Options All four body parsing middleware share several common options: ### inflate Controls whether compressed request bodies are automatically decompressed: ```javascript // Accept gzip/deflate compression (default) app.use(express.json({ inflate: true })); // Reject compressed bodies app.use(express.json({ inflate: false })); // Response: 415 [encoding.unsupported] content encoding unsupported ``` ### limit Controls the maximum request body size. Accepts byte values or string notation: ```javascript // String notation app.use(express.json({ limit: '1kb' })); app.use(express.json({ limit: '10mb' })); // Numeric bytes app.use(express.json({ limit: 1024 })); // Exceeding limit // Response: 413 [entity.too.large] request entity too large ``` ### type Filters which `Content-Type` headers trigger the parser: ```javascript // String (exact match or shorthand) app.use(express.json({ type: 'application/json' })); app.use(express.json({ type: 'json' })); // shorthand // Array of types app.use(express.json({ type: ['application/json', 'application/vnd.api+json'] })); // Function predicate app.use(express.json({ type: (req) => { return req.get('Content-Type').startsWith('application/'); } })); ``` ### verify Provides a hook to verify or transform the raw body before parsing: ```javascript app.use(express.json({ verify: (req, res, buf, encoding) => { // buf is the raw Buffer // Throw error to reject request if (buf.length > 1000000) { throw new Error('Body too large'); } // Compute signature for verification req.rawBody = buf.toString(encoding); } })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:121-194](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:211-239](), [test/express.text.js:95-169](), [test/express.raw.js:86-157]() --- ## Error Handling Body parsing middleware generate structured errors that can be caught by error-handling middleware: ```mermaid graph TB REQUEST["HTTP Request"] PARSER["Body Parser
Middleware"] CHECK_CT["Check
Content-Type"] READ_BODY["Read Body
from Stream"] CHECK_SIZE["Check Size
vs limit"] INFLATE_CHECK["Decompress
if needed"] PARSE["Parse Body
by Type"] POPULATE["Set req.body"] NEXT["next()"] ERROR["next(err)"] REQUEST --> PARSER PARSER --> CHECK_CT CHECK_CT -->|No Match| NEXT CHECK_CT -->|Match| READ_BODY READ_BODY --> CHECK_SIZE CHECK_SIZE -->|Too Large| ERROR CHECK_SIZE -->|OK| INFLATE_CHECK INFLATE_CHECK -->|Unsupported| ERROR INFLATE_CHECK -->|OK| PARSE PARSE -->|Parse Error| ERROR PARSE -->|Success| POPULATE POPULATE --> NEXT ERROR -.->|400 Bad Request| ERR_PARSE["entity.parse.failed"] ERROR -.->|413 Too Large| ERR_SIZE["entity.too.large"] ERROR -.->|415 Unsupported| ERR_ENCODING["encoding.unsupported"] ERROR -.->|400 Bad Length| ERR_LENGTH["request.aborted"] ``` **Body Parser Error Flow** ### Error Types | Error Code | Status | Description | Example Cause | |------------|--------|-------------|---------------| | `entity.parse.failed` | 400 | Parse failed | Malformed JSON | | `entity.too.large` | 413 | Body exceeds limit | File too large | | `encoding.unsupported` | 415 | Unknown encoding | Brotli compression | | `request.aborted` | 400 | Invalid Content-Length | Mismatch between header and body | | `request.size.invalid` | 400 | Invalid size | Negative Content-Length | ### Error Handling Example ```javascript const app = express(); app.use(express.json()); app.post('/api/data', (req, res) => { res.json(req.body); }); // Error handler catches body parsing errors app.use((err, req, res, next) => { if (err.status === 400) { return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid request body', message: err.message }); } if (err.status === 413) { return res.status(413).json({ error: 'Request too large', message: 'Body exceeds size limit' }); } next(err); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:90-119](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:23-42](), [test/express.json.js:122-150]() --- ## Request Processing Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express as Express App participant Parser as Body Parser participant Handler as Route Handler Client->>Express: POST /api/users
Content-Type: application/json
{"name":"tobi"} Express->>Parser: app.use(express.json()) Parser->>Parser: Check Content-Type header alt Content-Type matches Parser->>Parser: Read request stream Parser->>Parser: Check body size vs limit alt Size OK Parser->>Parser: Inflate if compressed Parser->>Parser: Parse body (JSON.parse) alt Parse Success Parser->>Parser: Set req.body = {name: "tobi"} Parser->>Express: next() Express->>Handler: Route matched Handler->>Handler: Access req.body.name Handler->>Client: 200 OK {"received": {...}} else Parse Error Parser->>Express: next(err) [400] Express->>Client: 400 Bad Request end else Size Too Large Parser->>Express: next(err) [413] Express->>Client: 413 Request Too Large end else Content-Type no match Parser->>Express: next() (skip) Express->>Handler: Continue to route end ``` **Body Parser Request Flow** **Sources:** [lib/express.js:77-81](), [lib/application.js:190-244]() --- ## Multiple Body Parsers Applications commonly use multiple body parsers to handle different content types: ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); // Parse JSON bodies app.use(express.json()); // Parse URL-encoded bodies (HTML forms) app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // Parse plain text app.use(express.text({ type: 'text/plain' })); // Parse raw binary app.use(express.raw({ type: 'application/octet-stream' })); // Routes now handle multiple content types app.post('/data', (req, res) => { // req.body populated based on Content-Type res.json({ received: req.body }); }); ``` ### Duplicate Middleware Body parsers can be used multiple times without issues. They skip requests that don't match their `Content-Type` filter: ```javascript // This is valid (though redundant) app.use(express.json()); app.use(express.json()); // Both parsers execute, but second one sees // req.body already populated and passes through ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:73-88](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:62-77]() --- ## Content-Type Filtering Body parsers only process requests matching their configured `Content-Type`. Non-matching requests skip the parser entirely: ```javascript const app = express(); // Only parses application/json app.use(express.json()); // POST with text/plain -> req.body undefined // POST with application/json -> req.body populated // POST with application/xml -> req.body undefined ``` ### Custom Type Filters ```javascript // Parse multiple JSON-like types app.use(express.json({ type: ['application/json', 'application/vnd.api+json'] })); // Function-based filtering app.use(express.json({ type: (req) => { const ct = req.get('Content-Type') || ''; return ct.includes('json') || ct.includes('api'); } })); // Parse all text/* types as JSON app.use(express.json({ type: 'text/*' })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:207-282](), [test/express.text.js:201-290](), [test/express.raw.js:189-271]() --- ## AsyncLocalStorage Support All body parsers preserve `AsyncLocalStorage` context across asynchronous operations: ```javascript const { AsyncLocalStorage } = require('async_hooks'); const asyncLocalStorage = new AsyncLocalStorage(); app.use((req, res, next) => { const store = { requestId: Date.now() }; asyncLocalStorage.run(store, next); }); app.use(express.json()); app.post('/data', (req, res) => { const store = asyncLocalStorage.getStore(); console.log(store.requestId); // Still accessible res.json(req.body); }); ``` This ensures that any context stored in `AsyncLocalStorage` remains available after body parsing completes, even though the parsing is asynchronous. **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:319-384](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:337-398](), [test/express.text.js:327-388](), [test/express.raw.js:311-372]() --- ## Integration with Request Object After successful parsing, body parsers populate `req.body`. This property is not initialized until a body parser runs: ```javascript app.post('/data', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body); // undefined before body parsing }); app.use(express.json()); app.post('/data', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body); // Object, string, or Buffer after parsing console.log(typeof req.body); // Varies by parser }); ``` ### Body Property Types | Parser | `req.body` Type | Empty Body Value | |--------|-----------------|------------------| | `express.json()` | Object | `{}` | | `express.urlencoded()` | Object | `{}` | | `express.text()` | String | `""` | | `express.raw()` | Buffer | `Buffer.alloc(0)` | **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:11-41](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:15-60](), [test/express.text.js:14-58](), [test/express.raw.js:15-63]() --- # Page: Static File Middleware # Static File Middleware
