## Purpose and Scope This document describes Express's cookie management system, including setting and clearing cookies through response methods, cookie options and security features, and the cookie signing mechanism. For information about session management built on top of cookies, see external middleware like `express-session`. For request body parsing, see [Body Parsing Middleware](#4.2). For general response methods, see [Response Object](#2.3). ## Cookie System Architecture Express provides cookie management through response methods and integrates with external middleware for cookie parsing. The system handles cookie serialization, signing, and HTTP header generation. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Response Methods" COOKIE[res.cookie] CLEAR[res.clearCookie] end subgraph "Dependencies" COOKIE_LIB["cookie module
serialize/parse"] SIGNATURE["cookie-signature module
sign/unsign"] end subgraph "Request Side" PARSER["cookie-parser middleware
(external)"] REQ_COOKIES["req.cookies"] REQ_SIGNED["req.signedCookies"] end subgraph "Storage & Options" SECRET["req.secret
(from cookie-parser)"] OPTIONS["Cookie Options
maxAge, signed, httpOnly,
secure, sameSite, etc."] end subgraph "HTTP Layer" SET_COOKIE["Set-Cookie header"] COOKIE_HEADER["Cookie header"] end COOKIE --> COOKIE_LIB COOKIE --> OPTIONS COOKIE --> SECRET COOKIE --> SIGNATURE SIGNATURE --> SET_COOKIE COOKIE_LIB --> SET_COOKIE CLEAR --> COOKIE COOKIE_HEADER --> PARSER PARSER --> REQ_COOKIES PARSER --> REQ_SIGNED PARSER --> SECRET SET_COOKIE -.sent to.-> CLIENT[HTTP Client] CLIENT -.sends.-> COOKIE_HEADER ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:709-775](), [package.json:39-40](), [test/res.cookie.js](), [test/req.signedCookies.js]() ## Setting Cookies with res.cookie() The `res.cookie()` method sets cookies in the HTTP response by appending to the `Set-Cookie` header. It accepts a name, value, and options object. ### Method Signature ```javascript res.cookie(name, value, options) ``` ### Parameters | Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | `name` | String | Cookie name | | `value` | String \| Object | Cookie value (objects are JSON-serialized) | | `options` | Object | Cookie configuration options (optional) | ### Basic Usage The method serializes values and delegates to the `cookie` module for header generation: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Handler as Route Handler participant ResCookie as res.cookie participant Serialize as Value Serialization participant Sign as cookie-signature participant CookieLib as cookie.serialize participant Response as HTTP Response Handler->>ResCookie: cookie(name, value, options) ResCookie->>ResCookie: Copy options alt value is object ResCookie->>Serialize: JSON.stringify(value) Serialize-->>ResCookie: 'j:' + json else value is string ResCookie->>Serialize: String(value) end alt signed option is true ResCookie->>ResCookie: Check req.secret exists ResCookie->>Sign: sign(val, secret) Sign-->>ResCookie: 's:' + signature end alt maxAge provided ResCookie->>ResCookie: Calculate expires date ResCookie->>ResCookie: Convert maxAge to seconds end ResCookie->>ResCookie: Set default path='/' ResCookie->>CookieLib: serialize(name, val, opts) CookieLib-->>ResCookie: Cookie string ResCookie->>Response: append('Set-Cookie', string) ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:742-775]() ### Value Serialization Express automatically serializes cookie values based on type: | Value Type | Serialization | Example | |------------|---------------|---------| | String | `String(value)` | `'tobi'` → `tobi` | | Object | `'j:' + JSON.stringify(value)` | `{name: 'tobi'}` → `j:{"name":"tobi"}` | | Signed String | `'s:' + sign(value, secret)` | `'tobi'` → `s:tobi.signature` | | Signed Object | `'s:' + sign('j:' + JSON, secret)` | `{name: 'tobi'}` → `s:j:{"name":"tobi"}.signature` | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:751-757]() ## Cookie Options The `options` parameter accepts various configuration properties that control cookie behavior and security. ### Options Reference | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `maxAge` | Number | - | Max-Age in milliseconds; converted to seconds and sets `expires` | | `expires` | Date | - | Expiration date (prefer `maxAge` for relative expiration) | | `path` | String | `'/'` | Cookie path | | `domain` | String | - | Cookie domain | | `secure` | Boolean | `false` | HTTPS-only flag | | `httpOnly` | Boolean | `false` | HTTP-only flag (not accessible to JavaScript) | | `sameSite` | Boolean \| String | - | `'strict'`, `'lax'`, `'none'`, or `true`/`false` | | `signed` | Boolean | `false` | Sign the cookie with `cookie-signature` | | `partitioned` | Boolean | `false` | Partitioned cookie (CHIPS) | | `priority` | String | - | `'low'`, `'medium'`, or `'high'` | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:721-740](), [test/res.cookie.js:54-243]() ### maxAge Processing The `maxAge` option receives special processing to compute both `Max-Age` and `Expires` attributes: ```mermaid graph LR INPUT["maxAge: 1000
(milliseconds)"] SUBTRACT["maxAge - 0
(coerce to number)"] CHECK{isNaN?} EXPIRES["expires = new Date
(Date.now() + maxAge)"] MAXAGE_SEC["maxAge = Math.floor
(maxAge / 1000)"] OUTPUT["Cookie with both
Max-Age and Expires"] INPUT --> SUBTRACT SUBTRACT --> CHECK CHECK -->|No| EXPIRES EXPIRES --> MAXAGE_SEC MAXAGE_SEC --> OUTPUT CHECK -->|Yes| SKIP[Skip processing] ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:759-766]() ### Path Default If no `path` option is provided, Express defaults to `'/'`: **Sources:** [lib/response.js:768-770]() ## Clearing Cookies with res.clearCookie() The `res.clearCookie()` method clears cookies by setting an expiration date in the past. ### Method Signature ```javascript res.clearCookie(name, options) ``` ### Implementation Details The method forces cookie expiration by setting `expires` to epoch time and removing any user-provided `maxAge` or `expires`: ```mermaid graph TB CLEAR_START["res.clearCookie(name, options)"] MERGE["opts = {
path: '/',
...options,
expires: new Date(1)
}"] DELETE["delete opts.maxAge"] DELEGATE["res.cookie(name, '', opts)"] HEADER["Set-Cookie: name=;
Path=/;
Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970
00:00:00 GMT"] CLEAR_START --> MERGE MERGE --> DELETE DELETE --> DELEGATE DELEGATE --> HEADER ``` **Key Behavior:** - Forces `expires: new Date(1)` (January 1, 1970, 00:00:01 GMT) - Removes user-provided `maxAge` and `expires` options - Preserves other options (path, domain, etc.) for proper cookie matching - Sets cookie value to empty string **Sources:** [lib/response.js:709-716](), [test/res.clearCookie.js:36-60]() ### Cookie Matching For `clearCookie()` to work correctly, the `path` and `domain` options must match those used when setting the cookie: | Set Cookie | Clear Cookie | Result | |------------|--------------|--------| | `cookie('id', 'val', {path: '/admin'})` | `clearCookie('id', {path: '/admin'})` | ✓ Cleared | | `cookie('id', 'val', {path: '/admin'})` | `clearCookie('id')` | ✗ Not cleared (path mismatch) | | `cookie('id', 'val', {domain: '.example.com'})` | `clearCookie('id', {domain: '.example.com'})` | ✓ Cleared | **Sources:** [test/res.clearCookie.js:22-34]() ## Signed Cookies Signed cookies provide tamper detection by appending an HMAC signature to the cookie value. ### Configuration Signed cookies require a secret key stored in `req.secret`, typically set by the `cookie-parser` middleware: ```mermaid graph LR PARSER["app.use(cookieParser('secret'))"] SECRET["req.secret = 'secret'"] COOKIE["res.cookie('name', 'val',
{signed: true})"] SIGN["cookie-signature.sign()"] OUTPUT["Set-Cookie: name=s:val.signature"] PARSER --> SECRET SECRET --> COOKIE COOKIE --> SIGN SIGN --> OUTPUT ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:744-757](), [test/res.cookie.js:245-276]() ### Signing Process When `signed: true` is specified: 1. Express checks if `req.secret` exists (throws error if missing) 2. Value is serialized (with `'j:'` prefix for objects) 3. `cookie-signature.sign(value, secret)` generates signature 4. Result is prefixed with `'s:'` and combined with signature ### Error Handling Attempting to create signed cookies without a secret throws an error: **Sources:** [lib/response.js:747-749](), [test/res.cookie.js:262-276]() ## Reading Cookies Express does not provide built-in cookie parsing on the request side. The `cookie-parser` middleware (external package) is required to populate `req.cookies` and `req.signedCookies`. ### Middleware Integration ```mermaid graph TB REQUEST["Incoming Request
Cookie: name=value; signed=s:val.sig"] PARSER["cookie-parser middleware"] PARSE["cookie.parse()"] UNSIGNED["req.cookies = {name: 'value'}"] SIGNED["req.signedCookies = {signed: 'val'}"] HANDLER["Route Handler
Access via req.cookies"] REQUEST --> PARSER PARSER --> PARSE PARSE --> UNSIGNED PARSE --> SIGNED UNSIGNED --> HANDLER SIGNED --> HANDLER ``` **Sources:** [test/req.signedCookies.js](), [test/res.cookie.js:249-250]() ### Signed Cookie Verification The `cookie-parser` middleware: - Parses all cookies from the `Cookie` header - Identifies signed cookies (prefixed with `'s:'`) - Verifies signatures using the provided secret - Places verified cookies in `req.signedCookies` - Places unsigned/invalid cookies in `req.cookies` **Sources:** [test/req.signedCookies.js:9-34]() ## Cookie Append Behavior Express uses `res.append()` internally to add cookies to the `Set-Cookie` header, allowing multiple cookies to be set in a single response: ```mermaid graph TB C1["res.cookie('name', 'tobi')"] C2["res.cookie('age', 1)"] C3["res.cookie('gender', '?')"] APPEND1["res.append('Set-Cookie', 'name=tobi; Path=/')"] APPEND2["res.append('Set-Cookie', 'age=1; Path=/')"] APPEND3["res.append('Set-Cookie', 'gender=%3F; Path=/')"] HEADER["Set-Cookie:
name=tobi; Path=/,
age=1; Path=/,
gender=%3F; Path=/"] C1 --> APPEND1 C2 --> APPEND2 C3 --> APPEND3 APPEND1 --> HEADER APPEND2 --> HEADER APPEND3 --> HEADER ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:772](), [test/res.cookie.js:37-51]() ## Dependencies and Modules Express's cookie management relies on two key external modules: | Module | Version | Purpose | Functions Used | |--------|---------|---------|----------------| | `cookie` | ^0.7.1 | Cookie parsing and serialization | `cookie.serialize()` | | `cookie-signature` | ^1.2.1 | Cookie signing and verification | `sign()` | **Sources:** [package.json:39-40](), [lib/response.js:26,30]() --- # Page: Headers and Caching # Headers and Caching

This document covers HTTP caching mechanisms in Express, including ETag generation, freshness checking, conditional requests, and cache-control headers. These features enable efficient HTTP caching to reduce bandwidth and improve performance. For cookie-related headers, see [Cookie Management](#5.4). For general response header manipulation using `res.set()`, `res.get()`, and `res.append()`, see [Response Object](#2.3). ## Overview Express provides automatic HTTP caching support through three primary mechanisms: | Mechanism | Purpose | Key Components | |-----------|---------|----------------| | **ETag Generation** | Generate entity tags for cache validation | `app.set('etag')`, `etag` module | | **Freshness Checking** | Compare ETags and timestamps for 304 responses | `req.fresh`, `fresh` module | | **Cache-Control** | Set cache directives for file serving | `send` module, `res.sendFile()` options | Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218](), [lib/request.js:469-486](), [lib/utils.js:130-152]() ## ETag Generation in res.send() ### Automatic ETag Flow When `res.send()` is called, Express automatically generates an ETag header based on the response body if the `etag` application setting is enabled. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["res.send(body)"] Start --> CheckType["Determine body type
(string, Buffer, object)"] CheckType --> GetETagFn["Get etag fn from
app.get('etag fn')"] GetETagFn --> CheckGenerate{"ETag header
already set?"} CheckGenerate -->|No| ShouldGenerate{"typeof etagFn
=== 'function'?"} CheckGenerate -->|Yes| CheckFresh ShouldGenerate -->|Yes| CalcLength["Calculate Content-Length"] ShouldGenerate -->|No| CheckFresh CalcLength --> GenerateETag["etag = etagFn(chunk, encoding)"] GenerateETag --> SetETag["res.set('ETag', etag)"] SetETag --> CheckFresh{"req.fresh?"} CheckFresh -->|Yes| Set304["res.status(304)
Strip body and headers"] CheckFresh -->|No| SendBody["res.end(chunk, encoding)"] Set304 --> End["Response complete"] SendBody --> End ``` **Diagram: ETag Generation and Freshness Check Flow in res.send()** Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218]() ### Implementation Details The ETag generation logic in `res.send()` follows these steps: 1. **Check if generation is needed** [lib/response.js:160-162](): - Skip if ETag header already set manually - Skip if `etag fn` is not a function 2. **Calculate content length** [lib/response.js:165-180](): - For `Buffer`: Use `chunk.length` - For small strings (<1000 bytes) without ETag: Use `Buffer.byteLength()` - For larger content: Convert to Buffer for accurate length 3. **Generate ETag** [lib/response.js:184-189](): ```javascript if (generateETag && len !== undefined) { if ((etag = etagFn(chunk, encoding))) { this.set('ETag', etag); } } ``` 4. **Check freshness** [lib/response.js:192](): - If `req.fresh` is true, set status to 304 5. **Strip headers for 304/204 responses** [lib/response.js:195-200](): - Remove Content-Type, Content-Length, Transfer-Encoding - Set empty body Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218]() ## ETag Configuration ### The etag Setting Express provides the `etag` application setting to control ETag generation behavior: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Settings" SetETag["app.set('etag', value)"] end subgraph "Compilation - compileETag()" SetETag --> CheckType{"typeof value?"} CheckType -->|"function"| ReturnFn["Return value as-is"] CheckType -->|"true or 'weak'"| UseWeak["Return utils.wetag"] CheckType -->|"'strong'"| UseStrong["Return utils.etag"] CheckType -->|"false"| ReturnUndef["Return undefined"] end subgraph "Storage" ReturnFn --> StoreFn["app.settings['etag fn'] = fn"] UseWeak --> StoreFn UseStrong --> StoreFn ReturnUndef --> StoreFn end subgraph "ETag Generation Functions" StoreFn --> Wetag["utils.wetag(body, encoding)
Weak ETag: W/\"..\""] StoreFn --> Etag["utils.etag(body, encoding)
Strong ETag: \"..\""] end subgraph "External Module" Wetag --> EtagModule["etag module
(SHA1 hash)"] Etag --> EtagModule end ``` **Diagram: ETag Setting Compilation and Function Selection** Sources: [lib/application.js:364-366](), [lib/utils.js:130-152](), [lib/utils.js:40-51]() ### Configuration Options | Setting Value | Behavior | ETag Format | |--------------|----------|-------------| | `true` or `'weak'` (default) | Generate weak ETags | `W/""` | | `'strong'` | Generate strong ETags | `""` | | `false` | Disable automatic ETag generation | None | | `function(body, encoding)` | Custom ETag generator | Custom | **Example configurations:** ```javascript // Default: weak ETags app.set('etag', 'weak'); // or app.set('etag', true) // Strong ETags app.set('etag', 'strong'); // Disable ETags app.set('etag', false); // Custom ETag function app.set('etag', function(body, encoding) { var buf = !Buffer.isBuffer(body) ? Buffer.from(body, encoding) : body; return '"custom-' + buf.length + '"'; }); ``` Sources: [lib/utils.js:130-152](), [test/res.send.js:361-568]() ### ETag Generation Functions Express uses the `etag` module to generate ETags based on SHA1 hashing: **Strong ETag** [lib/utils.js:40](): ```javascript exports.etag = createETagGenerator({ weak: false }) ``` **Weak ETag** [lib/utils.js:51](): ```javascript exports.wetag = createETagGenerator({ weak: true }) ``` The `createETagGenerator()` function [lib/utils.js:249-257](): ```javascript function createETagGenerator (options) { return function generateETag (body, encoding) { var buf = !Buffer.isBuffer(body) ? Buffer.from(body, encoding) : body return etag(buf, options) } } ``` Sources: [lib/utils.js:40-51](), [lib/utils.js:249-257]() ## Freshness Checking ### The req.fresh Getter The `req.fresh` getter determines if the cached response is still valid by comparing ETags and Last-Modified timestamps. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["req.fresh getter"] Start --> CheckMethod{"req.method === 'GET'
or 'HEAD'?"} CheckMethod -->|No| ReturnFalse1["return false"] CheckMethod -->|Yes| CheckStatus{"res.statusCode
2xx or 304?"} CheckStatus -->|No| ReturnFalse2["return false"] CheckStatus -->|Yes| GetHeaders["Get response headers:
- ETag
- Last-Modified"] GetHeaders --> CallFresh["Call fresh module:
fresh(req.headers, resHeaders)"] CallFresh --> Compare["Compare:
- If-None-Match vs ETag
- If-Modified-Since vs Last-Modified"] Compare --> Result{"Match?"} Result -->|Yes| ReturnTrue["return true"] Result -->|No| ReturnFalse3["return false"] ``` **Diagram: Freshness Checking Process (req.fresh)** Sources: [lib/request.js:469-486]() ### Implementation The `req.fresh` getter [lib/request.js:469-486](): ```javascript defineGetter(req, 'fresh', function(){ var method = this.method; var res = this.res var status = res.statusCode // GET or HEAD for weak freshness validation only if ('GET' !== method && 'HEAD' !== method) return false; // 2xx or 304 as per rfc2616 14.26 if ((status >= 200 && status < 300) || 304 === status) { return fresh(this.headers, { 'etag': res.get('ETag'), 'last-modified': res.get('Last-Modified') }) } return false; }); ``` The `fresh` module (external dependency) compares: - **If-None-Match** request header with **ETag** response header - **If-Modified-Since** request header with **Last-Modified** response header Sources: [lib/request.js:469-486](), [package.json:47]() ### Conditional Request Headers | Request Header | Response Header | Purpose | |---------------|-----------------|---------| | `If-None-Match` | `ETag` | Entity tag comparison for cache validation | | `If-Modified-Since` | `Last-Modified` | Timestamp comparison for cache validation | | `If-Match` | `ETag` | Precondition check (used by `send` module) | | `If-Unmodified-Since` | `Last-Modified` | Precondition check (used by `send` module) | Sources: [lib/request.js:469-486]() ## Cache-Control Headers in File Serving ### res.sendFile() Caching When serving files with `res.sendFile()`, Express delegates to the `send` module which provides comprehensive caching support: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "File Serving Flow" SendFile["res.sendFile(path, options)"] SendFile --> ConfigETag["Set opts.etag from
app.enabled('etag')"] ConfigETag --> CreateStream["Create send stream:
send(req, pathname, opts)"] CreateStream --> SendModule["send module"] end subgraph "send Module Caching" SendModule --> GenETag["Generate ETag from
file stats (mtime, size)"] GenETag --> GenLastMod["Set Last-Modified
from file mtime"] GenLastMod --> SetCacheControl["Set Cache-Control
(default: public, max-age=0)"] SetCacheControl --> CheckCond["Check conditional headers:
If-None-Match, If-Modified-Since"] CheckCond -->|"Fresh"| Send304["Send 304 Not Modified"] CheckCond -->|"Not Fresh"| SendFile2["Send file with 200"] end subgraph "Options" CacheControlOpt["options.cacheControl
(true/false)"] MaxAgeOpt["options.maxAge
(milliseconds)"] ETagOpt["options.etag
(from app setting)"] AcceptRangesOpt["options.acceptRanges
(true/false)"] CacheControlOpt --> SetCacheControl MaxAgeOpt --> SetCacheControl ETagOpt --> GenETag AcceptRangesOpt --> SendModule end ``` **Diagram: File Serving Caching Architecture** Sources: [lib/response.js:371-413](), [test/res.sendFile.js:57-123]() ### sendFile() Caching Options The `res.sendFile()` method accepts options that control caching behavior: | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `cacheControl` | Boolean | `true` | Enable/disable Cache-Control header | | `maxAge` | Number/String | `0` | Max age in milliseconds or parseable string | | `etag` | Boolean | `app.enabled('etag')` | Enable/disable ETag generation (automatically set) | | `acceptRanges` | Boolean | `true` | Enable/disable Accept-Ranges header | | `lastModified` | Boolean | `true` | Enable/disable Last-Modified header | **Example usage:** ```javascript // Default caching behavior res.sendFile('/path/to/file.pdf'); // Cache-Control: public, max-age=0 // ETag: "10-abc123" // Last-Modified: Wed, 01 Jan 2020 00:00:00 GMT // Custom max-age res.sendFile('/path/to/file.pdf', { maxAge: '1d' }); // Cache-Control: public, max-age=86400 // Disable caching res.sendFile('/path/to/file.pdf', { cacheControl: false }); // (no Cache-Control header) ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:371-413](), [test/res.sendFile.js:415-451]() ### express.static Caching The `express.static()` middleware (powered by `serve-static`) provides the same caching capabilities: ```javascript // Default static file serving app.use(express.static('public')); // Custom cache settings app.use(express.static('public', { maxAge: '1d', // Cache for 1 day etag: true, // Enable ETags (default) lastModified: true, // Enable Last-Modified (default) cacheControl: true, // Enable Cache-Control (default) immutable: true // Add immutable directive })); ``` Sources: [lib/express.js:79](), [test/express.static.js:188-211]() ## 304 Not Modified Responses ### Status Code 304 Behavior When a response has status code 304, Express automatically strips certain headers and the body: ```javascript // In res.send() [lib/response.js:195-200] if (204 === this.statusCode || 304 === this.statusCode) { this.removeHeader('Content-Type'); this.removeHeader('Content-Length'); this.removeHeader('Transfer-Encoding'); chunk = ''; } ``` This follows HTTP specification requirements for 304 responses, which: - MUST NOT contain a message body - MUST NOT include Content-Length, Transfer-Encoding - MAY include Cache-Control, ETag, and other cache-related headers Sources: [lib/response.js:195-200]() ### 204 No Content and 205 Reset Content Express also handles special status codes that affect body transmission: | Status Code | Body | Content-Length | Other Headers Removed | |-------------|------|----------------|-----------------------| | 204 No Content | Stripped | Removed | Content-Type, Transfer-Encoding | | 205 Reset Content | Stripped | Set to `'0'` | Transfer-Encoding | | 304 Not Modified | Stripped | Removed | Content-Type, Transfer-Encoding | **204 handling** [lib/response.js:195-200](): ```javascript if (204 === this.statusCode || 304 === this.statusCode) { this.removeHeader('Content-Type'); this.removeHeader('Content-Length'); this.removeHeader('Transfer-Encoding'); chunk = ''; } ``` **205 handling** [lib/response.js:203-207](): ```javascript if (this.statusCode === 205) { this.set('Content-Length', '0') this.removeHeader('Transfer-Encoding') chunk = '' } ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:195-207](), [test/res.send.js:239-270]() ## Manual Cache Control ### Setting Headers Manually You can manually set cache-related headers using `res.set()` or `res.header()`: ```javascript // Manual ETag res.set('ETag', '"custom-etag-value"'); res.send(data); // Manual Cache-Control res.set('Cache-Control', 'public, max-age=3600, must-revalidate'); // Manual Last-Modified res.set('Last-Modified', new Date().toUTCString()); // Manual Vary res.vary('Accept-Encoding'); ``` **Note:** When you manually set an ETag header, Express will NOT override it with an automatically generated one [lib/response.js:162](). Sources: [lib/response.js:664-686](), [test/res.send.js:429-443]() ### res.vary() Method The `res.vary()` method adds fields to the Vary response header: ```javascript res.vary(field) ``` The Vary header indicates which request headers affect the response, enabling proper cache key generation: ```javascript // Add Accept-Encoding to Vary res.vary('Accept-Encoding'); // Add multiple fields res.vary(['Accept', 'Accept-Encoding']); // Used internally by res.format() res.format({ 'text/html': () => res.send('

hello

'), 'application/json': () => res.json({ message: 'hello' }) }); // Automatically adds: Vary: Accept ``` Implementation [lib/response.js:875-879](): ```javascript res.vary = function(field){ vary(this, field); return this; }; ``` The `vary` module (external dependency) handles the header manipulation to ensure proper Vary header formatting. Sources: [lib/response.js:875-879](), [lib/response.js:569-594](), [package.json:62]() ## Complete Caching Flow Example ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express participant Utils as utils.compileETag participant ETagMod as etag module participant FreshMod as fresh module Note over Client,FreshMod: First Request (No Cache) Client->>Express: GET /data Express->>Express: res.send(data) Express->>Utils: Get etag fn Utils-->>Express: utils.wetag Express->>ETagMod: wetag(data, encoding) ETagMod-->>Express: W/"abc123" Express->>Express: Set ETag: W/"abc123" Express->>Client: 200 OK
ETag: W/"abc123"
Cache-Control: public, max-age=0
Body: data Note over Client,FreshMod: Second Request (With Cache) Client->>Express: GET /data
If-None-Match: W/"abc123" Express->>Express: res.send(data) Express->>Utils: Get etag fn Utils-->>Express: utils.wetag Express->>ETagMod: wetag(data, encoding) ETagMod-->>Express: W/"abc123" Express->>Express: Set ETag: W/"abc123" Express->>Express: Check req.fresh Express->>FreshMod: fresh(reqHeaders, resHeaders) FreshMod-->>Express: true (ETags match) Express->>Express: res.status(304) Express->>Express: Strip body and headers Express->>Client: 304 Not Modified
ETag: W/"abc123"
(no body) ``` **Diagram: Complete HTTP Caching Flow with ETag Validation** Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218](), [lib/request.js:469-486](), [lib/utils.js:40-51]() ## Testing Caching Behavior The test suite demonstrates various caching scenarios: **Basic ETag generation** [test/res.send.js:85-97](): - Tests automatic ETag generation for string responses - Verifies weak ETag format: `W/"-"` **Conditional requests** [test/res.send.js:304-318](): - Tests 304 responses when If-None-Match matches ETag - Verifies body is stripped for 304 responses **ETag settings** [test/res.send.js:361-568](): - Tests different `etag` setting values: true, false, 'weak', 'strong', function - Tests manual ETag overrides **File serving with caching** [test/res.sendFile.js:57-80](): - Tests ETag generation for files - Tests 304 responses for file requests **Static file caching** [test/express.static.js:103-115](): - Tests conditional requests with static files - Tests Cache-Control headers Sources: [test/res.send.js:85-568](), [test/res.sendFile.js:57-123](), [test/express.static.js:103-211]() --- # Page: View and Template System # View and Template System
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for generating this wiki page: - [lib/view.js](lib/view.js) - [test/app.engine.js](test/app.engine.js) - [test/app.render.js](test/app.render.js) - [test/fixtures/blog/post/index.tmpl](test/fixtures/blog/post/index.tmpl) - [test/fixtures/email.tmpl](test/fixtures/email.tmpl) - [test/fixtures/user.html](test/fixtures/user.html) - [test/fixtures/user.tmpl](test/fixtures/user.tmpl) - [test/res.render.js](test/res.render.js) - [test/support/tmpl.js](test/support/tmpl.js)
**Purpose**: This document explains Express's view and template system, which enables server-side rendering of HTML and other content through pluggable template engines. It covers the `View` class, template engine integration, view resolution, and the rendering process. **Scope**: This page focuses on the internal architecture of the view system. For detailed information about view resolution algorithms, see [View Resolution](#6.1). For documentation on rendering methods (`app.render()`, `res.render()`) and locals management, see [Template Rendering](#6.2). --- ## Overview The Express view system provides a modular architecture for server-side template rendering. It separates concerns between: - **View resolution**: Locating template files on disk - **Engine loading**: Dynamically loading and caching template engines - **Rendering orchestration**: Passing data to engines and handling callbacks The system is built around the `View` class in [lib/view.js:1-206](), which encapsulates all view-related functionality. Applications configure the view system through settings and register template engines using `app.engine()`. **Sources**: [lib/view.js:1-206]() --- ## View Class Architecture The `View` class is the core abstraction for template files. Each instance represents a single template file and manages its resolution, engine loading, and rendering. ```mermaid classDiagram class View { +string name +string ext +string root +string path +string defaultEngine +function engine +lookup(fileName) string +resolve(dir, file) string +render(options, callback) void } class TemplateEngine { <> +__express(path, options, callback) } class Application { +render(name, options, callback) +engine(ext, fn) +set(setting, value) } class Response { +render(view, options, callback) } View --> TemplateEngine : uses Application --> View : creates Response --> View : creates via app ``` **View Constructor** The `View` constructor accepts a view name and options object: | Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | `name` | string | View name (with or without extension) | | `options.root` | string or array | Root directory/directories for view lookup | | `options.defaultEngine` | string | Default template engine extension (e.g., 'ejs', 'pug') | | `options.engines` | object | Cache of loaded template engine functions | [lib/view.js:52-95]() **Key Properties** - `this.name`: Original view name passed to constructor - `this.ext`: File extension extracted from name or derived from `defaultEngine` - `this.root`: Root directory for view lookup - `this.path`: Resolved absolute path to the template file (set by `lookup()`) - `this.engine`: Template engine function for this view's extension **Sources**: [lib/view.js:52-95]() --- ## Template Engine Integration Express uses a convention-based approach for template engine integration. Engines must export a function named `__express` that follows a specific signature. ### Engine Registration Template engines are registered using `app.engine(ext, fn)`: ```javascript // Example: registering a custom engine app.engine('html', function(filePath, options, callback) { // Read template, process options, invoke callback fs.readFile(filePath, 'utf8', function(err, content) { if (err) return callback(err); // ... process content with options ... callback(null, result); }); }); ``` [test/app.engine.js:8-14]() ### Engine Loading Flow When a `View` is instantiated, if the engine for its extension is not already cached, Express dynamically loads it: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant App as "Application" participant View as "View Constructor" participant Cache as "opts.engines" participant Module as "require()" App->>View: "new View(name, opts)" View->>View: "Extract extension" View->>Cache: "Check opts.engines[ext]" alt Engine not cached View->>Module: "require(ext)" Module-->>View: "module" View->>Module: "Access module.__express" alt __express not found View-->>App: "throw Error" else __express exists View->>Cache: "Store engine function" View->>View: "this.engine = fn" end else Engine cached View->>View: "this.engine = cached fn" end ``` [lib/view.js:75-91]() **Engine Requirements** Template engine modules must export a function named `__express` with signature: ``` function(path: string, options: object, callback: function): void ``` If the module does not export `__express`, Express throws: `"Module \"[name]\" does not provide a view engine."` **Sources**: [lib/view.js:75-91](), [test/app.engine.js:1-84]() --- ## View Resolution Process View resolution determines the absolute file system path for a given view name. The process handles multiple root directories, automatic extension appending, and index file fallbacks. ### Resolution Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["View.lookup(fileName)"] --> IterateRoots["For each root in this.root"] IterateRoots --> ResolveRoot["resolve(root, fileName)"] ResolveRoot --> ExtractParts["dir = dirname(loc)
file = basename(loc)"] ExtractParts --> CallResolve["this.resolve(dir, file)"] CallResolve --> TryExact["Try: dir/file"] TryExact --> CheckExact{"fs.statSync
isFile?"} CheckExact -->|Yes| ReturnPath["return path"] CheckExact -->|No| TryIndex["Try: dir/basename(file, ext)/index.ext"] TryIndex --> CheckIndex{"fs.statSync
isFile?"} CheckIndex -->|Yes| ReturnPath CheckIndex -->|No| NextRoot["Try next root"] NextRoot --> IterateRoots IterateRoots -->|No more roots| ReturnUndefined["return undefined"] ReturnPath --> End["Path resolved"] ReturnUndefined --> End ``` **Resolution Steps** ([lib/view.js:104-123](), [lib/view.js:169-187]()): 1. **Iterate roots**: The `lookup()` method iterates through root directories (single string or array) 2. **Build paths**: For each root, construct potential file paths 3. **Try exact match**: Check if `root/fileName` exists and is a file 4. **Try index fallback**: Check if `root/baseName/index.ext` exists and is a file 5. **Return first match**: Return the first successfully resolved path **Example Resolution**: ``` View name: "blog/post" Extension: ".tmpl" Roots: ["/app/views"] Attempts: 1. /app/views/blog/post.tmpl (not found) 2. /app/views/blog/post/index.tmpl (found!) ✓ ``` [test/res.render.js:97-110](), [test/fixtures/blog/post/index.tmpl:1]() ### Multiple Root Directories When `views` setting is an array, Express searches directories in order until finding a match: ```javascript app.set('views', [ path.join(__dirname, 'themes/custom'), path.join(__dirname, 'themes/default') ]); ``` [test/app.render.js:152-186]() **Sources**: [lib/view.js:104-123](), [lib/view.js:169-187](), [test/res.render.js:97-110](), [test/app.render.js:152-203]() --- ## View Rendering Process The rendering process involves invoking the template engine with merged data (locals) and handling both synchronous and asynchronous engines. ### Rendering Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as "Handler" participant ResRender as "res.render()" participant AppRender as "app.render()" participant View as "View instance" participant Engine as "Template Engine" Client->>ResRender: "res.render(name, locals, cb)" ResRender->>ResRender: "Merge app.locals +
res.locals + locals" ResRender->>AppRender: "app.render(name, options, cb)" AppRender->>AppRender: "Get/create View instance" Note over AppRender: Check view cache if enabled alt View cached AppRender->>AppRender: "Retrieve from cache" else View not cached AppRender->>View: "new View(name, opts)" View->>View: "lookup() to resolve path" alt Path not found View-->>AppRender: "throw Error" AppRender-->>ResRender: "callback(err)" end alt Caching enabled AppRender->>AppRender: "Store in cache" end end AppRender->>View: "view.render(options, cb)" View->>Engine: "engine(path, options, onRender)" alt Engine synchronous Engine-->>View: "Immediate callback" View->>View: "Defer with process.nextTick()" View-->>AppRender: "callback(err, html)" else Engine asynchronous Engine-->>View: "Async callback" View-->>AppRender: "callback(err, html)" end AppRender-->>ResRender: "callback(err, html)" alt Callback provided ResRender-->>Client: "callback(err, html)" else No callback ResRender->>ResRender: "res.send(html)" end ``` ### Synchronous Engine Normalization To ensure consistent asynchronous behavior, the `View.render()` method detects synchronous engines and defers their callbacks: [lib/view.js:133-159]() ```javascript View.prototype.render = function render(options, callback) { var sync = true; this.engine(this.path, options, function onRender() { if (!sync) { // Engine was async, invoke callback directly return callback.apply(this, arguments); } // Engine was sync, defer callback to next tick var args = new Array(arguments.length); for (var i = 0; i < arguments.length; i++) { args[i] = arguments[i]; } return process.nextTick(function renderTick() { return callback.apply(this, args); }); }); sync = false; }; ``` This pattern prevents issues where synchronous engines might complete before the calling code is ready to handle the result. **Sources**: [lib/view.js:133-159](), [test/res.render.js:1-368](), [test/app.render.js:1-375]() --- ## View Configuration Settings Express provides several application-level settings to configure view behavior: | Setting | Type | Default | Description | |---------|------|---------|-------------| | `view engine` | string | (none) | Default template engine extension | | `views` | string \| array | `process.cwd() + '/views'` | Directory or directories for view lookup | | `view cache` | boolean | `true` in production | Enables caching of compiled view instances | | `view` | function | `View` | Constructor function for creating view instances | ### View Engine Setting Specifies the default file extension for templates. When set, view names without extensions automatically use this engine: ```javascript app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // Now can omit extension app.render('index'); // Looks for index.ejs ``` [test/res.render.js:24-37](), [test/app.engine.js:53-66]() ### Views Directory Setting Specifies one or more directories to search for templates: ```javascript // Single directory app.set('views', path.join(__dirname, 'templates')); // Multiple directories (searched in order) app.set('views', [ path.join(__dirname, 'views/custom'), path.join(__dirname, 'views/default') ]); ``` [test/res.render.js:149-201]() ### View Cache Setting Controls whether compiled `View` instances are cached. Caching improves performance by avoiding repeated file system lookups and engine loading: ```javascript // Enable caching (default in production) app.set('view cache', true); // Disable caching (useful in development) app.set('view cache', false); ``` [test/app.render.js:229-289]() When caching is enabled: - `View` instances are stored by name in `app.cache` - Subsequent renders reuse cached instances - File system lookups occur only once per view When caching is disabled: - New `View` instance created for each render - File system lookups occur on every render - Changes to templates are immediately visible ### View Constructor Setting Allows complete customization of view instantiation and rendering: ```javascript function CustomView(name, options) { this.name = name; this.path = 'required-property'; } CustomView.prototype.render = function(options, callback) { // Custom rendering logic callback(null, 'Custom output'); }; app.set('view', CustomView); ``` [test/app.render.js:206-227]() The custom constructor must: - Set a `path` property (signals successful instantiation) - Implement a `render(options, callback)` method **Sources**: [test/res.render.js:24-37](), [test/res.render.js:149-201](), [test/app.render.js:229-289](), [test/app.render.js:206-227]() --- ## Error Handling The view system generates specific errors for common failure scenarios: ### View Not Found Error When a view cannot be resolved in any of the configured directories: ``` Failed to lookup view "[name]" in views directory "[path]" Failed to lookup view "[name]" in views directories "[path1]" or "[path2]" ``` This error originates from the application's rendering logic when `view.path` is `undefined` after `lookup()` fails. [test/app.render.js:82-93](), [test/app.render.js:187-202]() ### No Default Engine Error When a view name lacks an extension and no default engine is configured: ``` No default engine was specified and no extension was provided. ``` [lib/view.js:60-62](), [test/res.render.js:53-65]() ### Engine Not Found Error When a required template engine module does not export `__express`: ``` Module "[moduleName]" does not provide a view engine. ``` [lib/view.js:83-85](), [test/res.render.js:39-51]() ### Rendering Errors Template engines may throw errors during rendering (syntax errors, missing variables, etc.). These are passed to the render callback: [test/res.render.js:112-130](), [test/app.render.js:95-107]() **Sources**: [lib/view.js:60-62](), [lib/view.js:83-85](), [test/res.render.js:39-65](), [test/res.render.js:112-130](), [test/app.render.js:82-107]() --- ## System Integration Points The view system integrates with other Express components: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Layer" App["Application"] AppSettings["app.settings
(view engine, views, view cache)"] AppLocals["app.locals"] AppEngines["app.engines
(registered engines)"] AppCache["app.cache
(cached View instances)"] end subgraph "Response Layer" Res["Response Object"] ResLocals["res.locals"] ResRender["res.render()"] end subgraph "View System" View["View Class"] ViewLookup["lookup()"] ViewResolve["resolve()"] ViewRender["render()"] end subgraph "File System" FS["Template Files"] FSCheck["fs.statSync()"] end subgraph "Template Engines" EngineModule["require(engineName)"] EngineExpress["engine.__express()"] end App --> AppSettings App --> AppLocals App --> AppEngines App --> AppCache Res --> ResLocals Res --> App ResRender --> App App --> View View --> AppSettings View --> AppEngines View --> AppCache View --> ViewLookup ViewLookup --> ViewResolve ViewResolve --> FSCheck FSCheck --> FS View --> ViewRender ViewRender --> EngineExpress View --> EngineModule EngineModule --> EngineExpress ViewRender --> ResRender AppLocals --> ViewRender ResLocals --> ViewRender ``` **Key Integration Points**: 1. **Application Settings**: View system reads `view engine`, `views`, `view cache`, and `view` settings 2. **Locals Merging**: Combines `app.locals`, `res.locals`, and render-time options (see [Template Rendering](#6.2)) 3. **Engine Registry**: Uses `app.engines` object to cache loaded template engines 4. **View Cache**: Uses `app.cache` object to cache compiled `View` instances 5. **Response Methods**: `res.render()` delegates to `app.render()` which uses the view system **Sources**: [lib/view.js:1-206](), [test/res.render.js:1-368](), [test/app.render.js:1-375]() --- # Page: View Resolution # View Resolution

## Purpose and Scope This document describes how Express resolves view template files from view names and loads template engines. View resolution is the process that occurs when creating a `View` instance, determining the file system path of a template and loading the appropriate rendering engine. For information about the actual rendering process and data merging, see [Template Rendering](#6.2). --- ## View Class Architecture The `View` class is responsible for resolving template file paths and loading template engines. It is instantiated whenever a view needs to be rendered. ### View Constructor The `View` constructor takes two parameters: a view name and an options object: ```javascript new View(name, { defaultEngine: 'ejs', // Default template engine extension root: '/path/to/views', // Root directory(ies) for view lookup engines: {} // Cache of loaded template engines }) ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:52-95]() ### View Properties | Property | Type | Description | |----------|------|-------------| | `name` | string | The view name as passed to the constructor | | `ext` | string | The file extension (e.g., `.ejs`, `.pug`) | | `root` | string or Array | Root directory or array of directories to search | | `defaultEngine` | string | Default engine to use if no extension provided | | `engine` | function | The loaded template engine render function | | `path` | string | Resolved absolute file system path to the template | **Sources:** [lib/view.js:52-95]() --- ## View Instantiation Flow ```mermaid graph TB START["new View(name, options)"] EXT_CHECK{"Does name
have extension?"} USE_EXT["Use extension
from name"] USE_DEFAULT["Use defaultEngine
as extension"] ERROR_NO_EXT["throw Error:
'No default engine
was specified'"] ENGINE_CHECK{"Is engine
in cache?"} LOAD_ENGINE["Load engine
via require()"] VALIDATE["Validate
__express function"] ERROR_NO_ENGINE["throw Error:
'Module does not
provide view engine'"] CACHE_ENGINE["Cache engine
in opts.engines[ext]"] GET_ENGINE["Get engine
from cache"] LOOKUP["Call this.lookup(fileName)"] RESOLVE_PATH["Resolve file path"] SET_PATH["Set this.path"] DONE["View instance
created"] START --> EXT_CHECK EXT_CHECK -->|"Yes"| USE_EXT EXT_CHECK -->|"No, has
defaultEngine"| USE_DEFAULT EXT_CHECK -->|"No defaultEngine"| ERROR_NO_EXT USE_EXT --> ENGINE_CHECK USE_DEFAULT --> ENGINE_CHECK ENGINE_CHECK -->|"Not in cache"| LOAD_ENGINE ENGINE_CHECK -->|"In cache"| GET_ENGINE LOAD_ENGINE --> VALIDATE VALIDATE -->|"Valid"| CACHE_ENGINE VALIDATE -->|"Invalid"| ERROR_NO_ENGINE CACHE_ENGINE --> LOOKUP GET_ENGINE --> LOOKUP LOOKUP --> RESOLVE_PATH RESOLVE_PATH --> SET_PATH SET_PATH --> DONE ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:52-95]() --- ## Engine Loading Process ### Engine Loading from Cache When a `View` is constructed, it checks if a template engine for the file extension is already loaded in the `engines` cache. The cache is passed via `options.engines` and is typically `app.engines`. **Sources:** [lib/view.js:75-88]() ### Requiring Template Engine Modules If an engine is not cached, Express attempts to load it by requiring a module with the extension name (minus the leading dot): ```javascript // For extension '.ejs' var mod = 'ejs' var fn = require(mod).__express ``` The engine must export a function named `__express` that matches the signature: ``` function(path, options, callback) ``` If the module doesn't provide `__express`, an error is thrown. **Sources:** [lib/view.js:76-88]() ### Engine Registration via app.engine() Applications can manually register template engines using `app.engine()`: ```javascript app.engine('html', require('ejs').renderFile); ``` This stores the engine function in `app.engines['.html']`, bypassing the require-based loading. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:294-308]() ### Template Engine Loading Diagram ```mermaid graph TB CHECK_CACHE["Check opts.engines[ext]"] IN_CACHE{"Engine
in cache?"} RETURN_CACHED["Return cached engine"] REQUIRE["mod = ext.slice(1)
require(mod)"] CHECK_EXPRESS{"Has __express
property?"} EXTRACT["fn = module.__express"] VALIDATE{"typeof fn
=== 'function'?"} ERROR["throw Error:
'Module does not provide
a view engine'"] CACHE["opts.engines[ext] = fn"] RETURN_FN["Return fn"] CHECK_CACHE --> IN_CACHE IN_CACHE -->|"Yes"| RETURN_CACHED IN_CACHE -->|"No"| REQUIRE REQUIRE --> CHECK_EXPRESS CHECK_EXPRESS -->|"Yes"| EXTRACT CHECK_EXPRESS -->|"No"| ERROR EXTRACT --> VALIDATE VALIDATE -->|"Yes"| CACHE VALIDATE -->|"No"| ERROR CACHE --> RETURN_FN ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:75-88]() --- ## Path Resolution Algorithm The `View` class uses a two-step process to locate template files: `lookup()` and `resolve()`. ### The lookup() Method The `lookup()` method iterates through the root directories (if multiple are configured) and attempts to resolve the file in each: ```javascript View.prototype.lookup = function lookup(name) { var path; var roots = [].concat(this.root); // Ensure array for (var i = 0; i < roots.length && !path; i++) { var root = roots[i]; var loc = resolve(root, name); var dir = dirname(loc); var file = basename(loc); path = this.resolve(dir, file); } return path; }; ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:104-123]() ### The resolve() Method The `resolve()` method tries two file patterns to locate the template: 1. **Direct file match**: `/` (e.g., `views/user.ejs`) 2. **Index file match**: `//index.` (e.g., `views/user/index.ejs`) For each pattern, it uses `tryStat()` to check if the file exists and is a regular file: ```javascript View.prototype.resolve = function resolve(dir, file) { var ext = this.ext; // Try . var path = join(dir, file); var stat = tryStat(path); if (stat && stat.isFile()) { return path; } // Try /index. path = join(dir, basename(file, ext), 'index' + ext); stat = tryStat(path); if (stat && stat.isFile()) { return path; } }; ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:169-187]() ### The tryStat() Helper The `tryStat()` helper performs a synchronous file system stat operation, returning `undefined` if the file doesn't exist: ```javascript function tryStat(path) { try { return fs.statSync(path); } catch (e) { return undefined; } } ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:197-205]() ### Path Resolution Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph TB START["lookup(fileName)"] INIT["roots = [].concat(this.root)"] LOOP{"For each root
while !path"} GET_ROOT["root = roots[i]"] RESOLVE_LOC["loc = resolve(root, name)"] GET_DIR["dir = dirname(loc)"] GET_FILE["file = basename(loc)"] CALL_RESOLVE["path = this.resolve(dir, file)"] TRY_DIRECT["Try: join(dir, file)"] STAT_DIRECT["stat = tryStat(path)"] CHECK_DIRECT{"stat && stat.isFile()?"} RETURN_DIRECT["return path"] TRY_INDEX["Try: join(dir, basename(file, ext),
'index' + ext)"] STAT_INDEX["stat = tryStat(path)"] CHECK_INDEX{"stat && stat.isFile()?"} RETURN_INDEX["return path"] RETURN_UNDEF["return undefined"] NEXT_ROOT["i++"] RETURN_FINAL["return path"] START --> INIT INIT --> LOOP LOOP -->|"More roots"| GET_ROOT LOOP -->|"No more roots"| RETURN_FINAL GET_ROOT --> RESOLVE_LOC RESOLVE_LOC --> GET_DIR GET_DIR --> GET_FILE GET_FILE --> CALL_RESOLVE CALL_RESOLVE --> TRY_DIRECT TRY_DIRECT --> STAT_DIRECT STAT_DIRECT --> CHECK_DIRECT CHECK_DIRECT -->|"Yes"| RETURN_DIRECT CHECK_DIRECT -->|"No"| TRY_INDEX TRY_INDEX --> STAT_INDEX STAT_INDEX --> CHECK_INDEX CHECK_INDEX -->|"Yes"| RETURN_INDEX CHECK_INDEX -->|"No"| RETURN_UNDEF RETURN_UNDEF --> NEXT_ROOT NEXT_ROOT --> LOOP RETURN_DIRECT --> RETURN_FINAL RETURN_INDEX --> RETURN_FINAL ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:104-123](), [lib/view.js:169-187]() --- ## View Caching ### Cache Structure Express maintains a view cache in `app.cache`, which is a plain object mapping view names to `View` instances: ```javascript app.cache = Object.create(null); ``` When `app.render()` is called with caching enabled, it first checks the cache: ```javascript if (renderOptions.cache) { view = cache[name]; } ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/application.js:543-546]() ### Cache Population If a view is not found in the cache, a new `View` instance is created. If caching is enabled and the view path is successfully resolved, the view is stored in the cache: ```javascript if (!view) { var View = this.get('view'); view = new View(name, { defaultEngine: this.get('view engine'), root: this.get('views'), engines: engines }); if (!view.path) { // Error: view not found } if (renderOptions.cache) { cache[name] = view; } } ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:549-571]() ### Cache Behavior | Scenario | Cache Behavior | |----------|---------------| | `view cache` enabled in production | Views are cached after first lookup | | `view cache` disabled or development mode | New `View` instance created on each render | | View path not found | View is NOT cached (error is thrown) | | Multiple `views` directories | Cache key is the view name, not the resolved path | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:138-141](), [lib/application.js:539-541]() ### Caching Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph TB RENDER["app.render(name, options)"] CHECK_CACHE_OPT{"renderOptions.cache
enabled?"} LOOKUP_CACHE["view = cache[name]"] FOUND{"view found
in cache?"} USE_CACHED["Use cached view"] CREATE_VIEW["view = new View(name, opts)"] PATH_CHECK{"view.path
exists?"} ERROR["throw Error:
'Failed to lookup view'"] CACHE_CHECK{"renderOptions.cache
enabled?"} STORE["cache[name] = view"] USE_NEW["Use new view"] RENDER --> CHECK_CACHE_OPT CHECK_CACHE_OPT -->|"Yes"| LOOKUP_CACHE CHECK_CACHE_OPT -->|"No"| CREATE_VIEW LOOKUP_CACHE --> FOUND FOUND -->|"Yes"| USE_CACHED FOUND -->|"No"| CREATE_VIEW CREATE_VIEW --> PATH_CHECK PATH_CHECK -->|"No"| ERROR PATH_CHECK -->|"Yes"| CACHE_CHECK CACHE_CHECK -->|"Yes"| STORE CACHE_CHECK -->|"No"| USE_NEW STORE --> USE_NEW ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:522-575]() --- ## Configuration Settings ### View-Related Application Settings | Setting | Type | Default | Description | |---------|------|---------|-------------| | `views` | string or Array | `'views'` | Root directory or directories for view lookup | | `view` | function | `View` | The View class constructor to use | | `view engine` | string | none | Default template engine extension | | `view cache` | boolean | `true` (production) | Enable view caching | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:90-141]() ### Setting Views Directories The `views` setting can be a single directory or an array of directories: ```javascript // Single directory app.set('views', path.join(__dirname, 'views')); // Multiple directories (searched in order) app.set('views', [ path.join(__dirname, 'views'), path.join(__dirname, 'fallback-views') ]); ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:135]() ### Setting the Default Engine The `view engine` setting specifies which engine to use when no extension is provided: ```javascript app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // Now res.render('index') will look for 'index.ejs' ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:52-56](), [lib/view.js:55-73]() ### Registering Custom Engines Use `app.engine()` to register custom template engines: ```javascript app.engine('html', require('ejs').renderFile); app.engine('tmpl', customTemplateEngine); ``` The engine function must accept `(path, options, callback)` as parameters. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:294-308]() --- ## View Resolution Examples ### Example 1: Basic View Resolution ```javascript // Configuration app.set('views', '/app/views'); app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // When rendering res.render('user'); // Resolution process: // 1. name = 'user' // 2. ext = '.ejs' (from view engine) // 3. fileName = 'user.ejs' // 4. Try: /app/views/user.ejs // 5. If not found, try: /app/views/user/index.ejs ``` ### Example 2: Multiple View Directories ```javascript // Configuration app.set('views', ['/app/views', '/app/shared-views']); app.set('view engine', 'pug'); // When rendering res.render('admin/dashboard'); // Resolution process: // Try first directory: // 1. /app/views/admin/dashboard.pug // 2. /app/views/admin/dashboard/index.pug // Try second directory: // 3. /app/shared-views/admin/dashboard.pug // 4. /app/shared-views/admin/dashboard/index.pug ``` ### Example 3: Explicit Extension ```javascript // Configuration app.set('views', '/app/views'); app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // When rendering with explicit extension res.render('email.html'); // Resolution process: // 1. name = 'email.html' // 2. ext = '.html' (from name, not view engine) // 3. Engine must be registered for '.html' // 4. Try: /app/views/email.html // 5. If not found, try: /app/views/email/index.html ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:52-95](), [lib/view.js:104-123]() --- ## Error Conditions ### View Resolution Errors | Error | Condition | Thrown By | |-------|-----------|-----------| | `"No default engine was specified and no extension was provided."` | View name has no extension and `defaultEngine` is not set | `View` constructor | | `"Module '...' does not provide a view engine."` | Required module doesn't export `__express` function | `View` constructor | | `"Failed to lookup view '...' in views directory '...'"` | View file not found in any root directory | `app.render()` | **Sources:** [lib/view.js:60-62](), [lib/view.js:83-85](), [lib/application.js:558-565]() --- ## Implementation Details ### Synchronous Path Resolution View path resolution is synchronous, using `fs.statSync()`. This is acceptable because: 1. Views are cached in production, so resolution happens once 2. Resolution occurs during application initialization for most views 3. The file system operations are fast for local files **Sources:** [lib/view.js:197-205]() ### Root Directory Normalization The `lookup()` method normalizes the `root` property to an array, allowing consistent iteration whether a single directory or multiple directories are configured: ```javascript var roots = [].concat(this.root); ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:106]() ### Extension Handling Extensions are normalized to always include a leading dot: ```javascript // If defaultEngine = 'ejs' this.ext = '.' + this.defaultEngine; // '.ejs' // If defaultEngine = '.ejs' this.ext = this.defaultEngine; // '.ejs' ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:67-70]() --- # Page: Template Rendering # Template Rendering
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for generating this wiki page: - [lib/view.js](lib/view.js) - [test/app.engine.js](test/app.engine.js) - [test/app.render.js](test/app.render.js) - [test/fixtures/blog/post/index.tmpl](test/fixtures/blog/post/index.tmpl) - [test/fixtures/email.tmpl](test/fixtures/email.tmpl) - [test/fixtures/user.html](test/fixtures/user.html) - [test/fixtures/user.tmpl](test/fixtures/user.tmpl) - [test/res.render.js](test/res.render.js) - [test/support/tmpl.js](test/support/tmpl.js)
## Purpose and Scope This document explains how Express renders templates into HTML using the View system and template engines. It covers the `app.render()` and `res.render()` methods, template engine integration, local variable handling, and the complete rendering flow from method invocation to final output. For information about how Express locates and resolves view files, see [View Resolution](#6.1). ## Rendering Methods Overview Express provides two methods for rendering templates to strings: | Method | Context | Sends Response | Primary Use Case | |--------|---------|----------------|------------------| | `app.render(view, [locals], callback)` | Application instance | No | Programmatic rendering, email generation | | `res.render(view, [locals], [callback])` | Response object | Yes (unless callback provided) | Sending HTML responses to clients | ### app.render() The `app.render()` method renders a view to a string without sending it as an HTTP response. It always requires a callback function to receive the rendered HTML. **Usage Pattern:** ```javascript app.render('email.tmpl', { user: user }, function(err, html) { if (err) return handleError(err); // html contains the rendered template sendEmail(html); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/app.render.js:9-20](), [test/app.render.js:293-305]() ### res.render() The `res.render()` method renders a view and automatically sends it as the HTTP response. If a callback is provided, the rendered HTML is passed to the callback instead of being sent. **Usage Pattern:** ```javascript // Automatic response res.render('user.tmpl', { user: user }); // With callback for post-processing res.render('user.tmpl', { user: user }, function(err, html) { html = html.replace('tobi', 'loki'); res.end(html); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/res.render.js:9-22](), [test/res.render.js:300-318]() ## Template Engine Integration ### Engine Registration Template engines are registered using `app.engine(ext, callback)`. The engine function must have the signature `(path, options, callback)`. ```mermaid graph LR Register["app.engine('.html', render)"] Store["opts.engines['.html'] = render"] View["View instance"] Load["View loads engine"] Register --> Store Store --> Load Load --> View ``` **Engine Function Signature:** - `path`: Absolute path to the template file - `options`: Object containing local variables - `callback(err, html)`: Called with rendered output **Sources:** [test/app.engine.js:17-30](), [lib/view.js:75-88]() ### Engine Loading When a View is created, Express loads the template engine in one of two ways: 1. **Pre-registered engine:** Uses the engine from `opts.engines[ext]` 2. **Auto-loading:** Requires the module and calls its `__express` export **Auto-loading Process:** ```mermaid graph TB CheckCache["Check opts.engines[ext]"] CacheExists{Engine cached?} UseCache["Use cached engine"] LoadModule["require(ext.slice(1))"] GetExport["engine = module.__express"] ValidateExport{typeof fn === 'function'?} ThrowError["throw Error('does not provide view engine')"] CacheEngine["opts.engines[ext] = fn"] StoreEngine["this.engine = opts.engines[ext]"] CheckCache --> CacheExists CacheExists -->|Yes| UseCache CacheExists -->|No| LoadModule LoadModule --> GetExport GetExport --> ValidateExport ValidateExport -->|No| ThrowError ValidateExport -->|Yes| CacheEngine CacheEngine --> StoreEngine UseCache --> StoreEngine ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:75-91]() ### Engine Invocation The engine is invoked through `View.prototype.render()`, which handles both synchronous and asynchronous callbacks. **Sources:** [lib/view.js:133-159]() ## Local Variables and Data Flow Express merges local variables from three sources when rendering templates: ```mermaid graph LR AppLocals["app.locals
(Global)"] ResLocals["res.locals
(Request-scoped)"] RenderLocals["render() options
(Call-specific)"] Merge["Merged Options Object"] Engine["Template Engine"] AppLocals -->|Lowest precedence| Merge ResLocals -->|Medium precedence| Merge RenderLocals -->|Highest precedence| Merge Merge --> Engine ``` ### Precedence Rules When the same variable exists in multiple sources, the following precedence applies (highest to lowest): 1. **render() options** - Variables passed directly to `res.render()` or `app.render()` 2. **res.locals** - Request-scoped variables set during request processing 3. **app.locals** - Application-wide variables **Examples:** | Scenario | app.locals.user | res.locals.user | render options.user | Final value | |----------|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|-------------| | 1 | `{name: 'tobi'}` | - | - | `{name: 'tobi'}` | | 2 | `{name: 'tobi'}` | `{name: 'jane'}` | - | `{name: 'jane'}` | | 3 | `{name: 'tobi'}` | `{name: 'jane'}` | `{name: 'loki'}` | `{name: 'loki'}` | **Sources:** [test/res.render.js:251-281](), [test/app.render.js:320-332]() ### app.locals Global variables available to all views. Commonly used for application-wide data like site title, helper functions, or configuration. ```javascript app.locals.title = 'My Application'; app.locals.email = 'support@example.com'; ``` The `app.locals` object always contains a `settings` property that references `app.settings`. **Sources:** [test/res.render.js:67-80](), [test/app.locals.js:15-24]() ### res.locals Request-scoped variables available only during the current request. Typically set in middleware for use by route handlers and views. ```javascript app.use(function(req, res, next) { res.locals.user = req.user; res.locals.authenticated = !!req.user; next(); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/res.render.js:236-249](), [examples/view-locals/index.js:86-100]() ## Complete Rendering Flow ### res.render() Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant RouteHandler as "Route Handler" participant Response as "res (Response)" participant Application as "app (Application)" participant View as "View Instance" participant Engine as "Template Engine" Client->>RouteHandler: "HTTP Request" RouteHandler->>Response: "res.render('user.tmpl', locals)" Response->>Response: "Merge app.locals, res.locals, locals" Response->>Application: "app.render(view, options, callback)" Application->>Application: "Get view cache setting" alt View cached Application->>Application: "Retrieve from cache" else View not cached Application->>View: "new View(name, options)" View->>View: "lookup(fileName)" View->>View: "resolve(dir, file)" opt Cache enabled Application->>Application: "Store in cache" end end Application->>View: "view.render(options, callback)" View->>Engine: "engine(path, options, onRender)" Engine->>Engine: "Read template, process locals" Engine->>View: "callback(err, html)" View->>Application: "callback(err, html)" Application->>Response: "callback(err, html)" alt No user callback Response->>Response: "Set Content-Type: text/html" Response->>Response: "res.send(html)" Response->>Client: "HTTP Response with HTML" else User callback provided Response->>RouteHandler: "callback(err, html)" RouteHandler->>Client: "Custom response handling" end ``` **Sources:** [lib/view.js:133-159](), [test/res.render.js:9-22](), [test/res.render.js:300-318]() ### View.prototype.render() Implementation The `View.prototype.render()` method handles synchronous callback normalization to ensure consistent asynchronous behavior: ```mermaid graph TB Start["view.render(options, callback)"] SetSync["sync = true"] CallEngine["engine(path, options, onRender)"] OnRender["onRender() invoked"] CheckSync{sync === true?} SyncCallback["Copy arguments
process.nextTick(callback)"] AsyncCallback["callback.apply(this, arguments)"] SetAsyncFlag["sync = false"] Start --> SetSync SetSync --> CallEngine CallEngine --> OnRender OnRender --> CheckSync CheckSync -->|Yes, synchronous| SyncCallback CheckSync -->|No, asynchronous| AsyncCallback CallEngine --> SetAsyncFlag ``` This pattern ensures that even if a template engine calls its callback synchronously, the application receives the callback asynchronously via `process.nextTick()`. This prevents zalgo issues and ensures consistent async behavior. **Sources:** [lib/view.js:133-159]() ## View Caching Express caches View instances to avoid repeated file system lookups and View construction. ### Cache Behavior | Setting | Value | Behavior | |---------|-------|----------| | `view cache` | `false` (development default) | View created on every render | | `view cache` | `true` (production default) | View cached after first creation | | render options | `{cache: true}` | Forces caching for this render | ```mermaid graph TB RenderCall["app.render() called"] CheckCache{cache enabled?} CheckExists{View in cache?} UseCache["Return cached View"] CreateView["new View(name, options)"] StoreCache["Store in cache"] InvokeRender["view.render()"] RenderCall --> CheckCache CheckCache -->|Yes| CheckExists CheckCache -->|No| CreateView CheckExists -->|Yes| UseCache CheckExists -->|No| CreateView CreateView --> StoreCache StoreCache --> InvokeRender UseCache --> InvokeRender ``` **Cache Key:** The cache key is the view name as provided to `render()`, before any path resolution. **Production Behavior:** In production (`NODE_ENV=production`), `view cache` is enabled by default. In development, it is disabled to allow template changes without restarting the server. **Sources:** [test/app.render.js:229-289](), [test/app.js:74-104]() ## Error Handling ### Template Engine Errors When a template engine encounters an error, it passes it to the callback. Express propagates this error through the rendering pipeline: ```mermaid graph LR Engine["Template Engine"] ViewRender["view.render(callback)"] AppRender["app.render(callback)"] ResRender["res.render(callback)"] Engine -->|"callback(err)"| ViewRender ViewRender -->|"callback(err)"| AppRender AppRender -->|"callback(err)"| ResRender ResRender -->|No user callback| Next["next(err)"] ResRender -->|User callback| UserCallback["User's callback(err)"] ``` **Common Error Scenarios:** 1. **View not found:** Error thrown during View construction if file doesn't exist 2. **Engine not found:** Error thrown if template engine module can't be loaded 3. **Render error:** Error from template engine during rendering (syntax errors, missing variables, etc.) **Error Messages:** - `"No default engine was specified and no extension was provided"` - No file extension and no `view engine` setting - `"Module 'X' does not provide a view engine"` - Module loaded but lacks `__express` export - `"Failed to lookup view 'X' in views directory 'Y'"` - Template file not found **Sources:** [test/res.render.js:112-130](), [test/res.render.js:39-51](), [test/app.render.js:61-80](), [test/app.render.js:82-93]() ## Custom View Constructors Express allows custom View implementations by setting the `view` application setting: ```javascript function CustomView(name, options) { this.name = name; this.path = '/path/to/template'; } CustomView.prototype.render = function(options, callback) { // Custom rendering logic callback(null, '...'); }; app.set('view', CustomView); ``` The custom View constructor must: 1. Accept `(name, options)` constructor parameters 2. Set `this.path` property (used as success indicator) 3. Implement `render(options, callback)` method **Sources:** [test/app.render.js:206-227](), [examples/view-constructor/index.js:1-48](), [examples/view-constructor/github-view.js:23-53]() ## Default Engine Configuration When `view engine` is set, Express appends this extension to view names that lack an extension: ```javascript app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // These are equivalent: res.render('user'); res.render('user.ejs'); ``` The `view engine` setting can include or omit the leading dot: - `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` ✓ - `app.set('view engine', '.ejs')` ✓ **Sources:** [test/res.render.js:132-147](), [test/app.engine.js:53-66](), [test/app.engine.js:68-81]() ## Absolute Path Rendering Both `app.render()` and `res.render()` accept absolute file paths, bypassing the view resolution system: ```javascript const path = require('path'); // Render with absolute path res.render(path.join(__dirname, 'templates', 'user.tmpl')); ``` When using absolute paths with a `view engine` setting, the extension is still appended if missing: ```javascript app.set('view engine', 'tmpl'); res.render(path.join(__dirname, 'templates', 'user')); // Resolves to: /absolute/path/templates/user.tmpl ``` **Sources:** [test/res.render.js:10-22](), [test/res.render.js:24-37](), [test/app.render.js:10-20]() ## Summary of Key Classes and Methods | Entity | File | Purpose | |--------|------|---------| | `View` | [lib/view.js:52-95]() | View constructor, loads engine and resolves path | | `View.prototype.render` | [lib/view.js:133-159]() | Invokes template engine with sync callback handling | | `app.render` | Referenced in response.js | Application-level rendering method | | `res.render` | Referenced in response.js | Response-level rendering with auto-send | | `app.engine` | Referenced in application.js | Registers template engines | | `app.locals` | Referenced in application.js | Global view variables | | `res.locals` | Referenced in response.js | Request-scoped view variables | **Sources:** [lib/view.js:1-206](), [test/res.render.js:1-368](), [test/app.render.js:1-375](), [test/app.engine.js:1-84]() --- # Page: Application Patterns and Examples # Application Patterns and Examples
