## Purpose and Scope This document demonstrates common Express.js application patterns through practical examples from the repository's `examples/` directory. Each pattern illustrates best practices for handling specific use cases including error handling, authentication, file serving, and API design. These examples serve as both documentation and acceptance tests for the framework. For core routing concepts, see [Routing System](#3). For middleware fundamentals, see [Middleware System](#4). For response method details, see [Response Methods](#5). ## Overview of Example Applications The Express.js repository includes multiple example applications that demonstrate real-world patterns. These applications are located in `examples/` and are tested as part of the acceptance test suite. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Example Applications" ERROR["error-pages
examples/error-pages/"] COOKIE["cookies
examples/cookies/"] SESSION["cookie-sessions
examples/cookie-sessions/"] AUTH["auth
examples/auth/"] DOWNLOADS["downloads
examples/downloads/"] end subgraph "Demonstrated Patterns" ERROR_HANDLING["Error Handling
404, 403, 500"] CONTENT_NEG["Content Negotiation
res.format"] SESSION_MGT["Session Management
cookie-session"] COOKIE_MGT["Cookie Management
res.cookie, res.clearCookie"] AUTH_FLOW["Authentication
Middleware Protection"] FILE_SERVE["File Downloads
res.download"] end subgraph "Testing Infrastructure" ERROR_TEST["test/acceptance/downloads.js"] COOKIE_TEST["test/acceptance/cookies.js"] SESSION_TEST["test/acceptance/cookie-sessions.js"] end ERROR --> ERROR_HANDLING ERROR --> CONTENT_NEG COOKIE --> COOKIE_MGT SESSION --> SESSION_MGT AUTH --> AUTH_FLOW DOWNLOADS --> FILE_SERVE DOWNLOADS --> ERROR_TEST COOKIE --> COOKIE_TEST SESSION --> SESSION_TEST ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:1-104](), [examples/cookie-sessions/index.js:1-26](), [test/acceptance/downloads.js:1-48](), [test/acceptance/cookies.js:1-72](), [test/acceptance/cookie-sessions.js:1-39]() ## Error Pages and HTTP Status Handling ### Error Handling Architecture The error pages example demonstrates a complete error handling strategy including custom error pages, content negotiation, and proper HTTP status codes. The pattern uses both catch-all middleware for 404s and dedicated error-handling middleware for other errors. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant App as "app (express)" participant Routes as "Route Handlers" participant NotFound as "404 Middleware" participant ErrorHandler as "Error Middleware" participant Response as "res.format / res.render" Client->>App: "GET /404" App->>Routes: "Match route" Routes->>Routes: "next() without response" Routes->>NotFound: "Fall through to 404 handler" NotFound->>NotFound: "res.status(404)" NotFound->>Response: "res.format({html, json, default})" Response->>Client: "404 Response (negotiated)" Client->>App: "GET /403" App->>Routes: "Match route" Routes->>Routes: "err = new Error('not allowed!')" Routes->>Routes: "err.status = 403" Routes->>ErrorHandler: "next(err)" ErrorHandler->>ErrorHandler: "res.status(err.status || 500)" ErrorHandler->>Response: "res.render('500', {error: err})" Response->>Client: "403 Error Page" Client->>App: "GET /500" App->>Routes: "Match route" Routes->>ErrorHandler: "next(new Error('keyboard cat!'))" ErrorHandler->>ErrorHandler: "res.status(500)" ErrorHandler->>Response: "res.render('500', {error: err})" Response->>Client: "500 Error Page" ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:30-96]() ### 404 Not Found Handler The 404 handler is implemented as the last non-error-handling middleware. It assumes that if no previous middleware responded, the resource was not found. The handler uses `res.format()` to support multiple content types. | Request Type | Response Format | Implementation | |-------------|----------------|----------------| | HTML (browser) | Rendered template | `res.render('404', { url: req.url })` | | JSON (API client) | JSON object | `res.json({ error: 'Not found' })` | | Plain text (default) | Text string | `res.type('txt').send('Not found')` | The implementation pattern: ``` app.use(function(req, res, next){ res.status(404); res.format({ html: function () { res.render('404', { url: req.url }) }, json: function () { res.json({ error: 'Not found' }) }, default: function () { res.type('txt').send('Not found') } }) }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:63-77]() ### Error Handler Middleware Error handlers have a 4-arity signature: `function(err, req, res, next)`. This distinguishes them from regular middleware. The error handler reads the error's `status` property (if present) or defaults to 500. Key characteristics: - **Arity detection:** Express identifies error handlers by their 4 parameters - **Status extraction:** Uses `err.status || 500` for HTTP status code - **Error object propagation:** Passes entire error object to template - **Conditional stack traces:** Uses `settings['verbose errors']` to control detail level **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() ### Verbose Error Configuration The example demonstrates environment-based error detail control using custom settings: ``` app.enable('verbose errors'); if (app.settings.env === 'production') app.disable('verbose errors'); ``` Templates can check this setting to conditionally display stack traces: ``` <% if (settings['verbose errors']) { %> 
<%= error.stack %>
<% } else { %>

An error occurred!

<% } %> ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:17-24](), [examples/error-pages/views/500.ejs:3-7]() ## Cookie Sessions Pattern ### Session Management Architecture The cookie-sessions example demonstrates stateless session management where session data is stored in encrypted cookies rather than server-side storage. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Request Processing" Client["Client Request"] CookieHeader["Cookie Header"] SessionMiddleware["cookie-session middleware"] SessionObject["req.session object"] end subgraph "Application Logic" RouteHandler["Route Handler"] SessionRead["req.session.count"] SessionWrite["req.session.count++"] end subgraph "Response Processing" SetCookie["Set-Cookie Header"] Encrypted["Encrypted Session Data"] ClientStore["Client Cookie Storage"] end Client --> CookieHeader CookieHeader --> SessionMiddleware SessionMiddleware --> SessionObject SessionObject --> RouteHandler RouteHandler --> SessionRead RouteHandler --> SessionWrite SessionWrite --> SessionMiddleware SessionMiddleware --> SetCookie SetCookie --> Encrypted Encrypted --> ClientStore ClientStore -.next request.-> CookieHeader ``` **Sources:** [examples/cookie-sessions/index.js:1-26]() ### Implementation Pattern The pattern uses the `cookie-session` middleware to provide `req.session`: ``` var cookieSession = require('cookie-session'); app.use(cookieSession({ secret: 'manny is cool' })); app.get('/', function (req, res) { req.session.count = (req.session.count || 0) + 1 res.send('viewed ' + req.session.count + ' times\n') }) ``` **Key aspects:** - **Stateless:** No server-side session storage required - **Encrypted:** Session data is encrypted using the secret - **Automatic persistence:** Middleware handles serialization/deserialization - **Simple API:** Session data accessed via `req.session` object **Sources:** [examples/cookie-sessions/index.js:13-19]() ### Session Testing Pattern The acceptance test demonstrates how to maintain session continuity across requests by preserving cookies: ``` request(app) .get('/') .expect(200, 'viewed 1 times\n', function (err, res) { if (err) return done(err) request(app) .get('/') .set('Cookie', getCookies(res)) .expect(200, 'viewed 2 times\n', done) }) ``` The helper function extracts cookies from the response: ``` function getCookies(res) { return res.headers['set-cookie'].map(function (val) { return val.split(';')[0] }).join('; '); } ``` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/cookie-sessions.js:20-38]() ## Authentication Pattern The authentication example demonstrates a typical login/logout flow with session-based authentication and middleware-based route protection. ### Authentication Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Browser participant LoginRoute as "/login (POST)" participant Session as "req.session" participant RestrictedRoute as "/restricted" participant AuthMiddleware as "restrict middleware" participant LogoutRoute as "/logout" Browser->>LoginRoute: "POST credentials" LoginRoute->>LoginRoute: "Validate username/password" alt "Valid Credentials" LoginRoute->>Session: "req.session.user = username" LoginRoute->>Browser: "Redirect to /" else "Invalid Credentials" LoginRoute->>Browser: "Render login with error" end Browser->>RestrictedRoute: "GET /restricted" RestrictedRoute->>AuthMiddleware: "Execute restrict()" AuthMiddleware->>Session: "Check req.session.user" alt "Authenticated" AuthMiddleware->>RestrictedRoute: "next()" RestrictedRoute->>Browser: "Render restricted content" else "Not Authenticated" AuthMiddleware->>Browser: "Redirect to /login" end Browser->>LogoutRoute: "GET /logout" LogoutRoute->>Session: "req.session.destroy()" LogoutRoute->>Browser: "Redirect to /" ``` **Sources:** [examples/auth/views/login.ejs:1-22]() ### Middleware-Based Protection The typical pattern for protecting routes uses a reusable middleware function: ``` function restrict(req, res, next) { if (req.session.user) { next(); } else { req.session.returnTo = req.originalUrl; res.redirect('/login'); } } app.get('/restricted', restrict, function(req, res){ res.send('Restricted content'); }); ``` **Key features:** - **Return URL tracking:** Stores `req.originalUrl` in session for post-login redirect - **Middleware composition:** Applied before route handler - **Reusability:** Same middleware protects multiple routes **Sources:** Referenced from [examples/auth/views/login.ejs:6]() context ## File Downloads and Serving Pattern ### Download Implementation The downloads example demonstrates secure file serving with automatic Content-Disposition headers and path validation. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Client Request" Browser["Browser GET request"] Path["Request path: /files/amazing.txt"] end subgraph "Route Handling" DownloadRoute["app.get('/files/:file')"] PathJoin["path.join(dir, req.params.file)"] Security["Path traversal check"] end subgraph "Response Processing" Download["res.download(filepath, filename)"] Headers["Content-Disposition: attachment"] Stream["File stream to client"] end subgraph "Error Handling" NotFound["404 if file not exists"] Forbidden["403 if path traversal"] end Browser --> Path Path --> DownloadRoute DownloadRoute --> PathJoin PathJoin --> Security Security --> Download Security --> Forbidden Download --> Headers Headers --> Stream Stream --> Browser Download -.file not found.-> NotFound ``` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/downloads.js:14-46]() ### Security Considerations The download pattern must prevent directory traversal attacks: | Request Path | Status | Reason | |-------------|--------|---------| | `/files/amazing.txt` | 200 | Valid file in allowed directory | | `/files/notes/groceries.txt` | 200 | Valid subdirectory access | | `/files/missing.txt` | 404 | File does not exist | | `/files/../index.js` | 403 | Directory traversal attempt | **Implementation considerations:** - **Path validation:** Use `res.download()` which validates paths - **Root directory:** Specify root option to restrict file access - **Filename sanitization:** Validate or sanitize user-provided filenames - **Content-Disposition:** Automatically set by `res.download()` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/downloads.js:14-46](), [examples/downloads/files/amazing.txt:1](), [examples/downloads/files/notes/groceries.txt:1-4]() ### Download Response Headers The `res.download()` method automatically sets appropriate headers: ``` Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="amazing.txt" ``` For nested files, the filename reflects the actual file, not the full path: ``` GET /files/notes/groceries.txt Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="groceries.txt" ``` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/downloads.js:16-20](), [test/acceptance/downloads.js:23-29]() ## Cookie Management Pattern ### Cookie Setting and Clearing The cookies example demonstrates a typical "remember me" pattern with cookie management. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Initial Visit" NoC["No cookies"] Form["Display form"] end subgraph "Form Submission" Post["POST /"] Check{"remember=1?"} SetCookie["res.cookie('remember', '1')"] NoCookie["No cookie set"] Redirect["res.redirect('/')"] end subgraph "Return Visit" Get["GET /"] CookiePresent{"Cookie present?"} ShowRemembered["Display 'Remembered'"] ShowForm["Display form"] end subgraph "Forget Action" Forget["GET /forget"] Clear["res.clearCookie('remember')"] RedirectHome["res.redirect('/')"] end NoC --> Form Form --> Post Post --> Check Check -->|"Yes"| SetCookie Check -->|"No"| NoCookie SetCookie --> Redirect NoCookie --> Redirect Redirect --> Get Get --> CookiePresent CookiePresent -->|"Yes"| ShowRemembered CookiePresent -->|"No"| ShowForm ShowRemembered --> Forget Forget --> Clear Clear --> RedirectHome ``` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/cookies.js:20-51]() ### Cookie Testing Patterns The acceptance tests demonstrate proper cookie handling verification: **Setting cookies:** ``` request(app) .post('/') .type('urlencoded') .send({ remember: 1 }) .expect('Set-Cookie', /remember=1/) .expect(302, done) ``` **Reading cookies:** ``` request(app) .get('/') .set('Cookie', res.headers['set-cookie'][0]) .expect(200, /Remembered/, done) ``` **Clearing cookies:** ``` request(app) .get('/forget') .set('Cookie', res.headers['set-cookie'][0]) .expect('Set-Cookie', /remember=;/) .expect(302, done) ``` **No cookie when not requested:** ``` request(app) .post('/') .send({}) .expect(utils.shouldNotHaveHeader('Set-Cookie')) .expect(302, done) ``` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/cookies.js:53-70](), [test/acceptance/cookies.js:20-33](), [test/acceptance/cookies.js:36-50](), [test/acceptance/cookies.js:8-19]() ## Summary of Pattern Categories | Pattern Category | Key Techniques | Example Files | |-----------------|----------------|---------------| | **Error Handling** | 4-arity middleware, `res.format()`, custom error objects with status | [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() | | **Content Negotiation** | `res.format()` with html/json/default handlers | [examples/error-pages/index.js:66-76]() | | **Session Management** | `cookie-session` middleware, `req.session` object | [examples/cookie-sessions/index.js:13-19]() | | **Authentication** | Middleware-based protection, session user storage | [examples/auth/views/login.ejs:7-19]() | | **File Downloads** | `res.download()`, path validation, Content-Disposition | [test/acceptance/downloads.js:23-29]() | | **Cookie Management** | `res.cookie()`, `res.clearCookie()`, cookie options | [test/acceptance/cookies.js:53-70]() | | **Template Rendering** | ## Purpose and Scope This document demonstrates practical patterns for implementing authentication and session management in Express applications. It covers session configuration, cookie-based authentication, route protection middleware, and secure login/logout flows using the example implementation in the Express repository. For cookie management methods (`res.cookie`, `res.clearCookie`), see [Cookie Management](#5.4). For redirect methods used in authentication flows, see [Redirects and Location](#5.3). For general middleware patterns, see [Middleware Execution Flow](#4.1). --- ## Authentication Example Overview The Express repository includes a complete authentication example at [examples/auth/index.js:1-135]() that demonstrates a session-based authentication system. The example uses: - `express-session` for server-side session management - `pbkdf2-password` for secure password hashing - Cookie-based session identifiers - Middleware-based route protection - Session regeneration to prevent fixation attacks **Architecture:** ```mermaid graph TB CLIENT["HTTP Client"] subgraph "Express Application" SESSION_MW["express-session middleware
req.session"] URL_MW["express.urlencoded middleware"] MSG_MW["Session Message Middleware
req.session.error/success → res.locals.message"] LOGIN_GET["GET /login
render login form"] LOGIN_POST["POST /login
authenticate credentials"] LOGOUT["GET /logout
req.session.destroy"] RESTRICTED["GET /restricted
protected route"] RESTRICT_FN["restrict(req, res, next)
middleware function"] AUTH_FN["authenticate(name, pass, fn)
credential verification"] end subgraph "Data Store" USERS["users object
{tj: {name, salt, hash}}"] SESSION_STORE["Session Store
memory/redis/db"] end CLIENT -->|"1. All requests"| SESSION_MW SESSION_MW -->|"loads session"| SESSION_STORE SESSION_MW --> URL_MW URL_MW --> MSG_MW MSG_MW -->|"public routes"| LOGIN_GET MSG_MW -->|"public routes"| LOGIN_POST MSG_MW -->|"public routes"| LOGOUT MSG_MW -->|"protected routes"| RESTRICT_FN RESTRICT_FN -->|"if req.session.user exists"| RESTRICTED RESTRICT_FN -->|"else redirect"| LOGIN_GET LOGIN_POST --> AUTH_FN AUTH_FN --> USERS AUTH_FN -->|"on success"| SESSION_MW LOGOUT --> SESSION_STORE ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:1-135]() --- ## Session Middleware Configuration ### express-session Setup The `express-session` middleware manages server-side sessions identified by cookies. Configuration at [examples/auth/index.js:22-26](): ```javascript app.use(session({ resave: false, // don't save session if unmodified saveUninitialized: false, // don't create session until something stored secret: 'shhhh, very secret' })); ``` **Session Configuration Options:** | Option | Value | Purpose | |--------|-------|---------| | `resave` | `false` | Prevents resaving unchanged sessions, reduces store operations | | `saveUninitialized` | `false` | Only creates sessions when data is stored, reduces empty sessions | | `secret` | string | Signs session ID cookie to prevent tampering | The middleware creates `req.session` object on all requests and persists changes after the response is sent. Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:22-26]() ### Session Properties Once configured, `req.session` provides: - `req.session.user` - Custom property for storing authenticated user data - `req.session.error` - Custom property for error messages - `req.session.success` - Custom property for success messages - `req.session.regenerate(callback)` - Generates new session ID - `req.session.destroy(callback)` - Removes session from store Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:30-39](), [examples/auth/index.js:92-98](), [examples/auth/index.js:111-120]() --- ## Cookie Management for Sessions ### Session Cookie Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express as "Express App" participant SessionMW as "express-session" participant Store as "Session Store" Client->>Express: "GET /login (no cookie)" Express->>SessionMW: "Initialize" SessionMW->>Store: "No session ID" SessionMW->>Express: "req.session = {}" Express->>Client: "Response (no Set-Cookie)" Client->>Express: "POST /login (credentials)" Express->>SessionMW: "Process request" SessionMW->>Express: "req.session = {}" Express->>Express: "authenticate(username, password)" Express->>SessionMW: "req.session.regenerate()" SessionMW->>Store: "Create new session ID" SessionMW->>SessionMW: "Generate session ID" Express->>Express: "req.session.user = user" SessionMW->>Client: "Set-Cookie: connect.sid=..." Client->>Express: "GET /restricted (with cookie)" Express->>SessionMW: "Parse session cookie" SessionMW->>Store: "Load session by ID" Store->>SessionMW: "Session data" SessionMW->>Express: "req.session = {user: ...}" Express->>Express: "restrict() finds req.session.user" Express->>Client: "Authorized response" Client->>Express: "GET /logout (with cookie)" Express->>SessionMW: "Parse session cookie" Express->>SessionMW: "req.session.destroy()" SessionMW->>Store: "Delete session" SessionMW->>Client: "Set-Cookie: connect.sid=; expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970" ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:22-26](), [examples/auth/index.js:92-98](), [examples/auth/index.js:111-120]() ### Cookie Parser for Additional Cookies While `express-session` manages its own cookies, applications may need additional cookie handling using `cookie-parser`: ```javascript app.use(cookieParser('my secret here')); ``` This enables: - `req.cookies` - Unsigned cookies object - `req.signedCookies` - Signed cookies object - `res.cookie(name, value, options)` - Set cookies - `res.clearCookie(name, options)` - Clear cookies **Signed Cookie Example:** When setting a signed cookie at [test/res.cookie.js:284-286](): ```javascript res.cookie('name', 'tobi', { signed: true }) // Result: name=s%3Atobi.xJjV2iZ6EI7C8E5kzwbfA9PVLl1ZR07UTnuTgQQ4EnQ; Path=/ ``` Retrieving signed cookie at [test/req.signedCookies.js:14-20](): ```javascript req.signedCookies // { name: 'tobi' } ``` Sources: [examples/cookies/index.js:19](), [test/res.cookie.js:245-293](), [test/req.signedCookies.js:8-36]() ### Cookie Options for Security **Common Security Options:** | Option | Type | Purpose | Example | |--------|------|---------|---------| | `httpOnly` | boolean | Prevents JavaScript access, mitigates XSS | `{ httpOnly: true }` | | `secure` | boolean | Only sends over HTTPS | `{ secure: true }` | | `signed` | boolean | Signs cookie value to detect tampering | `{ signed: true }` | | `maxAge` | number | Lifetime in milliseconds | `{ maxAge: 60000 }` | | `expires` | Date | Absolute expiration date | `{ expires: new Date(...) }` | | `sameSite` | string | CSRF protection | `{ sameSite: 'strict' }` | | `partitioned` | boolean | CHIPS (partitioned cookies) | `{ partitioned: true }` | Sources: [test/res.cookie.js:54-98]() --- ## Authentication Implementation ### Password Hashing The example uses `pbkdf2-password` for secure password storage at [examples/auth/index.js:7-55](): ```javascript var hash = require('pbkdf2-password')() // Hash password on user creation hash({ password: 'foobar' }, function (err, pass, salt, hash) { users.tj.salt = salt; users.tj.hash = hash; }); ``` **User Database Structure:** ```javascript var users = { tj: { name: 'tj', salt: '...', // Random salt from pbkdf2 hash: '...' // Hashed password } }; ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:7-55]() ### Authentication Function The `authenticate` function at [examples/auth/index.js:60-73]() verifies credentials: ```mermaid graph TB START["authenticate(name, pass, fn)"] LOOKUP["users[name]"] USER_EXISTS{"User exists?"} HASH_CHECK["hash({password: pass, salt: user.salt})"] HASH_MATCH{"hash === user.hash?"} SUCCESS["fn(null, user)"] FAIL["fn(null, null)"] ERROR["fn(err)"] START --> LOOKUP LOOKUP --> USER_EXISTS USER_EXISTS -->|"No"| FAIL USER_EXISTS -->|"Yes"| HASH_CHECK HASH_CHECK -->|"Error"| ERROR HASH_CHECK -->|"Success"| HASH_MATCH HASH_MATCH -->|"Match"| SUCCESS HASH_MATCH -->|"No match"| FAIL ``` Implementation: ```javascript function authenticate(name, pass, fn) { var user = users[name]; if (!user) return fn(null, null) hash({ password: pass, salt: user.salt }, function (err, pass, salt, hash) { if (err) return fn(err); if (hash === user.hash) return fn(null, user) fn(null, null) }); } ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:60-73]() --- ## Route Protection Patterns ### Route Protection Middleware The `restrict` middleware at [examples/auth/index.js:75-82]() protects routes requiring authentication: ```javascript function restrict(req, res, next) { if (req.session.user) { next(); } else { req.session.error = 'Access denied!'; res.redirect('/login'); } } ``` **Usage in route definitions:** ```javascript app.get('/restricted', restrict, function(req, res){ res.send('Wahoo! restricted area, click to logout'); }); ``` **Protection Flow:** ```mermaid graph LR REQUEST["HTTP Request
GET /restricted"] RESTRICT["restrict middleware"] CHECK{"req.session.user
exists?"} NEXT["next()
continue to handler"] HANDLER["Route Handler
Send restricted content"] REDIRECT["res.redirect('/login')
Set error message"] LOGIN_PAGE["Login Page"] REQUEST --> RESTRICT RESTRICT --> CHECK CHECK -->|"Yes"| NEXT CHECK -->|"No"| REDIRECT NEXT --> HANDLER REDIRECT --> LOGIN_PAGE ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:75-82](), [examples/auth/index.js:88-90]() ### Testing Route Protection Test suite at [test/acceptance/auth.js:65-87]() verifies protection behavior: | Test Case | Cookie | Expected Result | |-----------|--------|-----------------| | No cookie | None | 302 redirect to `/login` | | Valid cookie | `connect.sid=...` | 200 with restricted content | | Invalid cookie | Invalid session | 302 redirect to `/login` | Sources: [test/acceptance/auth.js:65-87]() --- ## Login and Logout Flows ### Login Flow with Session Regeneration The POST `/login` handler at [examples/auth/index.js:104-128]() implements secure login: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express as "POST /login handler" participant Auth as "authenticate()" participant Session as "req.session" Client->>Express: "POST username & password" Express->>Express: "Check req.body exists" Express->>Auth: "authenticate(username, password, callback)" alt "Authentication Success" Auth->>Express: "callback(null, user)" Express->>Session: "req.session.regenerate()" Note over Session: "Generates new session ID
Prevents session fixation" Session->>Express: "New session created" Express->>Session: "req.session.user = user" Express->>Session: "req.session.success = message" Express->>Client: "302 redirect to /" else "Authentication Failure" Auth->>Express: "callback(null, null)" Express->>Session: "req.session.error = message" Express->>Client: "302 redirect to /login" else "Error" Auth->>Express: "callback(err)" Express->>Client: "next(err) → error handler" end ``` **Session Regeneration Purpose:** Session regeneration at [examples/auth/index.js:111]() prevents **session fixation attacks** where an attacker forces a user to use a known session ID. By generating a new session ID after successful authentication, any pre-existing session ID becomes invalid. Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:104-128]() ### Session Message Middleware Messages are stored in session and displayed once at [examples/auth/index.js:30-39](): ```javascript app.use(function(req, res, next){ var err = req.session.error; var msg = req.session.success; delete req.session.error; // Clear after reading delete req.session.success; // Clear after reading res.locals.message = ''; if (err) res.locals.message = '

' + err + '

'; if (msg) res.locals.message = '

' + msg + '

'; next(); }); ``` This implements **flash messages** - one-time messages that persist across a single redirect but are cleared after being displayed. Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:30-39]() ### Logout Flow with Session Destruction The `/logout` handler at [examples/auth/index.js:92-98]() destroys the session: ```javascript app.get('/logout', function(req, res){ req.session.destroy(function(){ res.redirect('/'); }); }); ``` **Session Destruction:** 1. `req.session.destroy(callback)` removes session from store 2. Session cookie is cleared/expires 3. Callback executes after cleanup completes 4. Redirect to public page **Testing logout** at [test/acceptance/auth.js:56-63](): ```javascript request(app) .get('/logout') .expect('Location', '/') .expect(302, done) ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:92-98](), [test/acceptance/auth.js:56-63]() --- ## Complete Authentication Request Flow ```mermaid graph TB START["Client Request"] subgraph "Middleware Chain" SESSION["express-session
Load/create req.session"] URLENCODED["express.urlencoded
Parse POST body"] MESSAGES["Message Middleware
Transfer session messages to res.locals"] end subgraph "Route Matching" ROUTER["Express Router"] PUBLIC{"Public route?"} PROTECTED["Protected Route
Has restrict middleware"] end subgraph "Authentication Check" RESTRICT["restrict(req, res, next)"] HAS_USER{"req.session.user
exists?"} DENY["Set error message
res.redirect('/login')"] ALLOW["next()"] end subgraph "Route Handlers" LOGIN_GET["GET /login
res.render('login')"] LOGIN_POST["POST /login
authenticate → regenerate → redirect"] LOGOUT["GET /logout
destroy → redirect"] RESTRICTED["GET /restricted
Send protected content"] end START --> SESSION SESSION --> URLENCODED URLENCODED --> MESSAGES MESSAGES --> ROUTER ROUTER --> PUBLIC PUBLIC -->|"Yes"| LOGIN_GET PUBLIC -->|"Yes"| LOGIN_POST PUBLIC -->|"Yes"| LOGOUT PUBLIC -->|"No"| PROTECTED PROTECTED --> RESTRICT RESTRICT --> HAS_USER HAS_USER -->|"No"| DENY HAS_USER -->|"Yes"| ALLOW ALLOW --> RESTRICTED LOGIN_POST -.stores user in.-> SESSION LOGOUT -.destroys.-> SESSION DENY -.redirect to.-> LOGIN_GET ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:1-135]() --- ## Cookie Security Considerations ### Preventing XSS Cookie Theft Use `httpOnly` flag at [test/res.cookie.js:59]() to prevent JavaScript access: ```javascript res.cookie('name', 'tobi', { httpOnly: true, secure: true }); // Result: name=tobi; Path=/; HttpOnly; Secure ``` ### Signed Cookies for Tampering Detection Signed cookies at [test/res.cookie.js:246-259]() use HMAC to detect modification: ```javascript app.use(cookieParser('foo bar baz')); res.cookie('user', { name: 'tobi' }, { signed: true }) // Result: user=s%3Aj%3A%7B%22name%22%3A%22tobi%22%7D.K20xcwmDS%2BPb1rsD95o5Jm5SqWs1KteqdnynnB7jkTE ``` The signature (after the `.`) is an HMAC of the value using the secret `'foo bar baz'`. If the value is modified, signature validation fails and `req.signedCookies` will be empty. **Error handling for missing secret** at [test/res.cookie.js:263-276](): ```javascript // Attempting signed cookies without secret throws error res.cookie('name', 'tobi', { signed: true }) // Error: secret\S+ required for signed cookies ``` Sources: [test/res.cookie.js:245-276](), [test/req.signedCookies.js:9-34]() ### Cookie Clearing Use `res.clearCookie()` at [test/res.clearCookie.js:8-19]() to remove cookies: ```javascript res.clearCookie('sid') // Result: Set-Cookie: sid=; Path=/; Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT ``` **With options** at [test/res.clearCookie.js:23-34](): ```javascript res.clearCookie('sid', { path: '/admin' }) // Result: Set-Cookie: sid=; Path=/admin; Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT ``` Note: Cookie options (path, domain, etc.) must match the original cookie for successful clearing. Sources: [test/res.clearCookie.js:1-62]() --- ## Session Storage Options ### In-Memory Storage (Default) The example uses default in-memory storage suitable for development: ```javascript app.use(session({ // No store specified = MemoryStore (default) resave: false, saveUninitialized: false, secret: 'shhhh, very secret' })); ``` **Limitations:** - Not production-ready - Doesn't scale across processes - Lost on server restart - Memory leaks with many sessions ### Production Session Stores For production, use compatible session stores: | Store | Package | Use Case | |-------|---------|----------| | Redis | `connect-redis` | High-performance caching | | MongoDB | `connect-mongo` | Document-based persistence | | PostgreSQL | `connect-pg-simple` | Relational database | | Memcached | `connect-memcached` | Distributed caching | | File | `session-file-store` | Simple file persistence | Example with Redis: ```javascript const RedisStore = require('connect-redis')(session); const redis = require('redis'); const client = redis.createClient(); app.use(session({ store: new RedisStore({ client: client }), secret: 'keyboard cat', resave: false, saveUninitialized: false })); ``` Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:22-26]() --- ## View Integration ### Login Form Template The login view at [examples/auth/views/login.ejs:1-22]() displays messages from `res.locals.message`: ```html

Login

<%- message %> Try accessing /restricted

``` The `<%- message %>` outputs the HTML from `res.locals.message` set by the message middleware. Sources: [examples/auth/views/login.ejs:1-22]() ### Rendering with Session Data The login GET handler at [examples/auth/index.js:100-102]() simply renders: ```javascript app.get('/login', function(req, res){ res.render('login'); }); ``` Session messages are automatically available via `res.locals.message` due to the message middleware that runs before the handler. Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:100-102](), [examples/auth/index.js:30-39]() --- ## Testing Authentication Flows ### Test Structure The acceptance test at [test/acceptance/auth.js:1-118]() validates complete flows: **Helper function** at [test/acceptance/auth.js:4-6](): ```javascript function getCookie(res) { return res.headers['set-cookie'][0].split(';')[0]; } ``` **Login flow test** at [test/acceptance/auth.js:73-86](): ```javascript it('should succeed with proper cookie', function(done){ request(app) .post('/login') .type('urlencoded') .send('username=tj&password=foobar') .expect('Location', '/') .expect(302, function(err, res){ if (err) return done(err) request(app) .get('/restricted') .set('Cookie', getCookie(res)) .expect(200, done) }) }) ``` ### Test Coverage | Test | Endpoint | Expected Behavior | |------|----------|-------------------| | Redirect to login | `GET /` | 302 to `/login` | | Login form | `GET /login` | 200 with form HTML | | Bad username | `POST /login` | 302 to `/login` with error | | Bad password | `POST /login` | 302 to `/login` with error | | Correct credentials | `POST /login` | 302 to `/` with session cookie | | Protected without cookie | `GET /restricted` | 302 to `/login` | | Protected with cookie | `GET /restricted` | 200 with content | | Logout | `GET /logout` | 302 to `/` | Sources: [test/acceptance/auth.js:8-117]() --- ## Summary Authentication and session management in Express follows these key patterns: 1. **Session Middleware**: Use `express-session` to provide `req.session` object 2. **Cookie-Based Identity**: Session ID stored in cookie, data stored server-side 3. **Password Hashing**: Never store plain passwords, use libraries like `pbkdf2-password` 4. **Route Protection**: Middleware functions check `req.session.user` before allowing access 5. **Session Regeneration**: Generate new session ID on login to prevent fixation 6. **Session Destruction**: Call `req.session.destroy()` on logout 7. **Flash Messages**: Store one-time messages in session, display once, then delete 8. **Signed Cookies**: Use cookie signatures for additional security 9. **Security Options**: Set `httpOnly`, `secure`, `sameSite` on sensitive cookies 10. **Production Storage**: Use Redis, MongoDB, or other stores instead of memory Sources: [examples/auth/index.js:1-135](), [test/acceptance/auth.js:1-118]() --- # Page: File Downloads and Static Serving # File Downloads and Static Serving
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for generating this wiki page: - [examples/cookie-sessions/index.js](examples/cookie-sessions/index.js) - [examples/downloads/files/amazing.txt](examples/downloads/files/amazing.txt) - [examples/downloads/files/notes/groceries.txt](examples/downloads/files/notes/groceries.txt) - [examples/error-pages/index.js](examples/error-pages/index.js) - [test/acceptance/cookie-sessions.js](test/acceptance/cookie-sessions.js) - [test/acceptance/cookies.js](test/acceptance/cookies.js) - [test/acceptance/downloads.js](test/acceptance/downloads.js) - [test/express.json.js](test/express.json.js) - [test/express.raw.js](test/express.raw.js) - [test/express.static.js](test/express.static.js) - [test/express.text.js](test/express.text.js) - [test/express.urlencoded.js](test/express.urlencoded.js) - [test/res.download.js](test/res.download.js) - [test/res.sendFile.js](test/res.sendFile.js) - [test/support/utils.js](test/support/utils.js)
## Purpose and Scope This page documents practical patterns and examples for implementing file downloads and static asset serving in Express.js applications. It covers the use of `express.static()` middleware for serving static files, `res.download()` for file downloads, and `res.sendFile()` for controlled file access. For API reference details on `res.sendFile()` and `res.download()`, see [File Operations](#5.2). For `express.static()` middleware API reference, see [Static File Middleware](#4.3). --- ## Static File Serving Architecture ```mermaid graph TB Request["HTTP Request
GET /assets/style.css"] subgraph "Express Application" StaticMW["express.static(root, options)"] Router["Router Stack"] FallThrough["Next Middleware"] end subgraph "serve-static Module" SendModule["send module"] PathResolve["Path Resolution"] FileSystem["fs.statSync / fs.createReadStream"] end subgraph "Response Headers" ContentType["Content-Type: text/css"] CacheControl["Cache-Control: public, max-age=X"] ETag["ETag: W/\"abc123\""] LastMod["Last-Modified: Date"] AcceptRanges["Accept-Ranges: bytes"] end Request --> StaticMW StaticMW --> PathResolve PathResolve --> FileSystem FileSystem -->|File Exists| SendModule FileSystem -->|Not Found & fallthrough=true| FallThrough FileSystem -->|Not Found & fallthrough=false| Error404["404 Error"] SendModule --> ContentType SendModule --> CacheControl SendModule --> ETag SendModule --> LastMod SendModule --> AcceptRanges FallThrough --> Router style StaticMW fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style SendModule fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:16-135](), [test/res.sendFile.js:16-174]() ### Basic Static File Serving The most common pattern for serving static assets uses `express.static()` middleware: ```javascript app.use(express.static('public')) app.use('/static', express.static('public')) ``` The middleware serves files from the specified directory. When mounted at a path, it serves files relative to that path. Files are served with appropriate `Content-Type` headers based on file extensions. **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:30-47]() ### Static Middleware Options | Option | Default | Description | |--------|---------|-------------| | `dotfiles` | `'ignore'` | How to handle dotfiles: `'allow'`, `'deny'`, `'ignore'` | | `etag` | `true` | Enable ETag generation | | `extensions` | `false` | File extension fallbacks (e.g., `['html', 'htm']`) | | `fallthrough` | `true` | Call next() on 404 (false sends 404 response) | | `immutable` | `false` | Enable Cache-Control immutable directive | | `index` | `'index.html'` | Directory index file name | | `lastModified` | `true` | Enable Last-Modified header | | `maxAge` | `0` | Cache-Control max-age in milliseconds | | `redirect` | `true` | Redirect directories to trailing slash | | `setHeaders` | `undefined` | Function to set custom headers | **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:150-573]() --- ## File Download Patterns ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Download Flow" Route["Route Handler
app.get('/files/:name')"] Download["res.download(path, filename, options, callback)"] SendFile["Internal: res.sendFile()"] end subgraph "Path Resolution" RelPath["Relative Path + root option"] AbsPath["Absolute Path"] PathValidation["Path Validation
Prevent traversal"] end subgraph "Response Headers Set" ContentDisp["Content-Disposition:
attachment; filename=\"...\""] ContentType["Content-Type:
from mime lookup"] CacheHeaders["Cache-Control, ETag,
Last-Modified"] end subgraph "Error Handling" Callback["Callback(err)"] ErrorMW["Error Middleware"] ClientResp["Error Response"] end Route --> Download Download --> RelPath Download --> AbsPath RelPath --> PathValidation AbsPath --> PathValidation PathValidation -->|Valid| SendFile PathValidation -->|Invalid| Callback SendFile --> ContentDisp SendFile --> ContentType SendFile --> CacheHeaders Callback -->|Error| ErrorMW ErrorMW --> ClientResp style Download fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ContentDisp fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Sources:** [test/res.download.js:16-89](), [test/acceptance/downloads.js:5-47]() ### Basic File Download Implementation The `res.download()` method sends files with a `Content-Disposition: attachment` header, prompting browsers to save the file: ```javascript app.get('/files/:name', function(req, res, next) { const file = req.params.name const path = __dirname + '/files/' + file res.download(path, function(err) { if (err) { // Error occurred, headers may have been sent if (!res.headersSent) { res.status(err.status || 404).send(err.message) } } }) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.download.js:16-29]() ### Download with Custom Filename The second parameter to `res.download()` specifies the filename shown to the user, independent of the actual file path: ```javascript app.get('/files/:id', function(req, res) { const path = '/var/data/' + req.params.id + '.dat' const filename = 'report-' + req.params.id + '.pdf' res.download(path, filename) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.download.js:59-72]() ### Secure Download with Root Option Using the `root` option enables relative paths while preventing directory traversal: ```javascript app.get('/files/*', function(req, res, next) { const file = req.params[0] const options = { root: __dirname + '/files/', dotfiles: 'deny' } res.download(file, options, function(err) { if (err) { next(err) } }) }) ``` This pattern is demonstrated in the downloads example application. **Sources:** [test/res.download.js:287-354](), [test/acceptance/downloads.js:14-46]() --- ## Controlled File Access with sendFile ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "sendFile Options Flow" Path["File Path
(absolute or root+relative)"] Options["Options Object"] Callback["Callback Function"] Path --> SendFile["res.sendFile()"] Options --> SendFile Callback --> SendFile end subgraph "Key Options" Root["root: base directory"] DotFiles["dotfiles: 'allow'|'deny'|'ignore'"] Headers["headers: { 'X-Custom': 'value' }"] MaxAge["maxAge: milliseconds or string"] Accept["acceptRanges: boolean"] Cache["cacheControl: boolean"] end Options --> Root Options --> DotFiles Options --> Headers Options --> MaxAge Options --> Accept Options --> Cache subgraph "Security Checks" AbsCheck["Absolute Path
Required w/o root"] TraversalCheck["Path Traversal
Prevention"] DotCheck["Dotfile
Handling"] end SendFile --> AbsCheck SendFile --> TraversalCheck SendFile --> DotCheck style SendFile fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:16-902]() ### sendFile vs download While `res.download()` always sets `Content-Disposition: attachment`, `res.sendFile()` sends files inline (viewed in browser): ```javascript // Inline viewing (sendFile) app.get('/view/:file', function(req, res) { res.sendFile(req.params.file, { root: './documents' }) }) // Download prompt (download) app.get('/download/:file', function(req, res) { res.download(req.params.file, { root: './documents' }) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:41-47](), [test/res.download.js:17-29]() ### sendFile with Custom Headers The `headers` option allows setting custom response headers: ```javascript res.sendFile('report.pdf', { root: __dirname + '/files', headers: { 'X-Report-ID': reportId, 'X-Generated-Date': new Date().toISOString() } }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:509-583]() ### sendFile Error Handling The callback receives errors for missing files, path traversal attempts, or other issues: ```javascript res.sendFile(filename, { root: './files' }, function(err) { if (err) { if (err.status === 404) { res.status(404).send('File not found') } else if (err.status === 403) { res.status(403).send('Access denied') } else { next(err) } } }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:266-278]() --- ## Security Considerations ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Security Threats" Traversal["Path Traversal
../../etc/passwd"] Dotfiles["Hidden Files
.env, .git"] AbsPath["Absolute Path Leaks"] end subgraph "Protection Mechanisms" RootOption["root Option
Constrains to directory"] DotOption["dotfiles Option
'deny' or 'ignore'"] PathNorm["Path Normalization
Resolves .. segments"] BoundCheck["Boundary Check
Prevents escape"] end subgraph "Example Blocked Requests" Block1["GET /files/../../../etc/passwd
→ 403 Forbidden"] Block2["GET /files/.env
→ 404 Not Found (ignore)
→ 403 Forbidden (deny)"] Block3["GET /files/subdir/../../outside.txt
→ 403 Forbidden"] end Traversal --> PathNorm Traversal --> BoundCheck Dotfiles --> DotOption AbsPath --> RootOption PathNorm --> Block1 DotOption --> Block2 BoundCheck --> Block3 style RootOption fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style BoundCheck fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:845-900](), [test/express.static.js:575-591]() ### Path Traversal Prevention Both `res.sendFile()` and `express.static()` prevent directory traversal attacks: ```javascript // PROTECTED: Using root option app.get('/files/:name', function(req, res) { res.sendFile(req.params.name, { root: './public/files' }) }) // Request: GET /files/../../../etc/passwd // Result: 403 Forbidden - traversal detected // VULNERABLE: Using absolute paths without validation app.get('/files/:name', function(req, res) { res.sendFile(__dirname + '/files/' + req.params.name) }) // Still protected by sendFile, but requires absolute paths ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:874-900](), [test/acceptance/downloads.js:40-46]() ### Dotfile Protection The `dotfiles` option controls access to hidden files: ```javascript // Deny dotfiles (403 Forbidden) app.use(express.static('public', { dotfiles: 'deny' })) // Ignore dotfiles (404 Not Found) app.use(express.static('public', { dotfiles: 'ignore' })) // Allow dotfiles (serve normally) app.use(express.static('public', { dotfiles: 'allow' })) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:453-506](), [test/express.static.js:404-416]() ### Safe Root-Relative Pattern The secure pattern uses `root` with relative paths from user input: ```javascript const FILES_DIR = path.join(__dirname, 'files') app.get('/download/:folder/:file', function(req, res, next) { const filepath = path.join(req.params.folder, req.params.file) res.download(filepath, { root: FILES_DIR, dotfiles: 'deny' }, function(err) { if (err && !res.headersSent) { next(err) } }) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.download.js:286-354]() --- ## Caching and Performance ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Cache Headers" CacheControl["Cache-Control:
public, max-age=X"] ETag["ETag:
W/\"size-mtime-hex\""] LastMod["Last-Modified:
file mtime"] Immutable["immutable directive"] end subgraph "Conditional Requests" IfNoneMatch["If-None-Match:
etag value"] IfModSince["If-Modified-Since:
date"] Fresh["Freshness Check"] end subgraph "Response Types" FullResp["200 OK
Full file content"] NotMod["304 Not Modified
Empty body"] end CacheControl --> Fresh ETag --> Fresh LastMod --> Fresh IfNoneMatch --> Fresh IfModSince --> Fresh Fresh -->|Stale| FullResp Fresh -->|Fresh| NotMod Immutable --> CacheControl style Fresh fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style NotMod fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:57-93](), [test/express.static.js:103-122]() ### Cache-Control Configuration The `maxAge` option sets the Cache-Control header: ```javascript // Static files with 1 year cache app.use('/assets', express.static('public', { maxAge: '1y', immutable: true })) // Dynamic files with short cache app.get('/data/:id', function(req, res) { res.sendFile(dataFile, { root: './data', maxAge: '5m' }) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:694-757](), [test/express.static.js:452-466]() ### ETag and Conditional Requests ETags enable efficient caching with 304 Not Modified responses: ```javascript // Default behavior - ETag enabled app.use(express.static('public')) // Disable ETag app.disable('etag') app.use(express.static('public')) // Or per-request res.sendFile(file, { root: './files' }) ``` When a client sends `If-None-Match` with a matching ETag, Express responds with 304. **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:66-93](), [test/express.static.js:103-122]() ### Last-Modified Header The `lastModified` option controls the Last-Modified header: ```javascript // Enable Last-Modified (default) res.sendFile(file, { root: './files', lastModified: true }) // Disable Last-Modified res.sendFile(file, { root: './files', lastModified: false }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:624-691]() ### Immutable Cache Directive For versioned assets that never change, use the `immutable` directive: ```javascript app.use('/assets', express.static('public', { maxAge: '1y', immutable: true })) // Response: Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:586-621](), [test/express.static.js:418-430]() --- ## Range Requests and Partial Content ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express participant FileSystem Client->>Express: GET /video.mp4
Range: bytes=0-1023 Express->>Express: Check acceptRanges option Express->>FileSystem: Read bytes 0-1023 FileSystem->>Express: Partial data Express->>Client: 206 Partial Content
Content-Range: bytes 0-1023/10000
Accept-Ranges: bytes Client->>Express: GET /video.mp4
Range: bytes=1024- Express->>FileSystem: Read bytes 1024-end FileSystem->>Express: Partial data Express->>Client: 206 Partial Content
Content-Range: bytes 1024-9999/10000 ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:346-412](), [test/express.static.js:593-678]() ### Enabling Range Requests Range requests allow clients to request partial file content, useful for video streaming and large downloads: ```javascript // Enable range requests (default for express.static) app.use('/videos', express.static('videos', { acceptRanges: true })) // Enable for specific sendFile res.sendFile('video.mp4', { root: './videos', acceptRanges: true }) // Disable range requests res.sendFile('file.pdf', { root: './docs', acceptRanges: false }) ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:346-412](), [test/express.static.js:150-186]() ### Range Request Behavior When range requests are enabled: - **Accept-Ranges header**: Advertises byte range support - **206 Partial Content**: Returned when valid range requested - **Content-Range header**: Specifies which bytes are being sent - **416 Range Not Satisfiable**: Returned when range is invalid **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:598-678]() --- ## Complete Examples ### Secure Download Application This example demonstrates a complete file download application with security measures: ```javascript const express = require('express') const path = require('path') const app = express() const FILES_DIR = path.join(__dirname, 'files') // List available files app.get('/', function(req, res) { res.send('
    ' + '
  • amazing.txt
    • ' + '
  • groceries.txt
    • ' + '
') }) // Download with root option for security app.get('/files/*', function(req, res, next) { const file = req.params[0] res.download(file, { root: FILES_DIR, dotfiles: 'deny' }, function(err) { if (!err) return if (err.status !== 404) return next(err) res.status(404).send('File not found') }) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/downloads.js:1-47]() ### Static Assets with Custom Headers Setting custom headers for static files using `setHeaders`: ```javascript app.use('/assets', express.static('public', { maxAge: '1d', setHeaders: function(res, filepath, stat) { if (filepath.endsWith('.html')) { res.setHeader('Cache-Control', 'no-cache') } if (filepath.endsWith('.js')) { res.setHeader('X-Content-Type-Options', 'nosniff') } } })) ``` **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:541-573]() ### Fallthrough Behavior The `fallthrough` option controls whether the middleware calls `next()` on 404: ```javascript // Fallthrough enabled (default) - continues to next middleware app.use(express.static('public', { fallthrough: true })) app.use(function(req, res) { res.status(404).send('Custom 404') }) // Fallthrough disabled - sends error immediately app.use(express.static('public', { fallthrough: false })) // 404 errors are sent without calling next() ``` **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:247-401]() ### Directory Index and Redirects Control directory index behavior and redirects: ```javascript // Default: serves index.html and redirects to trailing slash app.use(express.static('public')) // Custom index file app.use(express.static('public', { index: 'default.html' })) // Disable directory redirect app.use(express.static('public', { redirect: false })) // Disable index files entirely app.use(express.static('public', { index: false })) ``` **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:75-81](), [test/express.static.js:468-539]() --- ## Summary Table: Method Comparison | Feature | `express.static()` | `res.sendFile()` | `res.download()` | |---------|-------------------|------------------|------------------| | **Purpose** | Serve directory of files | Send single file inline | Send file as download | | **Content-Disposition** | Not set (inline) | Not set (inline) | `attachment` (download) | | **Path Type** | Directory root | Absolute or root+relative | Absolute or root+relative | | **Mounted** | Yes (middleware) | No (response method) | No (response method) | | **Auto Content-Type** | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Range Requests** | Optional | Optional | Optional | | **Dotfile Handling** | Optional | Optional | Optional | | **Custom Headers** | Via `setHeaders` callback | Via `headers` option | Via `headers` option | | **Callback** | No | Yes (optional) | Yes (optional) | | **Typical Use** | CSS, JS, images | Protected files | User downloads | **Sources:** [test/express.static.js:16-678](), [test/res.sendFile.js:16-902](), [test/res.download.js:16-487]() --- # Page: Error Pages and HTTP Status Handling # Error Pages and HTTP Status Handling
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for generating this wiki page: - [examples/auth/views/login.ejs](examples/auth/views/login.ejs) - [examples/cookie-sessions/index.js](examples/cookie-sessions/index.js) - [examples/downloads/files/amazing.txt](examples/downloads/files/amazing.txt) - [examples/downloads/files/notes/groceries.txt](examples/downloads/files/notes/groceries.txt) - [examples/error-pages/index.js](examples/error-pages/index.js) - [examples/error-pages/views/404.ejs](examples/error-pages/views/404.ejs) - [examples/error-pages/views/500.ejs](examples/error-pages/views/500.ejs) - [test/Route.js](test/Route.js) - [test/Router.js](test/Router.js) - [test/acceptance/cookie-sessions.js](test/acceptance/cookie-sessions.js) - [test/acceptance/cookies.js](test/acceptance/cookies.js) - [test/acceptance/downloads.js](test/acceptance/downloads.js) - [test/app.router.js](test/app.router.js) - [test/app.use.js](test/app.use.js)
## Purpose and Scope This document demonstrates practical patterns for handling HTTP error responses in Express applications, including custom 404 Not Found pages, error middleware for 500 Internal Server Error responses, and content negotiation to serve different error formats (HTML, JSON, plain text) based on client preferences. For information about the underlying error handling mechanism in the routing system (including `next(err)`, `next('route')`, and 4-arity error middleware), see [Error Handling in Routes](#3.3). For general response methods, see [Content Delivery Methods](#5.1). ## HTTP Status Codes Express applications set HTTP status codes using `res.status()` to indicate the outcome of a request. Common status codes for error handling include: | Status Code | Name | Usage | |-------------|------|-------| | 400 | Bad Request | Malformed request syntax or invalid parameters | | 403 | Forbidden | Server understood request but refuses to authorize it | | 404 | Not Found | Requested resource does not exist | | 500 | Internal Server Error | Unhandled exception or error in application logic | | 503 | Service Unavailable | Server temporarily unable to handle request | Setting a status code: ```javascript res.status(404); // Set status code res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal error' }); // Chain with response ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:64-65](), [examples/error-pages/index.js:95]() ## Error Handling Middleware Stack Placement Error handling in Express follows a specific middleware stack pattern. Understanding the placement of 404 handlers and error middleware is critical for proper error handling. ### Error Handler Placement Diagram ```mermaid graph TB Request["HTTP Request"] subgraph "Application Middleware Stack" MW1["Regular Middleware
(logger, body parser)"] MW2["Route Handlers
(app.get, app.post, etc.)"] NotFound["404 Handler
3-arity: (req, res, next)"] ErrorHandler["Error Handler
4-arity: (err, req, res, next)"] end Response["HTTP Response"] Request --> MW1 MW1 --> MW2 MW2 -->|"No route matched"| NotFound MW2 -->|"next(err) called"| ErrorHandler NotFound --> Response ErrorHandler --> Response style NotFound fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ErrorHandler fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:63-77](), [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() ### 404 Not Found Handler The 404 handler is a **regular 3-arity middleware** placed after all routes. When no route matches a request, Express falls through to this middleware, which assumes the resource was not found. ```javascript // Placed AFTER all route handlers app.use(function(req, res, next){ res.status(404); res.format({ html: function () { res.render('404', { url: req.url }) }, json: function () { res.json({ error: 'Not found' }) }, default: function () { res.type('txt').send('Not found') } }) }); ``` Key characteristics: - **3 parameters**: `(req, res, next)` - **Placement**: After all route definitions but before error handlers - **Purpose**: Handles requests that don't match any defined route - **Triggered**: Automatically when no previous middleware responds **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:63-77]() ### Error Handling Middleware Error handlers are **4-arity middleware** that catch errors passed via `next(err)` or thrown during request processing. ```javascript // Placed LAST in middleware stack app.use(function(err, req, res, next){ res.status(err.status || 500); res.render('500', { error: err }); }); ``` Key characteristics: - **4 parameters**: `(err, req, res, next)` - the `err` parameter is required first - **Placement**: After all other middleware, including 404 handler - **Purpose**: Handles errors from route handlers and middleware - **Triggered**: When `next(err)` is called or an exception is thrown **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() ## Triggering Different Error Types ### Triggering 404 Errors A 404 occurs when a route handler calls `next()` without responding, causing the request to fall through to the 404 handler: ```javascript app.get('/404', function(req, res, next){ // Calling next() without sending a response // triggers 404 handler next(); }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:34-39]() ### Triggering HTTP Error Codes Create an Error object with a `status` property to set specific HTTP status codes: ```javascript app.get('/403', function(req, res, next){ var err = new Error('not allowed!'); err.status = 403; next(err); }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:41-46]() ### Triggering 500 Errors Pass an Error to `next()` without a status property to trigger a 500 Internal Server Error: ```javascript app.get('/500', function(req, res, next){ next(new Error('keyboard cat!')); }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:48-51]() ## Content Negotiation for Errors Use `res.format()` to serve different error representations based on the client's `Accept` header. This allows the same error endpoint to serve HTML pages to browsers, JSON to API clients, and plain text as a fallback. ### Content Negotiation Flow ```mermaid graph LR Client["HTTP Client"] Accept["Accept Header"] Format["res.format()"] subgraph "Response Handlers" HTML["html: function()
renders template"] JSON["json: function()
sends JSON"] Default["default: function()
sends plain text"] end Response["HTTP Response"] Client -->|"includes Accept header"| Accept Accept --> Format Format -->|"Accept: text/html"| HTML Format -->|"Accept: application/json"| JSON Format -->|"Accept: */*"| Default HTML --> Response JSON --> Response Default --> Response ``` ### 404 Handler with Content Negotiation ```javascript app.use(function(req, res, next){ res.status(404); res.format({ html: function () { res.render('404', { url: req.url }) }, json: function () { res.json({ error: 'Not found' }) }, default: function () { res.type('txt').send('Not found') } }) }); ``` Testing content negotiation: ```bash # HTML response $ curl http://localhost:3000/notfound # JSON response $ curl http://localhost:3000/notfound -H "Accept: application/json" # Plain text response $ curl http://localhost:3000/notfound -H "Accept: text/plain" ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:63-77]() ## Error Object Properties Error middleware receives an Error object with standard properties that control error display: | Property | Type | Description | |----------|------|-------------| | `message` | string | Human-readable error description | | `status` | number | HTTP status code (optional, defaults to 500) | | `stack` | string | Stack trace (available in development) | ### Accessing Error Properties ```javascript app.use(function(err, req, res, next){ // Use status from error or default to 500 res.status(err.status || 500); // Pass error to template res.render('500', { error: err // Contains message, status, stack }); }); ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() ## Development vs Production Error Display Control error verbosity using application settings. In development, show full stack traces; in production, hide internal details. ### Setting Verbose Errors ```javascript // Enable verbose errors (development) app.enable('verbose errors'); // Disable in production if (app.settings.env === 'production') { app.disable('verbose errors'); } ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:20-24]() ### Conditional Error Template In the error template, check the `verbose errors` setting: ```ejs

Error: <%= error.message %>

<% if (settings['verbose errors']) { %> 
<%= error.stack %>
<% } else { %>

An error occurred!

<% } %> ``` This displays: - **Development**: Full stack trace with file names and line numbers - **Production**: Generic error message without sensitive information **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/views/500.ejs:2-7]() ## Complete Error Handling Example ### Application Structure ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Setup" Config["Configuration
views, view engine,
verbose errors"] Logger["Middleware
morgan logger"] end subgraph "Route Handlers" Root["GET /
renders index"] R404["GET /404
calls next()"] R403["GET /403
throws 403 error"] R500["GET /500
throws generic error"] end subgraph "Error Handlers" NotFoundHandler["404 Handler
res.status(404)
res.format()"] ErrorHandler["Error Handler
res.status(err.status || 500)
res.render('500')"] end Config --> Logger Logger --> Root Root --> R404 R404 --> R403 R403 --> R500 R500 --> NotFoundHandler NotFoundHandler --> ErrorHandler style NotFoundHandler fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ErrorHandler fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### Full Implementation The complete example from [examples/error-pages/index.js](): 1. **Setup** ([examples/error-pages/index.js:13-26]()): - Configure view engine - Enable verbose errors in development - Add logging middleware 2. **Routes** ([examples/error-pages/index.js:30-51]()): - Normal routes that render pages - Routes that trigger specific error types 3. **404 Handler** ([examples/error-pages/index.js:63-77]()): - Placed after all routes - Uses `res.format()` for content negotiation - Renders 404 template or sends JSON/text 4. **Error Handler** ([examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]()): - Placed last in middleware stack - Extracts status from error or defaults to 500 - Renders error template with error details ### Testing Error Responses From test file [test/acceptance/downloads.js](): ```javascript // Test 404 response describe('GET /files/missing.txt', function(){ it('should respond with 404', function(done){ request(app) .get('/files/missing.txt') .expect(404, done) }) }) // Test 403 response describe('GET /files/../index.js', function () { it('should respond with 403', function (done) { request(app) .get('/files/../index.js') .expect(403, done) }) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/acceptance/downloads.js:32-46]() ## Error Handler Execution Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express participant Routes participant Handler404 as "404 Handler" participant HandlerErr as "Error Handler" participant Response Note over Client,Response: Scenario 1: Route not found Client->>Express: GET /notfound Express->>Routes: Match routes Routes->>Routes: No match found Routes->>Handler404: Fall through Handler404->>Handler404: res.status(404) Handler404->>Handler404: res.format() Handler404->>Response: Send response Response->>Client: 404 Not Found Note over Client,Response: Scenario 2: Error thrown in route Client->>Express: GET /500 Express->>Routes: Match GET /500 Routes->>Routes: next(new Error()) Routes->>HandlerErr: Pass error HandlerErr->>HandlerErr: res.status(err.status || 500) HandlerErr->>HandlerErr: res.render('500', { error }) HandlerErr->>Response: Send response Response->>Client: 500 Internal Server Error Note over Client,Response: Scenario 3: Custom status error Client->>Express: GET /403 Express->>Routes: Match GET /403 Routes->>Routes: next(err) with err.status = 403 Routes->>HandlerErr: Pass error HandlerErr->>HandlerErr: res.status(403) HandlerErr->>HandlerErr: res.render('500', { error }) HandlerErr->>Response: Send response Response->>Client: 403 Forbidden ``` **Sources:** [examples/error-pages/index.js:34-51](), [examples/error-pages/index.js:63-77](), [examples/error-pages/index.js:91-97]() ## Error Handler Middleware Characteristics ### Regular vs Error Middleware | Aspect | Regular Middleware | Error Middleware | |--------|-------------------|------------------| | Arity | 3 parameters: `(req, res, next)` | 4 parameters: `(err, req, res, next)` | | Execution | Every request in sequence | Only when `next(err)` called | | Placement | Anywhere in stack | After all other middleware | | Purpose | Request processing | Error handling | | Example | `app.use(function(req, res, next) {})` | `app.use(function(err, req, res, next) {})` | ### Error Middleware Invocation From [test/app.router.js:903-936](): ```javascript // Error handler triggered by next(err) app.get('/foo', function (req, res, next) { next(new Error('fail')); // Triggers error handler }); app.get('/foo', function () { // This is NOT executed - skipped due to error assert(0); }); // Error handler catches the error app.use(function (err, req, res, next) { res.json({ calls: calls, error: err.message }) }) ``` **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:903-936]() ## Error Middleware Chain Multiple error handlers can be chained. Each can modify the error or pass it along: ```javascript // First error handler app.use(function(err, req, res, next){ console.error('Error occurred:', err.message); next(err); // Pass to next error handler }); // Second error handler (final) app.use(function(err, req, res, next){ res.status(err.status || 500); res.render('500', { error: err }); }); ``` From [test/Router.js:273-290](): ```javascript router.use(function (err, req, res, next) { throw new Error('oops'); // Throw new error }); router.use(function (err, req, res, next) { assert.equal(err.message, 'oops'); // Catches thrown error done(); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/Router.js:273-290]() ## Summary Express error handling follows a clear pattern: 1. **Regular routes and middleware** process requests in order 2. **404 handler** (3-arity) catches unmatched routes - placed after all routes 3. **Error middleware** (4-arity) catches errors - placed last 4. Use `res.status()` to set HTTP status codes 5. Use `res.format()` for content negotiation 6. Use application settings to control error verbosity 7. Set `err.status` to control specific HTTP status codes This architecture provides consistent error handling across all routes while maintaining flexibility for different client needs and deployment environments. --- # Page: RESTful APIs and Content Negotiation # RESTful APIs and Content Negotiation
