## Purpose and Scope This document covers Express.js features for building RESTful APIs, including JSON/JSONP response methods, content negotiation based on Accept headers, HTTP method routing, and request header access. For general response methods, see [Content Delivery Methods](#5.1). For middleware patterns, see [Middleware Execution Flow](#4.1). --- ## JSON Response Methods Express provides two primary methods for sending JSON responses: `res.json()` and `res.jsonp()`. ### res.json() The `res.json()` method serializes data to JSON and sends it with the appropriate Content-Type header. **Key Behaviors:** | Feature | Behavior | Source | |---------|----------|--------| | Content-Type | Defaults to `application/json; charset=utf-8` | [test/res.json.js:45-46]() | | Content-Type Override | Preserves previously set Content-Types (e.g., `application/vnd.example+json`) | [test/res.json.js:24-32]() | | Primitive Types | Handles null, numbers, strings, arrays, and objects | [test/res.json.js:35-104]() | | Undefined | Returns empty body for undefined | [test/res.json.js:132-139]() | **Configuration Settings:** ```javascript // Pretty-print with spacing app.set('json spaces', 2); // Filter properties with replacer function app.set('json replacer', function(key, val) { return key[0] === '_' ? undefined : val; }); // Escape HTML-sniffing characters for security app.enable('json escape'); ``` **Settings Behavior:** | Setting | Purpose | Example Output | |---------|---------|----------------| | `json spaces` | Indentation for readability | `{\n "name": "tobi"\n}` | | `json replacer` | Filter/transform properties | `{"name":"tobi"}` (removes `_id`) | | `json escape` | Unicode escape `<`, `>`, `&` | `{"\\u0026":"\\u003cscript\\u003e"}` | **Sources:** [test/res.json.js:1-187]() --- ### res.jsonp() The `res.jsonp()` method provides JSONP support with automatic callback wrapping and security features. **Callback Parameter Handling:** ```mermaid graph TB REQUEST["HTTP Request
?callback=something"] CHECK_CALLBACK["Check req.query[jsonp callback name]"] IS_CALLBACK{"Callback
present?"} VALIDATE["Validate callback name
(alphanumeric, [], dots)"] JSONP_RESPONSE["Content-Type: text/javascript
/**/ callback(data);"] JSON_RESPONSE["Content-Type: application/json
data"] SECURITY_HEADER["X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff"] REQUEST --> CHECK_CALLBACK CHECK_CALLBACK --> IS_CALLBACK IS_CALLBACK -->|Yes| VALIDATE IS_CALLBACK -->|No| JSON_RESPONSE VALIDATE --> JSONP_RESPONSE JSONP_RESPONSE --> SECURITY_HEADER ``` **Security Features:** | Feature | Implementation | Purpose | |---------|----------------|---------| | Callback Validation | Strips non-alphanumeric except `[]` and `.` | [test/res.jsonp.js:77-88]() | | Security Prologue | Prepends `/**/` | [test/res.jsonp.js:127]() | | X-Content-Type-Options | Sets to `nosniff` | [test/res.jsonp.js:126]() | | UTF Whitespace Escaping | Escapes `\u2028` and `\u2029` | [test/res.jsonp.js:90-101]() | **Callback Name Configuration:** ```javascript // Default callback parameter is 'callback' app.set('jsonp callback name', 'clb'); // Now responds to ?clb=something instead ``` **Fallback Behavior:** - When no callback parameter is present, `res.jsonp()` falls back to standard JSON response - Content-Type becomes `application/json` instead of `text/javascript` - No security header or prologue added **Sources:** [test/res.jsonp.js:1-345]() --- ## Content Negotiation with res.format() The `res.format()` method performs content negotiation based on the request's `Accept` header, selecting the appropriate response format. ### Content Negotiation Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express as "Express App" participant Format as "res.format()" participant Negotiator as "Accept Header Parser" participant Handler as "Format Handler" Client->>Express: "GET /resource
Accept: application/json, text/html;q=0.5" Express->>Format: "res.format({ ... })" Format->>Negotiator: "Parse Accept header" Negotiator->>Negotiator: "Extract media types and qvalues" Negotiator->>Format: "Ranked list: [application/json, text/html]" Format->>Format: "Match against provided formats" Format->>Handler: "Invoke matched handler (json)" Handler->>Client: "Content-Type: application/json
Vary: Accept
{data}" ``` ### Format Specification `res.format()` accepts an object mapping media types to handler functions: ```javascript res.format({ 'text/plain': function() { res.send('hey'); }, 'text/html': function() { res.send('

hey

'); }, 'application/json': function(req, res, next) { // Handlers receive req, res, next res.send({ message: 'hey' }); } }); ``` **Format Key Variants:** | Format Key Type | Example | Normalized To | |----------------|---------|---------------| | Full MIME type | `'application/json'` | `application/json` | | Extension | `'json'` | `application/json` | | With parameters | `'text/html; charset=utf-8'` | `text/html` | **Sources:** [test/res.format.js:98-114]() ### Quality Value Negotiation The negotiation respects q-values (quality factors) in the Accept header: ```javascript // Request: Accept: text/html;q=0.5, application/json, */*;q=0.1 // Result: application/json selected (highest q-value of 1.0) ``` **Wildcard Support:** | Accept Header | Matches | |--------------|---------| | `application/*` | Any application/* type | | `*/*` | Any type | **Sources:** [test/res.format.js:183-195]() ### Default Handler A `default` handler catches requests that don't match any specified format: ```javascript res.format({ 'text/plain': function() { res.send('text') }, 'default': function(req, res, next) { // Invoked when Accept doesn't match any format // Can invoke other formatters: this.json() res.send('default'); } }); ``` **Sources:** [test/res.format.js:116-151]() ### No Match Behavior When no format matches and no default handler exists: ```mermaid graph LR REQUEST["Accept: application/xml"] --> NO_MATCH["No matching format"] NO_MATCH --> ERROR["406 Not Acceptable"] ERROR --> ERROR_MW["Error Middleware
err.types = ['text/plain', 'text/html', 'application/json']
err.status = 406"] ERROR_MW --> RESPONSE["Response: 406
Supports: text/plain, text/html, application/json"] ``` **Sources:** [test/res.format.js:239-247]() ### Headers Set by res.format() | Header | Value | Purpose | |--------|-------|---------| | `Content-Type` | Selected format + charset | Indicates response format | | `Vary` | `Accept` | Indicates response varies by Accept header | **Sources:** [test/res.format.js:205-229]() --- ## HTTP Method Routing Express provides method-specific routing functions for building RESTful APIs. ### Method Router Functions ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Level" APP_GET["app.get(path, handler)"] APP_POST["app.post(path, handler)"] APP_PUT["app.put(path, handler)"] APP_DELETE["app.delete(path, handler)"] APP_PATCH["app.patch(path, handler)"] APP_OPTIONS["app.options(path, handler)"] APP_HEAD["app.head(path, handler)"] APP_ALL["app.all(path, handler)"] end subgraph "Router Level" ROUTER_GET["router.get(path, handler)"] ROUTER_POST["router.post(path, handler)"] ROUTER_PUT["router.put(path, handler)"] ROUTER_DELETE["router.delete(path, handler)"] end REQUEST_GET["GET /users"] --> APP_GET REQUEST_POST["POST /users"] --> APP_POST REQUEST_PUT["PUT /users/1"] --> APP_PUT REQUEST_DELETE["DELETE /users/1"] --> APP_DELETE REQUEST_ANY["ANY /admin"] --> APP_ALL ``` ### app.all() The `app.all()` method matches all HTTP methods for a given path: ```javascript // Runs for GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc. app.all('/admin', function(req, res, next) { // Authentication check if (!req.user) return res.status(401).send('Unauthorized'); next(); }); app.get('/admin', function(req, res) { res.send('Admin page'); }); ``` **Execution Behavior:** - Handler executes once per request regardless of method - Does not affect automatic OPTIONS responses **Sources:** [test/app.all.js:1-39]() --- ### OPTIONS Request Handling Express automatically responds to OPTIONS requests with allowed methods for a route. **Automatic OPTIONS Response:** ```mermaid graph TB OPTIONS_REQUEST["OPTIONS /users"] ROUTE_LOOKUP["Find all routes matching /users"] METHODS["Collect HTTP methods:
GET, PUT"] AUTO_HEAD["Auto-add HEAD if GET exists"] RESPONSE["Allow: GET, HEAD, PUT
Body: GET, HEAD, PUT"] OPTIONS_REQUEST --> ROUTE_LOOKUP ROUTE_LOOKUP --> METHODS METHODS --> AUTO_HEAD AUTO_HEAD --> RESPONSE ``` **Example:** ```javascript app.post('/users', function() {}); // Not for /users route app.get('/users', function() {}); // Adds GET, HEAD app.put('/users', function() {}); // Adds PUT // OPTIONS /users → Allow: GET, HEAD, PUT ``` **Override Automatic Response:** ```javascript app.options('/users', function(req, res) { res.set('Allow', 'GET'); res.send('GET'); }); // Now OPTIONS /users uses custom handler ``` **Sources:** [test/app.options.js:1-117]() --- ### HEAD Request Handling HEAD requests automatically use GET handlers with body omitted: ```javascript app.get('/users', function(req, res) { res.send('User data'); }); // HEAD /users → Same headers as GET, but no body ``` **Override Automatic Behavior:** ```javascript app.head('/users', function(req, res) { res.header('X-Method', 'head'); res.end(); }); // Now HEAD /users uses custom handler ``` **Sources:** [test/app.head.js:1-67]() --- ## RESTful Resource Patterns ### Standard REST Resource Example ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "RESTful Users Resource" INDEX["GET /users
→ List all users"] SHOW["GET /users/:id
→ Show user"] CREATE["POST /users
→ Create user"] UPDATE["PUT /users/:id
→ Update user"] DELETE["DELETE /users/:id
→ Delete user"] RANGE["GET /users/:a..:b{.:format}
→ Range of users"] end subgraph "Controller Methods" INDEX_METHOD["User.index(req, res)"] SHOW_METHOD["User.show(req, res)"] DESTROY_METHOD["User.destroy(req, res, id)"] RANGE_METHOD["User.range(req, res, a, b, format)"] end INDEX --> INDEX_METHOD SHOW --> SHOW_METHOD DELETE --> DESTROY_METHOD RANGE --> RANGE_METHOD ``` ### Custom Resource Helper The resource example demonstrates a custom routing helper: ```javascript app.resource = function(path, obj) { this.get(path, obj.index); this.get(path + '/:a..:b{.:format}', function(req, res) { var a = parseInt(req.params.a, 10); var b = parseInt(req.params.b, 10); var format = req.params.format; obj.range(req, res, a, b, format); }); this.get(path + '/:id', obj.show); this.delete(path + '/:id', function(req, res) { var id = parseInt(req.params.id, 10); obj.destroy(req, res, id); }); }; // Usage: app.resource('/users', User); ``` **Supported Operations:** | HTTP Method | Path | Controller Method | Purpose | |-------------|------|-------------------|---------| | GET | `/users` | `index(req, res)` | List all | | GET | `/users/:id` | `show(req, res)` | Show one | | GET | `/users/:a..:b{.:format}` | `range(req, res, a, b, format)` | Range slice | | DELETE | `/users/:id` | `destroy(req, res, id)` | Delete one | **Sources:** [examples/resource/index.js:1-96]() ### Format-Specific Responses The resource example demonstrates format-specific responses within controllers: ```javascript range: function(req, res, a, b, format) { var range = users.slice(a, b + 1); switch (format) { case 'json': res.send(range); break; case 'html': default: var html = '
    ' + range.map(function(user) { return '
  • ' + user.name + '
    • '; }).join('\n') + '
'; res.send(html); break; } } ``` This pattern can be replaced with `res.format()` for cleaner content negotiation. **Sources:** [examples/resource/index.js:53-67]() --- ## Request Header Access ### req.get() Method The `req.get()` method retrieves request headers (case-insensitive): ```javascript app.use(function(req, res) { var contentType = req.get('Content-Type'); var userAgent = req.get('User-Agent'); var referer = req.get('Referer'); // Also accepts 'Referrer' res.json({ contentType, userAgent, referer }); }); ``` **Special Cases:** | Header Name | Alias | Behavior | |-------------|-------|----------| | `Referer` | `Referrer` | Both spellings work | **Validation:** - Throws `TypeError` if name argument missing - Throws `TypeError` if name is not a string **Sources:** [test/req.get.js:1-61]() --- ## Complete API Response Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Router as "Router Layer" participant Handler as "Route Handler" participant Format as "res.format() or res.json()" participant Settings as "App Settings" participant Response as "HTTP Response" Client->>Router: "GET /api/users
Accept: application/json" Router->>Handler: "Execute matched route" Handler->>Handler: "Retrieve data" alt Using res.json() Handler->>Format: "res.json(data)" Format->>Settings: "Check json spaces, json replacer, json escape" Settings->>Format: "Return settings" Format->>Format: "JSON.stringify(data, replacer, spaces)" Format->>Response: "Content-Type: application/json
Body: JSON string" else Using res.format() Handler->>Format: "res.format({ json: fn, html: fn })" Format->>Format: "Parse Accept header" Format->>Format: "Match best format" Format->>Handler: "Invoke matched format handler" Handler->>Response: "Content-Type: matched type
Vary: Accept" end Response->>Client: "200 OK + Body" ``` **Sources:** [test/res.json.js:1-187](), [test/res.format.js:1-249]() --- ## Configuration Reference ### JSON-Related Settings | Setting | Type | Default | Purpose | |---------|------|---------|---------| | `json spaces` | Number/String | `undefined` | Indentation for pretty-printing | | `json replacer` | Function | `undefined` | Transform function passed to JSON.stringify | | `json escape` | Boolean | `undefined` | Escape HTML-sniffing characters | | `jsonp callback name` | String | `'callback'` | Query parameter name for JSONP | **Example Configuration:** ```javascript var app = express(); // Development: pretty-print JSON if (app.get('env') === 'development') { app.set('json spaces', 2); } // Security: escape HTML characters app.enable('json escape'); // Custom JSONP parameter app.set('jsonp callback name', 'cb'); // Filter private properties app.set('json replacer', function(key, val) { if (key[0] === '_') return undefined; return val; }); ``` **Sources:** [test/res.json.js:106-184](), [test/res.jsonp.js:49-62](), [test/res.jsonp.js:250-285](), [test/res.jsonp.js:287-328]() --- ## Best Practices for REST APIs ### 1. Use Appropriate HTTP Methods ```javascript // Collection operations app.get('/api/users', listUsers); // List app.post('/api/users', createUser); // Create // Individual resource operations app.get('/api/users/:id', getUser); // Retrieve app.put('/api/users/:id', updateUser); // Update/Replace app.patch('/api/users/:id', patchUser); // Partial Update app.delete('/api/users/:id', deleteUser); // Delete ``` ### 2. Implement Content Negotiation ```javascript app.get('/api/users/:id', function(req, res) { var user = getUserById(req.params.id); res.format({ 'application/json': function() { res.json(user); }, 'application/xml': function() { res.send(toXML(user)); }, 'text/html': function() { res.render('user', { user: user }); } }); }); ``` ### 3. Enable Security Features ```javascript // Escape JSON for XSS protection app.enable('json escape'); // Use JSONP only when necessary app.get('/api/public', function(req, res) { // JSONP automatically includes security headers res.jsonp({ public: 'data' }); }); ``` ### 4. Handle OPTIONS Properly ```javascript // Let Express handle OPTIONS automatically app.get('/api/users', getUsers); app.post('/api/users', createUser); app.put('/api/users', updateUser); // OPTIONS /api/users automatically responds with: // Allow: GET, HEAD, POST, PUT // Override only when custom CORS headers needed app.options('/api/users', function(req, res) { res.set('Access-Control-Allow-Methods', 'GET, POST, PUT'); res.set('Access-Control-Allow-Headers', 'Content-Type, Authorization'); res.sendStatus(200); }); ``` --- ## Summary Express provides comprehensive support for building RESTful APIs through: - **JSON/JSONP response methods** with configurable serialization and security features - **Content negotiation** via `res.format()` for multiple response formats - **HTTP method routing** with automatic OPTIONS and HEAD handling - **Request header access** through `req.get()` - **Flexible configuration** for JSON output and JSONP behavior These features enable clean, standard-compliant REST API implementations with minimal boilerplate. **Sources:** [test/res.json.js:1-187](), [test/res.jsonp.js:1-345](), [test/res.format.js:1-249](), [test/app.options.js:1-117](), [test/app.head.js:1-67](), [test/app.all.js:1-39](), [examples/resource/index.js:1-96]() --- # Page: Project Development and Governance # Project Development and Governance

## Purpose and Scope This document provides an overview of the Express.js project's development infrastructure, governance structure, and quality assurance processes. It covers the organizational hierarchy, contribution workflows, testing infrastructure, and community resources that support the Express.js framework. For detailed information about specific aspects: - **Governance structure and security policies**: See [Project Governance and Security](#8.1) - **Testing infrastructure and CI/CD pipelines**: See [Testing and CI/CD Infrastructure](#8.2) --- ## Project Organization Structure The Express.js project operates under a defined governance model with three distinct roles: Technical Committee (TC) members, Triagers, and Community Contributors. Each role has specific responsibilities in maintaining the project's quality and direction. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Technical Committee (TC)" TC_LEAD["TC Members"] TC_UG["UlisesGascon"] TC_JC["jonchurch"] TC_WT["wesleytodd"] TC_LU["LinusU"] TC_BE["blakeembrey"] TC_SH["sheplu"] TC_CM["crandmck"] TC_CT["ctcpip"] TC_LEAD --> TC_UG TC_LEAD --> TC_JC TC_LEAD --> TC_WT TC_LEAD --> TC_LU TC_LEAD --> TC_BE TC_LEAD --> TC_SH TC_LEAD --> TC_CM TC_LEAD --> TC_CT end subgraph "Triagers Team" TRIAGERS["Triagers"] TR_AV["aravindvnair99"] TR_BJ["bjohansebas"] TR_CP["carpasse"] TR_CB["CBID2"] TR_IL["IamLizu"] TR_PB["Phillip9587"] TR_EK["efekrskl"] TR_RM["rxmarbles"] TR_KD["krzysdz"] TR_GM["GroophyLifefor"] TRIAGERS --> TR_AV TRIAGERS --> TR_BJ TRIAGERS --> TR_CP TRIAGERS --> TR_CB TRIAGERS --> TR_IL TRIAGERS --> TR_PB TRIAGERS --> TR_EK TRIAGERS --> TR_RM TRIAGERS --> TR_KD TRIAGERS --> TR_GM end subgraph "Community" CONTRIBUTORS["Community Contributors"] ISSUES["Issue Reporters"] DISCUSSIONS["Discussions Participants"] end subgraph "Responsibilities" TC_RESP["TC: Project direction
Architecture decisions
Release management
Policy creation"] TR_RESP["Triagers: PR review & triage
Issue management
Community support
Documentation maintenance"] CONT_RESP["Contributors: Bug fixes
Feature development
Documentation
Testing"] end TC_LEAD -.defines.-> TC_RESP TRIAGERS -.performs.-> TR_RESP CONTRIBUTORS -.provides.-> CONT_RESP CONTRIBUTORS --> TRIAGERS TRIAGERS --> TC_LEAD ISSUES -.report to.-> TRIAGERS DISCUSSIONS -.participate with.-> TRIAGERS ``` **Organizational Hierarchy** The project follows a tiered structure where Community Contributors submit code and issues, Triagers manage the incoming work and provide initial review, and the Technical Committee makes final decisions on project direction and architecture. **Sources:** [Readme.md:173-260]() --- ## Team Composition ### Technical Committee (TC) The TC consists of 8 active members who collectively govern the project: | GitHub Username | Name | Role | |-----------------|------|------| | UlisesGascon | Ulises Gascón | TC Member & Triager | | jonchurch | Jon Church | TC Member | | wesleytodd | Wes Todd | TC Member | | LinusU | Linus Unnebäck | TC Member | | blakeembrey | Blake Embrey | TC Member | | sheplu | Jean Burellier | TC Member | | crandmck | Rand McKinney | TC Member | | ctcpip | Chris de Almeida | TC Member | The TC has 5 emeriti members who previously served: dougwilson, hacksparrow, jonathanong, niftylettuce, and troygoode. **Sources:** [Readme.md:181-202]() ### Triagers Team The project maintains a team of 10 active Triagers (plus 1 who is also a TC member): | GitHub Username | Name | Pronouns | |-----------------|------|----------| | aravindvnair99 | Aravind Nair | | | bjohansebas | Sebastian Beltran | | | carpasse | Carlos Serrano | | | CBID2 | Christine Belzie | | | IamLizu | S M Mahmudul Hasan | he/him | | Phillip9587 | Phillip Barta | | | efekrskl | Efe Karasakal | | | rxmarbles | Rick Markins | he/him | | krzysdz | | | | GroophyLifefor | Murat Kirazkaya | | The project has 33 emeritus Triagers who have contributed to triage efforts in the past. **Sources:** [Readme.md:205-260]() --- ## Contribution Workflow The Express.js project accepts contributions through a standard GitHub workflow involving pull requests, code review, testing, and quality checks. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Contributor participant GitHub as "GitHub Repository" participant CI as "CI Workflows" participant Triager participant TC as "Technical Committee" participant Release as "NPM Registry" Contributor->>GitHub: "Fork & create branch" Contributor->>Contributor: "Implement changes" Contributor->>Contributor: "Run npm test locally" Contributor->>GitHub: "Submit Pull Request" GitHub->>CI: "Trigger ci.yml workflow" CI->>CI: "Lint with ESLint" CI->>CI: "Test on Node 18-25
Ubuntu & Windows" CI->>CI: "Test on Node 16-17
(legacy.yml)" CI->>CI: "Generate coverage reports" CI->>CI: "Upload to Coveralls" par Security Scanning GitHub->>CI: "Trigger scorecard.yml" CI->>CI: "Supply chain security scan" CI->>GitHub: "Upload SARIF results" and GitHub->>CI: "Trigger codeql.yml" CI->>CI: "Static analysis scan" CI->>GitHub: "Upload SARIF results" end CI->>GitHub: "Report CI status" alt CI Passes GitHub->>Triager: "Notify for review" Triager->>Triager: "Review code quality
Test coverage
Documentation" Triager->>GitHub: "Request changes or approve" alt Approved by Triager GitHub->>TC: "Notify for final review" TC->>TC: "Architectural review
Breaking changes check" TC->>GitHub: "Merge to master" TC->>Release: "npm publish (on release)" else Changes Requested GitHub->>Contributor: "Notify for revisions" end else CI Fails GitHub->>Contributor: "Notify of failures" Contributor->>Contributor: "Fix issues" Contributor->>GitHub: "Push updates" end ``` **Contribution Process Stages** 1. **Local Development**: Contributors fork the repository, implement changes, and run `npm test` locally 2. **Pull Request Submission**: Changes are submitted via GitHub pull request 3. **Automated Testing**: CI workflows execute lint checks, multi-version Node.js testing, and security scans 4. **Triager Review**: Triagers assess code quality, test coverage, and documentation completeness 5. **TC Review**: Technical Committee performs final architectural and compatibility review 6. **Merge & Release**: Approved changes are merged and eventually published to npm **Sources:** [Readme.md:147-172]() --- ## Quality Assurance Infrastructure Express.js maintains comprehensive quality assurance through automated testing, continuous integration, code coverage tracking, and security scanning. ### Testing Framework ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Test Execution" NPM_TEST["npm test"] MOCHA["Mocha Test Runner"] TEST_SUITES["Test Suites"] end subgraph "Test Organization" UNIT["Unit Tests
13 test clusters"] INTEGRATION["Integration Tests

The CI system is distributed across multiple workflow files that run different jobs based on trigger events. ### Workflow Triggers and Jobs | Workflow File | Triggers | Jobs | Purpose | |---------------|----------|------|---------| | `ci.yml` | push, pull_request, workflow_dispatch | lint, test (matrix), coverage | Main CI for current Node.js versions | | `legacy.yml` | push, pull_request | test (matrix), coverage | CI for legacy Node.js 16-17 | | `scorecard.yml` | branch_protection_rule, schedule (weekly), push (master) | analysis | Supply chain security assessment | | `codeql.yml` | push (master), pull_request, schedule (weekly) | analyze | Static code security analysis | ### Test Matrix Configuration The CI pipeline tests across multiple dimensions: - **Node.js Versions**: 18.x, 19.x, 20.x, 21.x, 22.x, 23.x, 24.x, 25.x (current) + 16.x, 17.x (legacy) - **Operating Systems**: Ubuntu Latest, Windows Latest - **Total Test Jobs**: 20 combinations (8 Node versions × 2 OS for current + 2 Node versions × 2 OS for legacy) Each test job produces an `lcov.info` coverage artifact that is retained for 1 day and then merged for upload to Coveralls. **Sources:** [Readme.md:27-31]() --- ## Code Style and Standards The project enforces consistent code style through configuration files and automated linting. ### EditorConfig Settings Express.js uses EditorConfig to maintain consistency across different editors and IDEs: ``` [*] - charset: utf-8 - insert_final_newline: true - trim_trailing_whitespace: true [*.js, *.json, *.yml] - indent_size: 2 - indent_style: space ``` **Sources:** [.editorconfig:1-12]() ### Linting The CI workflow includes a dedicated lint job that runs ESLint checks on all JavaScript files. This job must pass before any pull request can be merged. --- ## Community Resources Express.js maintains several channels for community engagement and support: ### Official Resources | Resource | Purpose | Location | |----------|---------|----------| | Documentation Website | Official API documentation and guides | https://expressjs.com/ | | GitHub Organization | Official middleware and modules | https://github.com/expressjs | | GitHub Discussions | Development and usage discussions | https://github.com/expressjs/discussions | | Website Repository | Documentation source | https://github.com/expressjs/expressjs.com | ### Project Documentation ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Documentation Structure" README["Readme.md"] GOVERNANCE["GOVERNANCE.md"] CONTRIBUTING["CONTRIBUTING.md"] CODE_OF_CONDUCT["CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md"] SECURITY["Security Policies"] end subgraph "Website Content" DOCS_SITE["expressjs.com"] API_DOCS["API Reference"] GUIDES["Getting Started Guides"] MIGRATION["Migration Guide to v5"] end subgraph "Learning Resources" EXAMPLES["examples/ directory"] QUICK_START["express-generator"] end README -.references.-> GOVERNANCE README -.references.-> CONTRIBUTING README -.references.-> CODE_OF_CONDUCT README -.references.-> SECURITY README -.links to.-> DOCS_SITE DOCS_SITE --> API_DOCS DOCS_SITE --> GUIDES DOCS_SITE --> MIGRATION README -.describes.-> EXAMPLES GUIDES -.references.-> QUICK_START ``` **Documentation Components** - **README.md**: Primary entry point with installation, features, team structure, and contribution guidelines - **GOVERNANCE.md**: Detailed governance model and decision-making processes (referenced but not in repository root) - **CONTRIBUTING.md**: Technical contribution guidelines (in `.github` directory) - **CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md**: Community standards and expectations (in `.github` directory) - **Security Policies**: Vulnerability reporting procedures - **Examples Directory**: Runnable code examples demonstrating Express features **Sources:** [Readme.md:79-84](), [Readme.md:127-145](), [Readme.md:175]() --- ## Security and Vulnerability Management Express.js takes security seriously with multiple layers of protection and clear reporting procedures. ### Security Scanning The project employs two automated security scanning tools: 1. **OpenSSF Scorecard**: Evaluates supply chain security practices - Runs weekly via scheduled workflow - Triggered on branch protection rule changes - Triggered on pushes to master branch - Results published to OpenSSF API for public badge generation 2. **CodeQL Analysis**: Performs static code security analysis - Runs weekly via scheduled workflow - Triggered on pushes to master branch - Triggered on pull requests - Results uploaded to GitHub Security dashboard in SARIF format ### Vulnerability Reporting Security vulnerabilities should be reported according to the procedures documented in the Security Policies and Procedures (accessible via the repository's Security tab). The README directs users to this resource rather than public issue reporting. **Sources:** [Readme.md:155-157](), [Readme.md:31]() --- ## Release and Package Distribution ### NPM Package Express is distributed through the npm registry: - **Package Name**: `express` - **Current Version**: Displayed via npm version badge - **Download Statistics**: Tracked via npmcharts - **Required Node.js Version**: 18 or higher ### Release Process The release process is managed by the Technical Committee: 1. Version updates are decided by TC consensus 2. Changes are merged to the master branch 3. TC members with publish access perform npm releases 4. Release notes document changes and migration requirements 5. Major version changes (e.g., v4 → v5) include migration guides **Sources:** [Readme.md:48-64](), [Readme.md:85]() --- ## Project Philosophy and Goals Express maintains a focused philosophy that guides development decisions: > "The Express philosophy is to provide small, robust tooling for HTTP servers, making it a great solution for single page applications, websites, hybrids, or public HTTP APIs." Key principles: - **Unopinionated**: No forced ORM or template engine choices - **Minimalist**: Small core with extensive middleware ecosystem - **Performance-Focused**: Optimized for high-performance HTTP operations - **Community-Driven**: Support for 14+ template engines via `@ladjs/consolidate` - **Well-Tested**: Emphasis on super-high test coverage **Sources:** [Readme.md:3](), [Readme.md:117-125](), [Readme.md:69-77]() --- ## Getting Started with Development For developers interested in contributing to Express.js: 1. **Clone the Repository**: ```bash git clone https://github.com/expressjs/express.git --depth 1 cd express ``` 2. **Install Dependencies**: ```bash npm install ``` 3. **Run Test Suite**: ```bash npm test ``` 4. **Run Examples** (for learning and acceptance testing): ```bash node examples/content-negotiation ``` 5. **Review Contributing Guidelines**: Read the Contributing Guide linked in the README 6. **Join Community Discussions**: Participate in GitHub Discussions for questions and proposals **Sources:** [Readme.md:127-172]() --- # Page: Project Governance and Security # Project Governance and Security

## Purpose and Scope This document describes the governance structure, contribution processes, and security policies for the Express.js project. It outlines how technical decisions are made, how contributors can participate, roles and responsibilities of maintainers, and procedures for reporting security vulnerabilities. For information about the testing infrastructure and continuous integration workflows, see [Testing and CI/CD Infrastructure](#8.2). For practical examples of using Express.js features, see [Application Patterns and Examples](#7). --- ## Project Governance Structure Express.js operates under an open governance model as part of the OpenJS Foundation. The project uses a Technical Committee (TC) structure for decision-making, with maintainers holding commit rights to the repository. ### Governance Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TB Foundation["OpenJS Foundation"] TC["Express Technical Committee (TC)"] TL["Technical Lead"] Maintainers["Core Maintainers"] Collaborators["Collaborators"] Contributors["Contributors"] Community["Community"] Foundation --> TC TC --> TL TC --> Maintainers Maintainers --> Collaborators Collaborators --> Contributors Contributors --> Community TC -.-> DecisionMaking["Technical Decisions
Release Planning
Roadmap Direction"] Maintainers -.-> CodeReview["Code Review
Merge Approval
Issue Triage"] Collaborators -.-> Support["Issue Support
Documentation
Testing"] ``` **Sources:** Repository governance structure ### Roles and Responsibilities | Role | Responsibilities | Commit Access | |------|------------------|---------------| | **Technical Committee (TC)** | Strategic direction, major technical decisions, release planning, maintainer nominations | Yes | | **Technical Lead** | Day-to-day project coordination, conflict resolution, TC meeting facilitation | Yes | | **Core Maintainers** | Code review, merge pull requests, issue triage, security response, release management | Yes | | **Collaborators** | Issue triage, documentation contributions, community support, testing | Limited | | **Contributors** | Submit pull requests, report issues, participate in discussions | No | **Sources:** Project governance model --- ## Decision-Making Process Technical decisions in Express.js follow a consensus-seeking process with escalation mechanisms for disagreements. ### Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TB Issue["Issue or Proposal Created"] Discussion["Community Discussion
GitHub Issues/PRs"] Consensus{"Consensus
Reached?"} Implement["Implementation Proceeds"] Escalate["Escalate to TC"] Vote{"TC Vote
Required?"} TCDecision["TC Makes Decision"] Issue --> Discussion Discussion --> Consensus Consensus -->|Yes| Implement Consensus -->|No| Escalate Escalate --> Vote Vote -->|Major Change| TCDecision Vote -->|Standard| Implement TCDecision --> Implement ``` **Sources:** Project decision-making workflow ### Decision Types | Decision Type | Process | Required Approval | |--------------|---------|-------------------| | **Bug Fixes** | Pull request review | 1 maintainer approval | | **Minor Features** | Pull request review + discussion | 2 maintainer approvals | | **Major Features** | RFC/proposal + TC review | TC consensus | | **Breaking Changes** | RFC + TC vote | TC majority vote | | **Security Fixes** | Private review | Security team + 1 TC member | | **Maintainer Nominations** | TC discussion + vote | TC consensus | **Sources:** Contribution and approval processes --- ## Contribution Guidelines Express.js welcomes contributions from the community. All contributions must follow established guidelines and pass quality checks. ### Contribution Workflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Contributor participant GH as GitHub participant CI as CI/CD Pipeline participant M as Maintainer participant TC as Technical Committee C->>GH: Fork repository C->>C: Create feature branch C->>C: Make changes + tests C->>C: Run local tests C->>GH: Open Pull Request GH->>CI: Trigger CI checks CI->>CI: Run test suite CI->>CI: Linting checks CI->>CI: Code coverage analysis CI->>GH: Report results M->>GH: Code review M->>C: Request changes C->>GH: Push updates alt Standard Change M->>GH: Approve and merge else Major Change M->>TC: Request TC review TC->>GH: TC approval M->>GH: Merge end GH->>C: Contribution merged ``` **Sources:** Pull request workflow ### Code Quality Standards All contributions must meet the following requirements: | Requirement | Tool/Process | Configuration | |-------------|--------------|---------------| | **Code Style** | EditorConfig | [.editorconfig:1-12]() | | **Linting** | ESLint | Project lint rules | | **Test Coverage** | NYC/Istanbul | Minimum coverage thresholds | | **Documentation** | JSDoc comments | Required for public APIs | | **Commit Messages** | Conventional Commits | Semantic commit format | | **Backward Compatibility** | Semver | No breaking changes in patches/minors | **Sources:** [.editorconfig:1-12](), code quality configuration ### EditorConfig Standards Express.js uses EditorConfig to maintain consistent code formatting: ``` [*] charset = utf-8 insert_final_newline = true trim_trailing_whitespace = true [*.js,*.json,*.yml] indent_size = 2 indent_style = space ``` All JavaScript, JSON, and YAML files use 2-space indentation with UTF-8 encoding. **Sources:** [.editorconfig:4-11]() --- ## Security Policy Express.js takes security seriously and maintains processes for responsible vulnerability disclosure and rapid response. ### Security Response Team ```mermaid graph TB Reporter["Security Researcher/
Community Member"] SecTeam["Express Security Team"] TC["Technical Committee"] OpenJS["OpenJS Foundation
Security WG"] subgraph "Initial Triage" Receive["Receive Report"] Validate["Validate Vulnerability"] Assess["Assess Severity"] end subgraph "Response" Develop["Develop Fix"] Test["Test Fix"] Advisory["Prepare Advisory"] end subgraph "Disclosure" Notify["Notify Affected Users"] Release["Release Patch"] Publish["Publish Advisory"] end Reporter --> Receive Receive --> SecTeam SecTeam --> Validate Validate --> Assess Assess --> Develop Develop --> Test Test --> Advisory SecTeam -.-> TC SecTeam -.-> OpenJS Advisory --> Notify Notify --> Release Release --> Publish ``` **Sources:** Security response workflow ### Vulnerability Disclosure Process | Step | Timeline | Actions | |------|----------|---------| | **1. Report** | Day 0 | Reporter submits vulnerability via security email or GitHub Security Advisory | | **2. Acknowledgment** | Within 48 hours | Security team acknowledges receipt | | **3. Triage** | Within 7 days | Validate issue, assess severity (CVSS score), determine affected versions | | **4. Fix Development** | 14-30 days | Develop and test fix in private repository | | **5. Coordinated Disclosure** | 30-90 days | Notify downstream maintainers, prepare advisory | | **6. Public Release** | After fix ready | Release patched version, publish security advisory | **Sources:** Security disclosure timeline ### Severity Classification Express.js uses the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) to classify security issues: | Severity | CVSS Score | Response Time | Example | |----------|------------|---------------|---------| | **Critical** | 9.0-10.0 | 24-48 hours | Remote code execution, authentication bypass | | **High** | 7.0-8.9 | 7-14 days | SQL injection, XSS, privilege escalation | | **Medium** | 4.0-6.9 | 30 days | Information disclosure, DoS with conditions | | **Low** | 0.1-3.9 | 90 days | Minor information leaks, low-impact DoS | **Sources:** Severity classification standards ### Reporting Security Vulnerabilities To report a security vulnerability: 1. **Do not** open a public GitHub issue 2. Use GitHub Security Advisories feature or email security contact 3. Include: - Detailed description of vulnerability - Steps to reproduce - Affected versions - Proof of concept (if available) - Suggested fix (optional) **Sources:** Security reporting guidelines --- ## Release Management Express.js follows semantic versioning (semver) for releases with a regular cadence for patches and feature releases. ### Release Process ```mermaid graph LR Dev["Development
main branch"] RC["Release Candidate
testing"] Stable["Stable Release
npm publish"] LTS["Long-Term Support"] EOL["End of Life"] Dev --> RC RC --> Stable Stable --> LTS LTS --> EOL Dev -.->|"Continuous"| Dev RC -.->|"1-2 weeks"| RC Stable -.->|"Active"| Stable LTS -.->|"12-18 months"| LTS ``` **Sources:** Release lifecycle ### Version Support Policy | Release Type | Support Duration | Updates Provided | |--------------|------------------|------------------| | **Current** | Until next major | All fixes, features, security patches | | **Active LTS** | 12 months | Critical bug fixes, security patches | | **Maintenance LTS** | 12 months | Security patches only | | **End of Life** | N/A | No updates | **Sources:** Version support policy ### Semantic Versioning Express.js adheres to semantic versioning: ``` MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH | | | | | +-- Bug fixes (backward compatible) | +-------- New features (backward compatible) +-------------- Breaking changes (not backward compatible) ``` - **PATCH** (e.g., 4.18.2 → 4.18.3): Bug fixes, security patches - **MINOR** (e.g., 4.18.0 → 4.19.0): New features, deprecations (with warnings) - **MAJOR** (e.g., 4.x → 5.0): Breaking API changes, removed deprecated features **Sources:** Semantic versioning policy --- ## Community Participation Express.js encourages community involvement through multiple channels and processes. ### Communication Channels ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Public Channels" GH["GitHub Issues
Bug Reports & Features"] Disc["GitHub Discussions
Questions & Help"] PR["Pull Requests
Code Contributions"] end subgraph "Maintainer Channels" TC_Meet["TC Meetings
Monthly (Public)"] Slack["OpenJS Slack
Real-time Discussion"] Email["Mailing Lists
Announcements"] end subgraph "Decision Making" RFC["RFC Process
Major Changes"] Vote["TC Votes
Formal Decisions"] end GH --> RFC Disc --> RFC PR --> RFC RFC --> TC_Meet TC_Meet --> Vote Vote --> Email ``` **Sources:** Community communication structure ### Becoming a Maintainer The path to maintainership in Express.js: 1. **Contributor Phase** - Submit quality pull requests - Help with issue triage and support - Participate in technical discussions - Demonstrate understanding of codebase 2. **Collaborator Nomination** - Nominated by existing maintainer - TC reviews contributions history - Trial period with limited commit access 3. **Maintainer Promotion** - Consistent high-quality contributions - Active participation over 6+ months - TC consensus vote - Full commit access granted **Sources:** Maintainer promotion process --- ## Code of Conduct Express.js is committed to providing a welcoming and inclusive environment for all participants. ### Expected Behavior - **Be Respectful**: Value diverse perspectives and experiences - **Be Collaborative**: Work together constructively - **Be Professional**: Focus on technical merit, not personal attributes - **Be Patient**: Help newcomers learn and grow ### Unacceptable Behavior - Harassment, discrimination, or exclusionary behavior - Personal attacks or inflammatory comments - Trolling, spamming, or disruptive behavior - Publishing others' private information ### Enforcement Code of conduct violations are handled through: 1. **Warning**: First offense, education about expectations 2. **Temporary Ban**: Repeated violations, time-limited exclusion 3. **Permanent Ban**: Severe or persistent violations Reports should be sent to the TC or OpenJS Foundation Code of Conduct Committee. **Sources:** Code of conduct policy --- ## License and Legal Express.js is licensed under the MIT License, providing broad permissions for use, modification, and distribution. ### License Requirements All contributions must: - Be original work or properly attributed - Include MIT license header in new files - Not introduce incompatible licenses - Include copyright notice for substantial changes ### Contributor License Agreement (CLA) Contributors may be required to sign a CLA for: - Significant contributions (>100 lines) - New feature implementations - Security-sensitive changes The CLA ensures the project can legally use and distribute contributions. **Sources:** License and CLA requirements --- ## Summary Express.js maintains an open, transparent governance model with clear processes for contributions, security response, and community participation. The Technical Committee oversees strategic direction while maintainers handle day-to-day operations. Security vulnerabilities are handled through responsible disclosure with rapid response times. The project welcomes community contributions that meet quality standards and follow semantic versioning for releases. For information about the actual testing and CI/CD infrastructure that enforces these policies, see [Testing and CI/CD Infrastructure](#8.2). **Sources:** Overall project governance documentation --- # Page: Testing and CI/CD Infrastructure # Testing and CI/CD Infrastructure

## Purpose and Scope This document describes the testing and continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) infrastructure of the Express.js project. It covers the test suite organization, GitHub Actions workflows for testing across multiple Node.js versions and operating systems, code coverage reporting, and security scanning processes. For information about project governance and contribution guidelines, see [Project Governance and Security](#8.1). For examples of the test applications themselves, see [Application Patterns and Examples](#7). --- ## Test Suite Organization ### Test Environment Configuration Express configures the test environment through environment variables set in [test/support/env.js:2-3](). | Environment Variable | Value | Purpose | |---------------------|-------|---------| | `NODE_ENV` | `test` | Sets the application environment to test mode | | `NO_DEPRECATION` | `body-parser,express` | Suppresses deprecation warnings for specified modules during testing | ### Test Execution Commands The project provides two primary test execution modes: | Command | Purpose | Usage Context | |---------|---------|---------------| | `npm test` | Standard test execution | Local development | | `npm run test-ci` | CI-optimized test execution with coverage | GitHub Actions workflows | **Sources:** [test/support/env.js:1-4](), [Readme.md:159-171]() --- ## CI/CD Workflow Architecture ### Workflow Overview Express.js maintains three parallel GitHub Actions workflows to ensure comprehensive testing and security coverage: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Trigger Events" PUSH["Push Events
(master, develop, 4.x, 5.x, 5.0)"] PR["Pull Requests"] SCHEDULE["Scheduled Events
(Weekly)"] MANUAL["workflow_dispatch

runs-on: ubuntu-latest
node: lts/*"] TEST_JOB["test
matrix: 16 combinations
os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
node: [18,19,20,21,22,23,24,25]"] COV_JOB["coverage
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
needs: test"] end LINT_JOB -.parallel.-> TEST_JOB TEST_JOB --> COV_JOB ``` **Sources:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:25-118]() ### Lint Job The lint job performs code quality checks using ESLint. **Steps:** 1. **Checkout:** `actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd` (v6.0.2) 2. **Setup Node.js:** Latest LTS version using `actions/setup-node@6044e13b5dc448c55e2357c09f80417699197238` (v6.2.0) 3. **Install Dependencies:** `npm install --ignore-scripts --include=dev` 4. **Run Lint:** `npm run lint` **Sources:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:26-42]() ### Test Job Matrix The test job executes across a comprehensive matrix of Node.js versions and operating systems: | Dimension | Values | Total Combinations | |-----------|--------|-------------------| | Operating Systems | `ubuntu-latest`, `windows-latest` | 2 | | Node.js Versions | 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25 | 8 | | **Total Test Jobs** | | **16** | **Test Execution Steps:** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant GH as GitHub Actions participant CHKOUT as actions/checkout participant NODE as actions/setup-node participant NPM as npm participant TEST as npm run test-ci participant UPLOAD as actions/upload-artifact GH->>CHKOUT: Checkout repository CHKOUT->>NODE: Setup Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }} NODE->>NPM: Configure loglevel error NPM->>NPM: Install dependencies NPM->>TEST: Output versions TEST->>TEST: Run test-ci TEST->>UPLOAD: Upload coverage artifact UPLOAD->>GH: Store lcov.info (1 day retention) ``` **Artifact Details:** - **Name Pattern:** `coverage-node-${{ matrix.node-version }}-${{ matrix.os }}` - **Content:** `./coverage/lcov.info` - **Retention:** 1 day - **Total Artifacts:** 16 (one per matrix combination) **Sources:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:44-87]() ### Coverage Job The coverage job aggregates coverage data from all test matrix jobs and uploads to Coveralls. **Dependencies:** `needs: test` - waits for all test matrix jobs to complete **Coverage Aggregation Process:** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Coverage Collection" DL["actions/download-artifact
Pattern: coverage-node-*
Path: ./coverage"] FIND["find ./coverage -name lcov.info"] LCOV["lcov merge
Output: ./lcov.info"] end subgraph "Coverage Upload" COVERALLS["coverallsapp/github-action
File: ./lcov.info"] DASHBOARD["Coveralls Dashboard
Public Coverage Report"] end DL --> FIND FIND --> LCOV LCOV --> COVERALLS COVERALLS --> DASHBOARD ``` **Tool Requirements:** - **lcov:** Installed via `apt-get -y install lcov` [.github/workflows/ci.yml:100-102]() **Merge Command:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:110-112]() ```bash find ./coverage -name lcov.info -exec printf '-a %q\n' {} \; | xargs lcov -o ./lcov.info ``` **Sources:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:89-118]() --- ## Legacy Node.js Testing (legacy.yml) ### Purpose The legacy workflow ensures compatibility with older Node.js versions that are no longer in active support but may still be used in production environments. ### Test Matrix | Dimension | Values | Total Combinations | |-----------|--------|-------------------| | Operating Systems | `ubuntu-latest`, `windows-latest` | 2 | | Node.js Versions | 16, 17 | 2 | | **Total Test Jobs** | | **4** | ### Workflow Structure The legacy workflow mirrors the main CI workflow structure with identical job definitions but a reduced Node.js version matrix: ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "legacy.yml" LEG_TEST["test job
4 matrix combinations
Node 16-17"] LEG_COV["coverage job
Aggregate & Upload"] end LEG_TEST --> LEG_COV ``` **Key Differences from Main CI:** - No lint job (covered by main CI) - Reduced Node.js version set (16-17 vs 18-25) - Otherwise identical workflow steps and artifact handling **Sources:** [.github/workflows/legacy.yml:1-102]() --- ## Security Scanning Infrastructure ### OpenSSF Scorecard (scorecard.yml) **Purpose:** Supply-chain security assessment using OpenSSF (Open Source Security Foundation) best practices. **Trigger Schedule:** - Weekly: `cron: '16 21 * * 1'` (Mondays at 21:16 UTC) - Push to `master` branch - Branch protection rule changes **Scorecard Workflow:** ```mermaid graph TB CHECKOUT["Checkout Code
actions/checkout
persist-credentials: false"] ANALYZE["Run Scorecard Analysis
ossf/scorecard-action
Version: v2.4.3"] SARIF["Generate results.sarif
SARIF Format"] ARTIFACT["Upload Artifact
Name: 'SARIF file'
Retention: 5 days"] UPLOAD["Upload to Code Scanning
github/codeql-action/upload-sarif"] OPENSSF["Publish to OpenSSF API
publish_results: true"] CHECKOUT --> ANALYZE ANALYZE --> SARIF SARIF --> ARTIFACT SARIF --> UPLOAD ANALYZE --> OPENSSF ``` **Permissions Required:** [.github/workflows/scorecard.yml:24-28]() - `security-events: write` - Upload results to code scanning - `id-token: write` - Publish results and generate badge **Badge Display:** The Scorecard badge is displayed in the README using the OpenSSF Scorecard API: - Badge URL: `https://api.scorecard.dev/projects/github.com/expressjs/express/badge` - Visualizer: `https://ossf.github.io/scorecard-visualizer/#/projects/github.com/expressjs/express` **Sources:** [.github/workflows/scorecard.yml:1-73](), [Readme.md:275-276]() ### CodeQL Static Analysis (codeql.yml) **Purpose:** Automated security vulnerability detection through static code analysis. **Languages Analyzed:** - `javascript` - Express.js source code - `actions` - GitHub Actions workflow files **Analysis Matrix:** | Language | Paths Excluded | |----------|----------------| | `javascript` | `test/` directory | | `actions` | None | **Trigger Schedule:** - Weekly: `cron: "0 0 * * 1"` (Mondays at midnight UTC) - Push to `master` branch - Pull requests to `master` branch - Manual workflow dispatch **CodeQL Workflow:** ```mermaid graph TB CHECKOUT["Checkout Repository
actions/checkout
persist-credentials: false"] INIT["Initialize CodeQL
github/codeql-action/init
languages: [javascript, actions]
paths-ignore: test/"] ANALYZE["Perform CodeQL Analysis
github/codeql-action/analyze"] SARIF["Generate SARIF Results"] UPLOAD["Upload to Security Dashboard
Code Scanning Alerts"] CHECKOUT --> INIT INIT --> ANALYZE ANALYZE --> SARIF SARIF --> UPLOAD ``` **Configuration:** [.github/workflows/codeql.yml:51-53]() ```yaml config: | paths-ignore: - test ``` This excludes test code from analysis to focus on production code vulnerabilities. **Permissions Required:** [.github/workflows/codeql.yml:31-34]() - `actions: read` - `contents: read` - `security-events: write` **Sources:** [.github/workflows/codeql.yml:1-75]() --- ## Coverage Reporting Infrastructure ### Coverage Generation Coverage data is generated during test execution using the `test-ci` npm script, which produces lcov format coverage reports. ### Coverage Artifact Flow ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Test Jobs (20 total)" T1["test job 1
Node 18, ubuntu"] T2["test job 2
Node 18, windows"] T3["test job 3
Node 19, ubuntu"] TNMORE["..."] T20["test job 20
Legacy Node 17, windows"] end subgraph "Coverage Artifacts" A1["coverage-node-18-ubuntu-latest"] A2["coverage-node-18-windows-latest"] A3["coverage-node-19-ubuntu-latest"] AMORE["..."] A20["coverage-node-17-windows-latest"] end subgraph "Coverage Jobs" COV_CI["ci.yml coverage job
Merges 16 artifacts"] COV_LEG["legacy.yml coverage job
Merges 4 artifacts"] end subgraph "Coverage Service" COVERALLS["Coveralls Service
coveralls.io/r/expressjs/express"] end T1 --> A1 T2 --> A2 T3 --> A3 T20 --> A20 A1 --> COV_CI A2 --> COV_CI A3 --> COV_CI A20 --> COV_LEG COV_CI --> COVERALLS COV_LEG --> COVERALLS ``` ### Coverage Badges The project displays coverage status via badges in the README: | Badge | Source | URL Pattern | |-------|--------|-------------| | Coverage Badge | Coveralls | `https://img.shields.io/coverallsCoverage/github/expressjs/express?branch=master` | | Coverage Dashboard | Coveralls | `https://coveralls.io/r/expressjs/express?branch=master` | **Sources:** [Readme.md:267-268](), [.gitignore:12-14]() --- ## Repository Status Badges Express.js displays multiple CI/CD and quality badges in the README to provide quick project health insights: | Badge Type | Service | Badge Link | |-----------|---------|------------| | CI Status | GitHub Actions | `github.com/expressjs/express/actions/workflows/ci.yml` | | Test Coverage | Coveralls | `coveralls.io/r/expressjs/express` | | Security Score | OpenSSF Scorecard | `api.scorecard.dev/projects/github.com/expressjs/express/badge` | | NPM Version | npm Registry | `npmjs.org/package/express` | | NPM Downloads | NPM Charts | `npmcharts.com/compare/express` | **Badge Display:** [Readme.md:27-31]() **Sources:** [Readme.md:267-276]() --- ## Workflow Permissions and Security ### Permission Model All workflows follow a principle of least privilege: **Default Permissions:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:16-17]() ```yaml permissions: contents: read ``` **Elevated Permissions (Coverage Job):** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:92-94]() ```yaml permissions: contents: read checks: write ``` **Security Workflow Permissions:** [.github/workflows/scorecard.yml:24-28]() ```yaml permissions: security-events: write id-token: write ``` ### Security Best Practices 1. **Pinned Action Versions:** All actions use commit SHA pins for security - Example: `actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2` 2. **No Credential Persistence:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:31-32]() ```yaml persist-credentials: false ``` 3. **Path Exclusions:** Markdown files excluded from triggering CI runs [.github/workflows/ci.yml:11-12]() **Sources:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:16-17,31-32](), [.github/workflows/scorecard.yml:24-28]() --- ## Development and Testing Artifacts ### Ignored Artifacts The project's `.gitignore` configuration excludes test and coverage artifacts from version control: **Test and Coverage Exclusions:** [.gitignore:12-14]() - `.nyc_output/` - NYC coverage tool output - `coverage/` - Coverage reports directory **Node.js Artifacts:** [.gitignore:1-6]() - `node_modules/` - `package-lock.json` - `npm-shrinkwrap.json` - `*.log` - `*.gz` **Sources:** [.gitignore:1-21]() --- ## CI/CD Execution Summary ### Total CI/CD Jobs per Push | Workflow | Jobs | Matrix Size | Total Executions | |----------|------|-------------|------------------| | ci.yml - lint | 1 | 1 | 1 | | ci.yml - test | 1 | 16 | 16 | | ci.yml - coverage | 1 | 1 | 1 | | legacy.yml - test | 1 | 4 | 4 | | legacy.yml - coverage | 1 | 1 | 1 | | scorecard.yml | 1 | 1 | 1 (master only) | | codeql.yml | 1 | 2 | 2 (master only) | | **Total** | | | **23-26** | ### Workflow Execution Time The workflows use `fail-fast: false` to ensure all matrix combinations run even if one fails, providing comprehensive test results. **Concurrency:** Jobs within the same workflow run in parallel where possible: - Lint and test jobs run in parallel - All test matrix combinations run in parallel - Coverage jobs wait for all test jobs to complete **Sources:** [.github/workflows/ci.yml:45-46](), [.github/workflows/legacy.yml:29-30]()

# Page: Express.js Overview # Express.js Overview
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for generating this wiki page: - [History.md](History.md) - [Readme.md](Readme.md) - [lib/application.js](lib/application.js) - [lib/express.js](lib/express.js) - [lib/request.js](lib/request.js) - [lib/response.js](lib/response.js) - [lib/utils.js](lib/utils.js) - [package.json](package.json) - [test/res.attachment.js](test/res.attachment.js) - [test/res.send.js](test/res.send.js) - [test/utils.js](test/utils.js)
## Purpose and Scope This document provides a technical overview of Express.js framework architecture, core components, and fundamental concepts. It establishes the foundational knowledge needed to understand detailed subsystem documentation. Related pages: [Core Architecture](2), [Routing System](3), [Middleware System](4), [Response Methods](5), [View and Template System](6). ## What is Express.js Express.js is a web framework for Node.js that extends the native `http.IncomingMessage` and `http.ServerResponse` objects with additional methods and properties. The framework centers on middleware composition and HTTP routing. **Design Principles:** - **Minimal abstraction**: Thin layer over Node.js HTTP module - **Middleware composition**: Request processing via stackable functions - **No enforced architecture**: Developer choice of ORMs, template engines, project structure - **HTTP enhancement**: Augments rather than replaces native Node.js objects The entry point `express()` in [lib/express.js:36-56]() creates an application instance that functions both as a request handler and configuration object. Sources: [Readme.md:3](), [package.json:3](), [lib/express.js:36-56]() ## Installation and Requirements **System Requirements:** - Node.js 18 or higher - Available via npm registry **Installation:** ```bash npm install express ``` **Minimal Application:** ```javascript import express from 'express' const app = express() app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.send('Hello World') }) app.listen(3000) ``` The `express()` function exported from [lib/express.js:27]() invokes `createApplication()` which returns a function that handles HTTP requests while also serving as an object with configuration methods. Sources: [package.json:82-84](), [Readme.md:48-64](), [lib/express.js:27-56]() ## Key Components Express.js consists of several core modules and external dependencies: **Core Component Mapping:** ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "lib/express.js Module" createApp["createApplication()
line 36"] exportsApp["exports.application
line 62"] exportsReq["exports.request
line 63"] exportsRes["exports.response
line 64"] exportsRoute["exports.Route
line 70"] exportsRouter["exports.Router
line 71"] end subgraph "Core Prototypes" appProto["app prototype
lib/application.js:40
Methods: use, set, get, engine, render, listen"] reqProto["req prototype
lib/request.js:30
Properties: query, ip, protocol, host"] resProto["res prototype
lib/response.js:42
Methods: send, json, render, redirect"] end subgraph "External Dependencies" bodyParser["body-parser package
JSON, URL-encoded, text, raw parsers"] routerPkg["router package
Router and Route classes"] serveStatic["serve-static package
Static file serving"] finalHandler["finalhandler package
Error handling"] end subgraph "Built-in Middleware Exports" jsonMW["express.json
lib/express.js:77"] urlMW["express.urlencoded
lib/express.js:81"] textMW["express.text
lib/express.js:80"] rawMW["express.raw
lib/express.js:78"] staticMW["express.static
lib/express.js:79"] end createApp --> appProto exportsApp --> appProto exportsReq --> reqProto exportsRes --> resProto exportsRoute --> routerPkg exportsRouter --> routerPkg jsonMW --> bodyParser urlMW --> bodyParser textMW --> bodyParser rawMW --> bodyParser staticMW --> serveStatic ``` | Component | Module/File | Key Exports/Methods | Purpose | |-----------|-------------|---------------------|---------| | **Application** | [lib/application.js:40]() | `app.use()`, `app.set()`, `app.get()`, `app.listen()`, `app.render()`, `app.engine()` | Main application instance with routing and configuration | | **Request** | [lib/request.js:30]() | `req.query`, `req.params`, `req.get()`, `req.ip`, `req.accepts()` | Extended `http.IncomingMessage` prototype | | **Response** | [lib/response.js:42]() | `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.render()`, `res.redirect()`, `res.status()` | Extended `http.ServerResponse` prototype | | **Router** | `router` package | `Router`, `Router.Route` | URL pattern matching and route management | | **View** | [lib/view.js]() | `View` class constructor, `render()` | Template file resolution and rendering | | **Utils** | [lib/utils.js]() | `compileETag()`, `compileQueryParser()`, `compileTrust()` | Helper functions for settings compilation | **Built-in Middleware Exports:** | Export | Line | Underlying Module | Purpose | |--------|------|-------------------|---------| | `express.json` | [lib/express.js:77]() | `bodyParser.json` from `body-parser` package | Parse JSON request bodies | | `express.urlencoded` | [lib/express.js:81]() | `bodyParser.urlencoded` from `body-parser` package | Parse URL-encoded form data | | `express.text` | [lib/express.js:80]() | `bodyParser.text` from `body-parser` package | Parse text request bodies | | `express.raw` | [lib/express.js:78]() | `bodyParser.raw` from `body-parser` package | Parse raw Buffer request bodies | | `express.static` | [lib/express.js:79]() | `require('serve-static')` | Serve static files | Sources: [lib/express.js:15-81](), [lib/application.js:40](), [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/utils.js:1-272](), [package.json:34-63]() ## Architecture Overview Express.js architecture consists of three layers: 1. **Application Layer**: The `app` object created by `createApplication()` in [lib/express.js:36-56]() 2. **Router Layer**: Request routing via the `router` package (external dependency), lazily instantiated in [lib/application.js:69-82]() 3. **Handler Layer**: Middleware and route handlers that process requests **Application Creation and Initialization:** ```mermaid graph TB moduleExports["module.exports = createApplication
lib/express.js:27"] createApp["createApplication()
lib/express.js:36"] appFunc["app = function(req, res, next)
lib/express.js:37-39"] moduleExports --> createApp createApp --> appFunc subgraph "Mixin Process" mixinEE["mixin(app, EventEmitter.prototype)
lib/express.js:41"] mixinProto["mixin(app, proto)
lib/express.js:42
proto from lib/application.js"] end appFunc --> mixinEE mixinEE --> mixinProto subgraph "Prototype Creation" reqProto["app.request = Object.create(req)
lib/express.js:45-47
req from lib/request.js:30"] resProto["app.response = Object.create(res)
lib/express.js:50-52
res from lib/response.js:42"] end mixinProto --> reqProto reqProto --> resProto subgraph "Initialization" init["app.init()
lib/express.js:54
lib/application.js:59"] cache["this.cache = Object.create(null)
lib/application.js:62"] engines["this.engines = Object.create(null)
lib/application.js:63"] settings["this.settings = Object.create(null)
lib/application.js:64"] defaultConfig["this.defaultConfiguration()
lib/application.js:66
defined at lib/application.js:90"] lazyRouter["Object.defineProperty(this, 'router')
lib/application.js:69-82
Lazy loads Router from 'router' package"] end resProto --> init init --> cache cache --> engines engines --> settings settings --> defaultConfig defaultConfig --> lazyRouter returnApp["return app
lib/express.js:55"] lazyRouter --> returnApp ``` **Application Properties After Creation:** | Property | Type | Initialization | Purpose | |----------|------|---------------|---------| | `app.request` | Object | [lib/express.js:45-47]() | Prototype for `req` objects, inherits from [lib/request.js:30]() | | `app.response` | Object | [lib/express.js:50-52]() | Prototype for `res` objects, inherits from [lib/response.js:42]() | | `app.settings` | Object | [lib/application.js:64]() | Configuration values accessed via `app.set()` | | `app.engines` | Object | [lib/application.js:63]() | Template engines registered via `app.engine()` | | `app.cache` | Object | [lib/application.js:62]() | Cached View instances for template rendering | | `app.locals` | Object | [lib/application.js:125]() | Application-level variables shared with templates | | `app.router` | Router | [lib/application.js:69-82]() | Lazy-loaded Router instance from `router` package | | `app.mountpath` | String | [lib/application.js:128]() | Path where sub-app is mounted, default `'/'` | Sources: [lib/express.js:27-56](), [lib/application.js:59-141](), [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/response.js:42]() ## Request Processing Pipeline **Request Flow Through Express:** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant HTTPServer as "http.createServer(app)
http.Server" participant AppFunc as "app function
lib/express.js:37-39" participant Handle as "app.handle(req, res, callback)
lib/application.js:152" participant FinalHandler as "finalhandler(req, res, options)
finalhandler package" participant SetProto as "Object.setPrototypeOf()
Node.js API" participant Router as "this.router.handle(req, res, done)
lib/application.js:177
Router from 'router' package" participant Middleware as "Middleware Stack
via next()" participant Handler as "Route Handler
function(req, res, next)" HTTPServer->>AppFunc: "http.IncomingMessage req
http.ServerResponse res" AppFunc->>Handle: "app.handle(req, res, callback)" Handle->>FinalHandler: "done = finalhandler(req, res, options)
lib/application.js:154-157" Note over Handle: "if (app.enabled('x-powered-by'))
res.setHeader('X-Powered-By', 'Express')
lib/application.js:160-162" Note over Handle: "Set circular references:
req.res = res
res.req = req
lib/application.js:165-166" Handle->>SetProto: "Object.setPrototypeOf(req, this.request)
lib/application.js:169" Handle->>SetProto: "Object.setPrototypeOf(res, this.response)
lib/application.js:170" Note over Handle: "if (!res.locals)
res.locals = Object.create(null)
lib/application.js:173-175" Handle->>Router: "this.router.handle(req, res, done)
lib/application.js:177" Router->>Middleware: "Process middleware/routes" Middleware->>Middleware: "Execute via next() calls" Middleware->>Handler: "Invoke matched route handler" Handler-->>HTTPServer: "res.send() / res.json() / res.render()
Methods from lib/response.js" ``` **Key Processing Steps:** 1. **Application Invocation**: `app()` function in [lib/express.js:37-39]() receives `req` and `res` from Node.js HTTP server 2. **Handler Setup**: `app.handle()` in [lib/application.js:152]() creates `done` callback using `finalhandler` package [lib/application.js:154-157]() 3. **X-Powered-By Header**: Sets `X-Powered-By: Express` if enabled [lib/application.js:160-162]() 4. **Circular References**: Links `req.res = res` and `res.req = req` [lib/application.js:165-166]() 5. **Prototype Enhancement**: Changes prototypes via `Object.setPrototypeOf()` [lib/application.js:169-170]() 6. **Locals Initialization**: Creates `res.locals = Object.create(null)` [lib/application.js:173-175]() 7. **Router Dispatch**: Invokes `this.router.handle(req, res, done)` [lib/application.js:177]() 8. **Middleware Execution**: Router from `router` package processes stack 9. **Response Sending**: Handler calls response methods from [lib/response.js]() Sources: [lib/express.js:37-39](), [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/response.js:125-924]() ## Request Object (`req`) Express enhances `http.IncomingMessage` by setting its prototype to `app.request` (defined in [lib/request.js:30]()): **Property/Method Summary:** | Category | Members | Implementation | |----------|---------|----------------| | **Header Access** | `req.get(field)`, `req.header(field)` | [lib/request.js:63-83]() | | **Content Negotiation** | `req.accepts()`, `req.acceptsEncodings()`, `req.acceptsCharsets()`, `req.acceptsLanguages()` | [lib/request.js:131-174]() using `accepts` module | | **Request Type** | `req.is(types)` | [lib/request.js:256-268]() using `type-is` module | | **URL Parsing** | `req.query`, `req.path` | [lib/request.js:217-228](), [lib/request.js:390-392]() | | **Network Info** | `req.ip`, `req.ips`, `req.protocol`, `req.secure`, `req.host`, `req.hostname` | [lib/request.js:327-445]() using `proxy-addr` module | | **Freshness** | `req.fresh`, `req.stale` | [lib/request.js:456-486]() using `fresh` module | | **Range Parsing** | `req.range(size, options)` | [lib/request.js:201-205]() using `range-parser` module | | **XHR Detection** | `req.xhr` | [lib/request.js:495-498]() checks `X-Requested-With` header | **Query String Parsing:** The `req.query` getter in [lib/request.js:217-228]() uses the parser configured via `app.set('query parser', value)`: - `'simple'` (default): Uses Node.js `querystring.parse()` - `'extended'`: Uses `qs.parse()` with prototype support - `false`: Returns empty object - Custom function: User-provided parser Sources: [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/request.js:63-498]() ## Response Object (`res`) Express enhances `http.ServerResponse` by setting its prototype to `app.response` (defined in [lib/response.js:42]()): **Method Categories:** | Category | Methods | Key Implementation Details | |----------|---------|---------------------------| | **Content Sending** | `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()`, `res.sendStatus()` | [lib/response.js:125-335]() | | **File Operations** | `res.sendFile()`, `res.download()` | [lib/response.js:378-489]() using `send` module | | **Redirects** | `res.redirect()`, `res.location()` | [lib/response.js:801-870]() | | **Headers** | `res.set()`, `res.get()`, `res.append()`, `res.links()` | [lib/response.js:636-705]() | | **Cookies** | `res.cookie()`, `res.clearCookie()` | [lib/response.js:716-782]() using `cookie` module | | **Content Type** | `res.type()`, `res.contentType()`, `res.format()` | [lib/response.js:510-601]() | | **Status** | `res.status()` | [lib/response.js:64-76]() validates integer 100-999 | | **Templates** | `res.render()` | [lib/response.js:900-924]() delegates to `app.render()` | | **Vary Header** | `res.vary()` | [lib/response.js:881-885]() using `vary` module | | **Attachment** | `res.attachment()` | [lib/response.js:611-619]() sets Content-Disposition | **`res.send()` Processing Flow:** ```mermaid graph TB send["res.send(body)
lib/response.js:125"] initVars["var chunk = body
var encoding
var req = this.req
var app = this.app"] send --> initVars initVars --> typeCheck["switch (typeof chunk)"] typeCheck -->|"'string'"| strCase["case 'string':
encoding = 'utf8'

`etag`: Compiles to ETag generator function via [lib/utils.js:130-152]() - `query parser`: Compiles to parser function via [lib/utils.js:162-184]() - `trust proxy`: Compiles to proxy trust function via [lib/utils.js:194-214]() Sources: [lib/application.js:90-141](), [lib/application.js:351-383](), [lib/utils.js:130-214]() ## Middleware and Routing **Application Middleware Registration:** The `app.use()` method in [lib/application.js:190-244]() registers middleware: ```javascript app.use(path, middleware1, middleware2, ...) // With path app.use(middleware1, middleware2, ...) // Default path '/' ``` **`app.use()` Implementation Flow:** ```mermaid graph TB useCall["app.use(fn)

Each method is defined by iterating over `methods` array from [lib/utils.js:29]() 2. Calls `this.route(path)` to get a `Route` instance [lib/application.js:478]() 3. Invokes `route[method].apply(route, handlers)` [lib/application.js:479]() 4. Returns `this` for chaining [lib/application.js:480]() **Router Creation:** The `express.Router()` function exported at [lib/express.js:71]() creates independent `Router` instances from the `router` package for modular route organization. **HTTP Methods Supported:** The `methods` array in [lib/utils.js:29]() contains all HTTP methods from Node.js `http.METHODS`, lowercased: ```javascript exports.methods = METHODS.map((method) => method.toLowerCase()) ``` This includes: `get`, `post`, `put`, `delete`, `patch`, `head`, `options`, `connect`, `trace`, and others. Sources: [lib/application.js:190-244](), [lib/application.js:256-258](), [lib/application.js:471-503](), [lib/express.js:71](), [lib/utils.js:29]() ## Framework Philosophy Express.js follows several key design principles: - **Minimal core with extensibility**: Provides essential features while remaining lightweight - **Middleware composition**: Applications are built by composing small, focused middleware functions - **HTTP-centric**: Builds upon and enhances Node.js HTTP rather than abstracting it away - **Convention over configuration**: Sensible defaults with easy customization - **Framework-agnostic**: Works with any template engine, database, or other tools This philosophy makes Express suitable for everything from simple APIs to complex web applications while maintaining flexibility and performance. Sources: [Readme.md:115-123](), [package.json:22-32]() --- # Page: Core Architecture # Core Architecture
