This document covers the foundational components of Express.js: the application factory, request/response prototype objects, and core utility functions. It explains how Express applications are created, how prototypes are structured, and how the framework sets up the basic request-response infrastructure. For information about the routing system, see [Routing System](#3). For detailed documentation of individual response methods, see [Response Methods](#5). For view rendering, see [View and Template System](#6). --- ## Application Factory and Module Structure Express uses a factory pattern to create application instances. The main entry point is the `createApplication` function exported from [lib/express.js](). When you call `express()`, it returns a function that is also an EventEmitter with application methods mixed in. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Module Entry Point" EXPORT["module.exports = createApplication"] CREATE["createApplication()"] end subgraph "Factory Process" APPFN["app = function(req, res, next)"] MIXIN1["mixin(app, EventEmitter.prototype)"] MIXIN2["mixin(app, proto)"] INIT["app.init()"] end subgraph "Prototypes Created" REQPROTO["app.request = Object.create(req)"] RESPROTO["app.response = Object.create(res)"] end subgraph "Exports" EXP_APP["exports.application"] EXP_REQ["exports.request"] EXP_RES["exports.response"] EXP_ROUTER["exports.Router"] EXP_JSON["exports.json"] EXP_URLENCODED["exports.urlencoded"] EXP_STATIC["exports.static"] EXP_TEXT["exports.text"] EXP_RAW["exports.raw"] end EXPORT --> CREATE CREATE --> APPFN APPFN --> MIXIN1 MIXIN1 --> MIXIN2 MIXIN2 --> REQPROTO REQPROTO --> RESPROTO RESPROTO --> INIT INIT --> RETURN["return app"] CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_APP CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_REQ CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_RES CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_ROUTER CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_JSON CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_URLENCODED CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_STATIC CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_TEXT CREATE -.exposes.-> EXP_RAW ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:1-82]() --- ## The Three Core Prototypes Express is built around three prototype objects that define the behavior of applications, requests, and responses. These prototypes are created once per application instance and can be extended globally or per-instance. ### Application Prototype Structure The application prototype is defined in [lib/application.js]() and exported as `module.exports`. It contains all `app.*` methods. | Method Category | Methods | Purpose | |----------------|---------|---------| | **Initialization** | `init`, `defaultConfiguration` | Set up internal state and default settings | | **Settings** | `set`, `get`, `enable`, `disable`, `enabled`, `disabled` | Manage configuration values | | **Routing** | `use`, `route`, `all`, `get`, `post`, `put`, `delete`, etc. | Register middleware and routes | | **Template Engines** | `engine`, `render` | Configure and use view engines | | **Parameters** | `param` | Define parameter preprocessing callbacks | | **Server** | `listen`, `handle` | Create HTTP server and handle requests | | **Utility** | `path` | Get application mount path | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:40-632]() ### Request Prototype Structure The request prototype extends `http.IncomingMessage.prototype` and adds Express-specific properties and methods. ```mermaid graph TB HTTP_INCOMING["http.IncomingMessage.prototype"] REQ_PROTO["req = Object.create(http.IncomingMessage.prototype)"] HTTP_INCOMING --> REQ_PROTO subgraph "Methods" GET["req.get(field)"] ACCEPTS["req.accepts(types)"] IS["req.is(type)"] RANGE["req.range(size)"] end subgraph "Getters (Lazy)" QUERY["req.query"] PROTOCOL["req.protocol"] SECURE["req.secure"] IP["req.ip"] IPS["req.ips"] HOST["req.host"] HOSTNAME["req.hostname"] SUBDOMAINS["req.subdomains"] PATH["req.path"] FRESH["req.fresh"] STALE["req.stale"] XHR["req.xhr"] end REQ_PROTO --> GET REQ_PROTO --> ACCEPTS REQ_PROTO --> IS REQ_PROTO --> RANGE REQ_PROTO -.defines.-> QUERY REQ_PROTO -.defines.-> PROTOCOL REQ_PROTO -.defines.-> IP REQ_PROTO -.defines.-> HOST ``` All getters are defined using `defineGetter(obj, name, getter)` which creates computed properties that execute on access rather than storing values. **Sources:** [lib/request.js:30-528]() ### Response Prototype Structure The response prototype extends `http.ServerResponse.prototype` and provides methods for sending various types of responses. | Method Category | Methods | Purpose | |----------------|---------|---------| | **Status** | `status`, `sendStatus` | Set HTTP status code | | **Content Sending** | `send`, `json`, `jsonp` | Send response body | | **Files** | `sendFile`, `download` | Transfer files | | **Content Type** | `type`, `contentType`, `format` | Set and negotiate content types | | **Headers** | `set`, `header`, `get`, `append`, `vary` | Manage response headers | | **Cookies** | `cookie`, `clearCookie` | Manage cookies | | **Redirection** | `redirect`, `location` | Redirect requests | | **Rendering** | `render` | Render templates | | **Misc** | `links`, `attachment` | Set Link header, attachment disposition | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:42-1048]() --- ## Application Initialization Sequence When `createApplication()` is called, a specific initialization sequence occurs to set up the application with default configuration. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User participant createApplication participant app participant proto participant EventEmitter User->>createApplication: express() createApplication->>app: Create function(req, res, next) createApplication->>app: mixin(app, EventEmitter.prototype) createApplication->>app: mixin(app, proto) createApplication->>app: Create app.request prototype Note over app: app.request = Object.create(req, {app: ...}) createApplication->>app: Create app.response prototype Note over app: app.response = Object.create(res, {app: ...}) createApplication->>app: app.init() app->>app: Initialize internal objects Note over app: this.cache = Object.create(null)
this.engines = Object.create(null)
this.settings = Object.create(null) app->>app: defaultConfiguration() Note over app: Set default settings:
- etag: 'weak'
- query parser: 'simple'
- trust proxy: false
- subdomain offset: 2 app->>app: Define lazy router getter Note over app: Router created on first access app->>app: Set up 'mount' event handler Note over app: Inherit prototypes from parent app->>app: Initialize app.locals createApplication-->>User: return app ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/application.js:59-141]() ### Default Settings The following settings are configured during initialization: | Setting | Default Value | Compiled To | |---------|--------------|-------------| | `etag` | `'weak'` | `etag fn` (function) via `compileETag` | | `env` | `process.env.NODE_ENV \|\| 'development'` | - | | `query parser` | `'simple'` | `query parser fn` via `compileQueryParser` | | `subdomain offset` | `2` | - | | `trust proxy` | `false` | `trust proxy fn` via `compileTrust` | | `view` | `View` class | - | | `views` | `resolve('views')` | - | | `jsonp callback name` | `'callback'` | - | | `x-powered-by` | enabled | - | | `view cache` | enabled in production | - | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:90-141]() --- ## Request-Response Lifecycle The core request processing flow involves prototype extension and circular references to enable method access between `req` and `res` objects. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Server as http.Server participant app as app.handle participant req as req (IncomingMessage) participant res as res (ServerResponse) participant router as app.router Server->>app: (req, res) Note over app: Set circular references app->>req: req.res = res app->>res: res.req = req Note over app: Extend prototypes app->>req: Object.setPrototypeOf(req, app.request) app->>res: Object.setPrototypeOf(res, app.response) Note over app: Initialize locals app->>res: res.locals = Object.create(null) Note over app: Set X-Powered-By header (if enabled) app->>res: res.setHeader('X-Powered-By', 'Express') Note over app: Delegate to router app->>router: router.handle(req, res, done) Note over router: Process middleware and routes router-->>app: done() or next(err) app-->>Server: Response sent or error handled ``` The `app.handle` method ([lib/application.js:152-178]()) performs several critical setup steps: 1. **Circular References**: Sets `req.res = res` and `res.req = req` so methods can access the paired object 2. **Prototype Extension**: Uses `Object.setPrototypeOf` to give req/res access to Express methods 3. **Locals Initialization**: Creates `res.locals` object for view rendering 4. **Header Setting**: Adds `X-Powered-By` header if enabled 5. **Router Delegation**: Calls `this.router.handle(req, res, done)` to process middleware **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178]() --- ## Core Utility Functions The [lib/utils.js]() module provides compilation and normalization functions used throughout Express. ### Compiler Functions These functions convert configuration values into executable functions: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "compileETag(val)" ETAG_IN["Input: true/'weak'/'strong'/false/function"] ETAG_OUT["Output: function(body, encoding) or undefined"] end subgraph "compileQueryParser(val)" QUERY_IN["Input: true/'simple'/'extended'/false/function"] QUERY_OUT["Output: function(querystring) or undefined"] end subgraph "compileTrust(val)" TRUST_IN["Input: true/false/number/string/array/function"] TRUST_OUT["Output: function(addr, i)"] end ETAG_IN --> ETAG_LOGIC{"Type?"} ETAG_LOGIC -->|"'weak' or true"| WETAG["utils.wetag"] ETAG_LOGIC -->|"'strong'"| STRONGETAG["utils.etag"] ETAG_LOGIC -->|"false"| NOETAG["undefined"] ETAG_LOGIC -->|"function"| ETAG_OUT WETAG --> ETAG_OUT STRONGETAG --> ETAG_OUT NOETAG --> ETAG_OUT QUERY_IN --> QUERY_LOGIC{"Type?"} QUERY_LOGIC -->|"'simple' or true"| SIMPLE["querystring.parse"] QUERY_LOGIC -->|"'extended'"| EXTENDED["parseExtendedQueryString (qs.parse)"] QUERY_LOGIC -->|"false"| NOQUERY["undefined"] QUERY_LOGIC -->|"function"| QUERY_OUT SIMPLE --> QUERY_OUT EXTENDED --> QUERY_OUT NOQUERY --> QUERY_OUT TRUST_IN --> TRUST_LOGIC{"Type?"} TRUST_LOGIC -->|"true"| TRUSTALL["() => true"] TRUST_LOGIC -->|"number"| TRUSTHOP["(a, i) => i < val"] TRUST_LOGIC -->|"string/array"| TRUSTLIST["proxyaddr.compile(val)"] TRUST_LOGIC -->|"function"| TRUST_OUT TRUSTALL --> TRUST_OUT TRUSTHOP --> TRUST_OUT TRUSTLIST --> TRUST_OUT ``` **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:130-214]() ### ETag Generation Express provides two ETag generator functions: - `utils.etag(body, encoding)` - Generates strong ETags (format: `"length-hash"`) - `utils.wetag(body, encoding)` - Generates weak ETags (format: `W/"length-hash"`) Both use the `etag` package internally with different options. **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:40-51](), [lib/utils.js:249-257]() ### Type Normalization | Function | Input | Output | Purpose | |----------|-------|--------|---------| | `normalizeType(type)` | `'html'` or `'text/html'` | `{value: 'text/html', params: {}}` | Convert extension to MIME type | | `normalizeTypes(types)` | Array of types | Array of normalized types | Batch normalize | | `setCharset(type, charset)` | `'text/html'`, `'utf-8'` | `'text/html; charset=utf-8'` | Add/update charset parameter | **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:61-238]() --- ## Settings Compilation and Application When certain settings are changed via `app.set()`, Express automatically compiles them into optimized functions. This compilation happens in [lib/application.js:351-383](). ```mermaid graph LR SET["app.set(setting, val)"] SET --> SWITCH{"Switch on setting"} SWITCH -->|"'etag'"| COMPILE_ETAG["compileETag(val)"] SWITCH -->|"'query parser'"| COMPILE_QUERY["compileQueryParser(val)"] SWITCH -->|"'trust proxy'"| COMPILE_TRUST["compileTrust(val)"] COMPILE_ETAG --> SET_ETAG["app.set('etag fn', fn)"] COMPILE_QUERY --> SET_QUERY["app.set('query parser fn', fn)"] COMPILE_TRUST --> SET_TRUST["app.set('trust proxy fn', fn)"] SET_ETAG --> STORAGE["Store in app.settings"] SET_QUERY --> STORAGE SET_TRUST --> STORAGE ``` This allows Express to validate configuration at set-time and store optimized functions for runtime use. For example: - `app.set('etag', 'weak')` compiles to `app.settings['etag fn'] = utils.wetag` - `app.set('query parser', 'extended')` compiles to a function that calls `qs.parse` with specific options - `app.set('trust proxy', '127.0.0.1')` compiles to a function that checks if the IP matches **Sources:** [lib/application.js:351-383]() --- ## Lazy Router Initialization Express uses a getter to lazily create the router instance. The router is not created until it is first accessed, which happens when: - `app.use()` is called - Any HTTP method function is called (`app.get()`, `app.post()`, etc.) - `app.route()` is called ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Code participant app participant RouterGetter participant Router Code->>app: app.use('/path', middleware) app->>RouterGetter: Access this.router alt Router is null RouterGetter->>Router: new Router({caseSensitive, strict}) Router-->>RouterGetter: router instance RouterGetter->>RouterGetter: Store router reference end RouterGetter-->>app: return router app->>Router: router.use('/path', middleware) ``` The getter is defined in [lib/application.js:69-82](). It reads the `case sensitive routing` and `strict routing` settings to configure the router appropriately. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:59-83]() --- ## Prototype Chain and Inheritance Express uses prototype inheritance to share methods across all instances and to support sub-application mounting. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Global Prototypes" PROTO_APP["application.js (exports)"] PROTO_REQ["request.js (exports)"] PROTO_RES["response.js (exports)"] end subgraph "Application Instance" APP["app function + EventEmitter"] APP_REQ["app.request"] APP_RES["app.response"] end subgraph "Node.js Prototypes" HTTP_IN["http.IncomingMessage.prototype"] HTTP_RES["http.ServerResponse.prototype"] EE["EventEmitter.prototype"] end subgraph "Runtime Objects" REQ["req (per request)"] RES["res (per request)"] end EE -->|"mixin"| APP PROTO_APP -->|"mixin"| APP HTTP_IN --> PROTO_REQ PROTO_REQ -->|"Object.create"| APP_REQ APP_REQ -->|"Object.setPrototypeOf"| REQ HTTP_RES --> PROTO_RES PROTO_RES -->|"Object.create"| APP_RES APP_RES -->|"Object.setPrototypeOf"| RES ``` ### Sub-Application Mounting When a sub-application is mounted, it inherits prototypes from the parent application via the `'mount'` event handler: ```javascript // lib/application.js:109-122 this.on('mount', function onmount(parent) { // inherit trust proxy if (this.settings[trustProxyDefaultSymbol] === true && typeof parent.settings['trust proxy fn'] === 'function') { delete this.settings['trust proxy']; delete this.settings['trust proxy fn']; } // inherit protos Object.setPrototypeOf(this.request, parent.request) Object.setPrototypeOf(this.response, parent.response) Object.setPrototypeOf(this.engines, parent.engines) Object.setPrototypeOf(this.settings, parent.settings) }); ``` This creates a prototype chain where sub-apps inherit settings and extensions from parent apps. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:109-122](), [lib/express.js:36-56]() --- ## Architecture Summary The Express core architecture consists of: 1. **Factory Pattern**: `createApplication()` creates application instances that are functions with mixed-in methods 2. **Three Prototypes**: Separate prototype objects for applications, requests, and responses that can be extended globally 3. **Lazy Initialization**: Router is created on first use to avoid unnecessary overhead 4. **Compiled Settings**: Configuration values are compiled into optimized functions at set-time 5. **Prototype Extension**: Runtime `req` and `res` objects have their prototypes swapped to gain Express methods 6. **Circular References**: `req.res` and `res.req` enable bidirectional access 7. **Inheritance Chain**: Sub-applications inherit from parent applications via prototype chains This architecture provides: - **Performance**: Methods are shared via prototypes, not copied per-instance - **Extensibility**: Global and per-instance prototype modification - **Flexibility**: Settings can be functions, strings, or booleans and are compiled appropriately - **Modularity**: Clean separation between core objects and routing/middleware systems **Sources:** [lib/express.js:1-82](), [lib/application.js:1-632](), [lib/request.js:1-528](), [lib/response.js:1-1048](), [lib/utils.js:1-272]() --- # Page: Application Instance # Application Instance

## Purpose and Scope This document describes the Express application instance (`app`), which is the primary interface for building web applications with Express. It covers how application instances are created, initialized, configured, and used to handle HTTP requests. For information about the request and response objects that flow through the application, see [Request Object](#2.2) and [Response Object](#2.3). For details on the complete request-response lifecycle, see [Request-Response Lifecycle](#2.4). For application configuration details, see [Settings and Utilities](#2.5). --- ## Application Creation ### The `createApplication()` Factory Express applications are created by calling the default export function from the Express module. This invokes the `createApplication()` factory function, which returns an application instance. ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); ``` The `createApplication()` function is defined in [lib/express.js:36-56]() and performs the following initialization sequence: **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56]() ```mermaid graph TB START["express()"] --> CREATE["createApplication()"] CREATE --> FUNC["Create app function
function(req, res, next)"] FUNC --> MIXIN1["mixin(app, EventEmitter.prototype)"] MIXIN1 --> MIXIN2["mixin(app, proto)"] MIXIN2 --> REQ["Create app.request prototype"] REQ --> RES["Create app.response prototype"] RES --> INIT["app.init()"] INIT --> RETURN["Return app instance"] MIXIN2 -.adds.-> METHODS["app.use()
app.set()
app.engine()
app.listen()
app.get/post/etc()"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/application.js:40]() ### Application Structure The application instance has a unique dual nature: it is both a function and an object with methods. | Aspect | Description | Source | |--------|-------------|--------| | **Function** | `app(req, res, next)` delegates to `app.handle()` | [lib/express.js:37-39]() | | **EventEmitter** | Inherits from `EventEmitter.prototype` | [lib/express.js:41]() | | **Application Methods** | Inherits from application prototype | [lib/express.js:42]() | | **Request Prototype** | `app.request` extends base request | [lib/express.js:45-47]() | | **Response Prototype** | `app.response` extends base response | [lib/express.js:50-52]() | **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/application.js:40]() ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "app Instance" APPFN["app
(Function)"] APPOBJ["app
(Object)"] end APPFN --> HANDLE["Delegates to
app.handle()"] APPOBJ --> EE["EventEmitter.prototype
(emit, on, once)"] APPOBJ --> PROTO["application prototype
(use, set, engine, etc)"] APPOBJ --> REQUEST["app.request
(extends req prototype)"] APPOBJ --> RESPONSE["app.response
(extends res prototype)"] APPOBJ --> SETTINGS["app.settings
(configuration)"] APPOBJ --> LOCALS["app.locals
(app-wide variables)"] APPOBJ --> CACHE["app.cache
(view cache)"] APPOBJ --> ENGINES["app.engines
(template engines)"] APPOBJ --> ROUTER["app.router
(lazy-loaded Router)"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/application.js:59-83]() --- ## Initialization ### The `app.init()` Method After creation, `app.init()` is automatically called to set up internal structures and default configuration. ```mermaid graph TB INIT["app.init()"] --> CACHE["this.cache = Object.create(null)"] CACHE --> ENGINES["this.engines = Object.create(null)"] ENGINES --> SETTINGS["this.settings = Object.create(null)"] SETTINGS --> DEFCONF["this.defaultConfiguration()"] DEFCONF --> ROUTER["Define lazy router getter"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> ENABLE1["enable('x-powered-by')"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> SET1["set('etag', 'weak')"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> SET2["set('env', NODE_ENV)"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> SET3["set('query parser', 'simple')"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> SET4["set('subdomain offset', 2)"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> SET5["set('trust proxy', false)"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> LOCALS["this.locals = {}"] DEFCONF -.calls.-> MOUNT["Setup mount event handler"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/application.js:90-141]() ### Default Configuration The `defaultConfiguration()` method establishes default settings: | Setting | Default Value | Purpose | |---------|---------------|---------| | `x-powered-by` | enabled | Whether to send `X-Powered-By: Express` header | | `etag` | `'weak'` | ETag generation strategy | | `env` | `process.env.NODE_ENV \|\| 'development'` | Application environment | | `query parser` | `'simple'` | Query string parser mode | | `subdomain offset` | `2` | Number of domain parts to remove to access subdomains | | `trust proxy` | `false` | Proxy trust configuration | | `view` | `View` class | View class constructor | | `views` | `'./views'` | Directory for view templates | | `jsonp callback name` | `'callback'` | Default JSONP callback parameter name | | `view cache` | enabled in production | Whether to cache compiled view templates | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:90-141]() ### Lazy Router Initialization The router is not created immediately but is lazily initialized on first access: ```javascript Object.defineProperty(this, 'router', { get: function getrouter() { if (router === null) { router = new Router({ caseSensitive: this.enabled('case sensitive routing'), strict: this.enabled('strict routing') }); } return router; } }); ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:69-82]() --- ## Core Methods ### Settings Management #### `app.set(name, value)` / `app.get(name)` The `set()` method assigns settings, while `get()` retrieves them. Some settings trigger compilation of utility functions when set. ```javascript app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); app.set('port', 3000); const port = app.get('port'); // 3000 ``` | Special Setting | Triggers | Compiled To | |----------------|----------|-------------| | `etag` | `compileETag(val)` | `etag fn` | | `query parser` | `compileQueryParser(val)` | `query parser fn` | | `trust proxy` | `compileTrust(val)` | `trust proxy fn` | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:351-383]() ```mermaid graph TB SET["app.set(setting, val)"] --> CHECK{"arguments.length === 1?"} CHECK -->|Yes| GET["Return this.settings[setting]"] CHECK -->|No| STORE["this.settings[setting] = val"] STORE --> SWITCH{"Special setting?"} SWITCH -->|etag| ETAG["set('etag fn', compileETag(val))"] SWITCH -->|query parser| QP["set('query parser fn',
compileQueryParser(val))"] SWITCH -->|trust proxy| TP["set('trust proxy fn',
compileTrust(val))"] SWITCH -->|other| RETURN["Return this"] ETAG --> RETURN QP --> RETURN TP --> RETURN ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:351-383](), [lib/utils.js:130-152](), [lib/utils.js:162-184](), [lib/utils.js:194-214]() #### `app.enable(name)` / `app.disable(name)` Convenience methods for boolean settings: ```javascript app.enable('trust proxy'); // app.set('trust proxy', true) app.disable('view cache'); // app.set('view cache', false) ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:451-465]() #### `app.enabled(name)` / `app.disabled(name)` Check if a setting is enabled or disabled: ```javascript if (app.enabled('trust proxy')) { // use proxy headers } ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:420-441]() ### Middleware and Routing #### `app.use([path], ...middleware)` Mounts middleware functions or sub-applications at an optional path: ```javascript // Mount middleware at root app.use(express.json()); // Mount at specific path app.use('/api', apiRouter); // Mount multiple middleware app.use(middleware1, middleware2); ``` The `use()` method flattens arrays and delegates to the router, with special handling for sub-applications: **Sources:** [lib/application.js:190-244]() ```mermaid graph TB USE["app.use(fn)"] --> PARSE["Parse arguments
(path, middleware)"] PARSE --> FLATTEN["Flatten middleware array"] FLATTEN --> VALIDATE{"fns.length === 0?"} VALIDATE -->|Yes| ERROR["throw TypeError"] VALIDATE -->|No| GETROUTER["Get router instance"] GETROUTER --> FOREACH["For each middleware fn"] FOREACH --> CHECK{"Is fn a sub-app?
fn.handle && fn.set"} CHECK -->|Yes| SUBAPP["Set fn.mountpath = path
Set fn.parent = this
Wrap with mounted_app middleware"] CHECK -->|No| DIRECT["router.use(path, fn)"] SUBAPP --> EMIT["fn.emit('mount', this)"] EMIT --> NEXT["Next middleware"] DIRECT --> NEXT NEXT --> RETURN["Return this"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:190-244]() #### `app.route(path)` Creates a new route for the specified path: ```javascript app.route('/users') .get(getAllUsers) .post(createUser) .put(updateUser); ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:256-258]() #### HTTP Verb Methods The application provides convenience methods for all HTTP verbs: ```javascript app.get('/users', getUsers); app.post('/users', createUser); app.put('/users/:id', updateUser); app.delete('/users/:id', deleteUser); // ... patch, head, options, etc. ``` These methods are generated dynamically from the HTTP methods list and delegate to the router: **Sources:** [lib/application.js:471-482]() ```mermaid graph LR METHODS["methods.forEach()"] --> GEN["Generate app[method]"] GEN --> SPECIAL{"method === 'get' &&
arguments.length === 1?"} SPECIAL -->|Yes| GETSETTING["Return this.set(path)"] SPECIAL -->|No| CREATEROUTE["route = this.route(path)"] CREATEROUTE --> DELEGATE["route[method].apply(route, args)"] DELEGATE --> RET["Return this"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:471-482](), [lib/utils.js:29]() #### `app.all(path, ...handlers)` Matches all HTTP methods for a route: ```javascript app.all('/api/*', authenticate, rateLimit); ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:494-503]() ### Parameter Processing #### `app.param(name, callback)` Registers a callback for route parameters. The callback is invoked when the parameter is present in a route: ```javascript app.param('userId', function(req, res, next, userId) { User.findById(userId, function(err, user) { if (err) return next(err); if (!user) return next(new Error('User not found')); req.user = user; next(); }); }); app.get('/users/:userId', function(req, res) { res.json(req.user); // user is already loaded }); ``` Supports arrays of parameter names: ```javascript app.param(['id', 'userId'], loadEntity); ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:322-334]() ### Template Engine Registration #### `app.engine(ext, callback)` Registers a template engine for a given file extension: ```javascript app.engine('html', require('ejs').renderFile); app.engine('pug', require('pug').__express); ``` The callback signature should be `(path, options, callback)`: **Sources:** [lib/application.js:294-308]() ```mermaid graph TB ENGINE["app.engine(ext, fn)"] --> VALIDATE{"typeof fn === 'function'?"} VALIDATE -->|No| ERROR["throw Error('callback function required')"] VALIDATE -->|Yes| EXT["extension = ext[0] === '.' ? ext : '.' + ext"] EXT --> STORE["this.engines[extension] = fn"] STORE --> RETURN["Return this"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:294-308]() ### Template Rendering #### `app.render(name, options, callback)` Renders a view template. This method is typically called internally by `res.render()` but can be used directly: ```javascript app.render('index', { title: 'Home' }, function(err, html) { if (err) return console.error(err); console.log(html); }); ``` The rendering process: 1. Merges `app.locals`, `res.locals` (if available), and provided options 2. Looks up or creates a View instance 3. Checks the view cache (if enabled) 4. Resolves the template file path 5. Invokes the template engine **Sources:** [lib/application.js:522-575]() ```mermaid graph TB RENDER["app.render(name, options, callback)"] --> PARSE["Parse arguments"] PARSE --> MERGE["Merge data:
app.locals + _locals + options"] MERGE --> CACHE{"renderOptions.cache?"} CACHE -->|Yes| LOOKUP["view = cache[name]"] CACHE -->|No| NOCACHE["view = null"] LOOKUP --> CHECK{"view exists?"} NOCACHE --> CHECK CHECK -->|No| CREATE["view = new View(name, {
defaultEngine,
root,
engines
})"] CHECK -->|Yes| RENDER2["view.render(renderOptions, done)"] CREATE --> VALIDATE{"view.path exists?"} VALIDATE -->|No| ERR["Error: Failed to lookup view"] VALIDATE -->|Yes| PRIME{"renderOptions.cache?"} PRIME -->|Yes| STORE["cache[name] = view"] PRIME -->|No| RENDER2 STORE --> RENDER2 ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:522-575]() ### HTTP Server Integration #### `app.listen([port], [hostname], [backlog], [callback])` Creates an HTTP server and starts listening for connections: ```javascript const server = app.listen(3000, function() { console.log('Server listening on port 3000'); }); ``` Internally, this creates an `http.Server` and passes the app as the request handler: ```javascript var server = http.createServer(this); return server.listen.apply(server, args); ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:598-606]() ```mermaid graph TB LISTEN["app.listen(port, host, backlog, callback)"] --> CREATE["server = http.createServer(this)"] CREATE --> ARGS["Parse arguments"] ARGS --> CHECK{"Last arg is function?"} CHECK -->|Yes| WRAP["Wrap callback with once()"] WRAP --> ONERROR["server.once('error', callback)"] CHECK -->|No| APPLY["server.listen.apply(server, args)"] ONERROR --> APPLY APPLY --> RETURN["Return server"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:598-606]() --- ## Request Handling ### The `app.handle(req, res, callback)` Method When the application receives an HTTP request (either directly or through `app.listen()`), the `app.handle()` method processes it: ```mermaid graph TB HANDLE["app.handle(req, res, callback)"] --> DONE["done = callback || finalhandler()"] DONE --> XPOWERED{"enabled('x-powered-by')?"} XPOWERED -->|Yes| HEADER["res.setHeader('X-Powered-By', 'Express')"] XPOWERED -->|No| CIRCULAR HEADER --> CIRCULAR["Set circular refs:
req.res = res
res.req = req"] CIRCULAR --> PROTO["Set prototypes:
req.__proto__ = app.request
res.__proto__ = app.response"] PROTO --> LOCALS{"res.locals exists?"} LOCALS -->|No| CREATE["res.locals = Object.create(null)"] LOCALS -->|Yes| DISPATCH["this.router.handle(req, res, done)"] CREATE --> DISPATCH ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178]() This method: 1. Creates a final error handler using `finalhandler` if no callback provided 2. Optionally sets the `X-Powered-By` header 3. Establishes circular references between `req` and `res` 4. Extends request and response with application-specific prototypes 5. Initializes `res.locals` if not present 6. Delegates to the router for request processing **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178]() --- ## Sub-Application Mounting ### The Mount Event When a sub-application is mounted using `app.use()`, it emits a `'mount'` event with the parent application as an argument. This triggers prototype and settings inheritance: ```javascript const app = express(); const admin = express(); admin.on('mount', function(parent) { console.log('Admin mounted on', parent); }); app.use('/admin', admin); ``` The mount event handler in `defaultConfiguration()` performs inheritance: **Sources:** [lib/application.js:109-122]() ```mermaid graph TB MOUNT["Sub-app mounted via app.use()"] --> EMIT["fn.emit('mount', parent)"] EMIT --> HANDLER["Mount event handler"] HANDLER --> TRUST{"Trust proxy default?"} TRUST -->|Yes| INHERIT["Delete child's trust proxy settings
Inherit from parent"] TRUST -->|No| PROTOS["Inherit prototypes"] INHERIT --> PROTOS PROTOS --> REQ["Object.setPrototypeOf(this.request, parent.request)"] REQ --> RES["Object.setPrototypeOf(this.response, parent.response)"] RES --> ENG["Object.setPrototypeOf(this.engines, parent.engines)"] ENG --> SET["Object.setPrototypeOf(this.settings, parent.settings)"] ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:109-122]() ### Mountpath and Path Sub-applications have `mountpath` and `parent` properties: ```javascript const app = express(); const blog = express(); const admin = express(); app.use('/blog', blog); blog.use('/admin', admin); console.log(blog.mountpath); // '/blog' console.log(blog.parent === app); // true console.log(admin.path()); // '/blog/admin' ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:128](), [lib/application.js:399-403]() --- ## Application Properties ### Core Properties | Property | Type | Description | Initialization | |----------|------|-------------|----------------| | `locals` | Object | Application-wide local variables available in views | [lib/application.js:125]() | | `mountpath` | String | Path where app is mounted (default: `'/'`) | [lib/application.js:128]() | | `settings` | Object | Application settings map | [lib/application.js:64]() | | `cache` | Object | View cache storage | [lib/application.js:62]() | | `engines` | Object | Registered template engines | [lib/application.js:63]() | | `router` | Router | Lazy-loaded routing layer | [lib/application.js:69-82]() | | `parent` | Application | Parent application (when mounted) | [lib/application.js:227]() | | `request` | Object | Request prototype | [lib/express.js:45-47]() | | `response` | Object | Response prototype | [lib/express.js:50-52]() | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:59-141](), [lib/express.js:45-52]() ### Prototype Extension The exported prototypes can be extended globally: ```javascript const express = require('express'); // Extend all apps express.application.myMethod = function() { return 'custom'; }; // Extend all requests express.request.myProperty = 'value'; // Extend all responses express.response.helper = function() { this.send('helped'); }; ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:62-64]() --- ## Complete Application Lifecycle ```mermaid graph TB START["const app = express()"] --> CREATE["createApplication()"] CREATE --> INIT["app.init()"] INIT --> CONFIG["defaultConfiguration()"] CONFIG --> SETUP["Application Setup Phase"] SETUP --> MIDDLEWARE["app.use(middleware)"] SETUP --> ROUTES["app.get/post/etc(routes)"] SETUP --> SETTINGS["app.set(settings)"] SETUP --> ENGINES["app.engine(ext, fn)"] MIDDLEWARE --> LISTEN["app.listen(port)"] ROUTES --> LISTEN SETTINGS --> LISTEN ENGINES --> LISTEN LISTEN --> SERVER["http.createServer(app)"] SERVER --> RUNNING["Server running"] RUNNING --> REQUEST["HTTP Request arrives"] REQUEST --> HANDLE["app.handle(req, res)"] HANDLE --> SETUP_REQ["Setup req/res prototypes"] SETUP_REQ --> ROUTER["router.handle(req, res, done)"] ROUTER --> MIDDLEWARE2["Execute middleware chain"] MIDDLEWARE2 --> HANDLER["Execute route handler"] HANDLER --> RESPONSE["res.send/render/etc()"] RESPONSE --> END["Response sent"] END --> REQUEST ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/application.js:598-606]() --- ## Export Structure The Express module exports: | Export | Type | Description | Source | |--------|------|-------------|--------| | `express()` | Function | Application factory | [lib/express.js:27]() | | `express.application` | Object | Application prototype | [lib/express.js:62]() | | `express.request` | Object | Request prototype | [lib/express.js:63]() | | `express.response` | Object | Response prototype | [lib/express.js:64]() | | `express.Router` | Constructor | Router constructor | [lib/express.js:71]() | | `express.Route` | Constructor | Route constructor | [lib/express.js:70]() | | `express.json` | Function | JSON body parser middleware | [lib/express.js:77]() | | `express.urlencoded` | Function | URL-encoded body parser middleware | [lib/express.js:81]() | | `express.text` | Function | Text body parser middleware | [lib/express.js:80]() | | `express.raw` | Function | Raw body parser middleware | [lib/express.js:78]() | | `express.static` | Function | Static file serving middleware | [lib/express.js:79]() | **Sources:** [lib/express.js:27-81]() --- ## Summary The Express application instance is a sophisticated object that serves as: 1. **Request Handler**: A function that can be passed to `http.createServer()` 2. **Event Emitter**: Supporting lifecycle events like `'mount'` 3. **Middleware Container**: Managing a stack of middleware functions via the router 4. **Configuration Store**: Holding application settings and compiled utility functions 5. **Template Engine Registry**: Mapping file extensions to rendering functions 6. **Prototype Base**: Providing extendable request and response prototypes Key architectural patterns: - **Lazy initialization** for the router (created on first access) - **Settings compilation** for performance-critical settings - **Prototype inheritance** for sub-applications - **Mixin pattern** for composing capabilities from EventEmitter and application prototype **Sources:** [lib/express.js:1-82](), [lib/application.js:1-632]() --- # Page: Request Object # Request Object

The Request Object (`req`) represents the HTTP request in Express.js. It provides properties and methods for accessing request headers, URL components, client information, query parameters, and body data. The request object is an enhanced version of Node.js's `http.IncomingMessage` with additional Express-specific functionality. For information about the Response Object and its methods, see [Response Object](#2.3). For details on the complete request-response lifecycle, see [Request-Response Lifecycle](#2.4). --- ## Request Prototype Architecture The Express request object is created by extending Node.js's `http.IncomingMessage.prototype`. During application initialization, Express creates a prototype chain that allows all request objects to inherit Express-specific properties and methods. **Diagram: Request Object Creation and Prototype Chain** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Initialization" createApp["createApplication()
(lib/express.js:36)"] appReq["app.request
Object.create(req)"] reqProto["req prototype
(lib/request.js:30)"] httpIncoming["http.IncomingMessage.prototype"] end subgraph "Request Handling" appHandle["app.handle(req, res)
(lib/application.js:152)"] setProto["Object.setPrototypeOf(req, app.request)
(lib/application.js:169)"] incomingReq["Incoming HTTP Request"] end subgraph "Request Object Properties" reqApp["req.app"] reqRes["req.res"] reqNext["req.next"] end createApp --> appReq reqProto --> httpIncoming appReq --> reqProto incomingReq --> appHandle appHandle --> setProto setProto --> appReq setProto --> reqApp setProto --> reqRes setProto --> reqNext reqApp -.references.-> createApp reqRes -.circular reference.-> Response Response["Response Object"] style reqProto fill:#f9f9f9 style appReq fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/application.js:152-178]() The request prototype is defined in `lib/request.js` as `Object.create(http.IncomingMessage.prototype)`. Each Express application creates its own request prototype instance that inherits from this base. When handling requests, `app.handle()` sets the prototype of the incoming request object to the application's request prototype using `Object.setPrototypeOf()`. --- ## Property Categories Overview The request object properties can be organized into several functional categories: **Diagram: Request Object Property Categories** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "req Object" req["Request Object Instance"] end subgraph "Header Access" reqGet["req.get(name)
req.header(name)
(lib/request.js:63-83)"] end subgraph "Content Negotiation" reqAccepts["req.accepts(types)
(lib/request.js:127-130)"] reqAcceptsEnc["req.acceptsEncodings()
(lib/request.js:140-143)"] reqAcceptsChar["req.acceptsCharsets()
(lib/request.js:171-174)"] reqAcceptsLang["req.acceptsLanguages()
(lib/request.js:185-187)"] end subgraph "Client Information" reqProtocol["req.protocol (getter)
(lib/request.js:297-315)"] reqSecure["req.secure (getter)
(lib/request.js:326-328)"] reqIp["req.ip (getter)
(lib/request.js:340-343)"] reqIps["req.ips (getter)
(lib/request.js:357-366)"] reqHost["req.host (getter)
(lib/request.js:418-431)"] reqHostname["req.hostname (getter)
(lib/request.js:444-458)"] end subgraph "URL Components" reqPath["req.path (getter)
(lib/request.js:403-405)"] reqQuery["req.query (getter)
(lib/request.js:230-241)"] reqSubdomains["req.subdomains (getter)
(lib/request.js:383-394)"] end subgraph "Request State" reqFresh["req.fresh (getter)
(lib/request.js:469-486)"] reqStale["req.stale (getter)
(lib/request.js:497-499)"] reqXhr["req.xhr (getter)
(lib/request.js:508-511)"] end subgraph "Other Methods" reqIs["req.is(types)
(lib/request.js:269-281)"] reqRange["req.range(size, options)
(lib/request.js:214-218)"] end req --> reqGet req --> reqAccepts req --> reqAcceptsEnc req --> reqAcceptsChar req --> reqAcceptsLang req --> reqProtocol req --> reqSecure req --> reqIp req --> reqIps req --> reqHost req --> reqHostname req --> reqPath req --> reqQuery req --> reqSubdomains req --> reqFresh req --> reqStale req --> reqXhr req --> reqIs req --> reqRange style req fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:1-528]() --- ## Header Access The request object provides methods to retrieve HTTP request headers. ### `req.get(name)` and `req.header(name)` These methods retrieve the value of a request header by name (case-insensitive). They are aliases of each other. **Special case:** The `Referrer` and `Referer` headers are treated interchangeably, allowing either spelling. **Implementation:** [lib/request.js:63-83]() | Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | `name` | String | Header name (case-insensitive) | | Returns | String | Header value or `undefined` | **Example Usage:** ```javascript req.get('Content-Type'); // => "text/plain" req.get('content-type'); // => "text/plain" req.get('Referrer'); // Returns either referrer or referer header req.get('Something'); // => undefined ``` **Validation:** The method throws `TypeError` if `name` is not provided or is not a string. **Sources:** [lib/request.js:63-83](), [test/req.host.js]() --- ## Content Negotiation Express provides methods for HTTP content negotiation based on the `Accept*` headers sent by the client. ### `req.accepts(types)` Determines if the request accepts the given content type(s). Uses the `accepts` module to parse the `Accept` header and return the best match. [lib/request.js:127-130]() **Returns:** The best matching type, an array of acceptable types, or `false` if none match. ### `req.acceptsEncodings(...encodings)` Checks if the given encodings are acceptable based on the `Accept-Encoding` header. [lib/request.js:140-143]() ### `req.acceptsCharsets(...charsets)` Checks if the specified charsets are acceptable based on the `Accept-Charset` header. [lib/request.js:171-174]() ### `req.acceptsLanguages(...languages)` Checks if the given languages are acceptable based on the `Accept-Language` header. [lib/request.js:185-187]() **Sources:** [lib/request.js:127-187]() --- ## Client Information and Trust Proxy Several request properties provide information about the client making the request. Many of these properties are affected by the `trust proxy` application setting. **Diagram: Trust Proxy Data Flow** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Settings" trustProxy["app.set('trust proxy', value)
(lib/application.js:370-380)"] compileTrust["compileTrust(value)
(lib/utils.js:194-214)"] trustProxyFn["app.get('trust proxy fn')"] end subgraph "Request Properties Affected" reqProtocol["req.protocol"] reqIp["req.ip"] reqIps["req.ips"] reqHost["req.host"] reqHostname["req.hostname"] reqSecure["req.secure"] end subgraph "HTTP Headers Checked" xForwardedProto["X-Forwarded-Proto"] xForwardedFor["X-Forwarded-For"] xForwardedHost["X-Forwarded-Host"] end subgraph "Trust Proxy Values" trustTrue["true: trust all proxies"] trustFalse["false: trust no proxies"] trustNumber["number: trust N hops"] trustString["string: IP/CIDR list"] trustArray["array: IP/CIDR list"] trustFn["function: custom logic"] end trustProxy --> compileTrust compileTrust --> trustProxyFn trustTrue -.option.-> compileTrust trustFalse -.option.-> compileTrust trustNumber -.option.-> compileTrust trustString -.option.-> compileTrust trustArray -.option.-> compileTrust trustFn -.option.-> compileTrust trustProxyFn --> reqProtocol trustProxyFn --> reqIp trustProxyFn --> reqIps trustProxyFn --> reqHost trustProxyFn --> reqHostname reqProtocol --> xForwardedProto reqIp --> xForwardedFor reqIps --> xForwardedFor reqHost --> xForwardedHost reqHostname --> xForwardedHost reqProtocol --> reqSecure style trustProxyFn fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:370-380](), [lib/utils.js:194-214](), [lib/request.js:297-458]() ### `req.protocol` Returns the protocol string `'http'` or `'https'`. When trust proxy is enabled, the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header is consulted. [lib/request.js:297-315]() **Behavior:** - Default: Uses `req.socket.encrypted` to determine protocol - With trust proxy: Checks `X-Forwarded-Proto` header first - Handles comma-separated values by taking the first entry ### `req.secure` Boolean shorthand for `req.protocol === 'https'`. [lib/request.js:326-328]() ### `req.ip` Returns the remote IP address of the client. Uses the `proxy-addr` module for trust proxy logic. [lib/request.js:340-343]() **Behavior:** - Default: Returns `req.socket.remoteAddress` - With trust proxy: Parses `X-Forwarded-For` header to extract client IP ### `req.ips` Returns an array of IP addresses from `X-Forwarded-For`, ordered from farthest to closest, excluding the socket address. [lib/request.js:357-366]() ### `req.host` Parses the `Host` header to a host string (including port if present). When trust proxy is enabled, checks `X-Forwarded-Host` first. [lib/request.js:418-431]() **Special handling:** - IPv6 literals are supported (e.g., `[::1]` or `[::1]:3000`) - Handles comma-separated values in `X-Forwarded-Host` ### `req.hostname` Returns the hostname without the port number. Extracts the hostname portion from `req.host`. [lib/request.js:444-458]() **Sources:** [lib/request.js:297-458](), [test/req.protocol.js](), [test/req.ip.js](), [test/req.ips.js](), [test/req.host.js](), [test/req.secure.js]() --- ## URL Components ### `req.path` Returns the pathname portion of the request URL. [lib/request.js:403-405]() Uses the `parseurl` module to parse `req.url`. ### `req.query` Returns the parsed query string as an object. This is a getter property that lazily parses the query string using the configured query parser. [lib/request.js:230-241]() **Diagram: Query String Parsing Flow** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Configuration" queryParser["app.set('query parser', value)
(lib/application.js:367-369)"] compileQP["compileQueryParser(value)
(lib/utils.js:162-184)"] queryParserFn["app.get('query parser fn')"] end subgraph "Parser Options" simple["'simple': querystring.parse"] extended["'extended': qs.parse with allowPrototypes"] disabled["false: returns empty object"] customFn["function: custom parser"] end subgraph "Request Query Access" reqQuery["req.query getter"] parseurl["parseurl(req).query"] queryString["Raw query string"] parsedObject["Parsed object"] end queryParser --> compileQP compileQP --> queryParserFn simple -.option.-> compileQP extended -.option.-> compileQP disabled -.option.-> compileQP customFn -.option.-> compileQP reqQuery --> queryParserFn reqQuery --> parseurl parseurl --> queryString queryParserFn --> parsedObject style queryParserFn fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:230-241](), [lib/utils.js:162-184](), [lib/application.js:367-369](), [test/req.query.js]() **Query Parser Settings:** | Setting | Description | Implementation | |---------|-------------|----------------| | `'simple'` (default) | Uses Node.js `querystring.parse()` | [lib/utils.js:172]() | | `'extended'` | Uses `qs.parse()` with complex object support | [lib/utils.js:177]() | | `false` | Disables query parsing, returns empty object | [lib/utils.js:174]() | | Custom function | User-provided parser function | [lib/utils.js:165-167]() | ### `req.subdomains` Returns an array of subdomains in the domain name. The `subdomain offset` setting determines how many parts to remove from the end. [lib/request.js:383-394]() **Example:** For domain `tobi.ferrets.example.com`: - With offset 2 (default): `["ferrets", "tobi"]` - With offset 3: `["tobi"]` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:383-394](), [lib/request.js:403-405](), [lib/request.js:230-241]() --- ## Content Type Checking ### `req.is(types)` Checks if the incoming request contains the `Content-Type` header field and matches the given MIME type(s). Uses the `type-is` module. [lib/request.js:269-281]() **Returns:** The matching type as a string, or `false` if no match, or `null` if no `Content-Type` header exists. **Example:** ```javascript // With Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8 req.is('html'); // => 'html' req.is('text/html'); // => 'text/html' req.is('text/*'); // => 'text/*' // With Content-Type: application/json req.is('json'); // => 'json' req.is('application/*'); // => 'application/*' req.is('html'); // => false ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:269-281]() --- ## Range Header Parsing ### `req.range(size, options)` Parses the `Range` header field using the `range-parser` module. [lib/request.js:214-218]() | Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | `size` | Number | Resource size in bytes | | `options` | Object | Options (e.g., `{ combine: true }`) | | Returns | Number/Array | Parsed ranges or error code | **Return values:** - `undefined`: No `Range` header present - `-1`: Unsatisfiable ranges - `-2`: Syntactically invalid header - Array: Valid range objects with `start` and `end` properties **Sources:** [lib/request.js:214-218]() --- ## Request State Properties ### `req.fresh` Boolean indicating whether the request is "fresh" based on `Last-Modified` and/or `ETag` headers. Uses the `fresh` module. [lib/request.js:469-486]() **Conditions for freshness:** - Request method must be `GET` or `HEAD` - Response status must be 2xx or 304 - `Last-Modified` and `ETag` headers match conditional headers in request **Used for conditional GET optimization:** If `req.fresh` is true, the response should return 304 Not Modified. ### `req.stale` Boolean opposite of `req.fresh`. [lib/request.js:497-499]() ### `req.xhr` Boolean indicating if the request was issued by an `XMLHttpRequest` (AJAX request). Checks if the `X-Requested-With` header equals `'xmlhttprequest'` (case-insensitive). [lib/request.js:508-511]() **Sources:** [lib/request.js:469-511](), [test/req.xhr.js]() --- ## Implementation: Lazy Property Evaluation Many request properties are implemented as getters using the `defineGetter()` helper function. This enables lazy evaluation—properties are only computed when accessed. **Diagram: Getter Property Implementation** ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "defineGetter Helper" helper["defineGetter(obj, name, getter)
(lib/request.js:521-527)"] defineProperty["Object.defineProperty()"] end subgraph "Property Descriptors" configurable["configurable: true"] enumerable["enumerable: true"] getFunc["get: getter function"] end subgraph "Getter Properties" query["req.query"] protocol["req.protocol"] secure["req.secure"] ip["req.ip"] ips["req.ips"] subdomains["req.subdomains"] path["req.path"] host["req.host"] hostname["req.hostname"] fresh["req.fresh"] stale["req.stale"] xhr["req.xhr"] end helper --> defineProperty defineProperty --> configurable defineProperty --> enumerable defineProperty --> getFunc query -.uses.-> helper protocol -.uses.-> helper secure -.uses.-> helper ip -.uses.-> helper ips -.uses.-> helper subdomains -.uses.-> helper path -.uses.-> helper host -.uses.-> helper hostname -.uses.-> helper fresh -.uses.-> helper stale -.uses.-> helper xhr -.uses.-> helper style helper fill:#f9f9f9 ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:521-527]() The `defineGetter()` function wraps `Object.defineProperty()` to create configurable, enumerable getter properties. This pattern provides several benefits: 1. **Lazy evaluation:** Expensive computations only occur when the property is accessed 2. **Always current:** Properties that depend on mutable state (like headers) always reflect the current state 3. **Clean API:** Properties appear as simple attributes rather than method calls **Example property definition:** ```javascript defineGetter(req, 'protocol', function protocol(){ var proto = this.socket.encrypted ? 'https' : 'http'; var trust = this.app.get('trust proxy fn'); if (!trust(this.socket.remoteAddress, 0)) { return proto; } var header = this.get('X-Forwarded-Proto') || proto; var index = header.indexOf(','); return index !== -1 ? header.substring(0, index).trim() : header.trim(); }); ``` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:297-315](), [lib/request.js:521-527]() --- ## Summary Table: Request Object Properties and Methods | Category | Property/Method | Type | Affected by Trust Proxy | Sources | |----------|-----------------|------|-------------------------|---------| | **Headers** | `req.get(name)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:63-83]() | | **Content Negotiation** | `req.accepts(types)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:127-130]() | | | `req.acceptsEncodings()` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:140-143]() | | | `req.acceptsCharsets()` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:171-174]() | | | `req.acceptsLanguages()` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:185-187]() | | **Client Info** | `req.protocol` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:297-315]() | | | `req.secure` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:326-328]() | | | `req.ip` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:340-343]() | | | `req.ips` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:357-366]() | | | `req.host` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:418-431]() | | | `req.hostname` | Getter | Yes | [lib/request.js:444-458]() | | **URL Components** | `req.path` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:403-405]() | | | `req.query` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:230-241]() | | | `req.subdomains` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:383-394]() | | **Request State** | `req.fresh` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:469-486]() | | | `req.stale` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:497-499]() | | | `req.xhr` | Getter | No | [lib/request.js:508-511]() | | **Other** | `req.is(types)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:269-281]() | | | `req.range(size, options)` | Method | No | [lib/request.js:214-218]() | **Sources:** [lib/request.js:1-528]() --- # Page: Response Object # Response Object

The Response Object is one of the three core prototypes in Express (alongside Application and Request). It extends Node.js's `http.ServerResponse` with additional methods and properties for sending various types of HTTP responses, managing headers, handling cookies, rendering views, and performing redirects. This page documents the Response prototype, its methods, and how it integrates with the Application instance. For information about the Request Object, see [2.2](#2.2). For details on how the response flows through the middleware stack, see [2.4](#2.4). For specific response method implementations like `res.send()` and `res.json()`, see [5.1](#5.1). ## Response Prototype Structure The Response Object is defined as a prototype in [lib/response.js:42]() that extends Node.js's native `http.ServerResponse.prototype`. This prototype is then instantiated per application in [lib/express.js:50-52](), where each Express application creates its own response prototype with a reference back to the application instance. **Diagram: Response Object Prototype Chain and Initialization** ```mermaid graph TB ServerResponse["http.ServerResponse.prototype
(Node.js built-in)"] BaseRes["res prototype
(lib/response.js:42)
Object.create(http.ServerResponse.prototype)"] AppResponse["app.response
(lib/express.js:50-52)
Object.create(res, {app})"] ActualRes["Actual response object
(Node.js http.ServerResponse instance)"] ServerResponse -->|"inherits from"| BaseRes BaseRes -->|"Object.create"| AppResponse AppResponse -->|"Object.setPrototypeOf
(lib/application.js:170)"| ActualRes AppResponse -.->|"app property"| AppInstance["Application instance"] ActualRes -.->|"req property
(lib/application.js:166)"| Request["Request object"] BaseRes -->|"defines methods"| Methods["res.send()
res.json()
res.redirect()
res.render()
etc."] ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/express.js:50-52](), [lib/application.js:164-177]() When an HTTP request arrives, the Application's `handle()` method sets up the prototype chain by calling `Object.setPrototypeOf(res, this.response)` at [lib/application.js:170](). This allows the actual Node.js response object to access all Express response methods while maintaining access to the Application instance through `res.app`. ## Core Response Methods The Response Object provides several methods for sending different types of response bodies. These methods form a hierarchy where higher-level methods delegate to lower-level ones. **Diagram: Response Method Hierarchy** ```mermaid graph TB send["res.send(body)
(lib/response.js:125)"] json["res.json(obj)
(lib/response.js:232)"] jsonp["res.jsonp(obj)
(lib/response.js:260)"] sendStatus["res.sendStatus(code)
(lib/response.js:321)"] sendFile["res.sendFile(path)
(lib/response.js:371)"] download["res.download(path)
(lib/response.js:433)"] render["res.render(view)
(lib/response.js:894)"] redirect["res.redirect(url)
(lib/response.js:812)"] format["res.format(obj)
(lib/response.js:569)"] json --> send jsonp --> json sendStatus --> send download --> sendFile render --> appRender["app.render()
(lib/application.js:522)"] appRender --> send redirect --> format format --> send send --> stringify["stringify()
(lib/response.js:1023)"] send --> etagFn["app.get('etag fn')
(lib/response.js:161)"] send --> fresh["req.fresh
(lib/request.js:469)"] send --> end["res.end()
(Node.js http.ServerResponse)"] json --> stringify jsonp --> stringify ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218](), [lib/response.js:232-304](), [lib/response.js:321-328]() ### res.send() The `res.send()` method at [lib/response.js:125-218]() is the foundation for sending responses. It performs the following operations: 1. **Type Detection** ([lib/response.js:133-158]()): Determines content type based on the body type: - `string`: Sets `Content-Type` to `text/html` with charset - `number`, `boolean`, or plain `object`: Delegates to `res.json()` - `Buffer` or `ArrayBuffer.isView()`: Sets `Content-Type` to `application/octet-stream` - `null`: Converts to empty string 2. **Content-Length Calculation** ([lib/response.js:164-181]()): Calculates and sets the `Content-Length` header 3. **ETag Generation** ([lib/response.js:184-189]()): If the `etag fn` setting exists and no ETag header is already set, generates an ETag using the configured function 4. **Freshness Check** ([lib/response.js:192]()): If `req.fresh` is true (request has matching ETag or Last-Modified), sets status to 304 5. **Status Code Handling** ([lib/response.js:195-207]()): - **204/304**: Strips `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, and `Transfer-Encoding` headers, sends empty body - **205**: Sets `Content-Length` to `'0'`, removes `Transfer-Encoding`, sends empty body 6. **HEAD Method** ([lib/response.js:209-211]()): Skips body transmission for HEAD requests Sources: [lib/response.js:125-218]() ### res.json() and res.jsonp() The `res.json()` method at [lib/response.js:232-246]() stringifies the provided object using application settings: - `json replacer`: Custom replacer function for `JSON.stringify()` - `json spaces`: Indentation for pretty-printing - `json escape`: HTML-escaping for security (using `stringify()` helper at [lib/response.js:1023-1047]()) The `res.jsonp()` method at [lib/response.js:260-304]() extends `res.json()` with JSONP support: - Reads callback name from `req.query[app.get('jsonp callback name')]` (default: `'callback'`) - Sanitizes callback name by removing non-safe characters at [lib/response.js:286]() - Wraps JSON in callback function with security prologue `/**/ typeof callback === 'function' && callback(...);` - Sets `Content-Type` to `text/javascript` and adds `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` header Sources: [lib/response.js:232-304](), [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() ## Header Management The Response Object provides several methods for managing HTTP headers. | Method | Location | Purpose | |--------|----------|---------| | `res.set(field, val)` | [lib/response.js:664-686]() | Set header field(s), auto-adds charset to Content-Type | | `res.header(field, val)` | [lib/response.js:664]() | Alias for `res.set()` | | `res.get(field)` | [lib/response.js:696-698]() | Get header field value | | `res.append(field, val)` | [lib/response.js:629-641]() | Append value to header field | | `res.vary(field)` | [lib/response.js:875-879]() | Add field to Vary header | Sources: [lib/response.js:629-698](), [lib/response.js:875-879]() ### res.set() and Content-Type Handling The `res.set()` method at [lib/response.js:664-686]() has special handling for the `Content-Type` header. When setting `Content-Type`, it calls `mime.contentType(value)` at [lib/response.js:676]() to automatically add the appropriate charset if not already present. ```mermaid graph LR resSet["res.set('Content-Type', value)"] check{"Is field
'content-type'?"} mimeContentType["mime.contentType(value)
(mime-types module)"] setHeader["res.setHeader(field, value)"] resSet --> check check -->|"Yes"| mimeContentType check -->|"No"| setHeader mimeContentType --> setHeader ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:664-686]() ## Status Code Management The `res.status()` method at [lib/response.js:64-76]() sets the HTTP status code with strict validation: - **Type Check** ([lib/response.js:66-68]()): Throws `TypeError` if not an integer - **Range Check** ([lib/response.js:70-72]()): Throws `RangeError` if not between 100 and 999 - **Chainable**: Returns `this` for method chaining The `res.sendStatus()` method at [lib/response.js:321-328]() is a convenience method that sets the status, sets `Content-Type` to `text/plain`, and sends the status message as the body. Sources: [lib/response.js:64-76](), [lib/response.js:321-328]() ## Content Negotiation with res.format() The `res.format()` method at [lib/response.js:569-594]() performs content negotiation based on the `Accept` header: 1. Extracts keys from the provided object (excluding `'default'`) 2. Calls `req.accepts(keys)` to find the best match 3. Sets `Vary: Accept` header 4. If a match is found, sets `Content-Type` and invokes the corresponding handler 5. If no match and `default` handler exists, invokes default handler 6. Otherwise, calls `next(createError(406))` for Not Acceptable **Diagram: res.format() Flow** ```mermaid graph TB format["res.format({
'text/html': handlerA,
'application/json': handlerB,
'default': handlerC
})"] keys["Extract keys
(exclude 'default')
(lib/response.js:573-574)"] accepts["req.accepts(keys)
(lib/request.js:127-130)"] vary["res.vary('Accept')
(lib/response.js:580)"] match{"Match found?"} setType["Set Content-Type
(lib/response.js:583)"] callHandler["Call handler
(lib/response.js:584)"] hasDefault{"Has 'default'
handler?"} callDefault["Call default handler
(lib/response.js:586)"] error406["next(createError(406))
(lib/response.js:588-590)"] format --> keys keys --> accepts accepts --> vary vary --> match match -->|"Yes"| setType setType --> callHandler match -->|"No"| hasDefault hasDefault -->|"Yes"| callDefault hasDefault -->|"No"| error406 ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:569-594]() ## File Operations ### res.sendFile() The `res.sendFile()` method at [lib/response.js:371-413]() transfers a file using the `send` module: 1. **Path Validation** ([lib/response.js:378-394]()): - Requires `path` argument - `path` must be a string - Path must be absolute or `opts.root` must be specified 2. **ETag Configuration** ([lib/response.js:400]()): Wires `app.enabled('etag')` to send options 3. **File Streaming** ([lib/response.js:401-412]()): Uses `send` module and internal `sendfile()` helper at [lib/response.js:921-1009]() to pipe the file to the response Sources: [lib/response.js:371-413](), [lib/response.js:921-1009]() ### res.download() The `res.download()` method at [lib/response.js:433-482]() is a convenience wrapper around `res.sendFile()` that: 1. Sets `Content-Disposition` header to `attachment` with optional filename 2. Resolves the full path if no `root` option is provided 3. Delegates to `res.sendFile()` with merged options Sources: [lib/response.js:433-482]() ## Redirects and Location ### res.location() The `res.location()` method at [lib/response.js:794-796]() sets the `Location` header. It encodes the URL using `encodeUrl()` from the `encodeurl` module. ### res.redirect() The `res.redirect()` method at [lib/response.js:812-864]() performs HTTP redirects: 1. **Status Code** ([lib/response.js:815-821]()): Defaults to 302, accepts optional status as first argument 2. **Location Header** ([lib/response.js:836]()): Sets using `res.location()` 3. **Content Negotiation** ([lib/response.js:839-853]()): Uses `res.format()` to send appropriate response body: - `text`: Status message + redirect text - `html`: Full HTML document with DOCTYPE, title, and body (as of Express 5.x per [History.md:5]()) - `default`: Empty body 4. **Method Handling** ([lib/response.js:859-863]()): Skips body for HEAD requests Sources: [lib/response.js:794-796](), [lib/response.js:812-864]() ## Cookie Management ### res.cookie() The `res.cookie()` method at [lib/response.js:742-775]() sets cookies: 1. **Value Encoding** ([lib/response.js:751-757]()): - Objects are prefixed with `'j:'` and JSON-stringified - Other values are converted to strings - Signed cookies are prefixed with `'s:'` and signed using `cookie-signature` module 2. **MaxAge Conversion** ([lib/response.js:759-766]()): Converts `maxAge` option (milliseconds) to `expires` date and `maxAge` (seconds) for the cookie module 3. **Default Path** ([lib/response.js:768-770]()): Sets `path` to `'/'` if not specified 4. **Serialization** ([lib/response.js:772]()): Uses `cookie.serialize()` to create Set-Cookie header value and appends it Sources: [lib/response.js:742-775]() ### res.clearCookie() The `res.clearCookie()` method at [lib/response.js:709-716]() clears a cookie by: - Setting `expires` to `new Date(1)` (January 1, 1970) - Deleting any user-provided `maxAge` (to prevent conflicts) - Delegating to `res.cookie()` with empty value Sources: [lib/response.js:709-716]() ## View Rendering The `res.render()` method at [lib/response.js:894-918]() renders views using the template engine: 1. **Options Merging** ([lib/response.js:908]()): Merges `res.locals` into options as `opts._locals` 2. **Default Callback** ([lib/response.js:911-914]()): If no callback provided, creates one that calls `res.send()` or `req.next(err)` on error 3. **Delegation** ([lib/response.js:917]()): Delegates to `app.render()` which handles view lookup and template engine invocation Sources: [lib/response.js:894-918](), [lib/application.js:522-575]() ## Special Headers ### res.links() The `res.links()` method at [lib/response.js:97-110]() sets the `Link` header from an object. As of Express 5.1.0, it supports arrays for multiple links with the same `rel` value: ```javascript res.links({ next: 'http://api.example.com/users?page=2', pages: ['http://api.example.com/users?page=1', 'http://api.example.com/users?page=2'] }); ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:97-110](), [History.md:29]() ### res.attachment() The `res.attachment()` method at [lib/response.js:604-612]() sets the `Content-Disposition` header to `attachment`: - If filename is provided, also sets `Content-Type` based on file extension - Uses `content-disposition` module for proper encoding Sources: [lib/response.js:604-612]() ### res.type() / res.contentType() The `res.type()` method at [lib/response.js:503-510]() (aliased as `res.contentType()`) sets the `Content-Type` header: - If type contains `/`, uses it directly - Otherwise, looks up MIME type using `mime.contentType()` - Defaults to `application/octet-stream` if lookup fails Sources: [lib/response.js:503-510]() ## Integration with Application Settings The Response Object integrates closely with Application settings to control behavior: **Diagram: Response Object and Application Settings** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Settings" etagSetting["app.set('etag', val)
(lib/application.js:364-366)"] jsonEscape["app.set('json escape', bool)"] jsonReplacer["app.set('json replacer', fn)"] jsonSpaces["app.set('json spaces', n)"] jsonpCallback["app.set('jsonp callback name', str)"] end subgraph "Compiled Settings" etagFn["app.get('etag fn')
(compileETag)"] end subgraph "Response Methods" resSend["res.send()
(lib/response.js:161-162)"] resJson["res.json()
(lib/response.js:235-237)"] resJsonp["res.jsonp()
(lib/response.js:263-267)"] end etagSetting --> etagFn etagFn --> resSend jsonEscape --> resJson jsonReplacer --> resJson jsonSpaces --> resJson jsonEscape --> resJsonp jsonReplacer --> resJsonp jsonSpaces --> resJsonp jsonpCallback --> resJsonp ``` Sources: [lib/response.js:161-162](), [lib/response.js:234-238](), [lib/response.js:262-267](), [lib/application.js:363-380]() ### ETag Generation The `etag` setting controls automatic ETag generation in `res.send()`: | Setting Value | Compiled Function | Behavior | |---------------|-------------------|----------| | `false` | `undefined` | No ETag generation | | `true` or `'weak'` | `utils.wetag` | Generates weak ETags (prefix `W/"`) | | `'strong'` | `utils.etag` | Generates strong ETags (no prefix) | | Custom function | The function itself | Custom ETag generation | The compiled function is stored as `app.get('etag fn')` and used at [lib/response.js:161-162](). Sources: [lib/utils.js:130-152](), [lib/response.js:161-189]() ### JSON Settings Three settings control JSON serialization: 1. **json replacer** ([lib/response.js:236]()): Passed as second argument to `JSON.stringify()` 2. **json spaces** ([lib/response.js:237]()): Passed as third argument to `JSON.stringify()` for indentation 3. **json escape** ([lib/response.js:235]()): When enabled, escapes `<`, `>`, and `&` characters to prevent HTML injection attacks The internal `stringify()` helper at [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() handles the HTML escaping. Sources: [lib/response.js:232-246](), [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() ## Response Method Chaining Most Response methods return `this` to enable method chaining: ```javascript res.status(201) .set('Location', '/users/123') .json({ id: 123, name: 'John' }); ``` Terminal methods that send the response (`res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.redirect()`, etc.) also return `this` for consistency, though chaining after them has no practical effect since the response is finalized. Sources: [lib/response.js:75](), [lib/response.js:217](), [lib/response.js:245](), [lib/response.js:685]() ## Summary The Response Object extends Node.js's `http.ServerResponse` with Express-specific functionality organized into several categories: - **Core sending methods**: `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()`, `res.sendStatus()` - **File operations**: `res.sendFile()`, `res.download()` - **Header management**: `res.set()`, `res.get()`, `res.append()`, `res.vary()` - **Content type**: `res.type()`, `res.format()` - **Redirects**: `res.redirect()`, `res.location()` - **Cookies**: `res.cookie()`, `res.clearCookie()` - **View rendering**: `res.render()` - **Special headers**: `res.links()`, `res.attachment()` The Response prototype is created per application with a reference to the app instance, enabling integration with application settings like `etag`, `json replacer`, and `json spaces`. During request handling, the actual Node.js response object's prototype is set to the application's response prototype, giving it access to all Express response methods. Sources: [lib/response.js:1-1048](), [lib/express.js:50-52](), [lib/application.js:152-178]() --- # Page: Request-Response Lifecycle # Request-Response Lifecycle
