## Purpose and Scope This document explains the complete journey of an HTTP request through Express.js, from the moment it enters the application until a response is sent back to the client. It covers the initialization sequence in `app.handle()`, prototype extension, circular reference setup, and the handoff to the routing system. For details on the routing and middleware execution that occurs after initialization, see [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1). For information about specific response methods used to send data back to clients, see [Content Delivery Methods](#5.1). --- ## Overview of the Request-Response Flow When an HTTP request arrives at an Express application, it passes through several initialization steps before reaching application middleware and route handlers. The entry point is the Express application function itself, which delegates to `app.handle()` for request processing. **Request-Response Flow Overview** ```mermaid flowchart TB HTTP["HTTP Server
(Node.js http.Server)"] APP["Express Application
app(req, res, next)"] HANDLE["app.handle()
[lib/application.js:152]"] FINAL["Setup finalhandler
Error handling callback"] HEADER["Set X-Powered-By
(if enabled)"] CIRCULAR["Circular References
req.res = res
res.req = req"] PROTO["Prototype Extension
Object.setPrototypeOf()"] LOCALS["Initialize res.locals
Object.create(null)"] ROUTER["Router Delegation
this.router.handle()"] MIDDLEWARE["Middleware Stack
Execution"] ROUTE["Route Handlers
Execution"] RESPONSE["Response Sent
res.send/json/etc"] HTTP --> APP APP --> HANDLE HANDLE --> FINAL FINAL --> HEADER HEADER --> CIRCULAR CIRCULAR --> PROTO PROTO --> LOCALS LOCALS --> ROUTER ROUTER --> MIDDLEWARE MIDDLEWARE --> ROUTE ROUTE --> RESPONSE ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/express.js:36-56]() --- ## Entry Point: The Application Function The Express application created by `createApplication()` is itself a function that Node.js's HTTP server calls with incoming requests. This function immediately delegates to `app.handle()`. **Application Creation and Invocation** ```mermaid graph TB CREATE["createApplication()
[lib/express.js:36]"] APP_FN["app function
function(req, res, next)"] MIXIN1["mixin EventEmitter.prototype"] MIXIN2["mixin application prototype"] REQ_PROTO["app.request = Object.create(req)"] RES_PROTO["app.response = Object.create(res)"] INIT["app.init()"] HTTP_CALL["HTTP Server Invocation
server.on('request', app)"] HANDLE_CALL["app.handle(req, res, next)"] CREATE --> APP_FN APP_FN --> MIXIN1 APP_FN --> MIXIN2 APP_FN --> REQ_PROTO APP_FN --> RES_PROTO APP_FN --> INIT APP_FN --> HTTP_CALL HTTP_CALL --> HANDLE_CALL ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:36-56](), [lib/application.js:152]() The application function is a simple wrapper: | Component | Location | Purpose | |-----------|----------|---------| | `app` function | [lib/express.js:37-39]() | Receives HTTP requests and delegates to `app.handle()` | | EventEmitter mixin | [lib/express.js:41]() | Enables event emission (e.g., 'mount' event) | | Application prototype | [lib/express.js:42]() | Provides methods like `app.get()`, `app.use()`, `app.set()` | | `app.request` | [lib/express.js:45-47]() | Template prototype for all request objects | | `app.response` | [lib/express.js:50-52]() | Template prototype for all response objects | --- ## Request Initialization in app.handle() The `app.handle()` method performs critical initialization before dispatching the request to the router. This method is defined at [lib/application.js:152-178](). **Initialization Sequence in app.handle()** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant HTTP as HTTP Server participant App as app.handle() participant Final as finalhandler participant Req as req object participant Res as res object participant Router as router.handle() HTTP->>App: app.handle(req, res, callback) App->>Final: Create finalhandler(req, res, options) Note over Final: Handles errors and
unhandled requests App->>Res: Set X-Powered-By header
(if enabled) App->>Req: req.res = res App->>Res: res.req = req Note over Req,Res: Circular references
for bidirectional access App->>Req: Object.setPrototypeOf(req, app.request) Note over Req: Gains Express req methods:
req.get(), req.accepts(), etc. App->>Res: Object.setPrototypeOf(res, app.response) Note over Res: Gains Express res methods:
res.send(), res.json(), etc. App->>Res: res.locals = Object.create(null) Note over Res: Initialize locals object
for template variables App->>Router: router.handle(req, res, done) Note over Router: Router takes over
request processing ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178]() ### Step-by-Step Breakdown **1. Final Handler Setup** [lib/application.js:154-157]() ``` var done = callback || finalhandler(req, res, { env: this.get('env'), onerror: logerror.bind(this) }); ``` The final handler is invoked if no route handles the request or if an error occurs. It sends appropriate error responses or 404 pages. **2. X-Powered-By Header** [lib/application.js:160-162]() ``` if (this.enabled('x-powered-by')) { res.setHeader('X-Powered-By', 'Express'); } ``` This optional header advertises that the server is running Express. It's enabled by default but can be disabled with `app.disable('x-powered-by')`. **3. Circular References** [lib/application.js:165-166]() ``` req.res = res; res.req = req; ``` These circular references allow middleware and handlers to access the response from the request object and vice versa. This is useful when passing only `req` or `res` to helper functions. **4. Prototype Extension** [lib/application.js:169-170]() ``` Object.setPrototypeOf(req, this.request) Object.setPrototypeOf(res, this.response) ``` This is the mechanism by which the raw Node.js `http.IncomingMessage` and `http.ServerResponse` objects gain all Express-specific methods and properties. **5. Response Locals Initialization** [lib/application.js:173-175]() ``` if (!res.locals) { res.locals = Object.create(null); } ``` The `res.locals` object stores variables that are passed to template engines during rendering. It's initialized as an empty object with null prototype to avoid prototype pollution. **6. Router Delegation** [lib/application.js:177]() ``` this.router.handle(req, res, done); ``` Control is passed to the router, which processes middleware and route handlers. See [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1) for details. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178]() --- ## Prototype Extension Mechanism Express extends the native Node.js HTTP objects to add framework-specific functionality. This is done through prototype chaining rather than property copying, which allows for efficient memory usage and inheritance. **Prototype Chain Structure** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Request Prototype Chain" REQ_INST["Incoming Request
instance"] APP_REQ["app.request
[lib/express.js:45]"] LIB_REQ["lib/request.js
Express req prototype"] HTTP_REQ["http.IncomingMessage
Node.js prototype"] REQ_INST -->|"__proto__"| APP_REQ APP_REQ -->|"Object.create()"| LIB_REQ LIB_REQ -->|"Object.create()"| HTTP_REQ end subgraph "Response Prototype Chain" RES_INST["Outgoing Response
instance"] APP_RES["app.response
[lib/express.js:50]"] LIB_RES["lib/response.js
Express res prototype"] HTTP_RES["http.ServerResponse
Node.js prototype"] RES_INST -->|"__proto__"| APP_RES APP_RES -->|"Object.create()"| LIB_RES LIB_RES -->|"Object.create()"| HTTP_RES end ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:45-52](), [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/application.js:169-170]() ### Prototype Layers | Layer | Created At | Purpose | Example Methods/Properties | |-------|-----------|---------|----------------------------| | `http.IncomingMessage` | Node.js | Base request functionality | `req.url`, `req.headers`, `req.method` | | `lib/request.js` | [lib/request.js:30]() | Express request methods | `req.get()`, `req.accepts()`, `req.query`, `req.ip` | | `app.request` | [lib/express.js:45-47]() | App-specific request extensions | User can extend per-app with custom properties | | Request instance | Runtime | Actual incoming request | Specific request data | | Layer | Created At | Purpose | Example Methods/Properties | |-------|-----------|---------|----------------------------| | `http.ServerResponse` | Node.js | Base response functionality | `res.writeHead()`, `res.end()`, `res.statusCode` | | `lib/response.js` | [lib/response.js:42]() | Express response methods | `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.render()` | | `app.response` | [lib/express.js:50-52]() | App-specific response extensions | User can extend per-app with custom properties | | Response instance | Runtime | Actual outgoing response | Specific response data | **Sources:** [lib/request.js:30](), [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/express.js:45-52]() ### Extension Points Users can extend Express functionality globally or per-application: ```javascript // Global extension (affects all Express apps) express.request.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; express.response.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; // Per-app extension (affects only this app and its sub-apps) app.request.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; app.response.customMethod = function() { /* ... */ }; ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:62-64]() --- ## Circular References: req.res and res.req The circular references established at [lib/application.js:165-166]() serve important practical purposes in Express middleware and route handlers. **Circular Reference Pattern** ```mermaid graph LR REQ["req object
(IncomingMessage)"] RES["res object
(ServerResponse)"] REQ -->|"req.res"| RES RES -->|"res.req"| REQ style REQ fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333 style RES fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333 ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:165-166]() ### Use Cases | Scenario | Pattern | Example | |----------|---------|---------| | Helper functions receive only `req` | Access `res` via `req.res` | `function helper(req) { req.res.send('OK'); }` | | Helper functions receive only `res` | Access `req` via `res.req` | `function helper(res) { res.send(res.req.query); }` | | Template rendering | Access request from response context | Inside `res.render()` accessing `this.req.app` | | Error handlers | Access both from either object | Error handler receives only `res`, needs to check `res.req.method` | ### Circular References in Response Methods Several response methods use the circular reference to access request properties: - **`res.send()`** [lib/response.js:128](): Accesses `req.fresh` via `this.req` to check if response is fresh for 304 status - **`res.format()`** [lib/response.js:570](): Accesses `req.accepts()` via `this.req` for content negotiation - **`res.redirect()`** [lib/response.js:859](): Checks `req.method` via `this.req` to determine if body should be sent - **`res.render()`** [lib/response.js:895](): Accesses `req.app` via `this.req.app` to call `app.render()` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:128](), [lib/response.js:570](), [lib/response.js:859](), [lib/response.js:895]() --- ## Router Delegation and Middleware Execution After initialization, `app.handle()` delegates to `this.router.handle()` at [lib/application.js:177](). The router is lazily initialized and manages the middleware stack and route handlers. **Router Lazy Initialization** ```mermaid graph TB HANDLE["app.handle() invoked"] CHECK["Access this.router"] GETTER["router getter
[lib/application.js:72]"] NULL_CHECK{"router === null?"} CREATE["new Router()
with settings"] RETURN["Return router"] ROUTER_HANDLE["router.handle(req, res, done)"] HANDLE --> CHECK CHECK --> GETTER GETTER --> NULL_CHECK NULL_CHECK -->|Yes| CREATE NULL_CHECK -->|No| RETURN CREATE --> RETURN RETURN --> ROUTER_HANDLE ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:69-82](), [lib/application.js:177]() The router is created on first access with configuration from app settings: ```javascript router = new Router({ caseSensitive: this.enabled('case sensitive routing'), strict: this.enabled('strict routing') }); ``` See [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1) for details on how the router processes the request through middleware and routes. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:74-77]() --- ## Response Sending Once a route handler or middleware decides to send a response, it uses one of the response methods. These methods prepare headers, handle content negotiation, generate ETags, and finally call `res.end()` to send the response. **Common Response Methods and Their Flow** ```mermaid graph TB HANDLER["Route Handler
or Middleware"] subgraph "Response Methods" SEND["res.send()
[lib/response.js:125]"] JSON["res.json()
[lib/response.js:232]"] RENDER["res.render()
[lib/response.js:894]"] SENDFILE["res.sendFile()
[lib/response.js:371]"] REDIRECT["res.redirect()
[lib/response.js:812]"] end subgraph "Common Processing" HEADERS["Set Headers
(Content-Type, etc.)"] ETAG["Generate ETag
(if enabled)"] FRESH["Check Freshness
(304 Not Modified)"] BODY["Prepare Body
(Buffer/String)"] end END["res.end()
(Node.js method)"] HTTP["HTTP Response
to Client"] HANDLER --> SEND HANDLER --> JSON HANDLER --> RENDER HANDLER --> SENDFILE HANDLER --> REDIRECT SEND --> HEADERS JSON --> HEADERS RENDER --> HEADERS SENDFILE --> HEADERS REDIRECT --> HEADERS HEADERS --> ETAG ETAG --> FRESH FRESH --> BODY BODY --> END END --> HTTP ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125](), [lib/response.js:232](), [lib/response.js:894](), [lib/response.js:371](), [lib/response.js:812]() ### res.send() Processing Flow The `res.send()` method at [lib/response.js:125-218]() is the core response method that handles various body types: | Body Type | Processing | Content-Type | |-----------|------------|--------------| | `string` | Set charset to UTF-8 | `text/html; charset=utf-8` (default) | | `Buffer` or `Uint8Array` | Use as-is | `application/octet-stream` (if not set) | | `object`, `number`, `boolean` | Convert to JSON via `res.json()` | `application/json; charset=utf-8` | | `null` | Convert to empty string | (existing or none) | | `undefined` | Convert to empty string | (existing or none) | **Special Status Code Handling** [lib/response.js:195-207](): - **204 No Content**: Strips `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Transfer-Encoding`, and body - **205 Reset Content**: Sets `Content-Length: 0`, strips `Transfer-Encoding`, removes body - **304 Not Modified**: Strips `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Transfer-Encoding`, and body **HEAD Request Handling** [lib/response.js:209-214](): For HEAD requests, `res.send()` calls `res.end()` without a body, sending only headers. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125-218]() ### ETag Generation and Freshness If the `etag` setting is enabled (default: `'weak'`), Express generates an ETag header and checks request freshness: **ETag and Freshness Check Flow** [lib/response.js:161-192]() ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Send as res.send() participant ETag as ETag Generator participant Fresh as Freshness Check participant Req as req.fresh participant Response as res.end() Send->>ETag: Check etag setting alt etag enabled && no existing ETag ETag->>ETag: Generate ETag from body ETag->>Send: Set ETag header end Send->>Fresh: Check req.fresh alt req.fresh === true Fresh->>Send: Set status 304 Note over Send: Client has fresh cache end alt statusCode === 304 Send->>Send: Strip content headers end Send->>Response: res.end(chunk, encoding) ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:161-192](), [lib/request.js:469-486]() The `req.fresh` getter at [lib/request.js:469-486]() compares the request's `If-None-Match` (ETag) and `If-Modified-Since` headers against the response's `ETag` and `Last-Modified` headers using the `fresh` module. --- ## Complete Request-Response Lifecycle The following diagram shows the complete journey of a request through Express: **End-to-End Request-Response Lifecycle** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as HTTP Client participant Server as Node.js http.Server participant App as Express Application participant Handle as app.handle() participant Router as Router participant MW as Middleware Stack participant Param as app.param() callbacks participant Route as Route Handler participant Res as Response Method participant End as res.end() Client->>Server: HTTP Request Note over Server: TCP connection
parsed headers Server->>App: app(req, res) Note over App: Application is a function
[lib/express.js:37] App->>Handle: app.handle(req, res, next) Note over Handle: [lib/application.js:152] Handle->>Handle: Setup finalhandler Handle->>Handle: Set X-Powered-By (if enabled) Handle->>Handle: req.res = res
res.req = req Handle->>Handle: Object.setPrototypeOf(req)
Object.setPrototypeOf(res) Handle->>Handle: res.locals = {} Handle->>Router: router.handle(req, res, done) Note over Router: [lib/application.js:177] Router->>MW: Execute app.use() middleware MW->>MW: next() between middleware Router->>Param: Execute app.param() callbacks Note over Param: Process route parameters Router->>Route: route.dispatch(req, res, next) Note over Route: Match HTTP method Route->>Route: Execute route handler(s) Route->>Res: res.send() / res.json() / etc. Note over Res: [lib/response.js:125] Res->>Res: Set Content-Type header Res->>Res: Generate ETag (if enabled) Res->>Res: Check req.fresh (304) Res->>Res: Prepare body (Buffer/String) alt HEAD request Res->>End: res.end() (no body) else Res->>End: res.end(body, encoding) end End->>Server: Response complete Server->>Client: HTTP Response ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:37-39](), [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/response.js:125-218]() --- ## Key Lifecycle Components Summary | Component | Location | Responsibility | |-----------|----------|----------------| | **Application Function** | [lib/express.js:37-39]() | Receives requests from HTTP server, delegates to `app.handle()` | | **app.handle()** | [lib/application.js:152-178]() | Initializes request/response, sets up prototypes and circular refs, delegates to router | | **finalhandler** | [lib/application.js:154-157]() | Handles errors and unhandled requests, sends 404/500 responses | | **Prototype Extension** | [lib/application.js:169-170]() | Adds Express methods to req/res via `Object.setPrototypeOf()` | | **Circular References** | [lib/application.js:165-166]() | Creates `req.res` and `res.req` for bidirectional access | | **res.locals** | [lib/application.js:173-175]() | Initializes empty object for template variables | | **Router Delegation** | [lib/application.js:177]() | Hands off to router for middleware and route processing | | **Response Methods** | [lib/response.js]() | Methods like `send()`, `json()`, `render()` for sending responses | | **ETag Generation** | [lib/response.js:161-189]() | Generates ETag headers and checks freshness for 304 responses | | **res.end()** | Native Node.js | Finalizes and sends HTTP response to client | **Sources:** [lib/express.js:37-39](), [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/response.js:125-218]() --- # Page: Settings and Utilities # Settings and Utilities

This page documents Express's application settings system, compiled settings mechanism, and utility functions. It covers how settings are stored, retrieved, and compiled into optimized functions that affect request/response processing. For information about view-related settings and template rendering, see [View and Template System](#6). For request and response object properties, see [Request Object](#2.2) and [Response Object](#2.3). ## Overview Express uses a flexible settings system where configuration values can be stored using `app.set()` and retrieved using `app.get()`. Certain critical settings are automatically compiled into optimized functions when set, including `etag`, `query parser`, and `trust proxy`. The `lib/utils.js` module provides utility functions for ETag generation, content type normalization, and settings compilation. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:351-383](), [lib/utils.js:1-272]() ## Application Settings System ### Settings Storage and Access ```mermaid graph TB AppInit["app.init()"] Settings["this.settings = Object.create(null)"] Cache["this.cache = Object.create(null)"] Engines["this.engines = Object.create(null)"] SetMethod["app.set(setting, val)"] GetMethod["app.get(setting)"] EnableMethod["app.enable(setting)"] DisableMethod["app.disable(setting)"] EnabledMethod["app.enabled(setting)"] DisabledMethod["app.disabled(setting)"] SettingsObject["this.settings[setting]"] AppInit --> Settings AppInit --> Cache AppInit --> Engines SetMethod --> SettingsObject GetMethod --> SettingsObject EnableMethod --> SetMethod DisableMethod --> SetMethod EnabledMethod --> GetMethod DisabledMethod --> GetMethod ``` **Diagram: Settings System Architecture** The application object maintains three primary storage objects initialized in `app.init()`: - `this.settings` - stores configuration values - `this.cache` - stores compiled view objects - `this.engines` - stores registered template engine functions **Sources:** [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/application.js:351-383]() ### Settings Methods | Method | Description | Example | |--------|-------------|---------| | `app.set(setting, val)` | Assign value to setting | `app.set('etag', 'weak')` | | `app.get(setting)` | Retrieve setting value | `app.get('etag')` | | `app.enable(setting)` | Set setting to `true` | `app.enable('trust proxy')` | | `app.disable(setting)` | Set setting to `false` | `app.disable('view cache')` | | `app.enabled(setting)` | Check if setting is truthy | `app.enabled('etag')` | | `app.disabled(setting)` | Check if setting is falsy | `app.disabled('view cache')` | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:351-383](), [lib/application.js:420-465]() ### Default Configuration ```mermaid graph LR DefaultConfig["app.defaultConfiguration()"] XPoweredBy["x-powered-by: true"] Etag["etag: 'weak'"] Env["env: NODE_ENV"] QueryParser["query parser: 'simple'"] SubdomainOffset["subdomain offset: 2"] TrustProxy["trust proxy: false"] ViewSetting["view: View"] Views["views: resolve('views')"] JsonpCallback["jsonp callback name: 'callback'"] ViewCache["view cache: true (production)"] DefaultConfig --> XPoweredBy DefaultConfig --> Etag DefaultConfig --> Env DefaultConfig --> QueryParser DefaultConfig --> SubdomainOffset DefaultConfig --> TrustProxy DefaultConfig --> ViewSetting DefaultConfig --> Views DefaultConfig --> JsonpCallback DefaultConfig --> ViewCache ``` **Diagram: Default Settings Applied on Initialization** Express applies these default settings when an application is initialized: | Setting | Default Value | Purpose | |---------|---------------|---------| | `x-powered-by` | `true` | Send `X-Powered-By: Express` header | | `etag` | `'weak'` | Enable weak ETag generation | | `env` | `process.env.NODE_ENV \|\| 'development'` | Current environment | | `query parser` | `'simple'` | Use built-in querystring parser | | `subdomain offset` | `2` | Number of dot-separated parts to remove from host | | `trust proxy` | `false` | Do not trust proxy headers | | `view` | `View` constructor | View class for rendering | | `views` | `resolve('views')` | Directory for template files | | `jsonp callback name` | `'callback'` | Query parameter name for JSONP | | `view cache` | `true` (production only) | Cache compiled templates | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:90-141]() ## Compiled Settings ### Compilation Mechanism When specific settings are modified via `app.set()`, Express automatically compiles them into optimized functions. This compilation happens in the `app.set()` method through a switch statement. ```mermaid graph TB AppSet["app.set(setting, val)"] StoreValue["this.settings[setting] = val"] SwitchCase{"switch(setting)"} CaseEtag["case 'etag'"] CaseQuery["case 'query parser'"] CaseTrust["case 'trust proxy'"] CompileEtag["compileETag(val)"] CompileQuery["compileQueryParser(val)"] CompileTrust["compileTrust(val)"] SetEtagFn["this.set('etag fn', fn)"] SetQueryFn["this.set('query parser fn', fn)"] SetTrustFn["this.set('trust proxy fn', fn)"] AppSet --> StoreValue StoreValue --> SwitchCase SwitchCase --> CaseEtag SwitchCase --> CaseQuery SwitchCase --> CaseTrust CaseEtag --> CompileEtag CaseQuery --> CompileQuery CaseTrust --> CompileTrust CompileEtag --> SetEtagFn CompileQuery --> SetQueryFn CompileTrust --> SetTrustFn ``` **Diagram: Settings Compilation Flow** **Sources:** [lib/application.js:362-380]() ### ETag Compilation The `compileETag()` function in `lib/utils.js` converts ETag setting values into optimized ETag generator functions. ```mermaid graph TB CompileETag["compileETag(val)"] TypeCheck{"typeof val"} ValueCheck{"switch(val)"} FunctionType["'function'"] BoolString["Boolean/String"] ReturnVal["return val"] True["true / 'weak'"] False["false"] Strong["'strong'"] Invalid["other"] ReturnWetag["return wetag"] ReturnUndefined["return undefined"] ReturnEtag["return etag"] ThrowError["throw TypeError"] CompileETag --> TypeCheck TypeCheck --> FunctionType --> ReturnVal TypeCheck --> BoolString --> ValueCheck ValueCheck --> True --> ReturnWetag ValueCheck --> False --> ReturnUndefined ValueCheck --> Strong --> ReturnEtag ValueCheck --> Invalid --> ThrowError Wetag["exports.wetag = createETagGenerator({weak: true})"] Etag["exports.etag = createETagGenerator({weak: false})"] ReturnWetag -.-> Wetag ReturnEtag -.-> Etag ``` **Diagram: ETag Compilation Logic** | Input Value | Compiled Function | Description | |-------------|-------------------|-------------| | `true` | `wetag` | Weak ETag generator | | `'weak'` | `wetag` | Weak ETag generator | | `false` | `undefined` | No ETag generation | | `'strong'` | `etag` | Strong ETag generator | | `Function` | Input function | Custom ETag generator | | Other | Throws `TypeError` | Invalid value | **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:130-152]() ### ETag Generator Functions ```mermaid graph TB CreateGenerator["createETagGenerator(options)"] GenerateETag["generateETag(body, encoding)"] BufferCheck{"Buffer.isBuffer(body)"} CreateBuffer["Buffer.from(body, encoding)"] UseBody["buf = body"] EtagModule["etag(buf, options)"] ReturnETag["return ETag string"] CreateGenerator --> GenerateETag GenerateETag --> BufferCheck BufferCheck -->|false| CreateBuffer BufferCheck -->|true| UseBody CreateBuffer --> EtagModule UseBody --> EtagModule EtagModule --> ReturnETag Wetag["exports.wetag = createETagGenerator({weak: true})"] Etag["exports.etag = createETagGenerator({weak: false})"] CreateGenerator -.-> Wetag CreateGenerator -.-> Etag ``` **Diagram: ETag Generator Function Creation** The `createETagGenerator()` function creates closures that generate ETags using the `etag` npm module. Two default generators are exported: - `exports.wetag` - generates weak ETags (prefixed with `W/`) - `exports.etag` - generates strong ETags (no prefix) **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:40-52](), [lib/utils.js:249-257]() ### Query Parser Compilation ```mermaid graph TB CompileQuery["compileQueryParser(val)"] TypeCheck{"typeof val"} ValueSwitch{"switch(val)"} IsFunction["'function'"] OtherType["Boolean/String"] ReturnVal["return val"] TrueSimple["true / 'simple'"] False["false"] Extended["'extended'"] Invalid["other"] ReturnQuerystring["return querystring.parse"] ReturnUndefined["return undefined"] ReturnExtended["return parseExtendedQueryString"] ThrowError["throw TypeError"] CompileQuery --> TypeCheck TypeCheck --> IsFunction --> ReturnVal TypeCheck --> OtherType --> ValueSwitch ValueSwitch --> TrueSimple --> ReturnQuerystring ValueSwitch --> False --> ReturnUndefined ValueSwitch --> Extended --> ReturnExtended ValueSwitch --> Invalid --> ThrowError QuerystringParse["querystring.parse from Node.js"] ParseExtended["parseExtendedQueryString = qs.parse"] ReturnQuerystring -.-> QuerystringParse ReturnExtended -.-> ParseExtended ``` **Diagram: Query Parser Compilation Logic** | Input Value | Compiled Function | Description | |-------------|-------------------|-------------| | `true` | `querystring.parse` | Node.js built-in parser | | `'simple'` | `querystring.parse` | Node.js built-in parser | | `false` | `undefined` | No query parsing | | `'extended'` | `parseExtendedQueryString` | `qs` module parser | | `Function` | Input function | Custom query parser | | Other | Throws `TypeError` | Invalid value | The extended query parser uses the `qs` module with `allowPrototypes: true`. **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:162-184](), [lib/utils.js:267-271]() ### Trust Proxy Compilation ```mermaid graph TB CompileTrust["compileTrust(val)"] TypeCheck{"typeof val"} IsFunction["'function'"] IsTrue["true"] IsNumber["'number'"] IsString["'string'"] OtherTypes["Array / false / other"] ReturnVal["return val"] ReturnTrue["return () => true"] ReturnHop["return (a, i) => i < val"] SplitString["val.split(',').map(v => v.trim())"] ProxyCompile["proxyaddr.compile(val || [])"] CompileTrust --> TypeCheck TypeCheck --> IsFunction --> ReturnVal TypeCheck --> IsTrue --> ReturnTrue TypeCheck --> IsNumber --> ReturnHop TypeCheck --> IsString --> SplitString TypeCheck --> OtherTypes --> ProxyCompile SplitString --> ProxyCompile ``` **Diagram: Trust Proxy Compilation Logic** | Input Value | Compiled Function | Description | |-------------|-------------------|-------------| | `Function` | Input function | Custom trust function | | `true` | `() => true` | Trust all proxies | | `Number` | `(a, i) => i < val` | Trust first N hops | | `String` | `proxyaddr.compile(...)` | Trust specific IPs/subnets | | `Array` | `proxyaddr.compile(val)` | Trust specific IPs/subnets | | `false` / other | `proxyaddr.compile([])` | Trust no proxies | The `proxyaddr.compile()` function from the `proxy-addr` module creates functions that determine whether to trust proxy headers based on IP addresses. **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:194-214]() ## Impact on Request Processing ### Query Parsing ```mermaid graph TB ReqQuery["req.query getter"] GetParser["this.app.get('query parser fn')"] CheckParser{"queryparse exists?"} ReturnEmpty["return Object.create(null)"] ParseUrl["parse(this).query"] CallParser["queryparse(querystring)"] ReturnParsed["return parsed object"] ReqQuery --> GetParser GetParser --> CheckParser CheckParser -->|false| ReturnEmpty CheckParser -->|true| ParseUrl ParseUrl --> CallParser CallParser --> ReturnParsed ``` **Diagram: Query Parsing in Request Object** The `req.query` getter uses the compiled query parser function stored in `app.get('query parser fn')`. If no parser is configured (setting is `false`), an empty object is returned. **Sources:** [lib/request.js:230-241]() ### Trust Proxy in Request ```mermaid graph TB ReqProtocol["req.protocol getter"] ReqIP["req.ip getter"] ReqHost["req.host getter"] GetTrust["this.app.get('trust proxy fn')"] CheckSocket["trust(this.socket.remoteAddress, 0)"] UseSocket["Use socket values"] UseHeaders["Use X-Forwarded-* headers"] ReqProtocol --> GetTrust ReqIP --> GetTrust ReqHost --> GetTrust GetTrust --> CheckSocket CheckSocket -->|false| UseSocket CheckSocket -->|true| UseHeaders ProxyAddr["proxyaddr(this, trust)"] XForwardedProto["X-Forwarded-Proto header"] XForwardedHost["X-Forwarded-Host header"] ReqIP --> ProxyAddr ReqProtocol --> XForwardedProto ReqHost --> XForwardedHost ``` **Diagram: Trust Proxy Impact on Request Properties** The compiled trust proxy function affects multiple request properties: - `req.protocol` - uses `X-Forwarded-Proto` if proxy trusted - `req.ip` - determined by `proxyaddr()` using trust function - `req.ips` - array of trusted proxy addresses - `req.host` - uses `X-Forwarded-Host` if proxy trusted - `req.hostname` - derived from `req.host` **Sources:** [lib/request.js:297-315](), [lib/request.js:340-343](), [lib/request.js:418-431]() ### ETag Generation in Response ```mermaid graph TB ResSend["res.send(body)"] GetEtagFn["app.get('etag fn')"] CheckEtag{"ETag header exists?"} CheckFn{"typeof etagFn === 'function'"} GenerateETag["generateETag = true"] SkipETag["generateETag = false"] CheckLen{"len !== undefined"} CallFn["etag = etagFn(chunk, encoding)"] SetETag["this.set('ETag', etag)"] ResSend --> GetEtagFn GetEtagFn --> CheckEtag CheckEtag -->|exists| SkipETag CheckEtag -->|not exists| CheckFn CheckFn -->|true| GenerateETag CheckFn -->|false| SkipETag GenerateETag --> CheckLen CheckLen -->|true| CallFn CheckLen -->|false| SkipETag CallFn --> SetETag ``` **Diagram: ETag Generation in res.send()** The `res.send()` method automatically generates ETags when: 1. No `ETag` header is already set 2. The `etag fn` setting is a function 3. The response body length is known **Sources:** [lib/response.js:160-189]() ## Utility Functions ### Content Type Normalization ```mermaid graph TB NormalizeType["normalizeType(type)"] CheckSlash{"type.indexOf('/') !== -1"} AcceptParams["acceptParams(type)"] MimeLookup["mime.lookup(type)"] ReturnParsed["return {value, params, quality}"] ReturnMime["return {value: mimeType, params: {}}"] ReturnDefault["return {value: 'application/octet-stream', params: {}}"] NormalizeType --> CheckSlash CheckSlash -->|true| AcceptParams CheckSlash -->|false| MimeLookup AcceptParams --> ReturnParsed MimeLookup --> ReturnMime MimeLookup -->|null| ReturnDefault ``` **Diagram: Content Type Normalization Flow** The `normalizeType()` function converts type strings into structured objects: - If type contains `/` (e.g., `text/html`), parse as full MIME type - Otherwise (e.g., `html`), look up MIME type using `mime.lookup()` - Returns object with `value`, `params`, and `quality` properties The `normalizeTypes()` function maps an array of types through `normalizeType()`. **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:61-77]() ### Accept Parameters Parsing ```mermaid graph TB AcceptParams["acceptParams(str)"] FindSemicolon["colonIndex = str.indexOf(';')"] InitObject["ret = {value, quality: 1, params: {}}"] LoopParams["while (index < length)"] FindEquals["splitIndex = str.indexOf('=', index)"] FindNextSemi["endIndex = str.indexOf(';', index)"] ExtractKey["key = str.slice(index, splitIndex).trim()"] ExtractValue["value = str.slice(splitIndex + 1, endIndex).trim()"] IsQuality{"key === 'q'"} SetQuality["ret.quality = parseFloat(value)"] SetParam["ret.params[key] = value"] ReturnObject["return ret"] AcceptParams --> FindSemicolon FindSemicolon --> InitObject InitObject --> LoopParams LoopParams --> FindEquals FindEquals --> FindNextSemi FindNextSemi --> ExtractKey ExtractKey --> ExtractValue ExtractValue --> IsQuality IsQuality -->|true| SetQuality IsQuality -->|false| SetParam SetQuality --> LoopParams SetParam --> LoopParams LoopParams -->|done| ReturnObject ``` **Diagram: Accept Parameters Parsing Logic** The `acceptParams()` function parses MIME type strings with parameters (e.g., `text/html;charset=utf-8;q=0.9`) into structured objects. It handles the special `q` parameter for quality values. **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:89-120]() ### Charset Handling The `setCharset()` function modifies Content-Type strings to include or update charset parameters: ```mermaid graph TB SetCharset["setCharset(type, charset)"] CheckInputs{"type && charset"} ReturnType["return type"] ParseType["parsed = contentType.parse(type)"] SetCharsetParam["parsed.parameters.charset = charset"] FormatType["return contentType.format(parsed)"] SetCharset --> CheckInputs CheckInputs -->|false| ReturnType CheckInputs -->|true| ParseType ParseType --> SetCharsetParam SetCharsetParam --> FormatType ``` **Diagram: Charset Setting Flow** Example transformations: - `setCharset('text/html', 'utf-8')` → `'text/html; charset=utf-8'` - `setCharset('text/html; charset=iso-8859-1', 'utf-8')` → `'text/html; charset=utf-8'` **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:225-238]() ## Settings in Response Methods ### res.send() Settings Usage | Setting | Usage in res.send() | Effect | |---------|---------------------|--------| | `etag fn` | Generates ETag header | Enables conditional GET requests | | `json escape` | Escapes HTML characters in JSON | Security: prevents XSS in JSON responses | | `json replacer` | JSON.stringify replacer | Customizes JSON serialization | | `json spaces` | JSON.stringify spaces | Controls JSON formatting | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125-218](), [lib/response.js:232-246]() ### res.render() Settings Usage | Setting | Usage in res.render() | Effect | |---------|----------------------|--------| | `view` | View constructor | Class used to instantiate views | | `view engine` | Default template engine | Used when view name has no extension | | `views` | Views directory path | Where to find template files | | `view cache` | Enable template caching | Performance: reuse compiled templates | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:522-575](), [lib/response.js:894-918]() ### res.sendFile() Settings Usage ```mermaid graph TB SendFile["res.sendFile(path, options)"] GetEtagSetting["opts.etag = this.app.enabled('etag')"] CreateSend["file = send(req, pathname, opts)"] SendModule["send module"] HandleETag["Automatically generates ETag"] HandleCaching["Handles conditional requests"] SendFile --> GetEtagSetting GetEtagSetting --> CreateSend CreateSend --> SendModule SendModule --> HandleETag SendModule --> HandleCaching ``` **Diagram: Settings Usage in res.sendFile()** The `res.sendFile()` method wires the application's `etag` setting to the `send` module, which handles file streaming and automatic ETag generation. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:371-413]() ## Methods List Reference ### Application Settings Methods | Method | File Location | Description | |--------|---------------|-------------| | `app.set()` | [lib/application.js:351-383]() | Set or get application setting | | `app.get()` | [lib/application.js:351-383]() | Get application setting (when 1 arg) | | `app.enable()` | [lib/application.js:451-453]() | Set setting to `true` | | `app.disable()` | [lib/application.js:463-465]() | Set setting to `false` | | `app.enabled()` | [lib/application.js:420-422]() | Check if setting is truthy | | `app.disabled()` | [lib/application.js:439-441]() | Check if setting is falsy | ### Utility Compilation Functions | Function | File Location | Purpose | |----------|---------------|---------| | `compileETag()` | [lib/utils.js:130-152]() | Compile ETag setting to function | | `compileQueryParser()` | [lib/utils.js:162-184]() | Compile query parser setting to function | | `compileTrust()` | [lib/utils.js:194-214]() | Compile trust proxy setting to function | ### Utility Helper Functions | Function | File Location | Purpose | |----------|---------------|---------| | `etag()` | [lib/utils.js:40]() | Generate strong ETag | | `wetag()` | [lib/utils.js:51]() | Generate weak ETag | | `normalizeType()` | [lib/utils.js:61-65]() | Normalize content type string | | `normalizeTypes()` | [lib/utils.js:75-77]() | Normalize array of content types | | `setCharset()` | [lib/utils.js:225-238]() | Set charset in Content-Type | | `acceptParams()` | [lib/utils.js:89-120]() | Parse accept parameters | | `createETagGenerator()` | [lib/utils.js:249-257]() | Create ETag generator function | | `parseExtendedQueryString()` | [lib/utils.js:267-271]() | Parse query string with `qs` | **Sources:** [lib/utils.js:1-272]() --- # Page: Routing System # Routing System
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for generating this wiki page: - [test/Route.js](test/Route.js) - [test/Router.js](test/Router.js) - [test/app.router.js](test/app.router.js) - [test/app.use.js](test/app.use.js)
The Routing System is the core request dispatching mechanism in Express.js. It determines which handler functions execute for incoming HTTP requests based on the request path and HTTP method. This system provides hierarchical, modular route organization through three primary components: Router, Route, and Layer. For information about middleware execution within routes, see [Middleware System](#4). For details about request/response handling, see [Request Object](#2.2) and [Response Object](#2.3). ## Purpose and Architecture The routing system sits between the Application instance and handler functions, matching incoming requests to the appropriate handlers based on URL paths and HTTP methods. Express delegates routing to the external `router` package (version 2.2.0), which implements the pattern matching and request dispatching logic. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Express Application Layer" app["app (Application instance)"] app_lazyrouter["app.lazyrouter()"] app_handle["app.handle(req, res, done)"] end subgraph "Router Package Layer" Router["Router constructor
(from router package)"] router_handle["router.handle(req, res, out)"] router_stack["router.stack[]
(array of Layers)"] end subgraph "Layer Abstraction" Layer["Layer
(path matching wrapper)"] layer_match["layer.match(path)"] layer_handle_request["layer.handle_request(req, res, next)"] end subgraph "Route Handler Level" Route["Route
(method-specific handlers)"] route_dispatch["route.dispatch(req, res, done)"] route_stack["route.stack[]
(method handlers)"] end app --> app_lazyrouter app_lazyrouter -.creates once.-> Router app_handle --> router_handle Router --> router_stack router_stack --> Layer Layer --> layer_match Layer --> layer_handle_request layer_handle_request -.route layer.-> Route layer_handle_request -.middleware layer.-> HandlerFn["Handler Function"] Route --> route_dispatch Route --> route_stack route_stack --> HandlerFn ``` **Diagram: Routing System Architecture with Code Entities** **Core Components:** | Component | Code Location | Purpose | |-----------|---------------|---------| | **Router** | `router` package | Top-level routing container created by `app.lazyrouter()` with `caseSensitive` and `strict` options. Manages `router.stack` array of Layer objects. | | **Route** | `router` package | Represents a single path pattern. Contains `route.stack` array of method-specific handlers. Created via `router.route(path)`. | | **Layer** | `router` package | Internal wrapper that performs path matching via `layer.match(path)` and invokes handlers via `layer.handle_request()`. Each Layer holds either middleware or a Route object. | Sources: [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/express.js:26](), [package.json:57]() ## Router Integration Express applications lazily create a Router instance when routes or middleware are first registered. The Application prototype exposes routing methods that delegate to this internal router. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Methods" app_get["app.get(path, ...fns)"] app_post["app.post(path, ...fns)"] app_use["app.use([path], ...fns)"] app_route["app.route(path)"] app_param["app.param(name, fn)"] end subgraph "Lazy Router Creation" lazyrouter["app.lazyrouter()
(lib/application.js:59-83)"] router_check{"this._router
exists?"} router_create["new Router({
caseSensitive: app.get('case sensitive routing'),
strict: app.get('strict routing')
})"] end subgraph "Router Methods" router_get["router.get(path, ...fns)"] router_use["router.use([path], ...fns)"] router_route["router.route(path)"] router_param["router.param(name, fn)"] router_handle["router.handle(req, res, out)"] end app_get --> lazyrouter app_post --> lazyrouter app_use --> lazyrouter app_route --> lazyrouter app_param --> lazyrouter lazyrouter --> router_check router_check -->|"No (first use)"| router_create router_check -->|"Yes"| router_get router_create --> router_get app_get -.delegates to.-> router_get app_use -.delegates to.-> router_use app_route -.delegates to.-> router_route app_param -.delegates to.-> router_param ``` **Diagram: Lazy Router Creation and Method Delegation** The `app.lazyrouter()` method implements lazy initialization: 1. Check if `this._router` is `null` (uninitialized) 2. If `null`, create new `Router` instance with application settings: - `caseSensitive`: from `app.enabled('case sensitive routing')` - `strict`: from `app.enabled('strict routing')` 3. Store router in `this._router` 4. Return router instance All routing methods (`app.get()`, `app.post()`, `app.use()`, etc.) call `app.lazyrouter()` first to ensure the router exists before delegating to router methods. Sources: [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/application.js:471-482](), [lib/application.js:190-244]() ## Routing Hierarchy Express supports a three-level routing hierarchy that enables modular application structure: | Level | Scope | Definition Method | Example | |-------|-------|------------------|---------| | **Application** | Global to app | `app.get()`, `app.post()`, `app.use()` | `app.get('/users', handler)` | | **Router** | Mountable sub-application | `router.get()`, `router.use()` | `router.get('/:id', handler)` | | **Route** | Single path, multiple methods | `route.get()`, `route.post()` | `route.all(middleware).get(handler)` | ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Level" App["express() app"] AppRouter["app.router"] end subgraph "Router Level" Router1["express.Router()
mounted at /api"] Router2["express.Router()
mounted at /admin"] end subgraph "Route Level" Route1["Route: /api/users/:id"] RouteGET["GET handler"] RoutePOST["POST handler"] RouteDEL["DELETE handler"] end App -->|"app.use('/api', router)"| Router1 App -->|"app.use('/admin', router)"| Router2 Router1 -->|"router.route('/users/:id')"| Route1 Route1 -->|"route.get()"| RouteGET Route1 -->|"route.post()"| RoutePOST Route1 -->|"route.delete()"| RouteDEL ``` **Diagram: Three-Level Routing Hierarchy** ### Router Mounting Routers are mounted using `app.use()` with an optional path prefix. When mounted: - Router's `mountpath` property is set to the mount path - Router's `parent` property references the parent app/router - The `'mount'` event is emitted on the router - Parameters can merge with parent using `mergeParams: true` option Sources: [lib/application.js:190-244](), [test/app.router.js:287-421]() ## HTTP Method Routing Express automatically creates routing methods for all HTTP methods supported by Node.js. These methods are generated dynamically from the `methods` array imported from `node:http`. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Source: node:http" http_METHODS["http.METHODS array
['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE', ...]"] end subgraph "lib/utils.js" utils_methods["exports.methods =
http.METHODS.map(toLowerCase)"] end subgraph "lib/application.js:471-482" method_loop["methods.forEach(function(method) { ... })"] app_method["app[method] = function(path) {
this.lazyrouter();
var route = this._router.route(path);
route[method].apply(route, slice.call(arguments, 1));
return this;
}"] end subgraph "Generated Application Methods" app_get["app.get(path, ...handlers)"] app_post["app.post(path, ...handlers)"] app_put["app.put(path, ...handlers)"] app_delete["app.delete(path, ...handlers)"] app_patch["app.patch(path, ...handlers)"] app_etc["app.options(), app.head()
...and all other HTTP methods"] end http_METHODS --> utils_methods utils_methods --> method_loop method_loop --> app_method app_method -.creates.-> app_get app_method -.creates.-> app_post app_method -.creates.-> app_put app_method -.creates.-> app_delete app_method -.creates.-> app_patch app_method -.creates.-> app_etc ``` **Diagram: HTTP Method Route Generation from node:http** The method generation process in [lib/application.js:471-482](): 1. Import `methods` array from `lib/utils.js`, which lowercases `http.METHODS` 2. Loop through each method: `methods.forEach(function(method) { ... })` 3. Create function `app[method]` that: - Calls `this.lazyrouter()` to ensure router exists - Calls `this._router.route(path)` to get/create Route object - Delegates to `route[method].apply(route, arguments)` to register handlers - Returns `this` for chaining **Special Case**: `app.get(name)` with single string argument retrieves application settings via `this.set(name)` instead of registering a route. This is handled by checking `arguments.length === 1` in the function body. **Generated Methods**: All HTTP verbs from Node.js become routing methods: `get`, `post`, `put`, `delete`, `patch`, `head`, `options`, `trace`, `connect`, etc. Sources: [lib/utils.js:29](), [lib/application.js:471-482](), [test/app.router.js:39-92]() ## Route Definition Patterns Routes can be defined in multiple ways depending on application structure needs: ### Direct Application Routes ```javascript // Single handler app.get('/users', function(req, res) { res.send('User list'); }); // Multiple handlers (middleware chain) app.post('/users', validateUser, sanitizeInput, createUser ); ``` ### Router-Based Routes ```javascript const router = express.Router(); // Define routes on router router.get('/', listUsers); router.post('/', createUser); router.get('/:id', getUser); // Mount router on app app.use('/users', router); ``` ### Route Objects ```javascript // Get route object for path const userRoute = app.route('/users/:id'); // Chain multiple methods userRoute .get(getUser) .put(updateUser) .delete(deleteUser); ``` Sources: [test/app.router.js:39-92](), [lib/application.js:256-258](), [lib/application.js:471-503]() ## Request Dispatching Flow When an HTTP request arrives, it flows through the routing system in a specific sequence: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant req as "req (IncomingMessage)" participant res as "res (ServerResponse)" participant app_handle as "app.handle(req, res)" participant router_handle as "router.handle(req, res, done)" participant layer as "Layer" participant route as "Route" participant handler as "handler(req, res, next)" req->>app_handle: "HTTP request received" app_handle->>app_handle: "Set req.__proto__ = app.request
Set res.__proto__ = app.response" app_handle->>app_handle: "Set req.res = res, res.req = req" app_handle->>app_handle: "app.lazyrouter() ensures router exists" app_handle->>router_handle: "this._router.handle(req, res, done)" loop "For each layer in router.stack" router_handle->>layer: "layer.match(req.url)" alt "Path matches" layer->>layer: "Extract req.params from path" alt "Layer is Route" layer->>route: "layer.route.dispatch(req, res, next)" route->>route: "Find matching HTTP method handler" alt "Method matches" route->>handler: "Execute handler(req, res, next)" handler-->>route: "Send response or call next()" else "Method not found" route-->>router_handle: "next() - skip to next route" end else "Layer is Middleware" layer->>handler: "Execute middleware(req, res, next)" handler-->>router_handle: "next() - continue chain" end else "Path does not match" layer-->>router_handle: "Skip to next layer" end end router_handle-->>app_handle: "Complete or invoke done()" app_handle-->>res: "Send 404 if no match" ``` **Diagram: Request Dispatching Sequence with Code Methods** The dispatch process: 1. **Application Entry**: `app.handle(req, res)` in [lib/application.js:152-178]() receives request 2. **Prototype Setup**: Sets `req.__proto__` and `res.__proto__` to extend with Express methods 3. **Router Creation**: Calls `app.lazyrouter()` to ensure `this._router` exists 4. **Router Dispatch**: Calls `this._router.handle(req, res, done)` where `done` is the final handler 5. **Layer Iteration**: Router iterates `router.stack[]` array of Layer objects 6. **Path Matching**: Each Layer's `layer.match(path)` tests if path pattern matches 7. **Parameter Extraction**: Matching layers populate `req.params` with extracted parameters 8. **Route Dispatch**: If Layer contains a Route, calls `layer.route.dispatch(req, res, next)` 9. **Method Matching**: Route checks if handler exists for `req.method` 10. **Handler Execution**: Executes matching handler or middleware function 11. **Completion**: Either response is sent or `done()` callback invoked for 404 Sources: [lib/application.js:152-178](), [test/app.router.js:39-142](), [test/Router.js:162-178](), [test/app.router.js:818-869]() ## Route Parameters Routes can include named parameters that capture URL segments: ```javascript // Named parameters app.get('/users/:userId/posts/:postId', function(req, res) { // req.params.userId and req.params.postId available res.send(`User ${req.params.userId}, Post ${req.params.postId}`); }); // Regex patterns with capture groups app.get(/^\/user\/([0-9]+)$/, function(req, res) { // req.params[0] contains captured numeric ID res.send(`User ${req.params[0]}`); }); ``` Parameters are decoded using `decodeURIComponent`, and malformed percent-encoding results in a 400 error. The `mergeParams` router option controls whether parameters from parent routers are inherited. Sources: [test/app.router.js:94-142](), [test/app.router.js:287-421]() ## Case Sensitivity and Trailing Slashes Router behavior for path matching is controlled by application settings: | Setting | Default | Behavior | |---------|---------|----------| | `'case sensitive routing'` | `false` | When enabled, `/User` and `/user` are different routes | | `'strict routing'` | `false` | When enabled, `/user` and `/user/` are different routes | These settings are passed to the Router constructor when the router is created: ```javascript router = new Router({ caseSensitive: this.enabled('case sensitive routing'), strict: this.enabled('strict routing') }); ``` With default settings (both disabled), routes match case-insensitively and trailing slashes are optional. Sources: [lib/application.js:74-77](), [test/app.router.js:243-285](), [test/app.router.js:423-575]() ## Router Methods and Properties The Router instance (from the `router` package) exposes these primary methods used by Express: | Method | Signature | Purpose | Application Delegate | |--------|-----------|---------|---------------------| | `router.handle()` | `handle(req, res, out)` | Main entry point for request dispatching through Layer stack | `app.handle()` calls this in [lib/application.js:162]() | | `router.use()` | `use([path], ...handlers)` | Register middleware functions for optional path prefix | `app.use()` in [lib/application.js:190-244]() | | `router.route()` | `route(path)` | Create/retrieve Route instance for path | `app.route()` in [lib/application.js:256-258]() | | `router.param()` | `param(name, callback)` | Register parameter preprocessing callback | `app.param()` in [lib/application.js:322-334]() | | `router[METHOD]()` | `[method](path, ...handlers)` | Register handlers for specific HTTP method | `app[method]()` in [lib/application.js:471-482]() | | `router.all()` | `all(path, ...handlers)` | Register handlers for all HTTP methods | `app.all()` in [lib/application.js:485-503]() | **Router Properties:** | Property | Type | Purpose | |----------|------|---------| | `router.stack` | `Layer[]` | Array of Layer objects representing registered routes and middleware | | `router.params` | `Object` | Map of parameter names to preprocessing callback arrays | | `router.caseSensitive` | `boolean` | Whether path matching is case-sensitive | | `router.strict` | `boolean` | Whether trailing slashes are significant | The Application prototype delegates to these methods when routes are registered, ensuring `app.lazyrouter()` is called first to create the router if needed. Sources: [lib/application.js:59-83](), [lib/application.js:190-244](), [lib/application.js:256-258](), [lib/application.js:322-334](), [lib/application.js:471-503](), [test/Router.js:1-636]() ## Special Routing Features ### Multiple Handlers Routes accept arrays of handlers that execute sequentially: ```javascript app.get('/users', [ authenticate, authorize, loadUsers, renderUsers ]); ``` Nested arrays are flattened automatically. ### Regex Paths Routes can use regular expressions for complex matching: ```javascript // Match /user/123 but not /user/abc app.get(/^\/user\/[0-9]+$/, handler); // Named capture groups (Node.js with regex support) app.get(/^\/user\/(?[0-9]+)$/, function(req, res) { // req.params.userId available }); ``` Sources: [test/app.router.js:169-241](), [lib/application.js:190-244]() ## Integration Points The routing system integrates with other Express subsystems: - **Application**: Routes registered via `app.METHOD()` and `app.use()` - **Middleware**: Router treats middleware as routes with no method restriction - **Error Handling**: Error handlers (arity 4) bypass normal routes - **Request/Response**: Handler functions receive `req` and `res` objects - **Settings**: Case sensitivity and strict routing affect path matching For detailed information on these integration points, see: - Route and Router architecture details: [Router and Route Architecture](#3.1) - Parameter handling and path matching: [Path Matching and Parameters](#3.2) - Error propagation through routes: [Error Handling in Routes](#3.3) Sources: [lib/application.js:152-178](), [lib/application.js:471-503](), [test/app.router.js:1-1450]() --- # Page: Router and Route Architecture # Router and Route Architecture

## Purpose and Scope This document describes the routing architecture in Express.js, covering the three-tier hierarchy of Application, Router, and Route objects, and how they work together to dispatch HTTP requests to handler functions. This includes router creation, route registration, path matching, parameter extraction, and the request dispatch flow. For information about middleware execution and the `next()` function, see [4.1](#4.1). For path matching patterns and parameter types, see [3.2](#3.2). For error handling flow in routes, see [3.3](#3.3). --- ## Three-Tier Routing Hierarchy Express uses a three-tier architecture for routing: **Application** → **Router** → **Route**. Each tier provides increasingly specific request handling capabilities. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Tier 1: Application" APP["Express Application
(app function)"] APP_ROUTER["app.router
(lazily created)"] end subgraph "Tier 2: Router Instances" ROUTER1["Router Instance
(created via express.Router)"] ROUTER2["Nested Router
(mounted on paths)"] end subgraph "Tier 3: Route Objects" ROUTE1["Route('/users')
GET, POST handlers"] ROUTE2["Route('/products/:id')
GET, PUT, DELETE handlers"] ROUTE3["Route('/admin/*')
ALL handler"] end APP --> APP_ROUTER APP_ROUTER --> ROUTE1 APP_ROUTER --> ROUTE2 ROUTER1 --> ROUTE3 APP -.mounts via app.use.-> ROUTER1 ROUTER1 -.can nest.-> ROUTER2 ``` **Tier 1: Application** - The top-level Express application is itself a router with routing methods (`app.get()`, `app.post()`, etc.). It lazily creates an internal router instance when first accessed. **Tier 2: Router** - Standalone router instances created with `express.Router()`. Routers can be mounted on the application or other routers using `app.use()` or `router.use()`. They provide modular, mountable route handlers. **Tier 3: Route** - Individual route objects that match a specific path pattern and contain stacks of method-specific handler functions. Created when you call routing methods like `app.get('/path', handler)`. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:69-82](), [lib/express.js:70-71](), [test/app.router.js:12-37]() --- ## Router Class The `Router` class is provided by the external `router` package and exposed as `express.Router`. It manages a stack of layers (middleware and routes) and dispatches requests through them. ### Router Constructor and Options ```mermaid graph LR CREATE["express.Router(options)"] ROUTER["Router Instance"] OPTIONS["Options:
- caseSensitive
- strict
- mergeParams"] CREATE --> ROUTER OPTIONS -.configures.-> CREATE ROUTER --> HANDLE["router.handle(req, res, next)"] ROUTER --> USE["router.use(path, ...middleware)"] ROUTER --> ROUTE["router.route(path)"] ROUTER --> PARAM["router.param(name, callback)"] ROUTER --> METHODS["router.get/post/put/delete/..."] ``` **Key Router Options:** - `caseSensitive` - Enable case-sensitive routing (default: `false`) - `strict` - Enable strict routing (trailing slashes matter, default: `false`) - `mergeParams` - Preserve parent router's `req.params` values (default: `false`) **Sources:** [lib/application.js:74-77](), [test/Router.js:1-17](), [test/app.router.js:302-317]() ### Router Methods | Method | Purpose | Example | |--------|---------|---------| | `router.handle(req, res, done)` | Dispatch a request through the router stack | Called internally by Express | | `router.use(path?, ...fn)` | Mount middleware or sub-routers | `router.use('/api', apiRouter)` | | `router.route(path)` | Create a Route object for the path | `router.route('/users')` | | `router.param(name, fn)` | Register parameter callback | `router.param('id', loadUser)` | | `router.get/post/...` | Register method-specific routes | `router.get('/users', handler)` | **Sources:** [lib/express.js:70-71](), [test/Router.js:10-29](), [test/app.router.js:39-62]() --- ## Route Class The `Route` class represents a single path pattern and contains a stack of handlers for different HTTP methods. Routes are created when you register handlers via `app.get()`, `router.get()`, etc. ### Route Structure ```mermaid graph TB ROUTE["Route Object
path: '/users/:id'"] subgraph "Method Stacks" GET_STACK["GET handlers:
[auth, validate, getUser]"] POST_STACK["POST handlers:
[auth, createUser]"] ALL_STACK["ALL handlers:
[logger, cors]"] end DISPATCH["route.dispatch(req, res, done)"] ROUTE --> GET_STACK ROUTE --> POST_STACK ROUTE --> ALL_STACK ROUTE --> DISPATCH DISPATCH -.executes.-> ALL_STACK DISPATCH -.then executes.-> GET_STACK DISPATCH -.or.-> POST_STACK ``` ### Route Methods Each Route object provides methods for all HTTP verbs plus `all()`: ```javascript // Route methods (from tests) route.get(handler1, handler2, ...) // GET requests route.post(handler1, handler2, ...) // POST requests route.put(handler1, handler2, ...) // PUT requests route.delete(handler1, handler2, ...) // DELETE requests route.all(handler1, handler2, ...) // ALL methods ``` Handlers added via `route.all()` execute before method-specific handlers for all HTTP methods. **Sources:** [lib/express.js:70](), [test/Route.js:9-14](), [test/Route.js:47-104]() ### Route Dispatch Process The `route.dispatch(req, res, done)` method executes handlers in order: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Router participant Route participant AllHandlers participant MethodHandlers Router->>Route: dispatch(req, res, done) Route->>Route: Check req.method matches alt Method matches or is 'all' Route->>AllHandlers: Execute all() handlers AllHandlers->>AllHandlers: Call each with (req, res, next) AllHandlers-->>Route: Continue via next() Route->>MethodHandlers: Execute method-specific handlers MethodHandlers->>MethodHandlers: Call each with (req, res, next) MethodHandlers-->>Router: Complete via done() else Method doesn't match Route-->>Router: Skip route end ``` **Sources:** [test/Route.js:47-104](), [test/Route.js:143-168]() --- ## Application as Router The Express application itself implements the router interface through lazy initialization. When routing methods are first called, the application creates an internal `Router` instance. ### Lazy Router Initialization ```mermaid graph TB APP_INIT["app.init()"] DEFINE_PROP["Object.defineProperty(app, 'router', {...})"] GETTER["get: function getrouter()"] APP_INIT --> DEFINE_PROP DEFINE_PROP --> GETTER FIRST_ACCESS["First access to app.router"] CREATE_ROUTER["router = new Router({
caseSensitive: app.enabled('case sensitive routing'),
strict: app.enabled('strict routing')
})"] RETURN_ROUTER["return router"] GETTER --> FIRST_ACCESS FIRST_ACCESS --> CREATE_ROUTER CREATE_ROUTER --> RETURN_ROUTER LATER_ACCESS["Later accesses"] CACHED["return cached router"] LATER_ACCESS --> CACHED ``` The router is created with settings from the application configuration. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:59-83]() ### Application Routing Methods The application proxies routing methods to its internal router: | Application Method | Maps To | Description | |-------------------|---------|-------------| | `app.use(path?, ...fn)` | `router.use()` | Mount middleware/routers | | `app.route(path)` | `router.route()` | Create a Route object | | `app.param(name, fn)` | `router.param()` | Register param callback | | `app.get(path, ...fn)` | `router.get()` | Register GET handler | | `app.post(path, ...fn)` | `router.post()` | Register POST handler | | `app.put/delete/all/...` | `router.*()` | Other HTTP methods | **Sources:** [lib/application.js:190-244](), [lib/application.js:256-258](), [lib/application.js:322-334](), [lib/application.js:471-503]() --- ## Router Creation and Mounting ### Creating Router Instances Routers are created using `express.Router()`: ```javascript // Create router with options const router = express.Router({ caseSensitive: false, // Case-insensitive by default strict: false, // Trailing slashes optional mergeParams: false // Don't preserve parent params }); ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:71](), [test/Router.js:10-17](), [test/app.router.js:302-317]() ### Mounting Routers Routers are mounted using `app.use()` or `router.use()`: ```mermaid graph TB APP["Express Application"] ROUTER1["API Router
(mounted at /api)"] ROUTER2["User Router
(mounted at /users)"] ROUTER3["Admin Router
(mounted at /admin)"] APP -->|"app.use('/api', apiRouter)"| ROUTER1 ROUTER1 -->|"apiRouter.use('/users', userRouter)"| ROUTER2 APP -->|"app.use('/admin', adminRouter)"| ROUTER3 REQ1["/api/users/123"] REQ2["/admin/dashboard"] REQ1 -.routes to.-> ROUTER1 ROUTER1 -.routes to.-> ROUTER2 REQ2 -.routes to.-> ROUTER3 ``` When a router is mounted: 1. The mount path is stripped from `req.url` for the mounted router 2. The router's handlers execute in the context of the stripped path 3. After the router completes, `req.url` is restored **Sources:** [lib/application.js:190-244](), [test/app.use.js:22-61](), [test/Router.js:19-42]() ### Mounting with Parameter Inheritance When `mergeParams: true` is set, child routers preserve parent `req.params`: ```javascript const childRouter = express.Router({ mergeParams: true }); childRouter.get('/:action', (req, res) => { // req.params contains both 'user' and 'action' res.send(req.params); }); app.use('/user/:user', childRouter); // GET /user/tj/profile => req.params = { user: 'tj', action: 'profile' } ``` Without `mergeParams`, the child router overwrites `req.params` entirely. **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:288-333]() --- ## Route Registration Routes are registered using HTTP method functions on the application or router. ### Method-Specific Routes ```mermaid graph LR APP["app or router"] APP -->|"get(path, ...handlers)"| GET["Route (GET)"] APP -->|"post(path, ...handlers)"| POST["Route (POST)"] APP -->|"put(path, ...handlers)"| PUT["Route (PUT)"] APP -->|"delete(path, ...handlers)"| DELETE["Route (DELETE)"] APP -->|"all(path, ...handlers)"| ALL["Route (ALL)"] GET --> ROUTE1["Route Object
path: '/users'
method: 'GET'"] POST --> ROUTE2["Route Object
path: '/users'
method: 'POST'"] ``` Each method call creates or retrieves a `Route` object and adds handlers to it: ```javascript app.get('/users', handler1, handler2); app.post('/users', handler3); // Creates Route('/users') with GET and POST stacks ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:471-482](), [test/app.router.js:39-62]() ### Chaining Routes with app.route() The `app.route(path)` method creates a single Route object and returns it for chaining: ```javascript app.route('/users') .get(handler1) .post(handler2) .put(handler3); // All methods share the same Route object ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:256-258]() ### Multiple Handlers Per Route Routes accept multiple handler functions which execute in sequence: ```mermaid graph LR ROUTE["Route.get()"] H1["handler1"] H2["handler2"] H3["handler3"] ROUTE --> H1 H1 -->|"next()"| H2 H2 -->|"next()"| H3 H3 -->|"done()"| COMPLETE["Complete"] ``` **Sources:** [test/Route.js:84-104](), [test/app.router.js:12-37]() --- ## Request Dispatch Flow When a request enters Express, it flows through the routing hierarchy: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant app.handle participant app.router participant Router.handle participant Layer participant Route participant Handler Client->>app.handle: HTTP Request app.handle->>app.handle: Setup req/res prototypes app.handle->>app.router: Access router (lazy init) app.handle->>Router.handle: router.handle(req, res, done) loop For each layer in stack Router.handle->>Layer: Check if path matches alt Path matches Layer->>Layer: Extract req.params alt Layer is middleware Layer->>Handler: Execute middleware(req, res, next) Handler-->>Router.handle: next() else Layer is route Layer->>Route: route.dispatch(req, res, next) Route->>Route: Check if method matches alt Method matches Route->>Handler: Execute handlers Handler-->>Route: Continue or complete else Method doesn't match Route-->>Router.handle: Skip to next layer end end else Path doesn't match Layer-->>Router.handle: Skip to next layer end end Router.handle-->>app.handle: done() app.handle-->>Client: HTTP Response ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:152-178](), [test/Router.js:162-178](), [test/Route.js:9-44]() ### Path Matching in Layers Each layer in the router stack contains: - A path pattern (string, regex, or parameterized string) - A handler function (middleware or Route) - Path matching logic that extracts parameters When a layer's path matches, `req.params` is populated with extracted parameters before the handler executes. **Sources:** High-level diagrams (Diagram 4), [test/app.router.js:94-142]() --- ## Parameter Processing Parameter callbacks are executed before route handlers when a named parameter is present in the path. ### Registering Parameter Callbacks ```javascript // Application-level param app.param('id', (req, res, next, id) => { User.findById(id, (err, user) => { if (err) return next(err); req.user = user; next(); }); }); // Router-level param router.param('category', (req, res, next, category) => { req.category = category; next(); }); ``` ### Parameter Callback Execution ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Router participant ParamCallbacks participant Route participant Handler Router->>Router: Match layer with path '/users/:id' Router->>Router: Extract req.params.id = '123' Router->>ParamCallbacks: Execute app.param('id') callbacks ParamCallbacks->>ParamCallbacks: Load user by ID ParamCallbacks->>ParamCallbacks: Set req.user = user ParamCallbacks-->>Router: next() Router->>Route: route.dispatch(req, res, done) Route->>Handler: Execute route handlers Handler->>Handler: Access req.user (already loaded) ``` Parameter callbacks execute **before** route handlers, allowing you to load resources or validate parameters once per request. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:322-334](), [test/Router.js:232-271]() ### Parameter Arrays The `app.param()` method accepts an array of parameter names: ```javascript app.param(['id', 'userId'], (req, res, next, value) => { // Called for both :id and :userId parameters next(); }); ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:322-334]() --- ## Router Configuration ### Case Sensitivity By default, routes are case-insensitive: ```javascript app.get('/User', handler); // Matches: /User, /user, /USER app.enable('case sensitive routing'); app.get('/User', handler); // Only matches: /User ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:74-77](), [test/app.router.js:243-284]() ### Strict Routing By default, trailing slashes are optional: ```javascript app.get('/users', handler); // Matches: /users, /users/ app.enable('strict routing'); app.get('/users', handler); // Only matches: /users app.get('/users/', handler2); // Only matches: /users/ ``` Note: Middleware with `app.use()` is more lenient and matches regardless of strict routing. **Sources:** [lib/application.js:74-77](), [test/app.router.js:423-575]() --- ## Router Stack Management Routers maintain an internal stack of layers, processed in order: ```mermaid graph TB ROUTER["Router Instance"] subgraph "Layer Stack (Execution Order)" L1["Layer 1: Middleware
path: '/', handler: logger"] L2["Layer 2: Middleware
path: '/', handler: auth"] L3["Layer 3: Route
path: '/users', method: GET"] L4["Layer 4: Router
path: '/api', handler: apiRouter"] L5["Layer 5: Route
path: '/users/:id', method: GET"] end ROUTER --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` Layers are executed sequentially until: - A handler sends a response - An error occurs - No more layers match **Sources:** [test/Router.js:91-160](), [test/app.use.js:125-171]() --- ## Error Handling in Routes Route handlers support error handling via 4-arity functions: ```javascript route.all((req, res, next) => { next(new Error('Something failed')); }); // 3-arity handlers are skipped when error present route.all((req, res, next) => { // This won't execute }); // 4-arity handlers catch errors route.all((err, req, res, next) => { console.error(err); next(err); // Pass to next error handler }); ``` For detailed error handling flow, see [3.3](#3.3). **Sources:** [test/Route.js:170-274](), [test/Router.js:208-291]() --- ## Route Ordering and Precedence Routes are matched in the order they are registered: ```javascript app.get('/users/admin', handler1); // Matches first app.get('/users/:id', handler2); // Would match /users/admin if above wasn't registered ``` The first matching route handles the request. To allow multiple routes to process a request, use `next('route')` to skip to the next matching route (see [3.3](#3.3)). **Sources:** [test/app.router.js:169-241]() --- ## Module Exports Express exposes routing components for external use: ```javascript const express = require('express'); // Router constructor const Router = express.Router; // From 'router' package const router = new Router(); // Route constructor (less commonly used directly) const Route = express.Route; // From 'router' package // Convenience method const router2 = express.Router(); // Same as new Router() ``` **Sources:** [lib/express.js:68-82]() --- # Page: Path Matching and Parameters # Path Matching and Parameters
