## Purpose and Scope This document covers Express's built-in body parsing middleware functions that parse incoming request bodies and populate `req.body`. These middleware are re-exported from the `body-parser` package and handle JSON, URL-encoded forms, plain text, and raw binary data. For information about static file serving, see [Static File Middleware](#4.3). For request object properties, see [Request Object](#2.2). --- ## Overview Express provides four body parsing middleware functions that are re-exported from the `body-parser` dependency: | Middleware | Content-Type | Output Format | Primary Use Case | |------------|--------------|---------------|------------------| | `express.json()` | `application/json` | JavaScript Object | REST APIs, JSON payloads | | `express.urlencoded()` | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` | JavaScript Object | HTML forms | | `express.text()` | `text/plain` | String | Plain text data | | `express.raw()` | `application/octet-stream` | Buffer | Binary data, file uploads | All body parsers populate `req.body` with the parsed result and skip requests without matching `Content-Type` headers. **Sources:** [lib/express.js:77-81](), [package.json:36]() --- ## Module Exports and Dependencies ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Express Module" EXPRESS["lib/express.js
createApplication"] EXPORTS["Module Exports"] end subgraph "body-parser Dependency" BP_PKG["body-parser@^2.2.1"] BP_JSON["bodyParser.json"] BP_URL["bodyParser.urlencoded"] BP_TEXT["bodyParser.text"] BP_RAW["bodyParser.raw"] end subgraph "Exported Middleware" JSON["express.json"] URL["express.urlencoded"] TEXT["express.text"] RAW["express.raw"] end subgraph "Application Usage" APP["Express App"] MIDDLEWARE["app.use()"] REQ_BODY["req.body"] end BP_PKG --> BP_JSON BP_PKG --> BP_URL BP_PKG --> BP_TEXT BP_PKG --> BP_RAW EXPRESS --> EXPORTS BP_JSON --> JSON BP_URL --> URL BP_TEXT --> TEXT BP_RAW --> RAW EXPORTS --> JSON EXPORTS --> URL EXPORTS --> TEXT EXPORTS --> RAW JSON --> MIDDLEWARE URL --> MIDDLEWARE TEXT --> MIDDLEWARE RAW --> MIDDLEWARE MIDDLEWARE --> APP APP --> REQ_BODY style EXPRESS fill:#e1f5ff style BP_PKG fill:#fff4e1 style REQ_BODY fill:#ffe1e1 ``` **Middleware Re-export Architecture** The body parsing middleware are simple re-exports from the `body-parser` module. Express does not implement body parsing logic itself; it delegates to the external `body-parser` package. **Sources:** [lib/express.js:15](), [lib/express.js:77-81](), [package.json:36]() --- ## express.json() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: application/json` and populates `req.body` with a JavaScript object. Returns an empty object `{}` for empty bodies. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse JSON bodies app.use(express.json()); app.post('/api/users', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body.name); // Access parsed JSON res.json({ received: req.body }); }); ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `reviver` | Function | `null` | JSON.parse reviver function | | `strict` | Boolean | `true` | Only accept arrays and objects | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'application/json'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Behavior - Returns `400` when JSON is malformed - Returns `413` when body exceeds `limit` - Returns `415` when `Content-Encoding` is unsupported (if `inflate: false`) - Sets `req.body = {}` for empty request bodies - Skips requests without matching `Content-Type` ### Error Scenarios ```javascript // Malformed JSON POST /api/users Content-Type: application/json {"name": // Missing closing brace // Response: 400 [entity.parse.failed] Unexpected end of JSON input // Body too large POST /api/users Content-Type: application/json Content-Length: 1034 {"str": "...1028 bytes..."} // Response: 413 [entity.too.large] request entity too large ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:1-569](), [lib/express.js:77]() --- ## express.urlencoded() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and populates `req.body` with a JavaScript object. This is the format used by HTML form submissions. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse URL-encoded bodies app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: false })); app.post('/login', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body.username); // Access form field res.send('Logged in'); }); ``` ### Extended Option The `extended` option determines which parsing library is used: | Mode | Parser | Supports | Example | |------|--------|----------|---------| | `extended: false` | `querystring` | Simple key-value pairs | `user=tobi&age=2` | | `extended: true` | `qs` | Nested objects, arrays | `user[name]=tobi&user[age]=2` | When `extended: false` (default since Express 5.x): ```javascript // Input: user=Tobi&user=Loki // Output: { user: ["Tobi", "Loki"] } ``` When `extended: true`: ```javascript // Input: user[name][first]=Tobi // Output: { user: { name: { first: "Tobi" } } } // Input: foo[0]=bar&foo[1]=baz // Output: { foo: ["bar", "baz"] } ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `extended` | Boolean | `false` | Use `qs` (true) or `querystring` (false) | | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `parameterLimit` | Number | `1000` | Maximum number of parameters | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Depth Limiting When using `extended: true`, the `qs` parser limits nesting depth to prevent deep object attacks: ```javascript // Default depth limit (32 levels) app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // Custom depth app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true, depth: 10 })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.urlencoded.js:1-497](), [lib/express.js:81]() --- ## express.text() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: text/plain` and populates `req.body` with a string. Returns an empty string `""` for empty bodies. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse plain text bodies app.use(express.text()); app.post('/notes', (req, res) => { console.log(typeof req.body); // 'string' res.send(`Received: ${req.body}`); }); ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `defaultCharset` | String | `'utf-8'` | Default character encoding | | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'text/plain'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Character Encoding The parser respects the `charset` parameter in the `Content-Type` header: ```javascript // Request with explicit charset POST /notes Content-Type: text/plain; charset=koi8-r // Body bytes: 6e616d6520697320cec5d4 // Parsed as KOI8-R: "name is нет" // Default charset configuration app.use(express.text({ defaultCharset: 'iso-8859-1' })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.text.js:1-410](), [lib/express.js:80]() --- ## express.raw() Parses incoming request bodies with `Content-Type: application/octet-stream` and populates `req.body` with a `Buffer`. Returns an empty buffer for empty bodies. ### Basic Usage ```javascript const app = express(); // Parse raw binary bodies app.use(express.raw()); app.post('/upload', (req, res) => { console.log(Buffer.isBuffer(req.body)); // true console.log(req.body.length); // byte count res.send('Uploaded'); }); ``` ### Options | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `inflate` | Boolean | `true` | Enable deflate/gzip decompression | | `limit` | String/Number | `'100kb'` | Maximum request body size | | `type` | String/Array/Function | `'application/octet-stream'` | Content-Type filter | | `verify` | Function | `undefined` | Custom verification function | ### Binary Data Handling ```javascript // Receiving binary data POST /upload Content-Type: application/octet-stream // Handler app.post('/upload', (req, res) => { const hex = req.body.toString('hex'); const base64 = req.body.toString('base64'); res.json({ bytes: req.body.length, hex: hex }); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.raw.js:1-350](), [lib/express.js:78]() --- ## Common Options All four body parsing middleware share several common options: ### inflate Controls whether compressed request bodies are automatically decompressed: ```javascript // Accept gzip/deflate compression (default) app.use(express.json({ inflate: true })); // Reject compressed bodies app.use(express.json({ inflate: false })); // Response: 415 [encoding.unsupported] content encoding unsupported ``` ### limit Controls the maximum request body size. Accepts byte values or string notation: ```javascript // String notation app.use(express.json({ limit: '1kb' })); app.use(express.json({ limit: '10mb' })); // Numeric bytes app.use(express.json({ limit: 1024 })); // Exceeding limit // Response: 413 [entity.too.large] request entity too large ``` ### type Filters which `Content-Type` headers trigger the parser: ```javascript // String (exact match or shorthand) app.use(express.json({ type: 'application/json' })); app.use(express.json({ type: 'json' })); // shorthand // Array of types app.use(express.json({ type: ['application/json', 'application/vnd.api+json'] })); // Function predicate app.use(express.json({ type: (req) => { return req.get('Content-Type').startsWith('application/'); } })); ``` ### verify Provides a hook to verify or transform the raw body before parsing: ```javascript app.use(express.json({ verify: (req, res, buf, encoding) => { // buf is the raw Buffer // Throw error to reject request if (buf.length > 1000000) { throw new Error('Body too large'); } // Compute signature for verification req.rawBody = buf.toString(encoding); } })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:121-194](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:211-239](), [test/express.text.js:95-169](), [test/express.raw.js:86-157]() --- ## Error Handling Body parsing middleware generate structured errors that can be caught by error-handling middleware: ```mermaid graph TB REQUEST["HTTP Request"] PARSER["Body Parser
Middleware"] CHECK_CT["Check
Content-Type"] READ_BODY["Read Body
from Stream"] CHECK_SIZE["Check Size
vs limit"] INFLATE_CHECK["Decompress
if needed"] PARSE["Parse Body
by Type"] POPULATE["Set req.body"] NEXT["next()"] ERROR["next(err)"] REQUEST --> PARSER PARSER --> CHECK_CT CHECK_CT -->|No Match| NEXT CHECK_CT -->|Match| READ_BODY READ_BODY --> CHECK_SIZE CHECK_SIZE -->|Too Large| ERROR CHECK_SIZE -->|OK| INFLATE_CHECK INFLATE_CHECK -->|Unsupported| ERROR INFLATE_CHECK -->|OK| PARSE PARSE -->|Parse Error| ERROR PARSE -->|Success| POPULATE POPULATE --> NEXT ERROR -.->|400 Bad Request| ERR_PARSE["entity.parse.failed"] ERROR -.->|413 Too Large| ERR_SIZE["entity.too.large"] ERROR -.->|415 Unsupported| ERR_ENCODING["encoding.unsupported"] ERROR -.->|400 Bad Length| ERR_LENGTH["request.aborted"] ``` **Body Parser Error Flow** ### Error Types | Error Code | Status | Description | Example Cause | |------------|--------|-------------|---------------| | `entity.parse.failed` | 400 | Parse failed | Malformed JSON | | `entity.too.large` | 413 | Body exceeds limit | File too large | | `encoding.unsupported` | 415 | Unknown encoding | Brotli compression | | `request.aborted` | 400 | Invalid Content-Length | Mismatch between header and body | | `request.size.invalid` | 400 | Invalid size | Negative Content-Length | ### Error Handling Example ```javascript const app = express(); app.use(express.json()); app.post('/api/data', (req, res) => { res.json(req.body); }); // Error handler catches body parsing errors app.use((err, req, res, next) => { if (err.status === 400) { return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid request body', message: err.message }); } if (err.status === 413) { return res.status(413).json({ error: 'Request too large', message: 'Body exceeds size limit' }); } next(err); }); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:90-119](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:23-42](), [test/express.json.js:122-150]() --- ## Request Processing Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express as Express App participant Parser as Body Parser participant Handler as Route Handler Client->>Express: POST /api/users
Content-Type: application/json
{"name":"tobi"} Express->>Parser: app.use(express.json()) Parser->>Parser: Check Content-Type header alt Content-Type matches Parser->>Parser: Read request stream Parser->>Parser: Check body size vs limit alt Size OK Parser->>Parser: Inflate if compressed Parser->>Parser: Parse body (JSON.parse) alt Parse Success Parser->>Parser: Set req.body = {name: "tobi"} Parser->>Express: next() Express->>Handler: Route matched Handler->>Handler: Access req.body.name Handler->>Client: 200 OK {"received": {...}} else Parse Error Parser->>Express: next(err) [400] Express->>Client: 400 Bad Request end else Size Too Large Parser->>Express: next(err) [413] Express->>Client: 413 Request Too Large end else Content-Type no match Parser->>Express: next() (skip) Express->>Handler: Continue to route end ``` **Body Parser Request Flow** **Sources:** [lib/express.js:77-81](), [lib/application.js:190-244]() --- ## Multiple Body Parsers Applications commonly use multiple body parsers to handle different content types: ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); // Parse JSON bodies app.use(express.json()); // Parse URL-encoded bodies (HTML forms) app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // Parse plain text app.use(express.text({ type: 'text/plain' })); // Parse raw binary app.use(express.raw({ type: 'application/octet-stream' })); // Routes now handle multiple content types app.post('/data', (req, res) => { // req.body populated based on Content-Type res.json({ received: req.body }); }); ``` ### Duplicate Middleware Body parsers can be used multiple times without issues. They skip requests that don't match their `Content-Type` filter: ```javascript // This is valid (though redundant) app.use(express.json()); app.use(express.json()); // Both parsers execute, but second one sees // req.body already populated and passes through ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:73-88](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:62-77]() --- ## Content-Type Filtering Body parsers only process requests matching their configured `Content-Type`. Non-matching requests skip the parser entirely: ```javascript const app = express(); // Only parses application/json app.use(express.json()); // POST with text/plain -> req.body undefined // POST with application/json -> req.body populated // POST with application/xml -> req.body undefined ``` ### Custom Type Filters ```javascript // Parse multiple JSON-like types app.use(express.json({ type: ['application/json', 'application/vnd.api+json'] })); // Function-based filtering app.use(express.json({ type: (req) => { const ct = req.get('Content-Type') || ''; return ct.includes('json') || ct.includes('api'); } })); // Parse all text/* types as JSON app.use(express.json({ type: 'text/*' })); ``` **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:207-282](), [test/express.text.js:201-290](), [test/express.raw.js:189-271]() --- ## AsyncLocalStorage Support All body parsers preserve `AsyncLocalStorage` context across asynchronous operations: ```javascript const { AsyncLocalStorage } = require('async_hooks'); const asyncLocalStorage = new AsyncLocalStorage(); app.use((req, res, next) => { const store = { requestId: Date.now() }; asyncLocalStorage.run(store, next); }); app.use(express.json()); app.post('/data', (req, res) => { const store = asyncLocalStorage.getStore(); console.log(store.requestId); // Still accessible res.json(req.body); }); ``` This ensures that any context stored in `AsyncLocalStorage` remains available after body parsing completes, even though the parsing is asynchronous. **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:319-384](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:337-398](), [test/express.text.js:327-388](), [test/express.raw.js:311-372]() --- ## Integration with Request Object After successful parsing, body parsers populate `req.body`. This property is not initialized until a body parser runs: ```javascript app.post('/data', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body); // undefined before body parsing }); app.use(express.json()); app.post('/data', (req, res) => { console.log(req.body); // Object, string, or Buffer after parsing console.log(typeof req.body); // Varies by parser }); ``` ### Body Property Types | Parser | `req.body` Type | Empty Body Value | |--------|-----------------|------------------| | `express.json()` | Object | `{}` | | `express.urlencoded()` | Object | `{}` | | `express.text()` | String | `""` | | `express.raw()` | Buffer | `Buffer.alloc(0)` | **Sources:** [test/express.json.js:11-41](), [test/express.urlencoded.js:15-60](), [test/express.text.js:14-58](), [test/express.raw.js:15-63]() --- # Page: Static File Middleware # Static File Middleware

## Purpose and Scope This document describes the static file serving middleware in Express.js, which is exposed as `express.static()`. This middleware serves static files (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images, etc.) from a specified directory on the filesystem. It handles content negotiation, caching, range requests, and security considerations for file serving. For serving individual files programmatically, see [File Operations](#5.2) which covers `res.sendFile()` and `res.download()`. For general middleware concepts, see [Middleware System](#4). ## Overview The static file middleware is a built-in middleware function that serves static assets from a directory. It is implemented as a wrapper around the external `send` module, providing options for security, performance, and behavior customization. **Key Characteristics:** - Serves files from a specified root directory - Automatically sets appropriate `Content-Type` headers based on file extensions - Supports conditional requests (ETags, Last-Modified) - Handles byte-range requests for partial content delivery - Provides security by preventing directory traversal attacks - Configurable caching behavior Sources: [test/express.static.js:1-816]() ## Basic Usage Pattern ```mermaid graph LR App["express()"] --> Use["app.use(path, middleware)"] Use --> Static["express.static(root, options)"] Static --> ServeFiles["Serve files from
filesystem"] Request["HTTP GET /file.js"] --> App ServeFiles --> Response["200 + file content"] ``` **Diagram: Basic Static Middleware Setup** The static middleware is typically mounted at a specific path or at the application root: ```javascript // Serve files from 'public' directory at root app.use(express.static('public')) // Serve files from 'assets' at /static path app.use('/static', express.static('assets')) ``` Sources: [test/express.static.js:18-20](), [test/express.static.js:706-710]() ## Middleware Initialization ```mermaid graph TB StaticCall["express.static(root, options)"] ValidateRoot["Validate root path
(must be string)"] CreateMiddleware["Create middleware function
using send module"] StaticCall --> ValidateRoot ValidateRoot --> CreateMiddleware CreateMiddleware --> MiddlewareFunc["Return middleware(req, res, next)"] subgraph "Validation Rules" V1["Root path is required"] V2["Root must be string"] end ``` **Diagram: Static Middleware Creation** The middleware requires a root directory path and optionally accepts configuration options. The root path validation occurs immediately when `express.static()` is called. Sources: [test/express.static.js:22-28]() ## Configuration Options The static middleware accepts an options object to control its behavior: | Option | Type | Default | Description | |--------|------|---------|-------------| | `acceptRanges` | Boolean | true | Enable/disable byte-range request support | | `cacheControl` | Boolean | true | Enable/disable Cache-Control header | | `dotfiles` | String | 'ignore' | How to handle dotfiles: 'allow', 'deny', 'ignore' | | `etag` | Boolean | true | Enable/disable ETag generation | | `extensions` | Array/String/Boolean | false | File extensions to try when path has no extension | | `fallthrough` | Boolean | true | Continue to next middleware on error vs send error | | `immutable` | Boolean | false | Add 'immutable' to Cache-Control header | | `index` | String/Array/Boolean | 'index.html' | Directory index file name(s) | | `lastModified` | Boolean | true | Enable/disable Last-Modified header | | `maxAge` | Number/String | 0 | Cache max-age in milliseconds or time string | | `redirect` | Boolean | true | Redirect to trailing slash for directories | | `setHeaders` | Function | undefined | Callback to set custom headers | Sources: [test/express.static.js:150-802]() ### Accept Ranges Option Controls whether the middleware advertises and honors byte-range requests: ```mermaid graph LR Request["GET /file.txt
Range: bytes=0-4"] subgraph "acceptRanges: true" Check1["Check Range header"] Send206["Send 206 Partial Content
Content-Range: bytes 0-4/9"] end subgraph "acceptRanges: false" IgnoreRange["Ignore Range header"] Send200["Send 200 OK
Full content"] end Request --> Check1 Request --> IgnoreRange Check1 --> Send206 IgnoreRange --> Send200 ``` **Diagram: Accept Ranges Behavior** Sources: [test/express.static.js:150-186]() ### Cache Control Options The middleware provides fine-grained control over HTTP caching: ```mermaid graph TB CacheControl{"cacheControl
enabled?"} MaxAge["Apply maxAge setting
(default: 0)"] Immutable{"immutable
option?"} Header1["Cache-Control:
public, max-age=0"] Header2["Cache-Control:
public, max-age=3600, immutable"] NoHeader["No Cache-Control header"] CacheControl -->|Yes| MaxAge CacheControl -->|No| NoHeader MaxAge --> Immutable Immutable -->|Yes| Header2 Immutable -->|No| Header1 ``` **Diagram: Cache Control Header Generation** **Max-Age Values:** - Accepts milliseconds as number: `maxAge: 20000` → `max-age=20` - Accepts time strings: `maxAge: '30d'` → `max-age=2592000` - Capped at 1 year maximum - Floored to integer seconds Sources: [test/express.static.js:188-213](), [test/express.static.js:418-430](), [test/express.static.js:452-466]() ### Dotfiles Option Controls handling of files/directories starting with a dot: | Value | Behavior | |-------|----------| | `'allow'` | Serve dotfiles normally | | `'deny'` | Respond with 403 Forbidden | | `'ignore'` | Respond with 404 Not Found (default) | Sources: [test/express.static.js:404-416](), [test/express.static.js:130-134]() ### Extensions Option Enables automatic file extension resolution when the request path lacks an extension: ```javascript // With extensions: ['html', 'txt'] // Request: GET /readme // Tries: /readme.html, then /readme.txt app.use(express.static(fixtures, { extensions: ['htm', 'html', 'txt'] })) ``` Sources: [test/express.static.js:215-245]() ### Fallthrough Option Determines error handling behavior: ```mermaid graph TB Error["Error occurs
(404, 403, etc)"] Fallthrough{"fallthrough
option"} NextMiddleware["Call next()
Continue to next middleware"] SendError["Send error response
(405, 400, 403, 404)"] Error --> Fallthrough Fallthrough -->|true| NextMiddleware Fallthrough -->|false| SendError subgraph "Error Types (fallthrough: false)" E1["OPTIONS request → 405"] E2["Malformed URL → 400"] E3["Path traversal → 403"] E4["File not found → 404"] end ``` **Diagram: Fallthrough Error Handling** When `fallthrough: true` (default), errors pass through to subsequent middleware. When `false`, the static middleware sends error responses directly. Sources: [test/express.static.js:247-402]() ### SetHeaders Callback Allows custom header manipulation for successful file serves: ```javascript app.use(express.static(fixtures, { setHeaders: function(res, path, stat) { res.setHeader('X-Custom', 'value') } })) ``` **Important:** The callback is only invoked for successful file serves, not for 404s or redirects. Sources: [test/express.static.js:541-573]() ## Request Processing Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Static as express.static participant Send as send module participant FS as File System Client->>Static: GET /path/to/file.js Static->>Static: Validate request method
(GET or HEAD only) Static->>Static: Parse and normalize path Static->>Static: Check for directory traversal alt Path traversal detected Static->>Client: 403 Forbidden (or next()) else Valid path Static->>Send: Resolve file path Send->>FS: Check file existence alt File exists FS->>Send: File metadata (size, mtime) Send->>Send: Generate ETag Send->>Send: Check If-None-Match alt 304 Not Modified Send->>Client: 304 (no body) else Send file Send->>Send: Process Range header alt Range request Send->>Client: 206 Partial Content else Full file Send->>Client: 200 OK + file content end end else File not found Send->>Static: 404 error Static->>Client: 404 Not Found (or next()) end end ``` **Diagram: Static File Request Processing** Sources: [test/express.static.js:30-47](), [test/express.static.js:96-101](), [test/express.static.js:103-114]() ## Conditional Request Handling The static middleware supports HTTP conditional requests for efficient caching: ```mermaid graph TB Request["Incoming request"] CheckETag{"ETag header
enabled?"} CheckIfNoneMatch{"If-None-Match
header present?"} CompareETag{"ETag matches?"} CheckLastMod{"Last-Modified
enabled?"} CheckIfModSince{"If-Modified-Since
header present?"} CompareTime{"File modified
since date?"} Send304["Send 304 Not Modified
(no body)"] SendFull["Send full response
200 OK with body"] Request --> CheckETag CheckETag -->|Yes| CheckIfNoneMatch CheckETag -->|No| CheckLastMod CheckIfNoneMatch -->|Yes| CompareETag CheckIfNoneMatch -->|No| CheckLastMod CompareETag -->|Match| Send304 CompareETag -->|No Match| CheckLastMod CheckLastMod -->|Yes| CheckIfModSince CheckLastMod -->|No| SendFull CheckIfModSince -->|Yes| CompareTime CheckIfModSince -->|No| SendFull CompareTime -->|Not Modified| Send304 CompareTime -->|Modified| SendFull ``` **Diagram: Conditional Request Flow** **ETags:** - Automatically generated based on file size and modification time - Sent as `ETag` header in response - Client sends back in `If-None-Match` for validation - Can be disabled via `etag: false` option **Last-Modified:** - Based on file system modification time - Sent as `Last-Modified` header - Client sends back in `If-Modified-Since` for validation - Can be disabled via `lastModified: false` option Sources: [test/express.static.js:103-115](), [test/express.static.js:432-449]() ## Range Request Support The middleware supports HTTP range requests for partial content delivery: ```mermaid graph TB Request["GET /file.txt
Range: bytes=0-4"] ValidateRange{"Range header
syntactically valid?"} CheckBounds{"Range within
file bounds?"} SendPartial["206 Partial Content
Content-Range: bytes 0-4/9
Content-Length: 5"] SendFull["200 OK
Full file content"] Send416["416 Range Not Satisfiable
Content-Range: bytes */9"] Request --> ValidateRange ValidateRange -->|Invalid| SendFull ValidateRange -->|Valid| CheckBounds CheckBounds -->|In bounds| SendPartial CheckBounds -->|Out of bounds| Send416 subgraph "Supported Range Formats" R1["bytes=0-4 (positions 0 to 4)"] R2["bytes=-3 (last 3 bytes)"] R3["bytes=3- (from position 3 to end)"] end ``` **Diagram: Range Request Processing** **Range Request Behavior:** - Returns 206 Partial Content for valid ranges - Sets `Content-Range` header with byte positions - Adjusts `Content-Length` to partial size - Returns 416 if range exceeds file size - Returns 200 with full content for invalid range syntax Sources: [test/express.static.js:593-690]() ## Directory Handling ```mermaid graph TB DirRequest["GET /users"] IsDir{"Path is
directory?"} HasSlash{"Has trailing
slash?"} RedirectOpt{"redirect
option enabled?"} IndexOpt{"index option
enabled?"} IndexExists{"index.html
exists?"} Redirect["301 Redirect
Location: /users/"] ServeIndex["Serve index.html"] Next["Call next()
(404 if fallthrough)"] DirRequest --> IsDir IsDir -->|No| Next IsDir -->|Yes| HasSlash HasSlash -->|No| RedirectOpt HasSlash -->|Yes| IndexOpt RedirectOpt -->|Yes| Redirect RedirectOpt -->|No| Next IndexOpt -->|true| IndexExists IndexOpt -->|false| Next IndexExists -->|Yes| ServeIndex IndexExists -->|No| Next ``` **Diagram: Directory Request Handling** **Directory Behavior:** - Redirects directories without trailing slash (if `redirect: true`) - Serves index file from directory (default: `index.html`) - Index serving can be disabled with `index: false` - Custom index file names: `index: 'default.html'` or `index: ['index.html', 'index.htm']` Sources: [test/express.static.js:468-539](), [test/express.static.js:767-801]() ## Security Considerations ```mermaid graph TB Request["Request path"] Normalize["Normalize path
(resolve .. and .)"] CheckTraversal{"Path escapes
root directory?"} Serve["Serve file"] Deny["403 Forbidden
(or next() if fallthrough)"] Request --> Normalize Normalize --> CheckTraversal CheckTraversal -->|No| Serve CheckTraversal -->|Yes| Deny subgraph "Blocked Patterns" B1["../../etc/passwd"] B2["users/../../secret.txt"] B3["%2e%2e%2f (encoded)"] end ``` **Diagram: Path Traversal Prevention** **Security Features:** - Prevents access to files outside the root directory - Handles URL-encoded path traversal attempts - Validates and normalizes all file paths - Dotfiles hidden by default (configurable) **Examples of Blocked Requests:** - `/users/../../todo.txt` - traverses above root - `/%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2ftodo.txt` - URL-encoded traversal - `/../name.txt` - attempts to access parent directory Sources: [test/express.static.js:575-591]() ## Mounting and Path Resolution The static middleware resolves file paths relative to where it's mounted: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Mounted at Root" R1["app.use(express.static('/public'))"] R2["GET /file.js → /public/file.js"] end subgraph "Mounted at Path" M1["app.use('/static', express.static('/public'))"] M2["GET /static/file.js → /public/file.js"] end subgraph "Redirect Behavior" D1["GET /static/users → 301 /static/users/"] D2["Preserves mount point in redirect"] end ``` **Diagram: Mount Point Resolution** When mounted at a specific path, redirects and URL resolution maintain the mount prefix. Sources: [test/express.static.js:692-724]() ## Integration with Response Methods The static middleware internally uses similar mechanisms as `res.sendFile()`: | Feature | express.static | res.sendFile | |---------|---------------|--------------| | **Use Case** | Directory of files | Single file programmatically | | **ETag Support** | Yes | Yes | | **Range Requests** | Yes | Yes | | **Conditional Requests** | Yes | Yes | | **Security** | Root-constrained | Options-based | | **Caching** | Via options | Via options | Both ultimately delegate to the `send` module for file streaming and header management. Sources: [test/express.static.js:1-816](), [test/res.sendFile.js:1-914]() ## Error Handling Patterns ```mermaid graph TB Error["Error occurs"] Type{"Error type"} Fallthrough{"fallthrough
option"} E404["File not found"] E403["Forbidden"] E400["Bad request"] E405["Method not allowed"] Next["next() - pass to
next middleware"] SendErr["Send error response"] Error --> Type Type --> E404 Type --> E403 Type --> E400 Type --> E405 E404 --> Fallthrough E403 --> Fallthrough E400 --> Fallthrough E405 --> Fallthrough Fallthrough -->|true| Next Fallthrough -->|false| SendErr ``` **Diagram: Error Handling Strategy** With `fallthrough: true` (default), the application can provide custom 404 pages or error handlers. With `fallthrough: false`, the static middleware sends errors directly. **Common Error Scenarios:** - File not found (404) - Directory traversal attempt (403) - Malformed URL (400) - Non-GET/HEAD method (405 when `fallthrough: false`) Sources: [test/express.static.js:247-402]() ## Response Headers Headers automatically set by the static middleware: | Header | Source | Controlled By | |--------|--------|---------------| | `Content-Type` | File extension detection | Automatic | | `Content-Length` | File size | Automatic | | `ETag` | File stats (size + mtime) | `etag` option | | `Last-Modified` | File mtime | `lastModified` option | | `Cache-Control` | Configuration | `cacheControl`, `maxAge`, `immutable` options | | `Accept-Ranges` | Configuration | `acceptRanges` option | | `Content-Range` | Range request handling | `acceptRanges` option + Range header | | `Content-Security-Policy` | For redirects | Automatic (default-src 'none') | Sources: [test/express.static.js:42-61](), [test/express.static.js:515-520]() ## Performance Considerations **Efficient Caching:** - ETags enable client-side caching validation - `maxAge` controls browser cache duration - `immutable` directive prevents revalidation for versioned assets **Conditional Requests:** - 304 responses avoid transmitting unchanged files - Reduces bandwidth and improves load times **Range Requests:** - Enable resume for large file downloads - Allow seeking in video/audio files - Partial content delivery for mobile clients **Best Practices:** - Use longer `maxAge` for versioned/fingerprinted assets - Enable `immutable` for build artifacts with hash in filename - Keep `fallthrough: true` for custom 404 handling - Use `setHeaders` callback for route-specific headers Sources: [test/express.static.js:418-430](), [test/express.static.js:103-115]() ## Usage Patterns Summary ```javascript // Basic usage - serve from 'public' directory app.use(express.static('public')) // Mounted at path with options app.use('/static', express.static('assets', { maxAge: '1d', etag: true, lastModified: true, index: 'index.html' })) // High-performance configuration for CDN-like serving app.use('/cdn', express.static('dist', { maxAge: '1y', immutable: true, etag: true, fallthrough: false })) // Development configuration app.use(express.static('public', { maxAge: 0, etag: true, setHeaders: (res) => { res.setHeader('X-Dev-Mode', 'true') } })) ``` Sources: [test/express.static.js:17-20](), [test/express.static.js:706-710]() --- # Page: Response Methods # Response Methods

This document provides a comprehensive overview of the Express response object (`res`) and its methods for sending HTTP responses. Response methods handle content delivery, file transfers, redirects, cookies, headers, and caching. These methods are the primary interface for applications to send data back to clients. For detailed documentation of specific response method categories, see: - [Content Delivery Methods](#5.1) - `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()`, `res.format()` - [File Operations](#5.2) - `res.sendFile()`, `res.download()` - [Redirects and Location](#5.3) - `res.redirect()`, `res.location()` - [Cookie Management](#5.4) - `res.cookie()`, `res.clearCookie()` - [Headers and Caching](#5.5) - `res.status()`, `res.set()`, `res.get()`, ETag handling For template rendering, see [Template Rendering](#6.2). ## Response Object Architecture The Express response object extends Node.js's `http.ServerResponse` prototype and is created during application initialization. Each request receives a response object with its prototype chain set to the application's response prototype. **Response Prototype Creation and Extension** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Module Level" HTTP["http.ServerResponse.prototype
(Node.js)"] RES_PROTO["res = Object.create(http.ServerResponse.prototype)
[lib/response.js:42]"] end subgraph "Application Level" APP_INIT["app.init()
[lib/application.js:59]"] APP_RES["app.response = Object.create(res)
[lib/express.js:50]"] end subgraph "Request Level" HANDLE["app.handle(req, res)
[lib/application.js:152]"] PROTO_SET["Object.setPrototypeOf(res, app.response)
[lib/application.js:170]"] REQ_RES["res object with full prototype chain"] end HTTP --> RES_PROTO RES_PROTO --> APP_RES APP_INIT --> APP_RES APP_RES --> PROTO_SET HANDLE --> PROTO_SET PROTO_SET --> REQ_RES REQ_RES --> METHODS["Response Methods:
send(), json(), redirect(), etc."] ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:42](), [lib/express.js:45-52](), [lib/application.js:152-178]() ## Response Method Categories All response methods are defined on the response prototype and organized into functional categories: | Category | Methods | Purpose | |----------|---------|---------| | **Status Control** | `status()`, `sendStatus()` | Set HTTP status code and send status message | | **Content Delivery** | `send()`, `json()`, `jsonp()` | Send response body with automatic content-type handling | | **Content Negotiation** | `format()` | Select response format based on Accept header | | **File Transfer** | `sendFile()`, `download()`, `attachment()` | Stream files to client with proper headers | | **Redirection** | `redirect()`, `location()` | Send redirect responses and set Location header | | **Header Management** | `set()`, `get()`, `append()`, `type()`, `vary()`, `links()` | Manipulate response headers | | **Cookie Operations** | `cookie()`, `clearCookie()` | Set and delete cookies | | **Template Rendering** | `render()` | Render templates using view engines | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:1-1048]() ## Response Method Implementation Pattern Response methods follow a consistent pattern: they modify the response object state, set appropriate headers, and either return `this` for chaining or call `this.end()` to finalize the response. **Method Chaining and Response Finalization** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Handler as "Route Handler" participant Res as "res Object" participant HTTP as "http.ServerResponse" Handler->>Res: res.status(200) Note over Res: this.statusCode = 200 Res-->>Handler: return this Handler->>Res: res.type('json') Note over Res: Set Content-Type header Res-->>Handler: return this Handler->>Res: res.send({data}) Note over Res: Convert to JSON string
Set Content-Length
Generate ETag if enabled

[lib/utils.js:130-152] Client->>Send: Request with If-None-Match header Send->>Send: Check if ETag header already set alt ETag not set and etag fn exists Send->>ETagFn: etagFn(chunk, encoding) Note over ETagFn: Generate ETag hash
[lib/utils.js:249-257] ETagFn-->>Send: ETag value Send->>Send: this.set('ETag', etag) end Send->>Fresh: req.fresh Note over Fresh: Compares ETag and Last-Modified
[lib/request.js:469-486] alt Response is fresh Fresh-->>Send: true Send->>Send: this.status(304) Note over Send: 304 Not Modified else Response is stale Fresh-->>Send: false Note over Send: Send full response end Send->>Client: Response with ETag header ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:161-189](), [lib/response.js:192](), [lib/utils.js:130-152](), [lib/utils.js:249-257](), [lib/request.js:469-486]() ## Content-Type Determination Express sets Content-Type headers automatically based on the response body type: | Body Type | Method | Content-Type | |-----------|--------|--------------| | `String` | `res.send()` | `text/html; charset=utf-8` (unless already set) | | `Buffer` / `Uint8Array` | `res.send()` | `application/octet-stream` (unless already set) | | `Object` / `Array` / `Number` / `Boolean` | `res.send()` | Delegates to `res.json()` → `application/json; charset=utf-8` | | File path | `res.sendFile()` | Determined by file extension via `mime-types` module | | Template | `res.render()` | `text/html; charset=utf-8` (default, can be overridden) | The `mime-types` package is used for file extension to MIME type mapping [package.json:50](). **Sources:** [lib/response.js:133-157](), [lib/response.js:232-246](), [lib/response.js:371-413](), [lib/response.js:503-510]() ## Special Status Code Handling Certain HTTP status codes receive special treatment in `res.send()`: **Status Code Special Cases** ```mermaid graph TB SEND["res.send() with body"] SEND --> STATUS_CHECK{"this.statusCode"} STATUS_CHECK -->|"204 No Content"| STRIP_204["Remove:
- Content-Type
- Content-Length
- Transfer-Encoding
Set chunk = ''"] STATUS_CHECK -->|"304 Not Modified"| STRIP_304["Remove:
- Content-Type
- Content-Length
- Transfer-Encoding
Set chunk = ''"] STATUS_CHECK -->|"205 Reset Content"| STRIP_205["Set Content-Length: '0'
Remove Transfer-Encoding
Set chunk = ''"] STATUS_CHECK -->|"Other codes"| NORMAL["Send body as-is"] STRIP_204 --> END["this.end(chunk)"] STRIP_304 --> END STRIP_205 --> END NORMAL --> END ``` Per HTTP specification, 204 and 304 responses must not include a message body, while 205 must have Content-Length set to 0. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:195-207]() ## Response Method Reference Below is a comprehensive list of all response methods with brief descriptions: ### Status Methods - **`res.status(code)`** - Set HTTP status code [lib/response.js:64-76]() - **`res.sendStatus(statusCode)`** - Send status code with standard message [lib/response.js:321-328]() ### Content Delivery Methods (see [Content Delivery Methods](#5.1)) - **`res.send(body)`** - Send response with automatic content-type handling [lib/response.js:125-218]() - **`res.json(obj)`** - Send JSON response [lib/response.js:232-246]() - **`res.jsonp(obj)`** - Send JSONP response with callback support [lib/response.js:260-304]() - **`res.format(obj)`** - Perform content negotiation based on Accept header [lib/response.js:569-594]() ### File Methods (see [File Operations](#5.2)) - **`res.sendFile(path, options, callback)`** - Transfer file at path [lib/response.js:371-413]() - **`res.download(path, filename, options, callback)`** - Transfer file as attachment [lib/response.js:433-482]() - **`res.attachment(filename)`** - Set Content-Disposition to attachment [lib/response.js:604-612]() ### Redirect Methods (see [Redirects and Location](#5.3)) - **`res.redirect(url)`** or **`res.redirect(status, url)`** - Redirect to URL [lib/response.js:812-864]() - **`res.location(url)`** - Set Location header [lib/response.js:794-796]() ### Header Methods (see [Headers and Caching](#5.5)) - **`res.set(field, val)`** / **`res.header(field, val)`** - Set header field [lib/response.js:664-686]() - **`res.get(field)`** - Get header field value [lib/response.js:696-698]() - **`res.append(field, val)`** - Append to header field [lib/response.js:629-641]() - **`res.type(type)`** / **`res.contentType(type)`** - Set Content-Type [lib/response.js:503-510]() - **`res.vary(field)`** - Add field to Vary header [lib/response.js:875-879]() - **`res.links(links)`** - Set Link header with relations [lib/response.js:97-110]() ### Cookie Methods (see [Cookie Management](#5.4)) - **`res.cookie(name, value, options)`** - Set cookie [lib/response.js:742-775]() - **`res.clearCookie(name, options)`** - Clear cookie [lib/response.js:709-716]() ### Template Methods (see [Template Rendering](#6.2)) - **`res.render(view, options, callback)`** - Render template [lib/response.js:894-918]() ## Helper Functions The response module includes internal helper functions: - **`sendfile(res, file, options, callback)`** - Pipes file stream to response with error handling [lib/response.js:921-1009]() - **`stringify(value, replacer, spaces, escape)`** - JSON.stringify with HTML character escaping [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() These functions are not exposed publicly but are used internally by response methods. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:921-1009](), [lib/response.js:1023-1047]() ## Settings That Affect Response Methods Several application settings influence response method behavior: | Setting | Methods Affected | Description | |---------|------------------|-------------| | `etag` | `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()` | Controls ETag generation (true, false, 'weak', 'strong', or function) | | `json escape` | `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()` | When enabled, escapes `<`, `>`, `&` in JSON output | | `json replacer` | `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()` | Passed to JSON.stringify() as replacer function | | `json spaces` | `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()` | Passed to JSON.stringify() for pretty-printing | | `jsonp callback name` | `res.jsonp()` | Query parameter name for JSONP callback (default: 'callback') | | `trust proxy` | All (via `req.fresh`) | Affects proxy header trust for freshness checks | | `x-powered-by` | All | Sets X-Powered-By header when enabled | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:161-189](), [lib/response.js:232-246](), [lib/response.js:260-304](), [lib/application.js:90-141]() ## Method Chaining Most response methods return `this` to enable method chaining: ```javascript res.status(200) .type('json') .set('X-Custom-Header', 'value') .send({ data: 'example' }); ``` The exception is the final sending method (`send()`, `json()`, `jsonp()`, `sendFile()`, `download()`, `redirect()`, `render()`), which terminates the response by calling `this.end()`. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:64-76](), [lib/response.js:503-510](), [lib/response.js:664-686](), [lib/response.js:125-218]() --- # Page: Content Delivery Methods # Content Delivery Methods

## Purpose and Scope This document covers Express's primary methods for delivering content to clients: `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.jsonp()`, and `res.format()`. These methods handle the core responsibility of generating and sending HTTP response bodies with appropriate headers. For file-based responses, see [File Operations](#5.2). For HTTP redirects, see [Redirects and Location](#5.3). For cookie handling, see [Cookie Management](#5.4). --- ## Overview Express provides four primary content delivery methods that work together to handle different response scenarios: | Method | Purpose | Content-Type Behavior | |--------|---------|----------------------| | `res.send()` | Universal content sending | Auto-detected based on input type | | `res.json()` | JSON responses | Always `application/json` | | `res.jsonp()` | JSONP responses | `text/javascript` with callback, otherwise `application/json` | | `res.format()` | Content negotiation | Based on `Accept` header and handler mapping | All methods ultimately flow through `res.send()`, which handles the low-level details of response generation. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125-601]() --- ## Core Sending Method: res.send() ### Function Signature and Input Types The `res.send()` method accepts multiple input types and automatically determines the appropriate Content-Type header: ```javascript res.send([body]) ``` **Supported Input Types:** | Input Type | Content-Type | Behavior | |------------|--------------|----------| | `string` | `text/html; charset=utf-8` | Sent as UTF-8 encoded text | | `number` | `application/json; charset=utf-8` | Converted to JSON | | `boolean` | `application/json; charset=utf-8` | Converted to JSON | | `object` | `application/json; charset=utf-8` | Delegates to `res.json()` | | `Buffer` | `application/octet-stream` | Sent as binary data | | `Uint8Array` | `application/octet-stream` | Sent as binary data (Express 5.1+) | | `null` | No Content-Type | Empty body | | `undefined` | No Content-Type | Empty body | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125-154]() ### Content Delivery Flow ```mermaid graph TB send["res.send(body)"] typeCheck["Check typeof body"] string["string type"] number["boolean/number type"] object["object type"] setHTML["Set Content-Type: text/html"] checkView["ArrayBuffer.isView()?"] setBinary["Set Content-Type: application/octet-stream"] delegateJSON["Delegate to res.json()"] encoding["Set encoding = 'utf8'"] setCharset["Update Content-Type charset to utf-8"] etagCheck["ETag generation enabled?"] calcLength["Calculate Content-Length"] genETag["Generate ETag via app.get('etag fn')"] freshCheck["Check req.fresh"] set304["Set status 304"] statusCheck["Check statusCode"] strip204304["Strip headers for 204/304"] strip205["Strip Transfer-Encoding, set Content-Length: 0 for 205"] headCheck["req.method === 'HEAD'?"] endEmpty["res.end()"] endBody["res.end(chunk, encoding)"] send --> typeCheck typeCheck --> string typeCheck --> number typeCheck --> object string --> setHTML setHTML --> encoding encoding --> setCharset number --> delegateJSON object --> checkView checkView -->|Yes| setBinary checkView -->|No| delegateJSON setCharset --> calcLength setBinary --> calcLength delegateJSON --> calcLength calcLength --> etagCheck etagCheck -->|Yes| genETag etagCheck -->|No| freshCheck genETag --> freshCheck freshCheck -->|Fresh| set304 freshCheck -->|Not Fresh| statusCheck set304 --> statusCheck statusCheck -->|204 or 304| strip204304 statusCheck -->|205| strip205 statusCheck -->|Other| headCheck strip204304 --> headCheck strip205 --> headCheck headCheck -->|Yes| endEmpty headCheck -->|No| endBody ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125-225]() ### Content-Length and ETag Generation The method calculates `Content-Length` based on the body type: 1. **Buffer objects**: Use `chunk.length` directly 2. **Small strings (< 1000 bytes) without ETag**: Use `Buffer.byteLength(chunk, encoding)` 3. **Other cases**: Convert to Buffer and use `chunk.length` **ETag Generation Logic:** ```mermaid graph LR check["ETag header exists?"] hasEtagFn["typeof app.get('etag fn') === 'function'?"] calcETag["etagFn(chunk, encoding)"] setETag["Set ETag header"] skip["Skip ETag generation"] check -->|No| hasEtagFn check -->|Yes| skip hasEtagFn -->|Yes| calcETag hasEtagFn -->|No| skip calcETag --> setETag ``` The `etag fn` setting is configured via `app.set('etag', ...)` which accepts: - `'weak'` or `true`: Uses weak ETag (default) - `'strong'`: Uses strong ETag - `false`: Disables ETag generation - Custom function: `function(chunk, encoding) => string` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:167-196](), [lib/utils.js:40-51, 130-152]() ### Freshness Checking and Conditional Responses When `req.fresh` is `true` (meaning client cache is valid based on ETag or Last-Modified headers), `res.send()` automatically returns a `304 Not Modified` response: ```javascript // freshness check if (req.fresh) this.status(304); ``` The freshness check is performed by the `fresh` module using headers from both request and response: - Request: `If-None-Match`, `If-Modified-Since` - Response: `ETag`, `Last-Modified` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:198-199](), [lib/request.js:456-473]() ### Special Status Code Handling Specific status codes receive special treatment: | Status Code | Behavior | |-------------|----------| | `204` No Content | Removes `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Transfer-Encoding`; sets empty body | | `304` Not Modified | Removes `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Transfer-Encoding`; sets empty body | | `205` Reset Content | Removes `Transfer-Encoding`; sets `Content-Length: 0`; sets empty body | | `HEAD` method | Calls `res.end()` without body regardless of status | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:201-222]() ### Charset Handling For string responses, `res.send()` enforces UTF-8 encoding: ```javascript if (typeof chunk === 'string') { encoding = 'utf8'; type = this.get('Content-Type'); if (typeof type === 'string') { this.set('Content-Type', setCharset(type, 'utf-8')); } } ``` The `setCharset` utility function parses the existing Content-Type, updates the charset parameter, and reformats it. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:156-165](), [lib/utils.js:225-238]() --- ## JSON Responses: res.json() ### Method Implementation The `res.json()` method provides a streamlined interface for JSON responses: ```javascript res.json([body]) ``` ```mermaid graph LR json["res.json(obj)"] getSettings["Get app settings"] escape["app.get('json escape')"] replacer["app.get('json replacer')"] spaces["app.get('json spaces')"] stringify["stringify(obj, replacer, spaces, escape)"] setContentType["Content-Type: application/json"] send["res.send(body)"] json --> getSettings getSettings --> escape getSettings --> replacer getSettings --> spaces escape --> stringify replacer --> stringify spaces --> stringify stringify --> setContentType setContentType --> send ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:239-253]() ### Configuration Settings Three application settings control JSON serialization: | Setting | Purpose | Default | |---------|---------|---------| | `json spaces` | Indentation spaces for `JSON.stringify()` | `undefined` (compact) | | `json replacer` | Replacer function for `JSON.stringify()` | `undefined` | | `json escape` | Escape `<`, `>`, `&` for XSS protection | `undefined` (disabled) | **Example Configuration:** ```javascript app.set('json spaces', 2); // Pretty-print JSON app.set('json replacer', function(key, val) { return key[0] === '_' ? undefined : val; // Hide private properties }); app.enable('json escape'); // Escape HTML-sniffing characters ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:241-245]() ### The stringify() Helper Function The internal `stringify()` function handles JSON conversion with optional character escaping: ```javascript function stringify(value, replacer, spaces, escape) { var json = replacer || spaces ? JSON.stringify(value, replacer, spaces) : JSON.stringify(value); if (escape && typeof json === 'string') { json = json.replace(/[<>&]/g, function (c) { switch (c.charCodeAt(0)) { case 0x3c: return '\\u003c' case 0x3e: return '\\u003e' case 0x26: return '\\u0026' } }) } return json } ``` When `json escape` is enabled, the characters `<`, `>`, and `&` are replaced with Unicode escape sequences (`\u003c`, `\u003e`, `\u0026`) to prevent HTML sniffing attacks where browsers might interpret JSON as HTML. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:1029-1053]() --- ## JSONP Support: res.jsonp() ### Method Implementation JSONP (JSON with Padding) wraps JSON responses in a JavaScript function call for cross-origin requests: ```javascript res.jsonp([body]) ``` ```mermaid graph TB jsonp["res.jsonp(obj)"] getSettings["Get settings and serialize"] stringify["stringify(obj, replacer, spaces, escape)"] getCallback["Get callback from query params"] hasCallback{"callback exists\nand non-empty?"} setJSONP["Set Content-Type: text/javascript"] setNoSniff["Set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff"] sanitize["Sanitize callback name"] escapeUnicode["Escape \\u2028 and \\u2029"] wrap["Wrap in: /**/ typeof callback === 'function' && callback(body);"] setJSON["Set Content-Type: application/json"] send["res.send(body)"] jsonp --> getSettings getSettings --> stringify stringify --> getCallback getCallback --> hasCallback hasCallback -->|Yes| setJSONP setJSONP --> setNoSniff setNoSniff --> sanitize sanitize --> escapeUnicode escapeUnicode --> wrap wrap --> send hasCallback -->|No| setJSON setJSON --> send ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:267-311]() ### Callback Name Configuration The callback parameter name defaults to `'callback'` but can be customized: ```javascript app.set('jsonp callback name', 'cb'); // Use ?cb=myFunction instead of ?callback=myFunction ``` The callback is retrieved from the query string: ```javascript var callback = this.req.query[app.get('jsonp callback name')]; ``` If the query parameter is an array (e.g., `?callback=fn1&callback=fn2`), only the first element is used. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:274, 283-285]() ### Security Measures JSONP responses include multiple security protections: 1. **Callback Sanitization**: Only allows alphanumeric characters, `$`, `_`, `[`, `]`, and `.`: ```javascript callback = callback.replace(/[^\[\]\w$.]/g, ''); ``` 2. **X-Content-Type-Options Header**: Prevents MIME-type sniffing: ```javascript this.set('X-Content-Type-Options', 'nosniff'); ``` 3. **Rosetta Flash Mitigation**: Prepends `/**/` to prevent flash-based exploits: ```javascript body = '/**/ typeof ' + callback + ' === \'function\' && ' + callback + '(' + body + ');'; ``` 4. **Unicode Line Separator Escaping**: Escapes `\u2028` and `\u2029` which are valid JSON but invalid JavaScript string literals: ```javascript body = body .replace(/\u2028/g, '\\u2028') .replace(/\u2029/g, '\\u2029') ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:288-307]() ### JSONP Response Examples **With Callback:** ``` GET /api/users?callback=handleUsers Response: Content-Type: text/javascript; charset=utf-8 X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff /**/ typeof handleUsers === 'function' && handleUsers({"users":[...]}); ``` **Without Callback:** ``` GET /api/users Response: Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8 {"users":[...]} ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:267-311]() --- ## Content Negotiation: res.format() ### Method Overview The `res.format()` method performs server-driven content negotiation based on the `Accept` header: ```javascript res.format({ 'text/plain': function() { res.send('text') }, 'text/html': function() { res.send('

html

') }, 'application/json': function() { res.json({msg: 'json'}) }, 'default': function() { res.status(406).send('Not Acceptable') } }) ``` ```mermaid graph TB format["res.format(obj)"] extractKeys["Extract keys except 'default'"] negotiate["req.accepts(keys)"] hasMatch{"Match found?"} normalizeType["normalizeType(key)"] setContentType["Set Content-Type"] invoke["Invoke handler(req, res, next)"] hasDefault{"Has 'default'\nhandler?"} invokeDefault["Invoke default(req, res, next)"] create406["Create 406 error"] nextError["Call next(err)"] setVary["Set Vary: Accept"] format --> extractKeys extractKeys --> negotiate negotiate --> hasMatch format --> setVary hasMatch -->|Yes| normalizeType normalizeType --> setContentType setContentType --> invoke hasMatch -->|No| hasDefault hasDefault -->|Yes| invokeDefault hasDefault -->|No| create406 create406 --> nextError ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:576-601]() ### Content Type Matching The method uses the `accepts` module to match against the `Accept` header: 1. **Extract Handlers**: All object keys except `'default'` are collected 2. **Negotiate**: `req.accepts(keys)` returns the best match based on quality values 3. **Set Content-Type**: The matched type is normalized and set as the Content-Type header 4. **Invoke Handler**: The corresponding function is called with `(req, res, next)` **Normalization** converts short names to full MIME types: - `'html'` → `'text/html'` - `'json'` → `'application/json'` - `'text'` → `'text/plain'` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:580-591](), [lib/utils.js:61-77]() ### Handler Invocation Each handler receives three arguments: ```javascript function handler(req, res, next) { // req: the request object // res: the response object // next: the next function for passing control } ``` This allows handlers to perform additional middleware operations or error handling within content negotiation. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:591](), [test/res.format.js:20-26, 55-60]() ### Default Handler If no content type matches and a `'default'` handler is provided, it is invoked instead of returning a 406 error: ```javascript res.format({ 'text/html': function() { res.send('

html

') }, 'default': function() { // Called when Accept doesn't match text/html res.send('default response'); } }) ``` The default handler can also delegate to other format handlers: ```javascript res.format({ 'application/json': function() { res.json({data: 'json'}) }, 'default': function() { // Fallback to JSON this.json(); } }) ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:592-593](), [test/res.format.js:131-151]() ### Vary Header The method automatically sets `Vary: Accept` to inform caches that the response varies based on the Accept header: ```javascript this.vary("Accept"); ``` This is critical for proper HTTP caching when the same URL can return different content types. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:587]() ### Error Handling When no match is found and no default handler exists, a 406 Not Acceptable error is created and passed to the error handling middleware: ```javascript next(createError(406, { types: normalizeTypes(keys).map(function (o) { return o.value }) })) ``` The error includes a `types` array listing the available content types. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:595-597]() --- ## Helper Method: res.sendStatus() The `res.sendStatus()` method combines status code setting with a body containing the status message: ```javascript res.sendStatus(statusCode) ``` Implementation: ```javascript res.sendStatus = function sendStatus(statusCode) { var body = statuses.message[statusCode] || String(statusCode) this.status(statusCode); this.type('txt'); return this.send(body); }; ``` **Examples:** ```javascript res.sendStatus(200); // OK res.sendStatus(404); // Not Found res.sendStatus(500); // Internal Server Error res.sendStatus(999); // 999 (unknown status) ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:328-335]() --- ## Integration with Application Settings Content delivery methods integrate with various application-level settings: ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Application Settings" etagSetting["app.set('etag', value)"] jsonSpaces["app.set('json spaces', n)"] jsonReplacer["app.set('json replacer', fn)"] jsonEscape["app.enable('json escape')"] jsonpCallback["app.set('jsonp callback name', name)"] end subgraph "Compiled Functions" etagFn["app.get('etag fn')"] end subgraph "Response Methods" resSend["res.send()"] resJson["res.json()"] resJsonp["res.jsonp()"] end etagSetting -->|compileETag| etagFn etagFn -->|used by| resSend jsonSpaces -->|used by| resJson jsonReplacer -->|used by| resJson jsonEscape -->|used by| resJson jsonSpaces -->|used by| resJsonp jsonReplacer -->|used by| resJsonp jsonEscape -->|used by| resJsonp jsonpCallback -->|used by| resJsonp resJson -->|delegates to| resSend resJsonp -->|delegates to| resSend ``` **Setting Compilation:** When certain settings are assigned, Express compiles them into optimized functions: ```javascript app.set('etag', 'weak'); // Internally compiles to: // app.set('etag fn', utils.wetag); app.set('etag', function(body, encoding) { return customEtagGenerator(body); }); // Stored directly as function ``` **Sources:** [lib/application.js:364-366](), [lib/utils.js:130-152]() --- ## Request-Response Flow for Content Delivery ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Express participant Middleware participant Handler participant res.send participant res.json participant res.format Client->>Express: HTTP Request Express->>Middleware: Process middleware stack Middleware->>Handler: Route handler alt JSON Response Handler->>res.json: res.json(data) res.json->>res.json: Apply json settings res.json->>res.json: stringify(data) res.json->>res.send: Delegate to send else JSONP Response Handler->>res.jsonp: res.jsonp(data) res.jsonp->>res.jsonp: Check for callback res.jsonp->>res.jsonp: Wrap if callback exists res.jsonp->>res.send: Delegate to send else Content Negotiation Handler->>res.format: res.format(handlers) res.format->>res.format: Match Accept header res.format->>Handler: Invoke matched handler Handler->>res.send: res.send(content) else Direct Send Handler->>res.send: res.send(data) end res.send->>res.send: Detect Content-Type res.send->>res.send: Calculate Content-Length res.send->>res.send: Generate ETag if enabled res.send->>res.send: Check freshness res.send->>res.send: Handle special status codes res.send->>Express: Write headers and body Express->>Client: HTTP Response ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:125-601]() --- ## Code Entity Reference ### Primary Classes and Methods | Entity | Location | Purpose | |--------|----------|---------| | `res.send()` | [lib/response.js:125-225]() | Core content delivery method | | `res.json()` | [lib/response.js:239-253]() | JSON response method | | `res.jsonp()` | [lib/response.js:267-311]() | JSONP response method | | `res.format()` | [lib/response.js:576-601]() | Content negotiation method | | `res.sendStatus()` | [lib/response.js:328-335]() | Status code with message | | `stringify()` | [lib/response.js:1029-1053]() | JSON stringification with escaping | ### Utility Functions | Function | Location | Purpose | |----------|----------|---------| | `setCharset()` | [lib/utils.js:225-238]() | Modify Content-Type charset | | `normalizeType()` | [lib/utils.js:61-65]() | Convert short names to MIME types | | `normalizeTypes()` | [lib/utils.js:75-77]() | Normalize array of types | | `compileETag()` | [lib/utils.js:130-152]() | Compile ETag setting to function | | `createETagGenerator()` | [lib/utils.js:249-257]() | Create ETag generator with options | ### Dependencies | Module | Purpose | |--------|---------| | `statuses` | HTTP status code messages | | `mime-types` | MIME type lookup and handling | | `accepts` | Content negotiation | | `fresh` | HTTP freshness checking | | `etag` | ETag generation | | `content-type` | Content-Type header parsing | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:1-36](), [lib/utils.js:1-272]() --- # Page: File Operations # File Operations

This page documents Express's file serving capabilities through the `res.sendFile()` and `res.download()` response methods. These methods enable efficient streaming of files from the server's filesystem to HTTP clients with support for range requests, conditional requests, and automatic content-type detection. For general response methods including content delivery, see [Content Delivery Methods](#5.1). For headers and caching strategies, see [Headers and Caching](#5.5). --- ## Overview Express provides two primary methods for file operations: | Method | Purpose | Content-Disposition | |--------|---------|---------------------| | `res.sendFile()` | Stream a file directly | Not set (inline display) | | `res.download()` | Prompt file download | Set to `attachment` | Both methods use the `send` module internally for efficient file streaming and support features like ETag generation, range requests, and conditional requests (304 Not Modified). **Sources:** [lib/response.js:330-482]() --- ## Method Signatures and Parameters ### res.sendFile() ``` res.sendFile(path, [options], [callback]) ``` **Title: res.sendFile Parameter Flow** ```mermaid graph TB Input["res.sendFile(path, options, callback)"] Validate["Path Validation"] Input --> Validate CheckPath["Check path type"] Validate --> CheckPath CheckAbs["Check absolute path or root option"] CheckPath --> CheckAbs CreateSend["Create send(req, pathname, opts)"] CheckAbs --> CreateSend Pipe["sendfile() helper pipes to response"] CreateSend --> Pipe Events["Event Handlers:
directory, end, error,
file, stream, headers"] Pipe --> Events Callback["Invoke callback(err)"] Events --> Callback Error1["TypeError: path required"] CheckPath -->|"!path"| Error1 Error2["TypeError: path must be string"] CheckPath -->|"typeof !== 'string'"| Error2 Error3["TypeError: path must be
absolute or specify root"] CheckAbs -->|"!absolute && !root"| Error3 ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:371-413]() ### res.download() ``` res.download(path, [filename], [options], [callback]) ``` The `res.download()` method is a convenience wrapper around `res.sendFile()` that automatically sets the `Content-Disposition` header to prompt a file download. **Title: res.download to res.sendFile Delegation** ```mermaid graph LR Download["res.download(path, filename, options, callback)"] ParseArgs["Parse Arguments
(filename can be function)"] Download --> ParseArgs SetDisposition["Set Content-Disposition header
using contentDisposition()"] ParseArgs --> SetDisposition MergeHeaders["Merge user headers
(except Content-Disposition)"] SetDisposition --> MergeHeaders ResolvePath["Resolve full path
if no root option"] MergeHeaders --> ResolvePath CallSendFile["this.sendFile(fullPath, opts, done)"] ResolvePath --> CallSendFile ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:433-482]() --- ## File Streaming Architecture Express uses the `send` module for all file operations. The internal `sendfile()` helper function manages the streaming pipeline with comprehensive event handling. **Title: File Streaming Pipeline with send Module** ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Response Methods" SendFile["res.sendFile()"] Download["res.download()"] end subgraph "Internal sendfile() Helper" CreateStream["Create send instance:
send(req, pathname, opts)"] EventHandlers["Attach Event Listeners"] PipeStream["file.pipe(res)"] end subgraph "send Module Events" Directory["'directory' event
→ EISDIR error"] End["'end' event
→ success callback"] Error["'error' event
→ error callback"] File["'file' event
→ set streaming=false"] Stream["'stream' event
→ set streaming=true"] Headers["'headers' event
→ apply options.headers"] end subgraph "Response Lifecycle" OnFinished["onFinished(res, onfinish)"] CheckAbort["Check for ECONNABORTED"] InvokeCallback["Invoke callback(err)"] end SendFile --> CreateStream Download --> CreateStream CreateStream --> EventHandlers EventHandlers --> PipeStream PipeStream --> Directory PipeStream --> End PipeStream --> Error PipeStream --> File PipeStream --> Stream PipeStream --> Headers PipeStream --> OnFinished OnFinished --> CheckAbort CheckAbort --> InvokeCallback ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:921-1009]() --- ## Options Configuration Both methods accept an options object that is passed to the `send` module. Express also applies its own ETag configuration. **Title: Options Processing** | Option | Type | Default | Description | Applied By | |--------|------|---------|-------------|------------| | `maxAge` | Number/String | `0` | Cache max-age in ms or parseable string | `send` module | | `root` | String | - | Root directory for relative paths | `send` module | | `headers` | Object | - | Additional headers to set on successful transfer | `sendfile()` helper | | `dotfiles` | String | `'ignore'` | How to handle dotfiles: 'allow', 'deny', 'ignore' | `send` module | | `acceptRanges` | Boolean | `true` | Enable/disable Accept-Ranges support | `send` module | | `cacheControl` | Boolean | `true` | Enable/disable Cache-Control header | `send` module | | `lastModified` | Boolean | `true` | Enable/disable Last-Modified header | `send` module | | `start` | Number | - | Start byte position for partial content | `send` module | | `end` | Number | - | End byte position for partial content | `send` module | | `etag` | - | from `app.enabled('etag')` | ETag generation (set by Express) | Express | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:371-413](), [lib/response.js:433-482]() --- ## Path Resolution and Security ### res.sendFile() Path Requirements The `res.sendFile()` method has strict path requirements to prevent directory traversal attacks: **Title: Path Validation Logic** ```mermaid graph TD Start["res.sendFile(path, options)"] CheckPath["Check: path provided?"] Start --> CheckPath CheckString["Check: typeof path === 'string'?"] CheckPath -->|"Yes"| CheckString CheckAbsolute["Check: pathIsAbsolute(path)?"] CheckString -->|"Yes"| CheckAbsolute CheckRoot["Check: options.root provided?"] CheckAbsolute -->|"No (relative)"| CheckRoot EncodeURI["Encode path: encodeURI(path)"] CheckAbsolute -->|"Yes (absolute)"| EncodeURI CheckRoot -->|"Yes"| EncodeURI CreateSend["Create send instance"] EncodeURI --> CreateSend ErrorMissing["throw TypeError:
'path argument is required'"] CheckPath -->|"No"| ErrorMissing ErrorType["throw TypeError:
'path must be a string'"] CheckString -->|"No"| ErrorType ErrorAbsolute["throw TypeError:
'path must be absolute
or specify root'"] CheckRoot -->|"No"| ErrorAbsolute ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:378-394]() ### res.download() Path Resolution The `res.download()` method uses different path resolution logic: 1. **With `root` option:** Path is passed directly to `sendFile(path, opts)` 2. **Without `root` option:** Path is resolved using `resolve(path)` to create an absolute path This allows `res.download()` to accept relative paths when a `root` option is provided in the options object. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:475-481]() --- ## Content-Disposition Header Handling The `res.download()` method uses the `content-disposition` module to safely encode filenames in the `Content-Disposition` header. **Title: Content-Disposition Header Generation** ```mermaid graph LR Download["res.download(path, filename, options)"] DetermineName["Determine filename:
name || path"] Download --> DetermineName CreateHeader["contentDisposition(name)
Creates RFC-compliant header"] DetermineName --> CreateHeader SetHeaders["headers = {
'Content-Disposition': value
}"] CreateHeader --> SetHeaders MergeUser["Merge user headers
(skip Content-Disposition)"] SetHeaders --> MergeUser AttachOpts["opts.headers = headers"] MergeUser --> AttachOpts CallSendFile["this.sendFile(fullPath, opts, done)"] AttachOpts --> CallSendFile ``` The module handles special characters and non-ASCII filenames by creating both a `filename` and `filename*` parameter per RFC 5987. **Sources:** [lib/response.js:456-473]() --- ## Error Handling ### Error Types and Handling The `sendfile()` helper function handles various error conditions: **Title: Error Handling Flow** ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "Error Events" Directory["'directory' event
EISDIR error"] Error["'error' event
Generic error"] Abort["Connection abort
ECONNABORTED"] end subgraph "Error Classification" CheckCode["Check err.code"] IsEISDIR["err.code === 'EISDIR'"] IsAbort["err.code === 'ECONNABORTED'"] IsWrite["err.syscall === 'write'"] end subgraph "Error Handling" CallbackEISDIR["Callback with EISDIR"] CallNext["next() - Passes to middleware"] NextWithErr["next(err) - Error handler"] CallbackWithErr["callback(err)"] NoAction["No action (write error)"] end Directory --> CheckCode Error --> CheckCode Abort --> CallbackWithErr CheckCode --> IsEISDIR CheckCode --> IsAbort CheckCode --> IsWrite IsEISDIR -->|"Yes & !callback"| CallNext IsEISDIR -->|"Yes & callback"| CallbackEISDIR IsAbort -->|"No"| NoAction IsWrite -->|"Yes"| NoAction IsAbort -->|"No"| NextWithErr IsWrite -->|"No"| NextWithErr ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:936-943](), [lib/response.js:946-950](), [lib/response.js:404-411]() ### Callback vs. next() Behavior The behavior differs based on whether a callback is provided: | Scenario | With Callback | Without Callback | |----------|---------------|------------------| | EISDIR error | `callback(err)` | `next()` (404 middleware) | | File not found | `callback(err)` | `next(err)` (error handler) | | Connection aborted | `callback(err)` | `next()` | | Write error | `callback(err)` | No action (connection closed) | **Sources:** [lib/response.js:404-411]() --- ## Event Lifecycle The `sendfile()` helper attaches multiple event listeners to track the file streaming process: **Title: File Streaming Event Lifecycle** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant R as res.sendFile/download participant S as sendfile() helper participant F as send module (file) participant Res as HTTP Response R->>S: Call with path, options S->>F: Create send(req, pathname, opts) S->>F: Attach event listeners S->>F: file.pipe(res) alt File is directory F->>S: 'directory' event S->>S: ondirectory() → EISDIR error S->>R: callback(EISDIR) else File exists and is regular file F->>S: 'file' event S->>S: onfile() → streaming = false F->>S: 'stream' event S->>S: onstream() → streaming = true F->>S: 'headers' event S->>Res: setHeader(k, options.headers[k]) F->>Res: Pipe file data F->>S: 'end' event S->>S: onend() → mark done = true S->>S: onFinished(res) callback S->>S: Check streaming state S->>R: callback(null) else Error occurs F->>S: 'error' event S->>S: onerror(err) S->>R: callback(err) or next(err) end ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:987-1005]() --- ## Automatic Content-Type Detection Both `res.sendFile()` and `res.download()` automatically set the `Content-Type` header based on file extension: 1. The `send` module uses the `mime-types` package to look up the MIME type 2. Express sets `Content-Type` header before streaming begins 3. Applications can override by calling `res.contentType()` or `res.set()` before invoking the method **Example behavior:** - `.txt` → `text/plain; charset=utf-8` - `.html` → `text/html; charset=utf-8` - `.png` → `image/png` - `.pdf` → `application/pdf` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:133-145](), [test/res.download.js:26-28]() --- ## Range Request Support The `send` module provides automatic range request handling when `acceptRanges` is enabled (default): **Title: Range Request Processing** ```mermaid graph TB Request["Client sends Range header"] CheckOption["Check acceptRanges option"] Request --> CheckOption ParseRange["Parse Range header
(range-parser module)"] CheckOption -->|"true (default)"| ParseRange Validate["Validate range
against file size"] ParseRange --> Validate Partial["206 Partial Content
Content-Range header
Send requested bytes"] Validate -->|"Valid"| Partial Invalid["416 Range Not Satisfiable"] Validate -->|"Invalid"| Invalid Full["200 OK
Accept-Ranges: bytes
Send full file"] CheckOption -->|"false"| Full Validate -->|"No range"| Full ``` **Sources:** [test/res.sendFile.js:346-378](), [test/res.download.js:31-56]() --- ## Conditional Request Support (ETag and Last-Modified) Express automatically enables ETag generation for files when the application's `etag` setting is enabled: **Title: ETag Configuration Flow** ```mermaid graph LR SendFile["res.sendFile()"] GetETagSetting["opts.etag = this.app.enabled('etag')"] SendFile --> GetETagSetting CreateSend["send(req, pathname, opts)"] GetETagSetting --> CreateSend SendModule["send module checks opts.etag"] CreateSend --> SendModule GenerateETag["Generate ETag from file stats"] SendModule -->|"true"| GenerateETag SetHeader["Set ETag header"] GenerateETag --> SetHeader CheckIfNoneMatch["Check If-None-Match"] SetHeader --> CheckIfNoneMatch Return304["304 Not Modified"] CheckIfNoneMatch -->|"Match"| Return304 Return200["200 OK with file"] CheckIfNoneMatch -->|"No match"| Return200 ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:399-401](), [test/res.sendFile.js:66-80]() --- ## Usage Examples ### Basic File Transfer ```javascript app.get('/user/:id/photo', function(req, res) { res.sendFile('/uploads/' + req.params.id + '/photo.jpg'); }); ``` ### With Root Option ```javascript app.get('/files/:name', function(req, res) { res.sendFile(req.params.name, { root: './public/files' }); }); ``` ### With Callback for Error Handling ```javascript app.get('/download/:file', function(req, res) { const file = req.params.file; res.sendFile(file, { root: './files' }, function(err) { if (err) { res.status(err.status || 500).end(); } else { console.log('Sent:', file); } }); }); ``` ### File Download with Custom Filename ```javascript app.get('/report', function(req, res) { res.download('./reports/monthly.pdf', 'report-2024-01.pdf'); }); ``` ### With Options ```javascript app.get('/secure-file', function(req, res) { res.download('/secure/document.pdf', 'document.pdf', { headers: { 'X-Custom-Header': 'value' }, maxAge: '1d' }); }); ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:348-366](), [lib/response.js:430-431](), [test/res.sendFile.js:16-47](), [test/res.download.js:16-73]() --- ## Integration with send Module Express delegates all actual file streaming to the external `send` module, which provides: - **Stream management:** Efficient file streaming with backpressure handling - **Range parsing:** RFC 7233 compliant range request support - **Conditional requests:** ETag and Last-Modified validation - **Error handling:** 404, 403, 416 status code generation - **Security:** Directory traversal protection and dotfile handling - **MIME type detection:** Automatic Content-Type header setting The relationship is: ``` res.sendFile() ──> sendfile() helper ──> send module ──> file stream ──> HTTP response res.download() ─┘ ``` **Sources:** [lib/response.js:31](), [lib/response.js:401](), [package.json:58]() --- # Page: Redirects and Location # Redirects and Location
